Xo special ^polcfgy is necessary for the publication of an English translation of the Sushruta Samhita. The vast medical literature ancient India practically remains as yet unexplored, and any undertaking, which „has the obiect of making that terra incognita, known to thescientific world, is bound to be welcomedby the public. Spasmodic attempts have been made by several scholars and erudite bodies to bring out an English translation of the Sushruta Samhita, as the most representative work of the Ayurveda, but we regret to say that such efforts have hitherto proved abortive. In spite of incomplete infor- mation the subject many drugs the Ayurvedic ?vlateria Medica have been adopted by different foreign systems of medicine, and this has afforded us fresh impetus to issue an English translation of the book, which not only deals with the essentials of Indian Therapeutics but embraces the whole range of the science of A3airveda, it was understood and practised by the Vedic sages. sincerely hope that the English rendering of Sushruta, which have undertaken, will, when completed, supply a long-felt want and help start fuller inquiry into the properties of the indige- nous drugs India. Many institutions have been already started both England and Germany with the sole object of studying the reti(^logy of tropical diseases, and formulating empirical system their prevention and cure, and we, hope an I4nglish translation the Sushruta Samhita, embracing as ^11 does the results researches made our ancient Rishis ill the land of their origin(^ may contribute small amount useful information those bodies. "We have many things to learn" observes Lt. Col. C. Lukis, M.b., F.R.C.S., I.

chapter: Unknown
english_text: M.S., Principal, Medical College, Calcutta, "from the people of this country in respect of medicine and medical science," and five doubt not that an accurate knowledge of the contents this splendid monument of the Ayurveda in quarters where has every chance being utilised and improved upon will make the Inmian race better equipped too combat the ills of life. A few remarks on the method we have adopted editing this work are necessarv by way explana- tion. have carefully collated all the available texts the Sushruta Samhita, whether printed or otherwise, expunging from the body our work all texts, which, though not proved wholly spurious, are questionable authority, and putting them in foot-notes as "Different Readings" or "Additional Texts." cases of doubt or discrepancy of opinion have thought fit abide the decision our revered preceptor, Mahamahopadhyaya Kaviraj Dvarkanath Sen, Kaviratna, and inserted within brackets explanatory clauses, where strictly literal translation of texts would not convey their true meaning. In many instances is impossible to find in the English language equivalent words for the technical terms of the A3'urveda. such cases have put approximate English words within brackets, after the original Sanskrit terms. For exapiple we have translated the term Ojah as albumen. Sut the Ojah of the Ayurveda is a disputed thing, t It may mean something like but not exactl)' albumen; glycogen, which contributes largely the reproductive activity of tlfe body in certaiR)*instances. would appear to be the more correct description. cases like this we h'ave not put before our readers, any suggestions our own, but left them free draw their own inference. And for this purpose we further intend to Append to the last volume this work an index a,nd a glossary illustrating the possible meanings the Ayurvedic terms with English and Latin synonyms, wherever possible. The true meaning the Ayurveda can be better explained or understood only with the light of moder.n science, and we leave to our European colleagues to carry on the research on the lines we have suggested with regard to this ancient S3'stem of medicine, which a better knowledge its principles and methods will enable them to do. By a lamentable oversight, the terms Vayu, Pittani, Kaphah and Dosha have been translated as wind, bile, phlegm and humour in the first few chapters. For the sake of convenience we have divided the entire work into three volumes, the first containing only the Sutrasthanam, the second Xidanam, Sharira and Chikitsa, and the third Kalpa and Uttara Tantram. We have adopted the diagrams of surgical instruments from that most valuable work of the Thakore Saheb of Gondal, called the History the Aryan Medical Science, for which I am particularly indebted to His Highness. In conclusion, we beg to conve\' our sincerest thanks to our preceptor's son Kaviraj Jogendranath Sen Vidya- bhusana M. A., Dr. U. D. Banerji L. R. C. P., M. R. C. S. (Lond), and Lt. Col. K. P. Gupta M.

My thanks are also due to numerous learned authors, ancient and modern 'from whose writings, I ha\e found it necessary to make frequent quotcition^. 10, Kashi Ghose's Laxe. KUNJA LAL BIIISHAGKATNA. Sushruta :—His age and personality :—A few preliminary observations regarding the technique the Ayurvedic system medicine are necessary the outset correctly understand thp aim and scope of the Sushruta Samhita. Who was Sushruta When and where live and flourish These are questions that would naturally suggest themselves to the readers the following pages but the}^ can only imperfectly answered like all similar questions respecting the lives our ancient worthies. country like India where life itself was simply regarded an illusion, the lives of kings or commoners were deemed matters little moment to the vital economy of the race ; and all histories and biographies were looked upon the embodiment of thLe flimsy vanities of life. Lives of saints and canonised kings had been made use of in certain instances as themes of national epics. But they were intended more to elucidate enunciate the doctrines certain schools of Ethics or Metaphysics than record any historical fact event. Authentic history we have none beyond chronicles of state events and royal names in some instances ; and those which are usually found in the Sanskrit Puranas are strange com- binations of myths and legends, which often contradict each other. Hence the utter futility of attempts to explain a historical fact by the light votive npdal tablet unearthed perhaps frorri the ruins of one of sur ancient cities. Such an endeavour serves, most cases, only make the " darkness visible, " • and the confusion more confounded. 'iNTROniTCTIOX. Identity of Sushruta and Divodasa :— It is only safe to assert that Sushruta was of tl>e raceof Vishvamitra. The Mahabharatam f i) represents him as a son of that roval sage. This coincides with the description given of him in the present recension of the Samhita. The Garuda Puranam (2) places Divodasa fourth descent from Dhanvantari, the first propounder medical science earti}, whereas the Sushruta Samhita describes the two as identical persons.

english_text: But this apparent anomaly the Samhita can be accounted for, if we consider that in some parts India the custom still prevails of appending, for the purposes better identi- fication, the name of one's father, or of a glorious ancestor one's name, and therefore not surprising that Divodasa (the preceptor Sushruta), who was firm believer the doctrine of psychic transmigration, should represent himself incarnation Dhanvantari, and a=.sume his name and style in the usual wav. Revond this meagre genealogy we possess trustworthy information regarding the life and personalitv of Sushruta, the father of Indian Surgery. Age of the Sushruta Samhita:—We have no means of ascertaining what the Samhita was like as originally written hv Sushruta, the present being only a recension, or rather a Mahahhiralam— Anushasan Parva, Ch. english_text: We, on the other hand, hold the Uttaratantram neither interpolation, nor a subsequent addition, • but that it forms an integral portion of the book as was originally written, though not planned the Rishi. the first Chapter of Sutrasthanani Divodasa formally divides the Science of Ayurveda into eight subdivisions, such as, the Shalya (surgery), Sh^iakya (portion treating of diseases restricted super-clavicular regions such the eyes, etc.), Kaya-Chikitsa (general diseases such as, fever, etc.), but does not speak anything about them in the first five Sthcinas subdivisions the book. is only once in the 25th chapter the Sutrasthanani that mentions the name Netravartma (diseases of the eyelids) in con- nection with the classification of surgical operations. inxpossible that Divodasa would fall short his duties by omitting to give instructions on all the subdivisions the Ayurveda as he promises at the outset, or that Sushruta would leave his Samhita, which pre-eminently a work surgery, incomplete banishiiTg_^ophthalmic ^surgery, laryngotomy fever-therapeutics from his work. From the general plan the book we can safely assert that Sushruta dealt with easier more elementary topics in the first five subdivisions of his Samhita in the manner of our modern progressive readers, reserving the discussion those re^uirmg more advanced knowledge and skill for the Uttaratantram. The Uttara*^^antram has not been incluued within the five original subdivisions of the Samhita inasmuch it embraces and more elaborately discusses INTRODUCl'ION. lupics which legitimately beloi^g to, ^or are but»incidentairy mentioned those subdivisions. Hence more the nature of an appendix or supplement, arising out of the^ exigencies the original subdivisions. is probable that Nagarjuna might have redacted this part of the Samhita in common with its other portions. (i} Western opipions* on the subject :— The consensus western opinions place Nagarjuna the first quarter the third Century (2), and for fixing Sushruta contemporary of Sakya Sinha Buddha. i^ contended that the age immediately preceding Sakya Muni was a period decadence Hindu thought ; and the Sushruta Samhita must have been the fruit of a revived intellectual activity which usually follows the advent new creed^an assumption which favour the hypothesis of Greek influence the Hindu system medicine. But great men there had been in India before Buddha. The age which immediately preceded the age Buddha was no means an age of decadence properly speaking, the age which followed the downfall Buddliisin shows, on the contrar}', signs of true decadence. India had had eminent philosophers and scientists almost contempo- raneously with the great Buddha.

These facts lend a very plausible colour our hypothesis that the original Sushruta Samhita which, was first composed perhaps con- temporaneously with the latter portions the Atharvan, naturally discussed the opinions Shaunaka and other Vedic embryologists, while Nagarjuna, the time redacting that book, quoted the opinion of his contemporar}' Subhuti for the purpose of giving him an equal status with the Vedic Rishis, if for nothing else. Greek Influence :—As regards Hellenic influence on the Hindu system of medicine and on the Sushruta Samhita special, must disabuse our mind all sentiments of racial vanity and proceed investigate the case scientific and unprejudiced spirit before giving more detailed account of the contents of the SushrutaiSamhita. Nagarjuna Bodhisattva was well practised in the art of compound- ing medicine. N^gSrjuna Bodhisattva by moistening all the great stones with divine and superior decoction changed them into gold.— Bael's Buddhistic Records of the western world Vol. AnuvSk 19, 45. 46. 5. iNTRonurrioN. Su^hrufa and Hippocrates -.—From the very apparent similarity which exists between jhe contents of this Samhita •and tjie aphorisms of Hippocrates, many western scholars are apt conclude too hastily that the ancient Indians drew their inspiration in the healing art from the medical works the Greeks. But the reverse may be said the Greeks as well with the greater confidence because such an assertion is supported by historic facts, and confirmed by the researches the scholars of the west According to all accounts Pythagoras was the founder the healing art amongst the Greeks and the Hellenic peoples in general (2). This great philosopher imbibed his mysteries and metaphysics from the Brahmanas India. Mr. Pocock in his Jnt^ia in Greece identifies him with Buddhagurus Buddha, and but easy inference to suppose that carried many recipes and aphorisms his master's Ayurveda with him. The sacred bean Pythagoras thought to have been the (3) Indian Nelumbium (Utpalam). We know thai simnllaneously with the birth of Buddhism, Buddhist Sramanas were sent out Greece, Asia minor. Egypt and ether distant countries preach their new religion. They were known the Greeks and there good reason believe that the Greek Simnoi (venerable) were no other than the Buddhist Sramanas (4). Now missionary usually teaches the sciences his country in addition to the preaching his gospel. The distant mission stations or monasteries Buddhism were (1) There*1s no ground whatever to suppose that Sushruta borrowed his system of medicine from the Greeks. the contrary, there is much to tell against such an idea—Weber's History of Indian Literature. (2) The Origin and Growth of the Healing Art—Bedroe P. 162. (3) Pratt's FloNiering Plants. Vol. P. 57.

The Arabic version Sushruta is known the name " Kelale- Shawshoore-al-Hindi." These translations in their turn were rendered into Latin. The Latin versions formed the basis of European medicine, which remained indebted the Eastern science medicine down the seventeenth century."— History of the Aryan Medical science (Th^kore Saheb of Gondal) P. 196. english_text: Origin and History of the A yurveda : —In the science of medicine, as in all other branches of study, the ancient Aryans claim have derived their knowledge from the gods through direct revelation. Su*shruta his Samhita has described the Ayurveda as a subdivision (Upanga) of the Atharvan (i), while according to others the science of the Ayurveda has its origin in the verses the Rik Samhita (2). Indeed the origin the science lost anti- quity. Death and disease there had been in the world since the advent of man was by following the examples lower animals disease, that our primitive ancestors acquired by chance the knowledge about the properties many valuable medicinal drugs. There verse the Rigveda which shows that the lower animals were the preceptors of man matters selecting food stuffs and medicinal simples (3K Individual experiences in the realms of cure and hygiene were collected, and codified, and thus formed the bases of the present Ayurveda. The verses in the Vedas clearly mark each step in the progress of medical knowledge. The properties a new drug were always hymned in a Vedic verse with a regularity which enables us put our finger upon the very time when particular drug our Materia Medica first came to be of service of man (4). Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthanam. 3. (2) ^jrC^^T^^i? ^qt?: Charana ^^•uha by \^y5sa. (3) jitfwT^* ^w(f[ (4) A. ipc: wii: ^gfi'?Tt^5Tifn It^r'^q^rd'T^ Atharvan SamhitS Sec also Ibid 4. 7. 9. 25, 27 and 36. Discrepancies accounted' for :—Verses q\i medicine, hygiene, and surgery, etc. lie scattered throughout the four Vedas. Those having bearing on Medicine proper occur most in the Rigveda, and perhaps it was for this reasoT^i that Agnivesha, who was a physician, has ascribed the^origin the Ayurveda revelations in the Rik Samhitd. Precepts" relating to the art anu practice of surgery are found most in the Atharvan (i), which amply accounts for the fact Sushruta's opinion of holding the Ayurveda as a subdivision of the Atharvan, as he was pre-eminentl}' a surgeon himself. Different kinds of physicians :—Vedic India, like Ancient Egypt, recognised the principle of the division of labour among the followers of the healing art. There were Shalya Vaidyas (surgeons), Bhisaks (physicians) and Bhisag- atharvans (magic doctors), and we find that at the time of the Mahabharatam, which nearly approaches the age of our author, the number of the sects had increased to five which were named Rogaharas (physicians^ Shalyaharas (sur- geons), Vishaharas (poison curers), . Krityaharas (demon- doctors) and Bhisag-Atharvans (2). In the Vedic age (before the age of Sushruta) physicians had to go out into the open streets, calling out for patients (3^ They lived houses surrounded gardens medicinal herbs. The Rigveda mentions the names of a thousand and one medicinal drugs (4). Verses eulogising the virtues of water as an all-healer, and of certain trees and herbs as purifiers of the atmosphere are not uncommon in the Vedas.

Precepts" relating to the art anu practice of surgery are found most in the Atharvan (i), which amply accounts for the fact Sushruta's opinion of holding the Ayurveda as a subdivision of the Atharvan, as he was pre-eminentl}' a surgeon himself. Different kinds of physicians :—Vedic India, like Ancient Egypt, recognised the principle of the division of labour among the followers of the healing art. There were Shalya Vaidyas (surgeons), Bhisaks (physicians) and Bhisag- atharvans (magic doctors), and we find that at the time of the Mahabharatam, which nearly approaches the age of our author, the number of the sects had increased to five which were named Rogaharas (physicians^ Shalyaharas (sur- geons), Vishaharas (poison curers), . Krityaharas (demon- doctors) and Bhisag-Atharvans (2). In the Vedic age (before the age of Sushruta) physicians had to go out into the open streets, calling out for patients (3^ They lived houses surrounded gardens medicinal herbs. The Rigveda mentions the names of a thousand and one medicinal drugs (4). Verses eulogising the virtues of water as an all-healer, and of certain trees and herbs as purifiers of the atmosphere are not uncommon in the Vedas.

INTROgDUCTION. lated in thecage of the Vedas i^nd Brahmanas, and shall present^' see how from* these scanty- and confused materials Sushruta created a science and a Sanjhita which inv'ice the ddmirs^.ion of the world even after thousands years of human prc*gress. Origin of A'yurvedic Surgery India, all other countries, curative spells and hewing mantras preceded medicine (i) and the first man of medicirte India was a priest, a Bhisag Atharvan, who held a superior position to a surgeon society. The first Aryan setLlements in the Punjab were often assailed by the dark aborigines of the country, and the wars .that ensued surgeons had fre-' quently to attend to the Aryaa chiefs and soldiery. the Rigveda (2) find that legs were amputated and replaced by iron substitutes, injured eyes were plucked out, and arrow shafts were extracted from the limbs of the Aryan warriors. Nay have reasons believe that many difficult surgical operations were successfully per- formed, thougli some of them sound almost incredible. But although the aid surgery was constantly sought for, surgeons were not often allowed the Brahmanic society of Vedic India. This hinted our author when he says that it was during the wars tween the gods and demons that the Ashvins, the surgeons of heaven, did not become entitled to any sacrificial oblation till they had made themselves eligible for it by uniting the head of the god of sacrifice to his decapitated body. The story of the progress of Ayurvedic surgery is long and inter- esting, but must suffice here to mention that with the (1) Bedroe"s Origin the Healing Art, and Sir John Lubbock's Prehistoric times, (2) ^^ 5igi^i^?5l f^aj^i^^ait V% f|fIWfT% flsi^"*f' Rik Samhita I A. 8 Ad. 186 S. 116. 5. return of peace, the small Aiynn settlements grew in number and prosperit}-. And the rich Ar3'an nobles now travelled in stately carriages, and as there were constant accidents ihere arose a class of surgeons who exclusively devoted tlfemselves to the treatment of injured animals. The surgeons, now no longer required in camps and on battle fields, had to attend on the rich ladies at baronial castles during parturition, the magic doctor (Bhisag Atharvan) who could assuage fever and concoct lo\e potions (i) being held as the greatest of them all. But the Vedic Aryans had a regular armoury against pain and suffering, which in no way inferior to our present day Materia Medica. But of that we shall speak later in connection with the therapeutics of Sushruta. The scope and nature of Sushruta's Surgery :- So rnuch for the history of Vedic Surgery.

Sushruta had no desire of abandoning the Vedas in the darkness and pushing en an independent voyage of discovery. The crude methods and the still cruder implements of incision such as, bits of glass, bamboo skins etc., laid down and described in the Samhita, may bj the relics of primitive instrumentalogy which tiiund favour vviih our ancestors long before the hymnisation of any Rik verse. Practical surgery requires a good knowledge practical anatomy. The quartered animals at the Vedic sacriiices afforded excellent materials for the framing a comparative anatomy (2)'. Sushruta devoted his whole life to the pursuit of surgery proper, to Rik Samhit5. X M. 145 S. (2) Vide .^itareya Br^hmana 2. Ill, 37, • INTRODUCTION. which he b/'ought a niyid stored with luminous analogies from the lower animals. It was he who first classified all .surgical operations into five differe'nt kinds, and grouped them under heads such Aharya (extractions solid bodies), Bhedya (excising), Chhedya (incising), Eshya (prob- ing), Lekhya (scarifying), Sivya (suturing), Vedhya (punc- turing) and Visravaniya (evacuating fluids). The surgery of Sushruta recognises a hundred and twenty-five different instruments, constructed after the shape beasts and birds, and authorises the surgeon to devise new instruments according to the exigencies of each case. The qualifications and equipments surgeon are practically the sam*^ as are recommended the present time. light refresh- ment is enjoined to be given to the patient before a surgical operation, while abdominal operations, and operations m the mouth are advised to be performed while the patient fasting. Sushruta enjoins the sick room to be fumigated with the vapours white mustard, bdellium, Nimva leaves, and resinous gums of Shala trees, etc., which fore- shadows the antiseptic ^bacilli) theory of modern times. The number of surgical implements described in the Samhita decidedly small in comparison with the almost inexhaustible resources of western surgery, and one may be naturally led suspect the au' henticity of the glorious achievements claimed have been performed by the surgeons of yore but then their kno vledge of the properties and virtues of drugs were great that cases, which are reckoned surgical nowadays, were cured with the help medicines internally^pplied. "Surgery," says Tantram, is mutilation not doctoring (i). To Sushruta is attributed the glory discovering the art cataract-crouching which was unknown to the sur- ' geons of ancient Greece and Egypt. Limbs were amputated, abdominal sections were performed, fractures were set, dislocations, hernia and ruptures were reduced, hcemorrhoids rind fistula were removed, and we take pride in saying that the methods recommended the Sushruta Samhita some- times prove more successful than those adopted by the surgeons of modern Europe, as shall have occasion to observe later on. english_text: His directions regarding the management of the puerperal state, lactation and management of the chijd and the choice of a wet-nurse are substantial!}- the same are found modern scientific works of European authors. A feeling of pride and joy moves our heart when we contrast these glorious achievements of our ancestors with the meanness of results which modern Europe has gained in this department midwifer}-. those old days perhaps there were hospitals to huddle patients together in the same room and therebv to create artificiallv septicemic poisons which are now so common and so fatal lying-in rooms. built Iving-in room in an open space abunduntly suf^s-ea J with the rays the sun and heat of the burning f^ie for each individual case, the recommendation fresh bamboo-chip for the section the cord are suggestions the value which the west has yet learn from the east. Dissection :--Sushruta, himself a practical surgeon, was the first to advocate dissection of dead bodies as indispen- sable for a successful student of Surgery. The Paritschittas of ancient Egypt perhaps learnt their art from the Purusachettas (Dissector) of ancient India. With a candour less common among western scholars Dr. Wise observes that, ''the Hindu philosophers undoubtedly deserve the credit of having, though opposed bv strong preiudire, entertained sound and philosophical views respecting the uses of the dead to the living, and were the first scientific and successful cultivator? of the most important and essential of all the departments of medical knowledge, practical anatomy". A bungling burgeon is a public danger and Sushiuta savs iliat, "theorv without practice is like a onc-wingetl bird that is incapable of flight". lNTK01)UCT!(ON. Study of Practical Surgery :—To give effii-iency surgical operations, the pupils of Dhan^antari(Sushruta etc.) were asked to try their knives repeatedly first natural and artificial objects resembling the diseased parts ofv> the body before undertaking an actual operation. Incision, for example, was practised on Pushpafala /cucerbeta maxima), Alavu (Longenaris Vulgaris) or Trapusha (cucmis pubescuas), evacuating on leatfier bags full of water and on the urinary bladders dead animals, scarification the hides animals on which the hair was allowed to remain. Venesec- tion was practised on the vessels of dead animals and on the stalks of the water-lily : the art .of stuffing and probing on bamboo reeds etc. extraction of solid bodies Panasa (Artocarpus Integrifolia) and such like fruit, scraping wax spread on Shalmali (Bombox Malabaricum) plank, and suturing on pieces of cloth, skin hide. Ligaturing and bandaging were practised dummies, cauterisation (both actual and potential ) on pieces flesh, and cathe- terisation unbaked earthen vessels filled with water. is almost with a feeling of wonder hear him talk extirpation of uterine excrescences and discourse the necessity of observing caution surgically operating upon uterine tumours (Raktarvudai.

Venesec- tion was practised on the vessels of dead animals and on the stalks of the water-lily : the art .of stuffing and probing on bamboo reeds etc. extraction of solid bodies Panasa (Artocarpus Integrifolia) and such like fruit, scraping wax spread on Shalmali (Bombox Malabaricum) plank, and suturing on pieces of cloth, skin hide. Ligaturing and bandaging were practised dummies, cauterisation (both actual and potential ) on pieces flesh, and cathe- terisation unbaked earthen vessels filled with water. is almost with a feeling of wonder hear him talk extirpation of uterine excrescences and discourse the necessity of observing caution surgically operating upon uterine tumours (Raktarvudai.

Anatomical Anomalies the Samhita :— Having got so far in our analysis, before passing on to the study the Anatomical portion of the Sushruta Samhita, must try account for the many anomalies and discrepancies that have crept into or have been suffered to remain the present recension of the book. Take, for example, the line in which Dhanvantari is made to speak three hundred bones the human organism. is impossible that the human frame, in so short a time, has got rid of so many of of its skeletal accessories simply through disuse, because of their becoming superfluous in the altered condition of its environments. More absurd it to think that Sushruta, who discards all authority except the testimony of positive knowledge, would write a thing which none but the blind would believe in a dissecting room. The spirit the a^re which he flouri:>lied piecliided the possibility oT^ such an crrnr. Anomalies accounted for : -In ancient India, subjects chosen for the demonstration of practical anatomy were always children (2), and naturally those bones, which are Vide Aitann'a Br5hmana VII. 13. (2) The injunction of ihe Hindu Sllasiras ihat '-corpse of persons more than 2 years old should he burned." Cremation of dead bodies bein" xxi\- (\'^Roi)rc~Ti().\. fused or''«'inaslon"iised inlo'one whole durine: adult life, have been' separately enumerated— a circumstance which may, some extent, account for th'e excess in the number of bon'es described in this Samhita (i). Likewise the theory that Sushrula might have included the teeth and the cartilages within the list of s'iveletal bones comes very near the truth, but It does not reflect the whole trtith either. The fact that the orignial Sushruta Samhita has passed through several recensions and we have reasons to believe that the present one Nagarjuna neither the only nor the last one made. The redactors, according to their own light, have made man\- in'terpbLitions the text, and when Brahmanas, thev have tried to come to a sort of compromise at points of disagieement with the teachings of the Vedas 12).

'.Cell is not life, but there life in a cell. Cells may be called the true bearers of li-fe. Dr. Weismann insists that is more correct to sppak of the continuity the general protoplasm than "the germ ceils." Professors Geddes and Thomson observe that, "the bodies are but the torches which burn out, while the living flame has passed through- out the organic series unextinguished. The bodies are the leaves which fall in dying from the continuously growing branch. Thus although deafh take inexorable grasp of the individual, the continuance of the life still in a deep sense unaffected the reproductive elements (cells) have already claimed their protozoan immortality, are already recreating a new body.'" But to invest these reproductive cells with immortality, and to deny the same the individual self, which directs and controls these protoplasms, and before and behind them, like the statement of Prof. Huxlev when he admits the chance of the physical transmigration the organic constituents the human body, and yet denies the possibility of an individual self continuing in any other form. sensibility," observes Sushruta, "that precedes the senses and self, the sensibility proceeds from the self to which all such conditions are referred as mine." Sushruta's Theory of Cosmogony based the old S;inkhya Duality Prakriti (Objective) and Purusha (Subjective). The two are coeval and co-extensive realities. Out the Avyakta (unmanifest) or Prakriti has evolved the Mahai, the (inimatcd cosmic matter. Out this cosmic matter has evolved Ahamk;ira (the sense of indivi- dualit\- more correctly egoism) which divided into thrt-e kinjs such the Vaikarika (phenomenal, ihought-fornii, Taijasa (kinetic), and Bhut;idi (pertaining il^e first form matter). This \'"aik;irika Ahamkara combination with the Taijasa Ahamkara has fathered iNTRonurfioN. 1.1 the eleven sense organs, whith. combinatiqii with the Bnutadi, have produced the five Tanmatras {iroper sensibles touch, sight, ^ hearing, etc. The material principles sound, light, taste, smell, etc., are biJ't the modifications these five Tanmatras, which'* Akisha (ethereon), Vayu (ether), light, and soui-^d, etc. are the grosser forms. other wordfl, these Tanmatras may he defined the atomic essences of the material principles of sound, light, ether, etc. addition these, Sushruta, Hke Kapila, admits the existence a kind of atom-like units of consciousness, which he calls Purusha. Sexual Diamorphism :— Some light is thrown the relative preponderance of the sperm and ovum in the Birth female child. "When the maternal element prepon- derates the child is female ; when the 'fraternal element stronger the child is malfe. When both the elements are equal, the child is of no sex.'" In theory least Sushruta admits the possibility the birth of many children at a single conception. "When the seed is divided into two by its inherent force (Vayu), twins are born the womb"— statement which points the 'irresistible conclusion that multiplicity birth the outcome the multifarious fission the seed in the womb under certain abnormal conditions. Sushruta gives a reason for believing that, exceptional circumstances, and without sexual union, the unfertilised ovum may give rise to perfect oflTspring, thus giving a prevision of the modern theory of parthenogenesis. Pathological parthenogenesis has occasionally been noticed higher animals. Oellacher has noted this respect of hen's eggs, and Janosik has observed it in the ovarian ova of many mammals such as the guinea-pig, etc. (2) Sushruta extends the probability the human ova under certain conditions. admits the possibility conception without the admixture the male germinal element, though observes that like all asexual genesis" the development dots not proceed far the case." Shukra-Vahulyam (\) (preponderance the male reproductive element) and Shonita-Vahulyam (prepon- derance of the female reproductive element) etc. Sushruta, in common with 'the Brahmanic philosophers of Ind, believed that distinction of sex h'as evolved from pri- mordial hermaijhroditism. english_text: He observes that "the Purusha (Logos), by a stroke of Will, divided its body (animated cosmic matter) into two, one of which was male, and the other female." The Tantra says that, ''the male part was endued with energy (force) of its own, which is called Pitrika Shaktl and the corresponding female part, with the one, which called Matrika Shakti. Pitrika Shakti a disruptive force •Matrika Shakti constructive energy. Though the conception of force Sanskrit sciences but partially physical, the nearest approach the connotations of the Pitrika and Matrika Shakti made by the terms Ana- bolism and KatabolisiU the Western physiologists. Sanskrit physiology recognises the two opposite poles vital force living organism, and has not taken inconsiderate pains to determine their exact locations in mau and woman. Matrika Shakti, observes, predominates the left half of a woman's organism, which is negative as regards vital magnetism. {3) Now, Sushruta says that, cases where female offspring is desired, the enceinte should Shdiiia-sth^iiani Ch. XXXIU snufF through her left nostril (thq expressed juice Qf certain herbals), while the same should administered through her right nostril where njale* issue would be the object. other words, the anabolic (Mairika) or katabolic (Piti;}ka) forces of a mother's organism can be so adjusted with the help of drug-dynamics, as to determine,the sex of the child the womb. The b',rth of a male child is usually pre- saged by the appearance of the milk (which according Sushruta metamorphised menstraal blood) in the right breast of the enceinte ; and where that has been effected with the help of suitable medicines, must be presumed that the Katabolic pole of her glife-force has been acted upon, as desired. The original hermaphroditism, which forms the anterior condition of all subsequent sex distinctions, and the character of the two opposite poles of vital energy, have been very clearly set forth the Pauranik allegory Ardha- Narishvara(i). The figure, observes the Pauranik rhapsodist, is half male, half female half life, half death (since, death, fact, the father Hfe) (2) half anabolism, half katabolism with the crescent moon, the premise, the s3'mbol of progressive evolution on its brow, is made to sit on the eternal bull, the representative the inmiutable law of the universe (lit :— the four-footed order). The Rishis and Rasasiddhas ancient India were fully aware the fact that, conception is effected only enormous sacrifice the part the mother that the Matrika Shakti is the real manufacturer of life, and that the Pitrika Shakti (paternal element) evokes, or calls into play only through its disintegrating or disruptive effect by separating the two opposite life-poles, that lie neutralised through contact.

english_text: It is love that governs these two complementary Vishnu PurSnam Ch. 7. Vs. lo-ii. (2) mm: F?^n ^^^^^ ^t^ «TTJrfff ^^v. Mahdbhaialam. forces of .life and death (i), (though in' fact they represent the two different aspects of the same energy) and controls its evolutionary rhythms through the desire seeing itself many though one in reality. Does not modern biology endorse the same view when it says that the reproductive cells, as protozoons,.are immortal, and that bodies are the natural appendages which blossom.forth and fall off round these cells for the fructification of their innate purposes of being (2)? A little more investigation into the biological thesis of the Rishis would be necessary for the clear comprehen- sion of "Shukra-Vahulyani" and '"Shonita-Vahulyam" Sushruta and other Tantras (3). iMan is both animal and spirit ; and the Ayurvedic physiology recognises two distinct sets of apparatus in his organism answering to the different phases of his existence. The one helps him performing the organic functions, which are so essential to his animal existence, and keeps intact the co-ordination of those inter- nal functions with the incidents of his environments. The other is attuned to the finer forces of nature, and responds (I) The Evolution of Sex. Ch. XVIII. Prof. P. Gedde/i and J, A. Thomson. (2) "The body or soma'\ Weismann says, "thus appears to a certain extent as a subsidiary appendage of the true bearers of the life,—the repro- ductive cells". Ray Lankester has again well expressed this :—"Among the multicellular animals, certain cells are separated from the rest of the consti- tuent units the body, as egg-cells and sperm-cells ; these conjugate and continue to live, whilst the remaining cells, the mere carriers as it were the immortal reproductive cells, die and disintegrate. The bodies the higher aniivals which die, may from this point of view be regarded as something temporary and non-essential, destined merely to carry for a time, to nurse, and to nourish the mure important and deathless fission -products the unicellular egg."—Quoted the Evelution of Sex (P. Geddes and J, A. Thompton) 1901. Chap. XVIII. (3) (a) ^liTf^^T wtwrf^ ^f<f^ry^: HiTi^ Sarada Tilak Tantram. (15) Sushrula SamhilA (ShSrira Sthdnam the call his higher psychiq self. Tli'e one organic, the other is psyC^ic The one chains him down to the phenomenal, and is" governed by the laws of growth and decay the other opens the region of absolute realities where growth and decay have room Growth not the only condition of life. Man may exist without food (i)or respiration, only if he can manage to dive deep into the realities within himself. Between these two sets of apparatus there is the Jivatma, which, by its own peculiar energy (the will-force), can operate in phenomenal or organic pl?,in, or recede from thence into the psychic one, thus being contact with the world of the senses' and the one that is beyond the darkness of death.

english_text: Death, fact, the grand usherer life, which is only the rise of the curtain over the life's drama, all equipments for which are made in the green room of death. A man can not propagate at will. No amount of willing the part of the parent-animal can help him in creating progeny. The self of the child, who is about to come into life, chooses its own parents, according the dynamics its own acts or Karma, from the region of the lunar Pitris or quiescent life, if it be warrantable to use such an expres- sion (2). The self of the would-be child mixes with the self its human father, and hovers over the reproductive cells the latter's organism, and regulates the intensity its father's sexual desire, according to the nature the sex, determined necessary for the fruition the purposes of its advent into the world. greater intensity its father's desires ensures the preponderance tile Pitrika Shakti (katabolism) the impregnated ovum, which Skanda PurSnam quoted Shridhara SvSmi his commentaries on the Vishnu Purdnam. Ch. VI. V. i6. (2) ^^^m ^7m\^m Shruti. determiiies the male^sex of the child, while such thing, the part of the mother at the time, is followed by the relative preponderance of the Afatrika Shakti (anabolism) which accounts for the femininity of the issue. Equal tensity of sexual desires both the parents, creating an absence of the relative preponderance the Pitrika and Matrika Shaktis in the impregnated ovum, leaves the sex of the child practically undetermined. The relative prepon- derance of the Pitrikd or Matrika Shakti, as evidenced by the greater or less intensity of the sexual desire of either the parents, which results in the speedier emission of the paternal or maternal element (sperm or ovum) during an act of successful fecundation, contemplated the term ''Shukra-Vahulyam," or "Shonita-Vahulyam," by the framer of the Samhita, as ma}- be fully substantiated by a couplet by the venerable Daruvahi (i). So far Sushruta one with the modern Western theory of preponderant katabolism anabolism the ovum as the determining factor of the sexual diamorphism to the extent that seeds or reproductive cells are the bearers and not the manufacturers life, only containing those categories which foster life, and help its evolution into organic being. deny this would be to admit the chemi- cal, physiological basis of life, which, as a theory, was never acceptable the biologists ancient India. The number of reproductive cells may increased suitable dietary, and to say that the immortal reproductive cells, as the creators of life, come out of the mortal, organic food stufll* is to say that darkness is the father of light. english_text: The second is not co-extensive with the entire existence living organism, Sushruta observes some such distinction among the functions living organism when he denominates the living body as the "three supported one" (Tristhunam), and describes the normal Vayu, Pittam and Kapham its three supports. wonder how the term Vayu, meaning nerve force, can be confounded with the same term meaning air, since Sushruta derives the former from the root "Va," to move, to spread. Vayu, according to Sushruta, called from the fact of its sensory and motor functions such smelling, &c. But the Vayu in the Ayurveda not wholly a physical or organic force, has its spiritual aspect as well which does not legitimately fall within the scope of our enquiry. safe aver however, that the Ayurvedic physiology, like its sister science modern Europe, concerned more with the invisible molecular components the human organism, than with the workings its gross members. The holy Agnivesha warns the students physiology against the danger of regarding the human system as some- thing other than the aggregate of molecules (i). Charaka Saniliit^ ShArirasthAnam, Chap. VII. INTRODUCTI9N. The three fundamental principles of Vayu,,Pittam and Kaphah: — The actions living matter vary and may reduced three categories. They are either— (i), functions which affect the material conipositJDn of the body and determine its mass, which is the baUnce of the processes of waste on one hand and t^hose of assimilation on the other. Or (2), they are functions which subserve the process reproduction which essentially the detach- ment of a part endowed with the powers developing into independent whole, or (3), they are functions in virtues of which one part the body able to exert a direct influence another, and^ the body, by its parts as whole, becomes a source molar motion. The first may be termed Sustentative, the second Generative, and the third Correlative functions. The above is the sum and substance of the works which a living matter has to perform. But setting apart the processes reproduction subject for future discussion, shall now try examine what the other two functions are understood Oriental thinkers. the Mahfibhiratam the Prdna vayu is described as a force, akin to electricity. is some- what like a flash of lightning (1). This fact aHonce shows the errors of confounding Prana vdyzi with an effete material — with gases generated during the processes digestion. Shushruta describes it as a force, (2) which sets the whole organism into motion. Self-evolved, it acts as the principal HTOSIT^^^ JJcTRt Mm ?sifvf^?j^ MahSbMratam. ShAnti Pa^va S. 39. (2) Fo'ce may be defined ihat which tends to produce motion a body at lesi, or to produce change of motion in a body which is movjng. —Daschanel, INJRODUCTION. factor that deLerinines the genesis, continuance and disin- tegration of the livir-g body. the primary cause—an all-in-all that governs our organic as well our cognitive faQulties.

chapter: Unknown
english_text: Its special feature is that the vibration, that produqed in it, instead of travelling like light in a transverse direction, takes a course as the controller of the correlative functions of the system. It maintains an equilibrium between the Pittam and Shleshma which are said to be inert, (i) But for this adjustment the living body would stand in imminent danger of being consumed like fuel its internal heat or fire. Taking into consideration the various functions the living body has to perform, Sushruta attempts classif.ca- tion of Vdyu into Prana, Udana, Samana, Vyana and Apana, which, detail, correspond the divisions functions performed by the Cerebro-spinal and Sympathe- tic nerves of the Western physiology. Tintric literature abounds the descriptions the Nadichakras (nerve plexuses) and contains a more detailed account of the motor, sensory, and mixed nerves according to their differences in their functions and relations. In short, the term Vayu may not only be rightly interpreted to mean the nerve force, but is often extended to include any kind electro-motor or molecular force (as when we speak of the V^yu of the soil), though the term loosely applied now to signify gas air. The Rishis of yore gave the name V;iyu the bodily force in the absence of any suitable nomenclature, little suspecting that might confounded with the atmospheric air by the foreign translators of their works. Charaka, Sutraslh4nam. Chap. XII. Inert i.s Pittam, inert is Kaphah, inert are the Malas & 'Jh^lus Like clouds, they go wherever they arc!- carried by the V5yu, Pittam :—The function the »Pittam consists metamorphosing the chyle, .through a graduated series organic principles, protoplasmic substance like sperm in men, and the ovum in women. Thus we see that the Pittam the Ayurveda corresponds to metabolism of Western physiology. But b}? a confounding carelessness of terms the excreted portion Rasa and blood though ultimately connected with those normal physiological processes has been respectively styled as the Doshas defiling principles of Kaphah and Pittam. Again, as in the case, of soil, the terms V^yu, Pittam and Kaphah are extend- ed to denote magnetism, kinetic energy and humidity of its molecules. The circulation of blood is connected with the Pittam, while the circulation lymph chyle fRasa) related Shleshmi the two combinedly forming what called the sustentative function the Western Physiology. The term Pittam, which, its etymology, signifies the agent metabolism, has been loosely used by our Ayurvedic physiolgists denote two different organic principles from an observed similarity their nature and functions. Pittam Sanskrit means both bile and meta- bolism of tissues well as the bodily heat which the product of the latter.

english_text: Thus congestion and inanition (atony) are the two main forms of disease recognised by the Ayurvedic Patholo- gists, the former being held amenable resolution elimination, and the latter to local feeding or nourishment. Agni and Dh^tvagnis :—We can not better conclude this portion of our dissertation than by speaking a word or two about Agni. Sushruta raises the question whether there is any kind of fire in the human organism other than the Pittam ; or are they identical ? Sushruta holds that the Pittam is the only fire present in the system, in as much as all acts from the digestion of food to the disintegration of tissues are performed with the help the Pittam, which includes within its signification what connoted by Anabolism and Katabolism of Western Physiologists. english_text: " These facts and other considenitions, which might brought forward, lead tl\e tentative conception of protoplasm as being a substance (if we may use the word in somewhat loose sense) not only unstable in nature but subject to incessant change, existing indeed as the expression incessant molecular, chemical and physical cliange, ver3' much a fountain the expression incessant replacement water. We may picture ourselves the total <:'hange. which we denote by the term "metabolism," consisting on the one hand, of a downward series of i,Katabolic changes) a stair of many steps in which the more complex bodies are broken down with the setting free of energy into simpler waste bodies, and, on the other hand, of an upward series of changes (anabolic changes) also a stair of _ many steps, by which ihe dead food varying simplicity complexity with further assumption energ}- built into more and more complex bodies. The summit of the do'uble stair we call ''protoplosm" whether have right to speak it as a single hodv .'in the chemical sense of that word as a From whal has now been sUted regarding ihe func- tions and significations of the Vayu, l*ittam and Kaphah, it will appear that the Achai*yayas of the Ayurveda contem- plated three different sets principles in the domains of Biology and Pathology. Vayu, Pittam, and Kaphah are mixture in some way of several bodies. Whether we should regard it as tlie very summit of the double stair, or as embracing as well as the topmost steps either side, we can not present tell. Even if this be a simple substance forming the topmost summit, its existence is absolutely temporary, at one instance it is made, at the next is unmade matter, which passing through the phase of life, rolls up the ascending step t«5 the top and forthwith rolls down on the other side * Further the dead food itself fairly, but far from being wholly stable in character, becomes more and more complex living material. It becomes more and more explosive and when reaches the sum- mit its equilibrium is over-thrown and it actually explodes. The whole downward stair of events seems in fact to be a series of explosives by means of which the energy latent in the dead food and augmented the touches through which the dead food becomes living protoplasm, set free. Some those freed energy used again by the material itself, in order to carry on tliis same vivification of dead food, the rest leaves the body as heat or motion. If this be admitted almost inevitably follows, that what have called protoplasm, can not be alwnys the sumo thing that tliere must be many varieties of protoplasm witli dilVcrcnt qualities and with corresponding different molecular strncturL' and composition. Using the word '"protoplasm'' this sense, is oi'vious that the varieties of protoplasm are numerous indeed, almost innumerable.

english_text: The molecular protoplasm, whicli brings forth contractile kata- state must difEer in nature, composition, that construction from glandular protoplasm wliere kata-state mother ferment. Fur* her the protoplaspi of a swiftly contracting striped muscular fibre must differ from that of the torpid, smooth, unstriatcd fibre, the protoplasm of a human muscle must differ from that of a sheep or a frog, the protoplasm of one muscle must differ from that another muscle, in the aaine kind of animal, and the pro- toplasm of Smith's biceps must differ from that of Jone's—Foster. Called D/iaius fundaraental principles of the economy, when in virtue of their correlative and sustentative func- tions, with the help their subservient processes of metabolism and lymphatic circulation, they ensure an equi- poise among the diflferent vital and physiological processes in the u'hole economy which essential its perfect health. Biologically considered they are but the primary subtle djniamics organic life, or as Sayana expresses the three fundamental principles the body.* But when this healthy equilibrium disturbed either through the agency of any extrinsic or idiopathic factor, when any one of them abnormally augmented or dominates the other two, thus altering their mutual relation the economy, naturally certain pathological conditions arise which form the esse disease or in the parlance of the Ayurveda they are said have been transformed into Doshas or morbific diathesis. Even blood, which, according our Acharyayas, forms one of the fundamental principles (Dhatu) of the organism, may be designated Dosha (morbific diathesis), when owing its congestion in any particular organ or member of the body, it brings about a disturbance its general vascular system and produces pathological conditions which are offshoots its own deficient disturbed circulation. They are denominated MalaS, \ when observed still grosser superficial principles the organism producing those excretions, organic lesions which appertain to the sphere of morbid Anatomy. Thus we see that the Ayurvedic principles of Vayu, Pittam and Kaphah embrace both the biological and pathologi- .Sayanas Commentary Rig ^^ 1 A. Chanika. Hli cal principles the organism other wOrds, the Ayurvedic physiology elucidates and investigates the causes through which the 'same principles, which sustain life and the organism, are transformed into the dynari\*ics of disease, lastly pointing out the grosser excretory changes and organic lesions the external or "superficial plane of existence, which form thi subject of morbid anatomy and are sometimes confounded with the disease itself. In the Vayu, Pittam and Kaphah of the Acharyayas we have once a complete picture of the finer sustentative forces the human economy as well as their antithesis, the construc- tive as well as the expulsive forces* the inner man, gether with an exhaustive analysis of their grosser products which legitimately fall within the sphere morbid ana- tomy.

Even blood, which, according our Acharyayas, forms one of the fundamental principles (Dhatu) of the organism, may be designated Dosha (morbific diathesis), when owing its congestion in any particular organ or member of the body, it brings about a disturbance its general vascular system and produces pathological conditions which are offshoots its own deficient disturbed circulation. They are denominated MalaS, \ when observed still grosser superficial principles the organism producing those excretions, organic lesions which appertain to the sphere of morbid Anatomy. Thus we see that the Ayurvedic principles of Vayu, Pittam and Kaphah embrace both the biological and pathologi- .Sayanas Commentary Rig ^^ 1 A. Chanika. Hli cal principles the organism other wOrds, the Ayurvedic physiology elucidates and investigates the causes through which the 'same principles, which sustain life and the organism, are transformed into the dynari\*ics of disease, lastly pointing out the grosser excretory changes and organic lesions the external or "superficial plane of existence, which form thi subject of morbid anatomy and are sometimes confounded with the disease itself. In the Vayu, Pittam and Kaphah of the Acharyayas we have once a complete picture of the finer sustentative forces the human economy as well as their antithesis, the construc- tive as well as the expulsive forces* the inner man, gether with an exhaustive analysis of their grosser products which legitimately fall within the sphere morbid ana- tomy.

The shock is felt first at the centre of vitality, whence is transmitted outwards and thus affects the energy which holds the molecules together, Dvyanuks and Tryanuks (Binary and teriiary atoms) which the gross body is composed, and further opposes the dissolution those molecules into their elemental constituents in the living organism. Even in cases of external injuries such as snake- bite, etc. the potency of the virus is carried at once that centre from whence is almost instantaneously transmitted through the external channels of the body its surface, ff^TTUr *^^« Palanjala Uarshanani X^ibhudpAda 29—30 A. What do those myriads of Chaitanya- vahini Nadis (sensory ne,ivt^s) exist for the human system ? all diseases the subjective sensations are I'he first to be experienced. "I am ill," "I feel hot," etc. are the voices of sensations, which form the "esse" of the disease. Disease then is a force ar^d not matter.* Pathology of Tridosha :—Sushmta, though adopting the Vedic pathological dictum Tridhatu, has expressed a very clear opinion on the subject. He observes that the relation between a disease and the deranged Vayu (nerve force), Pittam (metabolism) and Kapham (unutilised product the system), and the pathogenic factors which lie at the root of that disease, not real but contingent. These morbific principles may permeate the whole organism without creating any discomfort, and only when they find a distinct lodgment, and are centred some distinct part or tissue of the body, that they become the exciting factors of disease. Drug Potency :—The next question which naturally arises in connection with such theory pathogeny, what is medicine, or in other words, what is it in the drug that cures Sushruta, after closely investigating all the theories on the subject, inclines towards the opinion that is the potency of the drug that is curative, though he observes that inasmuch as potency cannot exist independently drug, a drug is of primary interest for p11 practical purposes in therapy. Drug-Dynamisation :-"It is the potency drug that cures a disease". The potency administered best That Hahnemann's theory disea.se was long before fore- .shadowed Sushruta, will appear from the above extracts from his works. Hahnemann observes that, when person falls ill, it is only this spiritual self-acting vital force, everywhere present the organism, that primarily deranged the dynamic influence morbific agent inimical to life—Orgenon. INiTRODUCTIOK. when the physical chqjmical properties drug are annihilated. This best performed by subjecting heat pressure.

When Sushruta formulated the process of preparing mediciual oils and Ghritas, and laid down the use Shatadhautam Ghritam (clarified butter, hundred times washed with water succession), Sahasrapak Tailam (medicinal oil, successively codked thousand times), Kumbha-Ghritam (clarified butter, a hundred years old) it may be fairly said that he was in sight of the principle of drug-dynamisation. Principles of A yurvedic Treatment:— Ayurvedic phy- sicians piacticallv recognise two dififerent sets principles in the domain of practical therapeutics, which may be stated in the terms of ::heir western colleagues as Laws of Similars and Contraries.* This apparent contradiction has been fully accounted for and explained the writings the latter day commentators, but does not fall within our province to enter into these disquisitions. addition to those, Sushruta, in common with the Acharyayas his time, never fails emphasise the value of psycopathy in * Similar in ch.iracter to the exciting factors disease—Similar character to the £sse of a disease—Similar in character both to the exciting actors and £i>\se of a disease. Since the creation of man, the tput'h of the "Saintly" has been credited with the virtue of curing the sick and Av^feha (auto-hypnotism) and Samadhi (higher phases of ciairvoy- ance) have achieved many miracles . the art of healing in India, which was the, first country where was first successfully practised for the welfare of man. Samshodhanam and Samshamanam :— All kinds treatment may be grouped under two heads such Sam- shodhanam and Samshamanam, i.e. either the body should be cleansed (Samshodhitam) of the morbific diathesis with the help of emetics or purgatives, or steps should taken to restore the deranged Vayu, Pittam and Kapham to their normal condition with the help of proper medicinal drugs without resorting to any eliminating process. But in cases of inflammation, Sushruta enjoins that, instead of any Sam- shamanam remedies, diaphoresis should be first resorted In cases where counter-irritants are indicated and parts which are directly accessible, leeching and cauterisation should be practised with a due regard the season of the year and the requirements of the case. find his Samhita a detailed account of the several species leeches with their habits and habitats.

Samshodhanam and Samshamanam :— All kinds treatment may be grouped under two heads such Sam- shodhanam and Samshamanam, i.e. either the body should be cleansed (Samshodhitam) of the morbific diathesis with the help of emetics or purgatives, or steps should taken to restore the deranged Vayu, Pittam and Kapham to their normal condition with the help of proper medicinal drugs without resorting to any eliminating process. But in cases of inflammation, Sushruta enjoins that, instead of any Sam- shamanam remedies, diaphoresis should be first resorted In cases where counter-irritants are indicated and parts which are directly accessible, leeching and cauterisation should be practised with a due regard the season of the year and the requirements of the case. find his Samhita a detailed account of the several species leeches with their habits and habitats.

Rasayanam :—The Ayurveda being the science of life and health, the holy Agnivesha, at the very commencement ot the th-erapeutical porticMi his work, * has described several medicinal compounds, which improve general health and arrest the ravages of time. ' Theoretically speaking the sciehrce of the A3'urveda recognises no preordained limit human fexistence. Life can prolonged with the help suitable medicines. dint of observation and patient researches our Rishis devised maay such adjuncts which can rejuvenate an old man, and supply those vital elements old and exhausted human body, which ebb away with the progress years. Hence, find many rejuvenating medicines have been prescribed for men in health which would arrest decay and guard against the approach of senility increasing the fundamental vital principles the body and preventing Vayu, Pittam and Kapham from being transformed into morbific diatheses. Diet — "A good and proper diet disease is worth a hundred medicines and no amount of medication can do good to a patient who does not observe a strict regimen diet.'' Our A'jairveda, instead of being content with specify- ing the nature of diet in diseases in general, mentions the names of articles, which should, or should not taken any specific malady, judged by the light of their properties aggravating Vayu, Pittam Kapham. english_text: Therapeutics:— The exclusiont of salt and water from the food of ascites anasarca patient as laid down in our Samhitas shows that our Rishi possessed higher chemical knowledge regarding the effects of organic matter the human system than many are ready accord to these pioneers in medical science. Medical Botany;— After therapeutics comes the subject of Medical Botany. Sushruta divides the whole vegetable Charaka Samhiti, Chikiisa Sthanam, Ch. \'I. 23. The efficacy of such exclusion has been lately demonsU-ated by the researches of Dr. Benjamin Horniman (Lectures, Sanitarium, Park London.) Charaka Chikitsha Sthanam Ch, XIIL ^bid Chap. 13. Charaka Chikitsha Sthanam Chap, 1 2. kingdom into Vriksha, Gultlia, Vanaspati and Virudha. This classification has been minutely worked out works Hindu Botany where find si!ich nice subdivisions Agravija (whose toplings are only planted), Mulaja (whose roots only are planted), Parnayoni, Skandaja, Vijaruha (ger- minated from seeds) ^nd Sannurudhaja. But the botany Sushrnta is more of the nature of a" Materia Medica than a work on Botany proper, though sometimes mentions the habitat and describes the foliage certain plants that they may distinguished from others of a cognate species. The uses of metals and 'minerals for therapeutical pur- poses in India are old the Rigveda * itself. Sush- ruta describes the methods of preparing oxides, sulphates or chlorides of the six metals as the case may Mercury has been only once mentioned in the Samhita and then very vaguely too. Processes for the preparation of alkalis and the lixiviation ashes are very elaborately described. Beyond these the chemical knowledge of Sushruta scarcely extends. Hygiene and Public Health:— A? a writer of Hygiene and public health, Sushruta emphasises the importance cleanliness both sririt and body. Water whose disinfecting virtues have often been hymnised the Vedas t forms the subject discussion entire chapter of the Samhita. Outbreaks of epidemic have been attributed to contrary seasons, the floating of minute particles poisonous flower pollen in the air, and to the sin or unrighteous conduct of the community. Earthquakes, famines, and physical phenomena, which are at present attri- buted to magnetic disturbances the earth, have been Lead crystal (including diamond) gold and mineral poisons arc men- tioned in the I. i6. I. 29. 55. and IV 10. of the Atharva SamhitA. Rik Samhiti I. 23 19, described by Sushi ut a as the usual precursors of devastating epidemics sucli as jilague etc. Mortality among birds and an unusual death among rats and other burrowing rodents hav^ been iricluded aiuo.ig other presaging indications of visi- tation by Providence.

english_text: Interrogated as to the cause of such outbreaks, Dhanvautari observes that, the Viyu (molecular energy) of the soi' is disturoed or affected earthquakes, and seasons of unnatural drought or deluge, deranging their Pittam (kinetic energy) and Shleshma (humidity) which produce morbific factors that affect whole community. Sushyuta, as a true physician, has elaborately dealt with the regimen of diet and conduct during the different seasons of the year (Ch. 24 -U. T. 64) which, strictly followed, should act as a good prophylaxis against attacks of many epidemic diseases, being framed with most careful regard to the conditions of life which obtain it, and ward off those sad breakdowns health, which are, in many instances, the result of an unsuitable mode of living in this country. Twofold division of Time &C :—It fundamental dictum of Sushruta that in a case of medical treatment the then prevailing season the year should taken into account. In his Samhita we find two distinct classifications seasons, one based the peculiar physical pheno- mena which distinguish the different seasons of the year, a fact which emphaticall}' proves that Sushruta was an inhabitant of the sub-Himalayan Gangetic Doab, the other for the purpose of showing the respective accumulation, aggrava- tion and subsidence of morbific diatheses (Doshas). the same manner the different quarters the day and night have been minutely charted or set down to show the spontaneous aggravation and subsidence of the deranged Vayu, Pittam and Kaphah during the l^ours. The! influence of planets as to the production of certain diseases such as small-pox, measles, scarlet fever, almost proved fact. governs the' prevalence and non- prevalence certain maladies, the aggravation and non-aggravation of certain existing disorders as well owe much of tb.eir origin to this potent factor. The vegetable kingdom from which we glean our daily food is also subject to thi? influence, and hence the discrimination we exercise in selecting our food on certain davs of the lunar month. Countries have been divided into Jangala or A'nr.pa ac- cording their physical features partake of the character of a dry plateau a swamp marsh, Sadharana one possessing features, which are common to both. Diseases, which are natural or are spontaneously relieved each these kinds of countr}- have been treated with that scienti- fic insight which marks modern medical works sea-side or spring sanitariums. The virtues of the waters of different rivers of India were ascertained for the purposes of practical therapeutics.

english_text: The therapeutic properties the milk a she-goat, she-buflFalo, mare, cow-elephant, or woman, well as of any of their modifications such as curd, whey together with the properties of the flesh and urine the several groups she-animals, which are indigenous the land, were studied and analysed, thus placing at the disposal practical physician list dietarv different diseases soothe the taste the most fastidious patient, and which at the same time potent enough to cure the distemper is suffering from without the help of anj?^ special medicine. Thus it is that we find our Vaid\'as prescribing" the flesh of many carnivorous animals as a diet consumption, goat's meat phthisis, goat's milk in colitis and Tittira's flesh in fever &c. Diseases of the Kidneys and Bladder: — In treating of the diseases of the kidneys, blatlder and the urethra, Sushruta has described the symptoms and the colour the urine irii each specific variety without laying down any mode of testing the urine. But we know that Sn^hruta has enjoined his readers at the ver}- outset of his work to refer to other allied branches the science for information which is not contained his book. In the same manrier INTRODUCTION'. can account for the absence of.^ any instiuctions regards the feeling of the p'j'>se important auxiliary in making a correct diagnosis. need but repeat -.the statement that the readers of this Samhita must look for this information in the Kanada's Nadi Vijnanam, which has made our Vaidyas such expert sphygmologists. Kalpa: — In the Kalpasth:inam his Samhita, Sushruta has described the symptoms of hydrophobia and snake bites, etc as well as those developed in cases of vegetable poisoning, together with their therapeutical treatment and remedies, wkich, if rightly studied and investigated, may yet throw a new liglit upon the subject. Sushruta as an Observer :— It has been lately dis- covered by a German physiologist that tubercular bacilli do not thrive in goat's blood. The importance of goat's milk colitis as an efficient agent checking ferment the intestines, or of the close contact goat a powerful auxiliary in curing tuberculous phthisis was first demonstrated Sushruta. Not onl}' this—but the inhalation of the air of a cattle-shed and especially the fact that exhalations goats, bodies tend destroy the phthisis germs did not fail to attract the attention of the Indian Rishis the fumigation tlie sick-room with antiseptic preparations such isT^T^wq (Asthanga dhupas) purely Indian in its origin and in no way inferior to the modern introduction of Cogghill's respirators. The microscopic germs that are said to propagate septic fever otherwise called ^^^crrfvf q^'lsj f^^^j^ are found very often disappear under this Indian device where no medicines produce any impression. Thus many wonderful discovery like the above hails from the dimness bygone age.

Different kinds for and drink :— Descripti^^ns Sliali Dlianyam, Shasht'' udhanyam, Vaidal, Mudga, Sesamum, Barley, Wheat, Sp ' ..nanyametc.—Properties of Dlianyas according to their jnatui'ity—Description of the Vei'udha Dhanyas—Flesh group CONTENTS. —Classiticutiou of tlesh—The Vishkira group—General properties the flesh' of Vishkira, animal^—Etymology of the term Pratuda Enumeration the animals of the Pratuda group—Cave dwelling, and hole dwelling animals—The Prasalia group of animals—Beneficial use of flesh of the Prasaha group in,Phthisis—Detailed classification of animals with the etymology their generic names, and proper- ties of their flesh—Frvit group, and the properties of different fruits — Group pot herbs, and their properties—Flower groups—The group o£ edible leaves of plants, and their properties—The group of bulbous plants—The group of salts—General properties nitrate of potash, etc.— Properties of gold, iron, silver and other metals—Deter- mination of the properties of drugs other than those herein mentioned — Drinks and beverages—Rules of diet—Rules of serving out the meal—General causes indigestion—Symptoms the different types indigestion—Medical treatment the same—Symptoms which mark acts of over insufticient eating, or an act of eating a meal before a previous one digested—Reason of one's feeling hungi'y even when suffering from indigestion 469—571 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHUA CHAP T E R Well, we* shall now describe the origin of the Science of Medicine, as disclosed by the holy Dhanvantari to his disciple Sushruta. (Vedotpattimadhyaryam).

english_text: Once upon a time, when the holy Dhanvantari, the greatest of the mighty celestials, incarnated in the form of Divodasa, the king Kasi, was blissfulh^ seated, in his hermitage, surrounded concourse of holy Rishis Aupadhenava, Vaitarana, Aurabhra, Paush- kalavata, Karavirya, Gopura-rakshita, Sushruta and others addressed him follows Sire, it grieves us much to find men, though otherwise well befriended " The present work which originally formed the subject of a discourse by the holy sage Dhanvantari to his disciple Sushruta, has been compiled its present form by the venerable Nagarj una, and is accordingly designated as the Sushruta Samhila. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap the'ir kin and relations, falling a prey diseases, mental, physical, traumatic, natural, and piteously wailing agony like utterly friendless creatures on earth ; and we supplicate thee, O Lord, to illumine our minds with the truths the Eternal Ayurveda (Medical Science) so that we may faithfully discharge the duties allotted to us life, and alleviate the sufferings humanit)" large. Bliss this life and hereafter, in the gift of this eternal Ayurveda, and for this, O Lord, we have made bold to approach thee as thy humble disciples." them, thus replied the hoi}' Dhanvantari :—"Welcome all of you this blissful hermitage. All of you are worthy of the honour of true pupilship or tutelage." The A'yui'veda (which forms the subject of our present discourse), originally formed one of the sub- sections the Athar\'a A'eda ; and even before the creation of mankind, the self-begotten Brahma strung it together into a hundred thousand couplets (Shlokas), divided into thousand chapters. But then thought of the small duration of human life earth, and the failing character of human memory, and found it prudent to divide the whole of the Ayurveda into eight different branches such the Salya- Tantram, the Salakya-Tantram, the Kaya-Chikitsa, the Bhuta-Vidy.i, the KauniHr-Bhrityci, the Agada-Tantram, the Rusa\"ana-Tantram and the \'ajeekarana-Tantram. Chap. SUTRASTHAN'AM. The Sha'Ia'kya-Tantramt—embraces as its object the treatment of those diseases which are restricted to the upward (lit:—region above the clavicles) fissures or cavities of the body, such as the ears, the eyes, the cavity of the mouth, the nostrils, etc. The Ka'ya-Chikitsar (General diseases!

The Rasa'yana-Tantram Science Rejuvenation ,—has for its specific object the Chap. To which replied the venerable sage—",Be Now listen, Sushruta, m}- dear child. The object or utility the science which forms the subject of our present discus- sion, ma}' be gi^ouped under two distinct sub-heads such as (i) the cure of diseased persons, and (2) the presetva- tion of health in those who are not afflicted with any sort of bodily distempers." The etymological meaning the term "Ayur- veda" may be interpreted to denote either a science the knowledge of which life exists, which helps a man to enjoy a longer duration of life. The primary position of surgery:— [As regards time and importance among the other allied branches of the Science of Medicine]. Hear me discourse the Science of Surgery { Shalya-Tantram) which is the oldest of all the other branches of the Science Medicme (Ayurveda) and is fuither corroborated by the four classes of testimonies, viz., Perception, Inference, Analogv and Scriptural Truths (Agamas). The Ashvins reunited the severed head of Yajna his body prayed for. [Hence this branch of the Ayurveda (Shalyanga) is the oldest of all its subdivisions]. SUTRASTHA'NAM. with the view teach the Science of SurgeiV with all its allied branches of study to men. In the present science (Ayurveda), the Piirusha (self- conscious oro:anic individual) is described as the resultant of the combination the soul and the five primar>- material principles. All medical acts such surgical operations, administration of medicinal remedies and applications alkaline substances, cauterisation, etc.), are restricted to the Piirusha alone.* Why it so The answer simply because the created world composed two distinct classes, such the mobile and the immobile. f These two classes, their turn, are further sub-divided for the purposes the science medicine into the two orders, Agneya hot and Saumya (cool. Hence the world composed fi^'e material principles, though characterised by the twofold virtues, Agneya (hot) and Saumya (cool).i It may be questioned why they should be confined to the Puiusha ? Such a query may be successfully met by the statement that the Purusha alone is the receptacle of health and disease contradistinction the Self or Ego. t The vegetable world belongs to the latter category, while 'animals, possessed of locomotion, belong to the former. It may be argued here, since everything in the universe is composed the five fundamental material principles [ of earth, water, fire (heat), air and sky (ether) ], it is not competent assert that the universe possessed of the twofold attributes Agneya (heated or fiery) and Saumya (cool or watery), alone.
