english_text: Further cliassification of the mobile and the immobile :- The animated world may divided into four subdivisions, such as the Svedaja (born of sweat or heat and moisture abiogenous' the Andaja ( egg-born or oviparous the Udbhijja (vegetable) and the Jarayuja (placental or viviparous j. The Purusha or the subjective personalit}' (man) is the greatest of them all, because all other forms of life are made to minister to his wants on Earth. Disease Sts Definition :—The Purusha (man) is the receptacle of any particular disease, and that which proves a source of torment or pain to him, is deno- minated as a disease.* There are four different types of disease such as, Traumatic or of extraneous origin (Agantuka), Bodily (Sharira), jNfenla] (Manasa) and Natural (Svabhavika). A disease clue to an extraneous blow hurt called Agantuka. Diseases due irregularities food drink, incidental deranged state of the blood, or the bodily humours acting either singh' concert, are called Sharira. Excessive anger, grief, fear, joy, despondency, envy, miser};, ])ride, greed, lust, desire, malice, etc. are other fundamental material principles, not improper classify all under the head of hot or cold, a third factor being non-existent. Hence the world (^Trf) is possessed of the twofold virtues, hot and cold. disease may be defined as something whicli affiicts the Purusha (self-conscious personality), or those things or incidents which combine afflict the Purusha are usually interpreted to connote that meaning. Chap. included within the category of^ mental (Manasa) distempers whereas hm7.ger, thirst, decrepitude, imbecility, death, sleep, etc. are called the natural (Svabhavika) derangements of the body. The Mind and the Bodv are the seats of the abovesaid distempers according as they are restricted to either of them, or affect both of them in unison. Samshodhanam (Cleansing), and Samshamanam (Pacification of the deranged or agitated bodih' humours giving rise to the disease^ and the regimen of diet and conduct are the four fectors which should be duly emplo3"ed in order to successfully cope with a disease.! Food is the principal factor which materially contri- butes to the strength, complexion and vitality (Ojah) of animated beings. Food consists six different tastes * The Self or Ihe feevitma of a person is above all human concerns and, as such, can never be affected by any disease. t Cleansing (Samshodhanam) is of two kinds, viz. External and Internal. External purification consists employing such measures surgical operations, cauterisation the affected part organ, external use alkaline preparations and medicated plasters, the internal one including such measures exhibition purgatives and emetics, application intestinal enemas (Asthapanam) and blood-letting.

english_text: Diet comprises four different factors such as, food, drink, lambative, etc., which, for the purposes the Ayurveda, are again grouped under three different heads, such the pacifier of the deranged bodily humours (Dosha-prashamanam), thera- peutical (VyMhi-prashamanam) and health-giving (Svastha=Vrittikara). Achara (conduct) appertains three different factors, such as the body, the speech, and the mental acts. The abovesaid measures, duly employed, arc potent enough combat all sorts bodily distempers, the special exigencies each case arc carefully taken into consideration. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. (Rasa) '[which capnot exist independently of the subs- tances] in which they are inherent. These substances which are called the (3shadhis may be di^ided into two classes such the mobile and the immobile. The immobile Oshadhis in their turn, admit of being grouped under four sub-heads such the ^'"anaspatis, the Vrikshas. the Virudhas and the Oshadhis proper. Those trees which bear fruit without blossoming are called the Vanaspatis such as, the Plaksha and the Oudumvura). Those that bear both fruits and flowers are called the \'rikshas. Shrubs and creepers that trail on the ground are called Virudhas, whereas those plants which die with the ripening of their fruits, are called Oshadhis proper such as cereals The mobile Oshadhis or animals are divided into four classes such as the viviparous, the oviparous, the sweat-begotten, and those that are born of decomposed vegetable matter. Man and other mammals belong the first group ; birds, snakes, and reptiles belong to the second ; ants, worms, etc. belong to the third ; while frogs and Indragopas belong to the fourth. For medicinal pur- poses, bark, leaves, flowers, fruits, roots, bulbs, the pressed juice, and milky or resinous secretions of plants, etc.* are .obtained from the vegetable world. The * The use of oil expressed oui of diutjs and seeds, well as of iheir ashes or alkaline preparations are likewise indicated. Chap. SUTRASTHAWAM. skin, nails, wool, blood, flesh, fat., marrow, bones, are procured from the animal world. JMetals and minerals such as gold, silver, gems, and Manahshila i Realgar), well pearls, clay and Kapalas (bones^, etc. should included the list the earthy substances. * Gale, windfall, sunshine, shade, moonshine, dark- ness, heat, cold, rain, day, night, fortnight, month, seasons, and solstices, etc. should deemed as the works of eternal time, which, by virtue of their natural effects, contribute the accumulation, augmentation, pacification diminution the deranged bodilv humours (such as, wind, etc. Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—Physicians should look upon these four factors of ffood, conduct, earth and time , as the accumulators, aggravators and pacifiers of the deranged bodilv humours and of the diseases resulting therefrom in man. Diseases due causes which are extraneous to the bodv ma\' affect the mind or the body.

english_text: When it would affect the body in the shape of any traumatic disease (such as an inflammation due to a blow or a sword cut it "should be treated medicinally like the rest of the physical mala- dies, while the remedy should consist in the enjovmentof * Oxide of Iron, sand, yellow sulphurale of arsenic (Orpiment), sail, Gairika (ferruginous earth), Rasdnjana (antimony) should be regarded appertaining to the class of earthy substances. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. hap. pleasurable sound?, touch, sights, taste or smell where the mind would be found to be the seat of the distemper. Thus I have briefly dealt with the Purusha, Disease, Medicine, Appliances and the Specific Time. The term Purusha should interpreted include within its meaning the combination its five material com- ponents, and all things resulting therefrom, such as the limbs and members of the body, as well as the skin, the flesh, the blood, the veins and the nerves, etc. The term Disease signifies all distempers incidental to the several or combined actions of the three deranged bodily humours and blood. The term Medicine signifies drugs and their virtues, tastes, potency, inherent efficacy Prabhava and reactionary properties Vipaka Appliances (kriya denotes such processes as, surgical operations, injections, emulsive measures, lubrications, etc. The term Time signifies all opportune moments for medical appliances. Authoritative verses the sub- ject : —The primary principle of the Science of medicine has thus been briefly stated and will be fully dealt with in the'following one hundred and twenty chapters distri- buted among the fi-\e main sub-divisions Sthanas the present work. These hundred and twenty chapters will found elaborately discussed according to the specific import or significance of their denominations under the sub-heads of Sutra- Sthanam Chap. (Definitive Aphorisms Fundamental principles, Nidanam ^Etiology", Sharira-Sthanam (Anatomy and Physiology Chikitsa-Sthanam (Therapeutics) and Kalpa-Sthanam (Toxicology Subjects other than the preceding ones will be discussed in the closing chapters of the book by wa}- of an Appendix (Uttara-Tantranv. Authoritative verse the sub- ject :—The man who reads this Eternal Science of Medicine (Ayurveda-Shastram ) discoursed by the self- origined Brahma and propagated by the King of Kasi, becomes noted for his piety, is honoured by the kings on earth, and attains to the region of Indra (the lord of the celestials) after death. Thus ends the first chapter of the Sutra-SthSnam the Sushrula Samhitd which deals with the origin of the Ayurveda. CHAPTER Now we shall discuss the Chapter which deals with the rites formal initiation of a pupil into the science Medicine (Shishyopanayaniya- madhya'yam).

chapter: Unknown
english_text: Such initiation should be imparted to a student, belonging to one of the three twice-born castes such as, the Brahmana, the Kshatriya, and the Vaishya, and who should be of tender years, born a good family, possessed of, a desire to learn_, strength^ energy of action, contentment, character, self-control, a good retentive me- mory, intellect, courage, purity of mind and bod)', and a simple and clear comprehension, command a clear insight into tlie things studied, and should be found to have been further graced with the necessary qualifications thin lips, thin teeth and thin tongue, and possessed of a straight nose, large, honest, intelligent eyes, with a benign contour of the mouth, and a contented frame of mind, being pleasant in his speech and dealings, and usually painstaking his efforts. A man possessed contrary attributes should not admitted into the sacred precincts of) medicine. lYlode of Initiation :— A Brahmana preceptor sliould initiate a disciple or student in the following way —A square sand cushion or platform, measuring a cubit L"hap. 11. SUTkASTHANAM. in length and breadth, should be laid out on plot smooth, level and sacred' ground under the benign fluence of any auspicious phase of the moon or astral combination such as, the "Karanam," e»tc. and in a direc- tion of the compass whifch is held most auspicious to that end. The cusliion or the jilatform should be plastered over with a solution of water and cow-dung ; and blades of Kusha grass should be strewn over it. Then the gods, the Brahmanas and the ph5''sicians should worshipped with oblations flowers, fried paddy, gems and sun- dried rice. Then having drawn straight lines across the Sthandilara as to meet the top the furthest side of the square, and having sprinkled them over with holy water, the preceptor should lay down a blade of Kusha grass tied up in the form of a knot, known as the Brahmana, along the side of the sacred cushion to his right, and kindle the sacred fire close to his seat. Then having soaked the twigs of the four sacrificial trees Khadira, Palasha, Devadaru and Vilva, Vata, Oudumvara, Ashvattha and Madhuka in curd, honey and clarified butter, he should perform the rite of Homa according to the rules of a Darvi Homa ceremony^ Then libations of clarified butter should be cast into the sacrifi- cial fire with a repetition of the Maha Vyahriti Mantras preceded by the mystic Omkara. After that, libations of clarified butter should be cast into the fire in honour each of the gods and Rishis (celestial physicians) invoked THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. 1 Chap. 11. b}^ repeating the Svaha Mantra, and the disciple should be made to do the same. Brahmana preceptor competent initiate a student belonging' to any of the three twice-born castes. Kshatriya preceptor can initiate a student the Kshatriya the Vaish3'a caste, while Vaishya preceptor can initiate student his own caste alone.

english_text: Shudra student good character T.nd parentage may be initiated into the mysteries of the A5'urveda by omitting the Mantras enjoined recited on such an occasion. Then having thrice circumambulated the sacrificial fire, and having invoked the firegod to bear testimony to the fact, the preceptor should address the initiated dis- ciple follows :—"Thou shalt renounce lust, anger, greed, ignorance, vanity, egotistic feelings, envy, harsh- ness, niggardliness, falsehood, idleness, ndij all acts that soil the good name of a man. In proper season thou shalt pair thy nails and clip thy hair and put on the sacred cloth, dyed brownish yellow, live the life of a truthful, self-controlled anchorite and be obedient and respectful towards thy preceptor. In sleep, in rest, or while moving about—while meals studv, and all acts "^ The libations should oftered follows -Svah^ (obeisance) P,i-ahm4, Svah.4 Praj^pati (the lord the created beings), SvahA Ashvins, .Svaha to Indra, Sv.nhA Dhanvantari, Sv^hA to Bharadv^ja, and SvAh^ to A'treva. Chap. SUTRASTHA'NAAl. thou shalt guided by my directions. Thcju shalt what pleasant and beneficial otherwise thou shalt incur sin and' all thy study and knowledge shall fail to bear their wished for fruit, and thou shalt gain feme. the other hand, treat thee unjustly even w'ith thy perfect obedience and in full conformity to the terms agreed upon, may incur equal sin with thee, and may all my know- ledge prove futile, and never have any scope of work display. Thou shalt help with thy professional skill and knowledge, the Brahmanas, thy elders, preceptors and friends, the indigent, the honest, the anchorites, the helpless and those who shall come thee (from a distance;, or those who shall live close as well as thy relations and kinsmen [to the best of thy knowledge and ability], and thou shalt give them medicine [without charging for it any remuneration whatever], and God will bless thee for that. Thou shalt not treat medicinally professional hunter, fowler, a habitual sinner, or him who has been degrad- life ; and even doing thou shalt acquire friends, fame, piety, wealth and all wished for objects in life and thy knowledge shall gain pubhcity." Prohibited periods of the study of the Ayurveda :—The day of the new moon, the eighth day of the moon's wane, the fourteenth day of the dark fortnight, well the corresponding days tHE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. I Chap. the brio^ht one, the day oi the tlill moon, and the meet- ings olday and night such as (morning and evening) are occasions when the study of the Ayurveda is prohibited. Similarly, clap thunder heard improper season (months of Pousha, Phalguna and Chaitra), or Hash of lightning occurring at a' time when such pheno- mena are naturall)' rare, or an evil befalling one's country, relations, king, should deemed occasions jnohibiting the study the Ayurveda.

english_text: imperatively necessary that the book should be read ; and after having read it one should attend the practice the science The physician who has learnt these both, lit to be honour- ed by kings, THE SUSHKUTA SAiMHlTA. f Chap. III. Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—A ph^'sician, well versed in the principles of the science of medicine (Ayurveda), but unskilful his art through want practice, loses his wit the bedside his patient, just as, a coward his wit's end determine what when for the first time he finds himself in the ranks of a contending army. the other hand a physician, experienced in his art l)ut deficient in the knowledge of the Ayurveda, is condemned all good men quack, and deserves capital punishment the hands of the king. Both these classes ph3^sicians are not to be trusted, because are inexpert and half educated. Such men are incapable discharging the duties their vocation, just as a one-winged bird is incapable of taking flight in the air. Even a panacea or a medicine of ambrosial virtues administered b)' an unpractised or norant ph)^sician, will prove j^ositively baneful draught of poison, or a blow with a weapon, or a thunder- bolt. A physician, ignorant of the science and art of sur- gery and emollient measures Sneha-karma , etc. is but killer of men out of cupidity, and who allowed to carry his nefarious trade only through the advertence of the king. A physician well --ersed in the principles surger}-, and experienced the prac- tice of medicine, alone caj^able of curing distempers, just as only a two- wheeled cart can be of service field of battle. Chap. III. Now hear me, O child, describe the mode of st'adying the present science of the Ayurveda.) The pupil having worshipped and recited his daily prayers should calmly sit near his preceptor, pure body and mind, who should teach him full i^hloka or couplet the Ayurveda), or a half or a quarter part thereof, adapted to his intellectual capacity. Then he should make full and elaborate paraphrase the recited couplet or any piwi thereof, and ask his pupils individually to do the same. When the pupils have paraphrased the same to the satisfaction of the precep- tor, he should again recite the same stanza or couplet. The passages shlokas should not recited too hastily, nor drawled out in a timid or faltering voice, nor with a nasal intonation. The voice should be neither too loud, nor too weak, but each sound should be clearly and distinctly uttered, and the lips, the eyes, the eye- brows, and the hands, etc. should not be lifted or moved keep time with the recitation. one should be allowed pass between the pupil and the preceptor at the time of study. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject pupil who pure, obedient his preceptor, applies himself steadily his work, and abandons laziness and excessive sleep, will arrive at the end of the science (he has been studying .\ student or a pupil, having 'finished tire course THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. L Chap. III.

CHAPTER Now we shall discuss the Chapter which deals with General Explanations (Prabha'Saniya- madhyaryam). The endeavours of a man who has studied the entire Ayurveda (shastra) but fails to make a clear exposition of the same, are vain like the efforts of an ass that carries load sandal wood (without ever being able to enjoy its pleasing scent). Authoritative verse the sub- ject :—A foolish person who has gone through a large number of books without gaining any real insight into the knowledge propounded therein, like an ass laden with logs of sandal-wood, that labours under the weight which carries without being able appreciate its virtue. Hence the preceptor will clearly explain each shioka or a half quarter part thereof contained the present work, divided into a hundred and twenty chapters well as in the concluding portion the Uttara-Tantram appended to ; and the student the disciple shall attentively hear everything explained or discoursed on by the preceptor. chapter: Unknown
english_text: physician who studies the Science of Medicine from the h"ps of his preceptor, and practises medicine after having acquired experience in his art by constant practice, the true physician, while any other map dabbling in tne art, should be looked upon as an impostor. The Shalya-Tantras (surgical works) written propagated by Aupadhenava, Aurabhra, Sushruta and Paushkalavata, are the bases the works Tantras written by others {^Karavirya, Gopura-rakshita, etc, Thus ends the fourth chapter the the Sushruta Samhit^ which deals with General Explanations. CHAPTER Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of Preliminary measures in connection with the curative remedies of a disease).* (Agropaharaniyam- adhyaryam). The entire course of medical treatment connec- tion with disease grouped under Ihree subheads, as the Preliminary measures (Purva-karma) the Principal therapeutical surgical appliances (Pradhana-karma'> ; and the After-measures (Paschat- karma). These measures will discussed under the head each disease as we shall have occasion deal with them. As the present treatise principall)'- Several authorities hold that acts such fasting, administration of purgatives, etc. should be included within the preliminary measures application of absorbent (Pachana) or healing medicinal agents, within the second the principal measures ; and the administration tonics or restoratives within the third or the after-measure group. Others, the contrary, lay down that measures adopted for the absorption, lubrication (pacification by the application of oily substances) elimination the deranged bodily humours Ijy sweating should grouped under the first subhead ( Purva-karma), the administration of active purgatives, emeticsr etc., under the second (Pradhana-karma) and the giving of rice meal, etc. to the patient under the (Paschat-karma) last while according to others the active medicinal agents employed to cope with the deranged humours in the incubative stage of a bodily disease till the appearance of its first characteristic symptoms, should be denominated the Preliminary measure ; measures employed for the subjugation of a disease in its patent or fully developed stage the Pradhana-karma, and measures employed to guard against the recrudescence of a disease and for the restoration health in a patient is the sequel treatment or the Pasch^t-karma. Chap.^ V. SUTRASTHA'NAM. -^7 treats of surgical acts or operations, we shall discourse on them and their accessories at the outset. Surgical acts or operations are divided into eight different kinds such Incising (Chhedya), Excising (Bhedya), Scraping (Lekhj^as Puncturing (Vedhya), Searching probing (Eshya), Extracting (Abarj'-a), Secreting fluids (Visravya) and Suturing (Seevya).

english_text: surgeon (Vaidya) called upon to perform any (of the eight preceding kinds) of operations, must first equip himself with such accessories as surgical appliances and instru- ments, alkali, fire, probe or director (Shalaka), horns, leeches, gourd (Alavu), Jamvavoushtha (a kind of pencil shaped rod made of slate with its top-end cut into the shape of a Jamboline fruit), cotton, lint, thread, leaves, tow(Patta), honey, clarified butter, lard, milk, oil, Tarpanara (powdered wheat soaked in water), decoc- tions Kashaya , medicated plasters, paste (Kalka), fan, cold water, hot water, and cauldrons, etc., and moreover shall secure the services of devoted and strong- nerved attendants. Then under the auspices of blissful astral coi-Qbina- tions, etc., and having propitiated the Brahmanas and the physicians, with gifts of curd, sun-dried rice, cordials and gems, etc., and having made offerings to the gods and uttered benediction, etc., the surgeon should commence his work. The pa'dent should given light food (before the act), and made sit with THE SUSHRUTA SAMHTTA'. [ Chap. his face turned towards the east. His limbs should carefully fastened (so to guard against their least movement during the continuance the operation). Then the surgeon, sitting with his face towards the west, and carefulh' avoiding the vital parts (Marmas), Veins, nerves (Snayus), joints, bones and arteries of the patient,should insert the knife into the affected part along tlie proper direction till the suppurated part would be reached and swiftly draw out. In case of extended suppuration, the part opened (length of incision) should be made measure two or three finger's widths in length. incision (Vrana) which is wide, extended, equally and evenly divided, should be deemed the best. Authoritative verses the sub- ject incision which wide, extended, well divided, does not involve any vital part, etc. the patient, and well-matured regards time, the best of its kind*. Courage, light handedness, non-shak- ing, non-sweating, sharp instruments, self confidence and self command are what should possessed a surgeon engaged in opening a boil or an abscess. Two or three incisions should be made if a single opening does not seem large enough for the purpose. Certain commentators interpret the couplet follows boil or an abscess which is wide, extended, well defined its shape, equally suppurated in all its parts and does not involve any vital part the body is the fittest thing for a surgeon's knife— Tr. Chap, v.] Authoritative verse the 'sub- ject :—The knife (lancet) should freely used wherever fissure, sinus, cavity would appear boil, as to ensure a complete flowing out of the pus accumulated in it. Lateral (tirjak) incisions should be made in regions of the eye-brows, temple, forehead, cheeks, eyelids, lower lip, gums, armpits, loins, belly and the groins.

english_text: An incision made in the region of the hand root should be made to resemble the disc of the moon, while those about the anus and the penis should made semi-circular ! half-moon j in shape. Authoritative verse the sub- ject : —An incision in any of the abovesaid regions not made as directed, may give rise to extreme pain, pro- longed granulation (healing) and condylomatous growths in and about the ulcer, owing to an inadvertent cutting of the local veins, nerves. case of artificial instrumental parturition, ascites, piles, stone in the bladder, fistula in ano, and in diseases affecting the cavity of the mouth, the patient operated on should be kept on an empty stomach (before thS^act). Then sprays of cold water should dashed over the face and the eyes of the patient to relieve the pain and the sense of exhaustion incidental to the operation. The sides of the incision should be firmly pressed fso as ensure a good outflow of the accumulated pus) and THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. the margins the wound should rubbed with the fingers that they may have level surface and uniform structure throughout.) Then the wound should be washed with an astringent decoction (of Nimba, Triphala, etc.) which should be wiped and made thoroughly dry with a piece of clean linen. Then a lint plug 'Varti) plastered over with the (paste) Kalka of sesamum, hone)' and clarified butter, and soaked in disinfectant (lit :—purifying medicines such Ajagandha, etc. i should be inserted deep into the cavity the wound. After that, poultice made offi- cinal substances should applied over and the whole should be bound with thick layers of tow (Kavalikas—such as the leaves and bark of the Indian figtree etc.) which are neither too irritant nor too cooling in their effect ; and finally scraps of clean linen should be wound round them. The limb, [or the affected part] should be subsequently fumigated with the fumes pain-killing (anodyne) substances and also with those drugs which are supposed ward off all malignant spirits (from the bedside of the patient.)* Then it should be fumigated with the drugs, known Guggulu, Vacha, white mustard, Saindhava and the leaves of the Ximva tree, soaked clarified butter. The residue the clarified butter [dripped * Even the bedsheels, etc. of the patient should be fumigated as above. This foreshadows the germ theory of the modern days— Tr. Chap. SUTRASTHA'NAM. down and collected from the fumigating corftpound described above], should rubbed over the region of the heart and other vital parts of the patient, and the floor the chamber should washed and sprinkled over with drops of water previousl)^ kept a (new) pitcher for the purpose.

english_text: The rites of protection from the influences of baneful spirits, should then be performed reciting the Mantra which runs follows :—"I am about to practise the prophylactic incan- tation for guarding thy person against the malignant influences of Rakshas and conjured demonesses, and may the god Brahma be graciously pleased to approve of its performance. May the Gods and deities and mini- sters grace disperse and confound the hosts wrathful Nagas (celestial serpents), Pishachas, Gandhar- vas and Pitris that might maliciously disposed to strike thee in thy sickly confinement. May the spirits, which stir abroad in the night and roam about the sky and on earth, defend thy person in recognition thy fervent devotion to them. May the concourse Brahma-begotten sages 'such as, Sanaka, etc.), the saintly and canonised kings (Rajarshis) in heaven and the sacred mounts, streams and oceans the earth protect thee from evil. May the fire-god guard thy tongue the wind-god protect thy breath and the Moon-god, Parjanya, Vidyut lightning) and the spirit of the clouds preserve the healthy coursings of» those vital winds th}' organism which are respectively known Vy^na, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA [ Chap, v Apana; Udana and Samana. May Indra, the presiding deity of all physical energies, keep thy bodily strength immaculate. May Manu defend the two side tendons the nape thy neck, well thy facult)^ intellect the Gandharvas, thy faculty desire Indra, thy fortitude Varuna, thy faculty cogni- tion the Ocean, thy region of umbilicus the Sun- god, thy eyes the Quarters of the Heaven, thy ears the Moon-god, thy mind ; the Stars, thy complexion the Night, thy shadow ; the Water, thy vigour the Oshadhis, thy hair Infinite Ether, the space which imprisoned thy body \\isundhara, thy body Vaishvanara, thy head Vishnu, thy moral courage Purushottama (the foremost beings), thy energy of action (dynamical action of purposes); Brahma, thy self; and Dhruva (immutable being), thv eyebrows. May these divinities, which perpetually reside thy body, ensure thy safe continuance being and may thou enjoy a long life through their grace. May the gods such Brahma, etc., confer blessings on thy head. May the Sun, the Moon, the twin sages Narada and Parvata, the fire-god, the wind, and the other celestial helpmates of Indra, bring thee good. May the pro- phylaxis devised by Brahma keep thee from evil. Mav thou be spared witness the return of many a long and happy year on earth. May such abnormal physi- cal phenomena as, droyght, deluge, excessive downpour rain, and excessive germination wholesale Chap, v.] extinction such vermin rats, mosquitoes, flies which invariably portend evil and mortality community, well bloody feuds among kings, abate and cease. May thou be relieved of all pain and misery. We close the prayer with a "Svaha" (obeisance\ The present Vedic mantra exercises occult power in relieving ailments which are due the malignant influences of conjured up she-devils.

chapter: Chapter X: ow we shall discuss the Chapter which treats the characteristic features of the differetit seasons of the year and their influence health and drugs fRitucharya'dhya'yam). The Eternal Time is without origin, middle, end, self-l/egotten, and the lord of all attributes. Contrariety or non contrariety of the natural attributes drugs substances endued with characteristic tastes, such sweet, etc., are brought about by time and time the principal factor that controls the births or deaths of beings. Etymology of the term Ka'Ia (t^me):— The Kala the Eternal time called from the fact of its not suffering even one its own minutest particles subdivisions (Kala) perish, though perpetually moving, and in constant motion in itself it derives its epithet from the. fundamental quality its destroying all beings and laying their dead remains in heaps in succession. Some assert that the name is due the fact that time blends ( kalanam ) all beings with misery or happiness according their respective acts, or to its leading all beings to destruction ( kala). The Sun-god, his peculiar motions, divides eternal time which is measured by years ( Samvatsaras)
english_text: May thou acquire a long life through the protective energy the pro- phylactic prayer (lit :—incantation; now read by me. Then having protected the body of the patient with the recitation of the above Vedic Mantra, the surgeon shall see his patient taken his own chamber, and prescribe the proper course of medicine and diet accord- ing the exigencies of each case. The old bandage should loosened on the third day of the operation, when the wound or the ulcer should washed, and a fresh bandage should be wound round as before. The bandage should not be loosened the day following the lancing boil, such measure might give rise to a sort of excruciating pain and formation of knots the wound and retard the process of granulation (healing). On the third day, the surgeon (Vaidya) should prescribe the proper medicated plaster, diet, etc. after fully considering the strength of the patient, the nature of the disease, and the then prevailing season of the year. A wound should not be tried to be healed up, as long as THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA [ Chap. the least morbid matter, or pus remains in its inside, as it would lead to the formation of fresh cavities the surrounding health}^ tissues, and ultimately recrudescence of the disease. The authoritative verses the subject :—Accordingly a wound or an ulcer should be made to heal up after the perfect purification of both its inside and exterior has been fully brought about. Even after the healing the wound the patient should studiously avoid all sexual connections, indigestive viands, fatiguing physical exercises and indulgence in emotions of grief or fright, or in ecstasies of joy, until the cicatrix has acquired enough toughness. The dressings and bandages should untied and changed ever}' third day in winter, in spring and in the season of Hemanta, and on each alternate day in summer and in the rains. But physician (surgeon) should not be guided by these rules in cases where there would be reasons to apprehend imminent danger, and in such cases the wound the ulcer, like a house in flames, should be checked as speedily as possible. chapter: Chapter X: ow we shall discuss the Chapter which treats the characteristic features of the differetit seasons of the year and their influence health and drugs fRitucharya'dhya'yam). The Eternal Time is without origin, middle, end, self-l/egotten, and the lord of all attributes. Contrariety or non contrariety of the natural attributes drugs substances endued with characteristic tastes, such sweet, etc., are brought about by time and time the principal factor that controls the births or deaths of beings. Etymology of the term Ka'Ia (t^me):— The Kala the Eternal time called from the fact of its not suffering even one its own minutest particles subdivisions (Kala) perish, though perpetually moving, and in constant motion in itself it derives its epithet from the. fundamental quality its destroying all beings and laying their dead remains in heaps in succession. Some assert that the name is due the fact that time blends ( kalanam ) all beings with misery or happiness according their respective acts, or to its leading all beings to destruction ( kala). The Sun-god, his peculiar motions, divides eternal time which is measured by years ( Samvatsaras)
english_text: Etymology of the term Ka'Ia (t^me):— The Kala the Eternal time called from the fact of its not suffering even one its own minutest particles subdivisions (Kala) perish, though perpetually moving, and in constant motion in itself it derives its epithet from the. fundamental quality its destroying all beings and laying their dead remains in heaps in succession. Some assert that the name is due the fact that time blends ( kalanam ) all beings with misery or happiness according their respective acts, or to its leading all beings to destruction ( kala). The Sun-god, his peculiar motions, divides eternal time which is measured by years ( Samvatsaras) THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. VI. into 'increasingly progressive but smaller subdivisions) such as, Nimeshas (lit :—time taken in closing the eyelids^, Kashthas^ Kalas, Muhurtas, days and nights, fortnights, months, seasons, solstices, years and Yugas. Time taken in articulating any of the short vowels (such as A. etc.), is called an Akshi-Nimesha. Fifteen Akshi-Nimeshas make one Kashtha. Thirty Kashthas make one Muhurta. Thirty Muhurtas make one day and night. Fifteen days and nights make one fort- night. fortnight either dark or bright. Two fort- nights make one month. The twelve months such as, Magha, etc. are divided into six seasons such as. Winter, Spring, Summer, Rains, Autumn and Hemanta, each consisting of two months. The two months known Tapas and Tapasva (Magha and Phalgunai constitute the season of winter. Spring consists two months called Madhu and Madhava (Chaitra and Vaishaka . Summer is marked by two months known as Shuchi and Shukra Jaistha and Ashadha The rains or the rainy season is marked by two months called Nabhas and Xabhasya (Shravana and Bh^dra\ The two months known Isha and Urja (Ashvina and Kartika) constitute what called the season of Autumn. Hemanta is marked by two months called Sahas and Sahasya ' Agraha5^ana and Pousha). These six seasons are respectiveh^ characterised by cold, heat, rains, etc. Chap. SUTRASTHA'NAM. The two Ayanams are ushered in by the sun afid the moon changing their respective courses in the heavens (passing over the tropics of Cancer and Capricorn) as the measurers time. The rains, autumn and Hemanta follow one another succession when the sun over the Tropic Capricorn or the Winter Solstice (Dakshinayanami and the moon gains strength this part the year. Rasas (Serum sap) possessed acid, saline and sweet tastes, grow strong and become dominant when the sun over the Tropic of Capricorn ^Dakshinayanam) and all beings gain in strength and energy more and more. Winter, spring and summer mark the passing the sun over the Summer Solstice ' Uttarayanam The sun grows stronger in heat and light, and saps rasas i of bitter, pun- gent and sour tastes increase in intensity, and all animals gradually begin to lose strength and energy.

english_text: Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—The moon imparts the moisture and humi- dity the earth which soaked up by the sun his daily course, while the wind conjunction with the sun and the moon, contributes towards the preservation of animal life. The successive change the two solstices marks a year. Five such complete years count as a Yuga. The sub- divisions of eternal time from the- minutest Ximesha to a complete Yuga, are constantly revolving like a wheel_, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. and this constant or perpetual revolution is called the wheel C5Tle time ^,Kala-Chakra certain authorities. The six seasons such as, the Rains, etc., have been again adverted to in this chapter for the purpose of fully describing the accumulation, excitation aggravation) and pacification the bodily humours, such as wind, etc. According to some, the rainy season consists of two months known as Bhadra and Ashvina ; Autumn consists of the two months of Kartika and Margashirshya manta consists of the two months of Poushaand Magha spring consists the two months of Phalguna and Chaitra ; summer, of Vaishakha and Jaistha ; and Pr^^Tit, of Ashadha and Shravana. Oshadhis ' Medical plants and cereals sprout during the rains and are enfeebled their properties. Water becomes muddy turbid and the earth covered over with fresh deposits of washed off or silted mud. The sky becomes overcast with clouds, and the wind, charged with excess of humidity, dulls the appetite and organisms beings. Hence the food of beings which principally consists tender and new-grown vegetables of feeble potency, consider- ably vitiated by the turbid water partaken of as drink during the season, proves acid its digestive reaction, and germinates exce-ssive bile in the human system. In autumn the skv becomes cloudless, the mire is dried Chap. SUTRASTHA'NAM. up, and the bile originated and accumulated during the rains, is liquefied by the , rays the sun and gives rise to bilious diseases* Plants and vegetables (Oshadhis) tlmt grow or sprout during the rainy season, are matured in course of time and ripen in their virtues and potency in the season of Hemanta. The water becomes clear, cool and heavy in this season. The sun's rays become feeble and mild and the winds moistened with frost and snow, make the human system little numb and heavy. Hence water and vegetables partaken Hemanta are divested of their properties of acid reaction after being assimilated in the human system, but they give rise to an accumulation of phlegm the body owing their heaviness, sliminess, and cooling and oily character. In spring, the phlegm thus accumulated in the body hquefied and ushers in diseases due to deranged state of that bodily humour, t The said plants and vegetables, their turn, lose their sap, moisture and nutritive element summer, and become dry and extremely light.

english_text: The best safe- guard lies in not using such defiled water and vegetables when an epidemic breaks out in the country. THESUSHRUTA SAMHITA, [ Chap. vi. Sometimes a town city depopulated curse, anger, sin, or by a monster or a demoness conjured up by a spell or incantation. Sometimes the pollens of poisonous flowers grasses, etc., wafted the winds, invade a town village, and produce sort epidemic cough, asthma, catarrh, fever, irrespective all constitutional n^^nii-vit^^c kDr'^dei'Un^::! bodily humours agitateS'l^/Lkll^^,fTowns and villages are known have been depopulated through malignant astral influences, through houses^ wives*, beds, seats, carriages, riding animals, gems and precious stones assuming inauspicious features. Prophylactic measures:— In such cases migration healthy or unaffected locality, perfor- mances of rites of pacification and atonement, (wearing of prophylactic gems and drugs), recitations of mantras, libations of clarified butter cast into the sacrificial fire, offerings the gods, celebration sacrificial cere- monies, obeisance with clasped palms the gods, practice of penances, sell-control and charity, kindness, spiritual initiation; obedience one's elders and preceptors, and devotion to the gods and the Bramhanas, and observance of such like rules of conduct may prove beneficial to the affected community. Marriages with girls of prohibited description have been known as well to have ushered in an epidemic which devastated a whole town or a country. Chap. The characteristic features of the seasons Avhich not exhibit natural traits (Metrical texts) -Cold winds from the north blow the season Hemanta. The quarters of the sky are enveloped in smoke and assume dusky aspect. The sun hid the frost, and lakes and pools are frozen lie covered over with flakes, thin layers ice. Cro\^'s, rhinoceroses, buffaloes, lambs and elephants become excited and sprightly in this part of the year and the Lodhra, Priyangu, and Punnaga trees begin to blossom. Winter exhibits the same features above, only in a greater degree intensity and the quarters the sky are agitated strong gales wind and showers of rain. In spring, when the summits of the mount Malaya are besmeared red with the moist foot-prints of the brides of the Siddhas and the Vidyadharas, and are perfumed contact with the sweet-scented sandal forests, the lively south-wind is roused up from his lair and winnows gladness to damsels burning with desires, and kindles the flame of love and appeases the amorous anger of the beloved pairs turning their fancies to themes of love. The quarters of the sky are cleared and look joyful. The woods are decked with the full-blown flowers the Kinshuka, lotus, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. VI. Vakula, mango and Ashoka trees. The bee hums and the notes the Cuckoo are heard reverberate through the skies. The south wind fans this king the seasons, and the forests are hung with the festoons of tender and sprouting leaves in his honour.

Prophylactic measures:— In such cases migration healthy or unaffected locality, perfor- mances of rites of pacification and atonement, (wearing of prophylactic gems and drugs), recitations of mantras, libations of clarified butter cast into the sacrificial fire, offerings the gods, celebration sacrificial cere- monies, obeisance with clasped palms the gods, practice of penances, sell-control and charity, kindness, spiritual initiation; obedience one's elders and preceptors, and devotion to the gods and the Bramhanas, and observance of such like rules of conduct may prove beneficial to the affected community. Marriages with girls of prohibited description have been known as well to have ushered in an epidemic which devastated a whole town or a country. Chap. The characteristic features of the seasons Avhich not exhibit natural traits (Metrical texts) -Cold winds from the north blow the season Hemanta. The quarters of the sky are enveloped in smoke and assume dusky aspect. The sun hid the frost, and lakes and pools are frozen lie covered over with flakes, thin layers ice. Cro\^'s, rhinoceroses, buffaloes, lambs and elephants become excited and sprightly in this part of the year and the Lodhra, Priyangu, and Punnaga trees begin to blossom. Winter exhibits the same features above, only in a greater degree intensity and the quarters the sky are agitated strong gales wind and showers of rain. In spring, when the summits of the mount Malaya are besmeared red with the moist foot-prints of the brides of the Siddhas and the Vidyadharas, and are perfumed contact with the sweet-scented sandal forests, the lively south-wind is roused up from his lair and winnows gladness to damsels burning with desires, and kindles the flame of love and appeases the amorous anger of the beloved pairs turning their fancies to themes of love. The quarters of the sky are cleared and look joyful. The woods are decked with the full-blown flowers the Kinshuka, lotus, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. VI. Vakula, mango and Ashoka trees. The bee hums and the notes the Cuckoo are heard reverberate through the skies. The south wind fans this king the seasons, and the forests are hung with the festoons of tender and sprouting leaves in his honour.

The Eternal Time is without origin, middle, end, self-l/egotten, and the lord of all attributes. Contrariety or non contrariety of the natural attributes drugs substances endued with characteristic tastes, such sweet, etc., are brought about by time and time the principal factor that controls the births or deaths of beings. Etymology of the term Ka'Ia (t^me):— The Kala the Eternal time called from the fact of its not suffering even one its own minutest particles subdivisions (Kala) perish, though perpetually moving, and in constant motion in itself it derives its epithet from the. fundamental quality its destroying all beings and laying their dead remains in heaps in succession. Some assert that the name is due the fact that time blends ( kalanam ) all beings with misery or happiness according their respective acts, or to its leading all beings to destruction ( kala). The Sun-god, his peculiar motions, divides eternal time which is measured by years ( Samvatsaras)
english_text: The sun's rays become stronger and more intense summer. Unhealthy winds blow from the south-east. The earth is heated ; the rivers run narrow and shallow in their beds the quarters of the sky glare with blazing light, the birds Chakravakas with their mates roam about quest of cool ponds and reservoirs of water herds of deer are tormented and overwhelmed with thirst trees, plants and creepers are scorched by the intense heat, and withered leaves drop off from the trees which alone serve to make the identification of their parents possible. In the forepart of the rainy season (Pravrit', packs of detached clouds, spangled with lightning and driven before the gales of the west-wind, come thundering over and envelop the skies. The Earth robed green with luxurious growth corn, enlivened here and there the dark crimson the cochineal insects (Indragopa), and Kadamva, Nipa, Kutaja, and the Ketaki trees begin to flower. During the rainy season, the rivers overflow their banks, tumbling down the trees which grow them. Ponds and lakes are decked with the full-blown Kumud Chap. and Nilotpala flowers. The earth is covered with firofuse vegetation. All distinction between dry lands and resers^oirs of water becomes impossible, and the sun and the planets are enveloped dark clouds that shower torrents of rain but do not roar. In autumn the sun's rays assume mellow golden tint. Masses of white clouds are seen to sail the dark deep blue of heaven. Ponds are decked with the full blown lotus flowers, agitated the wings the diving swans The high grounds become dry, while the low- lands still retain their muddy character. The level plains are covered with shrubs and undergrowths, and plants and trees such Vana, Saptahva, Vandhuka, Kasha and Asana, flower in abundance. The bodily humours such as wind, etc. aie disturbed and aggravated the contrariety, excess vari- ations the characteristic features the seasons. Hence prudent check the deranged phlegm spring, to conquer the deranged bile in autumn, and to subdue the deranged bodily wind in the rains, before they develop themselves any patent or manifest bodily ailment. Thus ends the sixth chapter the the Sushruta Sanihit^ which treats of the characteristic features of the seasons and their influence on health and drugs. C H A P T E R Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats Surgical Appliances, their Uses and Construction. (Yantra-VicJhimadhya'yam). Surgical instruments number one hundred and one * all, which the hand is the most important, inas- much (all of them depend on the hand for • their principal auxiliary) and none of them can handled without and further because all sur- gical operations pre-eminentlj^ require its co-operation.

VII. enemas, etc, with passage aperture running through their entire .length^ are constructed variet}' shapes and for various purposes. Some them are open one end, while others are open at both. The Shalaka-Yantras bougies are various shapes and serve variety of purposes. The lengths and girths of these instruments should be determined 6o THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. : Chap. vil. according the necessity each individual case. Four probes directors shalaka in two pairs, are used for the purposes searching Eshana pus in a suppurated part limb, or in connection with uplifting, cutting and thereby withdrawing a shalyam from the part has pricked into, with a view to transfer such body from one place another Chalanam for the purpose extracting i Shalyam from the affected part. A couple directors are used for the pur- pose withdrawing foreign matter 'Shalyam; imbedded in any outer canal of the body (Srotas The top-ends of these directors are bent down little, and they resemble a lentil seed in size. english_text: The three sorts of directors used applying alkaline medicines, are shaped like ladles, and their mouths resemble the cavities little stone mortars (Khala.) Of the six sorts of directors used in connection with the process of cauterisation (Agni- Karma) three are mouthed like the Jamboline fruit, while the other three are faced like a mace spear (^Ankusha. A kind of director used in removing nasal Chap. VII. tumours, is mouthed like the half of the kernel 'found in the inside of a Jujube-stpne, with a little dip the middle, its lip end having a keen sharp edge. The ends the type probe used applying Anjanams medicated collyria the eyelids are wrought into two small round lobes like the Matara pulse and are blunted, while the sort probe used in cleansing the urethra, is made round like the end of tht; stem of a Mdlati flower. The Upa-yantras or minor surgical accessories—include such substances rope, the Venika (braided hair;, silk thread, the bark and the inner-skin of trees, creepers, linen, Ashthila 'stones , large oval shaped pebbles, a hammer, the palms of the hands, the soles of feet, fingers^ tongue, the teeth, the nails, hair, the mane of horses, branches of trees, a magnet, alkali, fire, and medicine, and such acts spitting, straining (kunthanam;, exhilaration and intimidation. IVIetrical texts : —These accessories should be applied to the entire body of a patient, or to any part thereof such as, the arteries, the ^'iscera, or the joints, according the necessities each case determined by the surgeon. The Functions of Surgical Instru- ments :—are striking out Nirghatanam-lit :—with- drawing Shalyam moving and fro), mjection filling, binding, up-lifting, cutting and THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. vii. thereby withdrawing Shalyam, resetting by means twirling motion, removing of a Shalyam from one place another, twisting, expanding, pressing, purifying passage, drawing off, attracting, bringing the surface, uplifting, lowering down, applying pressure all round a part, or an organ, agitating, sucking, searching, cutting or cleaving, straightening, washing or flushing, stuffing the nose and cleansing. They number twenty-four in all. IVIctrical texts :—The intelligent surgeon shall exercise his judgment and determine the nature the surgical operation required in each individual case, for surely the shalj^as requiring a surgeon's aid are infinitely varied in their character. An appliance A^antraUvhich is too thick, or. made of inferior metal and hence) not substantially made, or too short or too long, or does not admit of being easily handled and incapable taking the entire Shalyam, curved, loosely fitted, or soft-bolted, or loosely tied with cords, 'should not used surgical operations). These are the twelve defects of a surgical instrument.

VII. SUTRASTHA'NAM, 6^ Sinha-mukha (lion-mouthed) type, while those that can not be seen, should be removed with the help the Kanka-mukhas ( heron-mouthed instruments, etc., according to the directions laid down in the Shastras medical surgical works recognised authority). The Kanka-mukhas are the best of all other types of instruments, inasmuch as the}'- can be inserted and taken out without the least difficulty, are capable of drawing out a'Shalyam with the greatest ease, and are applicable to all parts of the human body 'be they an artery or a bone-joint.) Thus end.s the sevenih chapter ihc SiUiasthSnam of the Sushruta SamhitS which treats of the shape, construction and dimensions of surgical appliances. C H A P T E R Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats instruments used in connection with a surgical operation. (Shastrarvacharaniyamaclhya^am). These instruments are twenty in number such as, the Mandalagram, the Karapatram, the Vriddhipatram, the Nakhashastram, the Mudrik^, the Utpalapatram, the Arddhadh^ram, the Suchi, the Kushapatram, the Atemukham, the Shardrimukham, the Antarmukhanij the Trikurchakam, the Kuth^rika, the Vrihimukham, the Ar^i, the Vetasapatrakam, the Vadisha,the Dantashanku, and the Eshani.* The MandaMgram measures six fingers length and provided with a round or circular face. The Karapatram the same the modern saw. The term Vriddhipatram signifies razor. A Vriddhi- patram measures seven fingers length, the handle alone measuring five fingers. The Nakhasastram the same the modern nail-clipper, the blade of the instrument measuring a finger in breadth. The Utpala- patram resembles lotus leaf shape. The Arddhadhfiram (lancet) measures eight fingers' breadth' in length, being one finger broad the middle, and two fingers the blade. The Suchi. the same the modern needle. The Kushapatram is so called from its resemblance to the blade of a Kusha-grass. An Atemukham resembles the bill of a bird of the Ate species. The blade Atemukham measures two fingers length, the handle measuring five fingers and thus giving entire length of seven fingers. The SharSrimukham (scissors) is so-called from the resemblance of its blades to the bills of a Shariri bird and looks somewhat like a modern black-smith's clipper, the measure of its entire length being twelve fingers. The Antarmukham is semicircular in shape and provid- ed with a toothed edge like that of a hand-saw. The Trikurchakam (trocar) provided with three separate blades. The intervening space between the couple of blades attached to a handle measuring five fingers length, equal to ihe width of a Vrihiseed, its entire length being eight fingeis. Chap. VIII.

chapter: Chapter X: ow we shall discuss the Chapter which treats the characteristic features of the differetit seasons of the year and their influence health and drugs fRitucharya'dhya'yam). The Eternal Time is without origin, middle, end, self-l/egotten, and the lord of all attributes. Contrariety or non contrariety of the natural attributes drugs substances endued with characteristic tastes, such sweet, etc., are brought about by time and time the principal factor that controls the births or deaths of beings. Etymology of the term Ka'Ia (t^me):— The Kala the Eternal time called from the fact of its not suffering even one its own minutest particles subdivisions (Kala) perish, though perpetually moving, and in constant motion in itself it derives its epithet from the. fundamental quality its destroying all beings and laying their dead remains in heaps in succession. Some assert that the name is due the fact that time blends ( kalanam ) all beings with misery or happiness according their respective acts, or to its leading all beings to destruction ( kala). The Sun-god, his peculiar motions, divides eternal time which is measured by years ( Samvatsaras)
english_text: Of the abovesaid instruments the Mandalagram and the Karapatram should be 'used in incising and scraping. The Vriddhipatram, the Nakhasastram, the Mudrik^, the Utpalapatram, and the Arddhadharam, should employed incising f Chhedanam and excising (Bhedanani) and the Kushapatram, the Shuchi, the Atemukham, the Shararimukham, the Trikurchakam and the Antarmukham should be made use of in exudating or secreting (Visravanam.i The Kutharika, the Vrihimu- kham, the Ara, the Vetasapatram and the Suchi (needle) should be used puncturing. The Vadisha and the Danta-Shanku should be used in extracting sohd bodies. The Eshani 1 probe director) in probing or search- ing the course or direction of the pus suppurated part), and the Suchi (needle; should be used in suturing. Thus have explained the eight different func- tions ol the instruments connection with surgical operations. The kutharika (small, blunt axe) measures seven lingers and a half in liic handle, the blade is half a finger in width and is blunted like the tooth of'a cow. The Vrihimukham measures six fingers its entire length and its top is like that a Vrihi seed, and the edge is cut into small thorn- like projections. The Ar5 resembles the awl of a cobbler and measures ten fingers its entire length, the blade wide the seed sesamum and has the girth of a Durva (grass) stem. The Vetasapatram (knife) resembles the leaf of a Vetasa plant. The blade four fingers in length, one finger in width, and is keenly edged, the handle measur- ing four fingers length. The Vadisha shaped like modern fishing hook. The Danta-shanku (pincers for extracting teeth) somewhat resembles the Vrihimukham in shape. The face of an Eshani (probe) like that of a Gandupada (earth-worm). THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. Ylll. No^v shall deal with the mode handling the abovesaid instruments.—The Vriddhipatram and other instruments for excising (Bhedanam; should be caught hold part between the blade and the handle. In acts of scraping the Vriddhipatram and the MandaUgram should handled with the palm of the hand slightly turned The instruments for secreting should be caught hold the roots of their blades at the time using them, while the case king, old man, timid delicate person, child, a woman and specially in the case of a prince of the royal blood, the Trikurchakam should be used when any secreting or exudating opera- tion would necessary. The handle Vrihi- mukham should kept concealed within the palm the hand and the blade should be caught hold with the thumb and the index finger (Pradeshini). The Kuth^rika should be first supported the left hand and then struck with the thumb and third finger the right. The Ara, the Karapatram and the Eshani, should be caught hold of at their roots. The rest the surgical instruments should grappled according to requirements. The abovesaid instruments are shaped like things which their very names imply, ha^^e been ready described. The Nakashastram and the Eshani measure eight fingers length.

The top-ends of the Vadisha and the Danta-Shankhu pental pincers'; are little bent down and their faces are made resemble sharp thorns, the newly sprouted leaves barley plant. A Shararimukham measures ten fingers length. The rest the instruments are mostly made to measure six fingers in length. Commendable features in a Surgi- cal instrument : —Instruments that are fitted with handles easy grip and are made of good and pure iron, well shaped, sharp, and are set with edges that are not jagged and end well formed points tops, should be deemed as the best of their kind. Curvature, bluntness ( Kuntha—lit :— incapable cutting hair , unequal sharpness the edge, rough- edgedness, over- thickness, over-thinness, over-lengthi- ness, and over-shortness are the defective traits surgical instrument. Those possessed of contrary features should be used. But Karapatram set with very rough (dentated) edge may be used for the purpose of sawing the bones. A surgical instrument meant *'or excision 'Bhedanann should be set with an edge as thin as that of a Musura THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. VIII. pulse 'lentil seed , while an instrument used in scraping should set with an edge half as thin that the former. instrument used either in connection with the measures secretion or cutting by uplifting (Vyadhanam) should be set with an edge as fine as the human hair, while an instrument of incision should have an edge half as thin as that of the former. Surgical instruments should tempered with one the three substances such as, alkali, water, and oil. Instruments used in cutting an arrow, a bone, any foreign matter (Shalyami pricked into the human body, should be tempered with alkali, whereas those that are made use of in cutting, cleaving, and lopping off the flesh from an affected part), should be tempered with water. Instruments used opening Vyadhanam) vein (Shira) cutting open nerve .Sna)^!: should be tempered with oil, and should whetted upon a species of stone-slab resembling a Masha pulse in colour, and their set- edge should be protected by putting it in a sheath made of Sh^lmali wood. Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—An instrument, well-ground, well-shaped, fitted with convenient handle and capable (laterally) cutting a hair in two and made according to measures laid down in the Shastras, should alone used in surgical operation. Chap. VIII. SUTRASTHA'NAM.

english_text: The art of making excisions should be practically demonstrated by making openings in the body full water-bag, the bladder of a dead animal, or the side leather pouch full of slime or water. The art of scraping should be instructed piece skin which the hair has been allowed to remain. The art venesection THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. (Vedhya) should be taught on the vein of a dead animal, or with the help of a lotus stem. The art probing and stuffing should be taught on worm (Ghuna) eaten wood, or on the reed of a bamboo, or on the mouth of a dried Alavu (gourd). The art extracting should taught withdrawing seeds from the kernel Vimbi, Vilva Jack fruit, well as by extract- ing teeth trom the jaws of a dead animal. The act secreting evacuating should taught on the surface of a Shalmali plank covered over with coat bee's wax, and suturing pieces of cloth, skin or hide. Similarly the art of bandaging ligaturing should be practically learned by tying bandages round the specific limbs and members of a full-sized doll made of stuffed linen. The art of tying up Karna-sandhi (severed ear-lobe) should practically demonstrated a soft severed muscle or on flesh, or with the stem lotus lily. The art cauterising, or applying alkaline preparations \^causticsj should be demonstrated on a piece of soft flesh and lastly the art of inserting syringes and injecting enemas into the region the bladder or into an ulcerated channel, should be taught asking the pupil) insert tube into a lateral fissure of a pitcher, full of water _, into the mouth of a gourd (Alavu). Authoritative verses on the sub- ject :—An intelligent physician who has tried his Chap. prentice hand in surgery ion such articles of experiment as, gourds, etc., or has learnt the art with the help things as stated above , or has been instructed in the art of cauterisation blistering (application of alkali) experimenting things which are most akin, or simi- lar the parts or members of the human body they are usually applied to, will never lose his presence of mind in his professional practice. Thus ends the ninth chapter the SutrasthSnam the Sushruta Samhit^ which treats of Instructions in Surgical operations. C HAPTER Now we sliall discuss the Chapter wliich treats the essential qualifications physician before formally enters his profession (Vishlkha'- nupravcshaniya-madhya'yam). A physician haying thoroughly studied the Science of medicine, and fully pondered on and yerified the truths he has assimilated, both by obseryation and practice, and haying attained to that stage of (lucid ) kno^vledge, ^yhich \yould enable him to make clear exposition of the science (^vhene^er necessary), should open his medical career conmience practising) with the permission the king of his country. text: The art of making excisions should be practically demonstrated by making openings in the body full water-bag, the bladder of a dead animal, or the side leather pouch full of slime or water. The art of scraping should be instructed piece skin which the hair has been allowed to remain. The art venesection THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. (Vedhya) should be taught on the vein of a dead animal, or with the help of a lotus stem. The art probing and stuffing should be taught on worm (Ghuna) eaten wood, or on the reed of a bamboo, or on the mouth of a dried Alavu (gourd). The art extracting should taught withdrawing seeds from the kernel Vimbi, Vilva Jack fruit, well as by extract- ing teeth trom the jaws of a dead animal. The act secreting evacuating should taught on the surface of a Shalmali plank covered over with coat bee's wax, and suturing pieces of cloth, skin or hide. Similarly the art of bandaging ligaturing should be practically learned by tying bandages round the specific limbs and members of a full-sized doll made of stuffed linen. The art of tying up Karna-sandhi (severed ear-lobe) should practically demonstrated a soft severed muscle or on flesh, or with the stem lotus lily. The art cauterising, or applying alkaline preparations \^causticsj should be demonstrated on a piece of soft flesh and lastly the art of inserting syringes and injecting enemas into the region the bladder or into an ulcerated channel, should be taught asking the pupil) insert tube into a lateral fissure of a pitcher, full of water _, into the mouth of a gourd (Alavu). Ha^'ing made these observations the physician will try to cure diseases that are curable, adopt palliative measures in cases where palliation is the only remedy that can be offered, and give up a case which beyond Chap. ^7 all medical treatment, and mosth' those which are ofmore than year's standing. Diseases affecting a Br^hmana well versed in the Vedas_, or a king, or a woman, or infant, or an old man, or a timid person, or a man in the royal service, or a cunning man, or a man who pretends to possess a knowledge of the science of medicine, man who conceals his disease, or a man of an excessively irascible temperament, or a man who has control over his senses, or a man extremely indigent cir- cumstances of life or without any one take care him, are apt run into incurable type though appearing in a common or curable form the outset. The physician, who practises his art with regard these facts, acquires piety, wealth, fame and all wished for objects in life. Authoritative verse the sub- ject physician should abjure the company women, nor should speak private them or joke with them. physician forbidden to take anything but cooked rice from the hands of a woman. Thus ends the tenth Chapter the Sutrasthanam the Sushruta Samhiti which treats of the essential qualifications of a physician. CHAPTER Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of the pharmacy of alkalis or potential cauteries (KshaTa- pa'ka-vidhi-madhya'yam). In cases that require incising, excising and scraping, alkalis or alkaline preparations are of greater importance than surgical instruments and appliances (both principal and secondary or substitutive, as they are possessed of the virtues subduing the three deranged bodily humours such as wind, bile and phlegm). The etymological signification the term Kshara (alkalis) is based on their property of corroding i the skin or the flesh of an affected part of the body), or on their peculiar quality in destroying the skin and flesh where such an effect is desired Since a variety of substances enter into the composition of Kshara alkalis , they are endued with the virtue of subduing the three deranged bodily humours. Owing to their white colour, Ksharas should included within the category cooling substances ( Saumya'. But since many drugs or substances of a hot or fiery nature ( Agneya) enter into their composition, KshiCras (alkalis) are endued with the properties of blistering, burning, suppurating • Pachana >, opening etc., without Chap, SUTRASTHA'NAM, involving any contradiction to their generic iSanimya) nature, and lience the}- are included within the list of those substances which are both hot and cooling (Saum^^a and Agne^'a their ^'irtues.

english_text: Now you may ask the question how can acid subst'ance, which is fiery in its virtue and heat-making in its potency, tend to subdue the effects of an alkali which is possessed similar virtues and properties, instead of augmenting them, as can naturally be apprehended Well my child, the question can be fairly answered by stating, that substances of all tastes enter into the composition of an alkali except the acid one. The pungent (Katu taste is the principal taste of an alkali, while the saline :Lavana forms its minor accessory flavour LAnurasa). Xow this saline taste conjunc- tion with the acid one renounces its extremely sharp or irritating property and is thus transformed into one sweetness soothing virtue. Hence is that an acid taste tends to alla)^ the burning incidental an application alkali (potential caustic) the same way as water tends to put out fire. An operation of perfect cauterisation with an alkaline application brings about an amelioration of the disease, the disease entirely subdued, accompanied THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. XI. Hghtne^ss of the limbs and absence secretion from the affected part while insufficient burning the part] generally attended symptoms aggravation the malad}' and also gives rise local pain, itching and numbness, tire other hand], excessive burning the part] with alkaline preparation may ha^'e a fatal termination, and is attended by such symptoms as burning, suppuration, redness, secretion and from the seat of affeqtion. feeling languor and fatigue comes upon the patient accompanied with thirst, swooning and an aching sensation. An ulcer incidental to a burn by an alkali should be treated with a special eye to the nature of the disease and the deranged bodily humour specifically involved in the case. A weak person, an infant, an aged person, a man of timid disposition, patient suff'ering from abdominal dropsy with general anasarca from haemoptysis, a pregnant woman, a woman her menses, person suffering from an attack high fever urethral discharges, emaciated with chronic inflammation the lungs, a person subjected fits faint- ing abnormal thirst, person suffering from virile impotency, whose testes ha^'e become deranged either upwards or downwards, a woman suffering from retro\'ersion introversion the uterus or prolapsus the vagina, should deemed Chap. unfit for being cauterised with alkalis. More- over their application not to sanctioned over the veins, nerves, joints, gristles or tender bones cartilages, sutures, arteries, throat, umbilicus, genitals, regions Srotas (external channels), parts covered over with a thin layer flesh, inside the nails and other vulnerable parts the body, nor diseases of the eyes, excepting those which affect the eyelids.

english_text: Alkalis fail produce any beneficial effect patient suffering from oedema of the limbs, suffering from bone- ache, or laid up with a disease affecting the joints or the heart, or a person of impaired appetite who has lost all relish for food, even when their use is otherwise indicated. Authoritative verse the sub- ject An Alkali adnn'nistered by ignorant phv- sician is to be dreaded more than poison, fire, blows with a weapon, thunder-bolts, death itself ; while in the hand of an intelligent physician potent enougli to speedily subdue all serious diseases in which its use is indicated. Thus ends the eleventh Chapter of the Suliasthanam the Siishiuta Samhita which treats of the Pharmacv of AlkaHs. C H A PT E'R XII. Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats cauteries and the rules observed their use (Agni-Karma-Vidhimadhyayam). fire (cautery is better than an Alkali as far as its healing property is concerned. disease burnt with fire, is cured for good and knows no recrudescence ; and diseases which ordinarily baffle the skill a surgeon or a physician, and never prove themselves amenable to medicinal or surgical remedies, are found yield to fire I cauterisation The following drugs, articles and substances should be understood as accessories to an act of cauterisation, viz., Pippali, the excreta of goats, the tooth a cow I Godanta'', Shara, a rod, the surgical instrument known as the Jamvavaustha, articles made of copper or silver, honey, treacle, oil, or any other oily substance. Out of these, Pippali, the Godanta, Shara and the rod should be (made red hot and) used in cauterising the affected part disease which restricted only the skin similarly the surgical instsument known the Jamvavaustha, well the appliances made copper silver should used disease which is seated the flesh. Honey, treacle and oil should (boiled and employed cauterising the disease Chap. XII. which affects any of the veins, nerves, bones or'bone- joints. Cauterisation admissible all seasons the year except summer and autumn; but no such distinction should be observed in cases of impending danger, when it should be practised with the help of such appliances of a contrary (cooling) nature, [as wet sheets, cooling drinks and cooling plasters, etc.] In all diseases and all seasons the year, the patient should be fed on diet of slimy (mucilaginous) food before actually applying the cautery ; while the patient should be kept on an empty stomach before the act where the complaint would be a case of Mudagarbha rfalse presentation), fistula ano, haemorrhoids disease affecting the cavity of the mouth. According certain authorities the processes cauterisation may be grouped under two heads according as the skin or the flesh is cauterised. The present work does not lay an}^ injunction against the cauterisation of any nerve, vein, bone or bone joint (as stated before). burning of the skin accompanied by peculiar bursting or cracking sound.

english_text: Accord- ingly an ulcer incidental such burning (scald) characterised by extreme pain, etc. Burns may be grouped under four distinct heads viz., the Plushtam, the Dur-Dagdham, the Samyag-Dagdham and the Ati-Dagdham. burn characterised the discolouring of its seat and extreme burning and mark- ed by the absence of any vesicle or blister, is called the Plushtam, from the root "plusha" burn. A burn, which is characterised by the eruption oflarge vesicles or blisters, and assumes a red colour, and is characterised by excessive burning and a kind of drawing pain, and which suppurates and takes a long time to heal, called the Dur-Dagdham (bad burn or scald). A burn, which is not deep (superficial) and assumes the colour of a ripe Tala fruit, and does not present a raised elevated aspect and develops the preceding symptoms, called the Samyag-Dagdham (fully burnt one). A burn in which the flesh hangs down, and where the veins, nerves and bones are destroyed, accompanied with fever, burn- ing, thirst, fainting and such like disturbances, and which leads to a permanent disfiguration the body, retarding the healing of the incidental ulcer which leaves a discoloured cicatrix even after healing, called the Ati-Dagdham (over burnt one). physician should try to heal any of these four types burns with the measures already laid down before. Chap. XII. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject.—The blood a. man agitated and made hot fire, and the blood thus heated tends excite or causes it to raise the bile. And since fire and bile (Pittam) are similar in their taste, essence, effect, potency and natal factors, the effects of Pittam (burning sensation etc.), are naturally aggravated and augmented through a contact with fire. Blisters vesicles crop rapid' succession and mark the seat burning, and fever, thirst, etc., supervene. Now I shall describe the course of medical treat- ment to be adopted for the cure of burns. Hot and dry fomentations, as well as warm plasters should applied to a burn the Plushtam type, and a course of hot food and drink should be likewise prescribed for the patient. The blood becomes thin when the body is diaphorised by m.eans of warm fomentations, and water, virtue its natural cooling properties, tends thicken the blood. Hence warm fomentations or appli- cations exercise curative virtues the case burn of the foregoing t)^pe, and water cold appli- cations produce the contrary effect.* Both warm and cold measures are adopted in the case burn the Dur-Daghdha t5''pe, the arresting the radiation the incarcerated heat and thereby favouring the elevation of the local temperature and the increase the burning sensation. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA.

text: Accord- ingly an ulcer incidental such burning (scald) characterised by extreme pain, etc. Burns may be grouped under four distinct heads viz., the Plushtam, the Dur-Dagdham, the Samyag-Dagdham and the Ati-Dagdham. burn characterised the discolouring of its seat and extreme burning and mark- ed by the absence of any vesicle or blister, is called the Plushtam, from the root "plusha" burn. A burn, which is characterised by the eruption oflarge vesicles or blisters, and assumes a red colour, and is characterised by excessive burning and a kind of drawing pain, and which suppurates and takes a long time to heal, called the Dur-Dagdham (bad burn or scald). A burn, which is not deep (superficial) and assumes the colour of a ripe Tala fruit, and does not present a raised elevated aspect and develops the preceding symptoms, called the Samyag-Dagdham (fully burnt one). A burn in which the flesh hangs down, and where the veins, nerves and bones are destroyed, accompanied with fever, burn- ing, thirst, fainting and such like disturbances, and which leads to a permanent disfiguration the body, retarding the healing of the incidental ulcer which leaves a discoloured cicatrix even after healing, called the Ati-Dagdham (over burnt one). physician should try to heal any of these four types burns with the measures already laid down before. Chap. XII. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject.—The blood a. man agitated and made hot fire, and the blood thus heated tends excite or causes it to raise the bile. And since fire and bile (Pittam) are similar in their taste, essence, effect, potency and natal factors, the effects of Pittam (burning sensation etc.), are naturally aggravated and augmented through a contact with fire. Blisters vesicles crop rapid' succession and mark the seat burning, and fever, thirst, etc., supervene. Now I shall describe the course of medical treat- ment to be adopted for the cure of burns. Hot and dry fomentations, as well as warm plasters should applied to a burn the Plushtam type, and a course of hot food and drink should be likewise prescribed for the patient. The blood becomes thin when the body is diaphorised by m.eans of warm fomentations, and water, virtue its natural cooling properties, tends thicken the blood. Hence warm fomentations or appli- cations exercise curative virtues the case burn of the foregoing t)^pe, and water cold appli- cations produce the contrary effect.* Both warm and cold measures are adopted in the case burn the Dur-Daghdha t5''pe, the arresting the radiation the incarcerated heat and thereby favouring the elevation of the local temperature and the increase the burning sensation. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA.
