chapter: Chapter X: ow we shall discuss the Chapter which treats the characteristic features of the differetit seasons of the year and their influence health and drugs fRitucharya'dhya'yam). The Eternal Time is without origin, middle, end, self-l/egotten, and the lord of all attributes. Contrariety or non contrariety of the natural attributes drugs substances endued with characteristic tastes, such sweet, etc., are brought about by time and time the principal factor that controls the births or deaths of beings. Etymology of the term Ka'Ia (t^me):— The Kala the Eternal time called from the fact of its not suffering even one its own minutest particles subdivisions (Kala) perish, though perpetually moving, and in constant motion in itself it derives its epithet from the. fundamental quality its destroying all beings and laying their dead remains in heaps in succession. Some assert that the name is due the fact that time blends ( kalanam ) all beings with misery or happiness according their respective acts, or to its leading all beings to destruction ( kala). The Sun-god, his peculiar motions, divides eternal time which is measured by years ( Samvatsaras)

chapter: Chapter XIII: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats leeches and of how and which to use (Jalaukar- vacharraniyamadhyaryam). Leeches should applied where the patient would be found to old or imbecile, or a woman, or an infant, or a person of an extremely timid disposition, or a person of a delicate constitution, and as such not fit surgically operated upon, since this mode of bleeding is the gentlest that can possibly devised. The blood vitiated the deranged wind (Vayu\ bile (Pittam), and phlegm (Kapham) should respectively sucked through a horn^ by leeches and gourd appliance (Alavu-Yantra) or with whichsoever of them is available at the time, irrespective of the cause of such vitiation, whenever such bleeding or sucking would be found to be imperatively necessary. Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—A COwhom is described in the Shastras as of hot heat making potency, and possessed slightly cooling (Snigdha) soothing (Madhura) pro- perty. Accordingly it should be used sucking the blood vitiated through the action of the deranged bodily wind. Leeches, which are born in water, are possessed of Madhura (sweet or soothing) properties, and hence they

Gargles having sweet, saline, acid pungent (katu) taste restore the sense- perception of the patient, and gladden his mind. Medi- cated snuffs in adequate quantities should adminis- tered by a well-read physician to such a patient, whereby his head, eyes and neck would able resume their normal functions. And a course of diet, which light, emollient and not acid its reaction, should prescribed. Cooling measures applications should prescribed or made in the event of any part of the body being scorched by excessive heat, or by being exposed to a draught of hot and parched wind. Similarly, hot and emollient measures applications should sorted to where any part of the body has become frozen or shrivelled by snow or cold winds. person struck Chap. XII. chapter: Chapter XIII: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats leeches and of how and which to use (Jalaukar- vacharraniyamadhyaryam). Leeches should applied where the patient would be found to old or imbecile, or a woman, or an infant, or a person of an extremely timid disposition, or a person of a delicate constitution, and as such not fit surgically operated upon, since this mode of bleeding is the gentlest that can possibly devised. The blood vitiated the deranged wind (Vayu\ bile (Pittam), and phlegm (Kapham) should respectively sucked through a horn^ by leeches and gourd appliance (Alavu-Yantra) or with whichsoever of them is available at the time, irrespective of the cause of such vitiation, whenever such bleeding or sucking would be found to be imperatively necessary. Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—A COwhom is described in the Shastras as of hot heat making potency, and possessed slightly cooling (Snigdha) soothing (Madhura) pro- perty. Accordingly it should be used sucking the blood vitiated through the action of the deranged bodily wind. Leeches, which are born in water, are possessed of Madhura (sweet or soothing) properties, and hence they
english_text: Mode application :—The part from which the blood is to be sucked should be first scarified or slightly cut in two three places, and then the mouth or the open end, of the horn, covered with thin piece muslin tied round its edges should placed over it and sucked with the mouth through the aperture at its tip or top- end, or with a gourd appliance equipped with a lighted lamp placed in its inside. The term Jalauka (leeches) may etymolo- gically interpreted mean creatures whose life (Ayu) or whose longevity is in, or depends upon, water, whereas the derivative meaning the term Jalauka (leeches) is based upon the fact of their dwelling ("Oka"—dwelling place) in water (Jalam). Leeches may be divided into twelve distinct species of which six are venomous, and six non-venomous. The six venomous species are named Krishna, Karvura, Alagarda, Indrayudha, Sdmudrik^ and Gochandana. The leeches of the first-named species (Krishna) are marked by thick heads, and of a colour resembling powdered lampblack. The leeches of the Karvura type have extended lOO THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XIIl. elongated bodies like the Varmifishes, and are indented and thick the waist. The Alagarda leeches are hairy, thick and round at the sides, and black at the mouth. The leeches of the Indrayudha species are marked the surface with up-pointed rainbow coloured lines. The skins of the Samudrikas are black- ish yellow, dotted over with white spots variety of shapes. Leeches which are provided with narrow mouths and are marked bifurcating line the bottom like the scrotal sac bull are called Gochandanas. A person bitten by any of the abovesaid venomous leeches has an irresistble inclination to scratch the seat of the bite which is marked by a considerable swelling. Fever, with burning, retching, drowsiness and delirium supervenes and ultimately the patient loses all conscious- ness. The remedy consists in the administration anti-toxic medicine known Mahagada, as snuffs, potions and unguents, etc. bite by an Indrayudha usually proves fatal. Venomous leeches, as well as cures for their bites, have thus been described. The non-venomous species include Kapilas, Pingalas, Shankhamukhis, Musikas, Pundarimukhis and Saravikas. The Kapilas are coloured like Manah-Shila ( realgar ) the sides, and their backs are tinged with glossy hue like that of a Mudga pulse. The Pingalas have reddish colour, are round shape and Chap. XIII. SUTRASTHANAAI. loi capable of speedy locomotion. The Shankhamuldiis are marked blackish red hue like that the liver, are provided with sharp elongated moutlis, and are capable of sucking blood with the greatest swiftness. The Musikas are coloured like the common blind moles, and emit a fetid smell from their bodies. The Pundarimukhas are coloured like the IMudga pulse and are so called from the fact of the resemblance their mouths to the full-blown lotus hhes TPimdarikas).

english_text: The Saravikas have cold bodies marked with impress- ions like lotus leaves and measure eighteen fingers' width length, and they should employed sucking blood from the affected parts of lower animals. This exhausts the list of non-venomous leeches. The countries, such Turkesthan (Yavana), the Deccan (Pandya), the tract of land traversed by the Ghaut mountains (Sahya), and Pautana (modem Mathura), are the natural habitats these leeches. The leeches, found in the aforesaid countries, are specifically non- venomous, strong, large-bodied, greedy and ready suckers. The venomous leeches have their origin the composed urine and fecal matter of toads and venom- ous fishes in pools of stagnant and turbid water. The origin of the non-venomous species is ascribed such decomposed vegetable matter, as the petrified stems of the several aquatic plants known Padma, Utpalam, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XIII. Nalina, Kumuda, Pundarika, and the common zoophytes which live in clear waters. Authoritative verse the sub- ject.—The non-venomous leeches swim about in sweet scented waters, live on non-poisonous weeds, lie on the leaves of flowering water plants instead of on the dank and ooz)^ beds of pools, and suck blood from the affected part of a human organism without causing any discomfort. Leeches should caught hold of with a piece of wet leather, or by some similar article, and then put in to a large-sized new pitcher filled with the water and ooze or slime of a pool. Pulverised zoophytes and powder of dried meat and aquatic bulbs should thrown into the pitcher for their food, and blades grass and leaves of water-plants should be put into for them lie upon. The water and the edibles should changed every second or third day, and the pitchers should be changed each week, (the leeches should transferred a new pitcher the end every consecutive seven days). The authoritative verse on the sub- ject :—Leeches that are venomous, thick about the middle, elongated, slow locomotion, look fatigued, not readily take the part they are appHed to, and capable of sucking only a small quantity of blood, should be looked upon not belonging the proper or the commendable type. Chap. XIII. SUTRASTHA'NAM, Then having seated or laid down the patient 'suffer- ing from disease which requires the application leeches, the seat of bleeding, if not previously ulcerated, should be roughened b}' dusting it over with compo- sition of loose earth and pulverised cowdung. Then the leeches should be taken out their receptacles and sprinkled over with water saturated with mustard seed and pasted turmeric. Then for a moment they should be kept in a basin full water, and after they have regained their natural vivacity and freshness, they should be applied the affected part. Their bodies should be covered with piece of thin and wet linen, or with piece of white cotton.

Their bodies should be covered with piece of thin and wet linen, or with piece of white cotton.

chapter: Chapter XIII: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats leeches and of how and which to use (Jalaukar- vacharraniyamadhyaryam). Leeches should applied where the patient would be found to old or imbecile, or a woman, or an infant, or a person of an extremely timid disposition, or a person of a delicate constitution, and as such not fit surgically operated upon, since this mode of bleeding is the gentlest that can possibly devised. The blood vitiated the deranged wind (Vayu\ bile (Pittam), and phlegm (Kapham) should respectively sucked through a horn^ by leeches and gourd appliance (Alavu-Yantra) or with whichsoever of them is available at the time, irrespective of the cause of such vitiation, whenever such bleeding or sucking would be found to be imperatively necessary. Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—A COwhom is described in the Shastras as of hot heat making potency, and possessed slightly cooling (Snigdha) soothing (Madhura) pro- perty. Accordingly it should be used sucking the blood vitiated through the action of the deranged bodily wind. Leeches, which are born in water, are possessed of Madhura (sweet or soothing) properties, and hence they

That the leeches have taken to the affected part may inferred from the mouths the leeches assuming the shape of a horse-shoe, and the raised and arched position their necks after they had become attached the seat the disease. A sensation of itching and of a drawing pain the seat of the application would give rise the pre- ^^^ SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. After falling off, the leeches should dusted over with rice powder and their mouths should be lubricated with a composition of oil and common salt. Then they should be caught by the tail-end with the thumb and the forefinger the left hand and their backs should be gently rubbed with the same fingers the right hand from tail upward to the mouth with view to make them vomit or eject the full quantity blood they had sucked from the seat the disease. The process should continued until they manifest the fullest symptoms disgorging. Leeches that, had vomited the entire quantity blood sucked above, would briskly move about quest food if placed in water, while the contrary should inferred from their l5nng dull and inert. These should made disgorge again. Leeches not made emit the entire quantity the sucked The leeches, though a blissful dispensntion of Nature in themselves, instinctively draw off the vitiated blood from a diseased part, attacking the healthy vital fluid (red blood) \Yhen the former has been completely tapped or sucked. Chap, XIII. blood stand danger being attacked* with incurable disease peculiar their genus, and which is known as Indramada. The leeches should then put into a new pitcher, and treated as before laid down, after they had fully emitted the sucked blood. ulcer incidental to an application leeches should be rubbed with honey or washed with sprays of cold water, or bound up with an astringent (kashaya) sweet and cooling plaster, according to the quantity blood removed from the part.* Authoritative verse the sub- ject :—The physician who fully conversant with the habitat, mode of catching, preservation and appli- cation of leeches, can well aspire to cure the diseases which yield to them or in which their use is indicated.

chapter: Chapter XIV: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of blood (Shonita-Varnaniya-mad hya'yam). The food human being, which usually composed of the five fundamental material principles, admits being classified under four different heads drinks and edibles, etc.]. has six different tastes or is of two [cooling or heat-making] potencies, consists eightfold properties, [viz. hot, cool, dry, expansive, slimy, mild, sharp, etc.] and variety other active efficacious virtues. The food fully digested with the help of the internal heat and ultimately assimilated in the system, giving rise to lymph chyle (Rasa) which is extremely thin attenuated its consistency and which forms the essence of the assimilated food.* The lymph chyle (Rasaj, though running through the whole organism, has its primary seat in the heart, whence it flows through the twenty- four vessels which branch off from the latter (heart) to the remotest parts and extremities of the bod5\ the aforesaid twenty-four vessels, ten are up-coursing, ten are down-coursing, and four have a lateral direction. The Rasa the is free from all sorts impurities such fecal matter, etc., and permeates the minutest vessels and capillaries
english_text: In case of full and proper bleeding (Voga) the ulcer should rubbed with clarified butter technically known as the Shatadhautam (lit: hundred times washed) Ghritam (clarified butter), or piece cotton, soaked the same substance, applied a compress over the part. The ulcer should be rubbed with honey in a case insuflicient bleeding, while should washed with copious quantity cold water excessive bleeding (Ati-Yoga) should set in. Similarly in a case marked by the absence of any bleeding at all (Mithya-Yoga) a sour, sweet and cooling plaster should be applied over the ulcer. Thus ends the thirteenth Chapter of the Sulrasthinam the Sushruta Samhita which treats of Leeches and of how and which to use. CHAPTER XIV. Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of blood (Shonita-Varnaniya-mad hya'yam). The food human being, which usually composed of the five fundamental material principles, admits being classified under four different heads drinks and edibles, etc.]. has six different tastes or is of two [cooling or heat-making] potencies, consists eightfold properties, [viz. hot, cool, dry, expansive, slimy, mild, sharp, etc.] and variety other active efficacious virtues. The food fully digested with the help of the internal heat and ultimately assimilated in the system, giving rise to lymph chyle (Rasa) which is extremely thin attenuated its consistency and which forms the essence of the assimilated food.* The lymph chyle (Rasaj, though running through the whole organism, has its primary seat in the heart, whence it flows through the twenty- four vessels which branch off from the latter (heart) to the remotest parts and extremities of the bod5\ the aforesaid twenty-four vessels, ten are up-coursing, ten are down-coursing, and four have a lateral direction. The Rasa the is free from all sorts impurities such fecal matter, etc., and permeates the minutest vessels and capillaries. Chap. XIV. lymph chyle, thus flowing out of the heart, constantly soothes, maintains, and irrigates transudation the body, and further contributes to its growth, and supports life owing the dynamical effects causes which lie beyond the ken of human understanding. The nature and course of this lymph chyle, which runs through the whole system, can inferred from the growth, attenuation, or other modified conditions of the bod5\ Now it may asked, whether the Rasa, which permeates the entire body and limbs, and which flowing through different chambers (visceras) of the body is thus in constant contact with the excreta and other morbid humours, of a cooling (Saum3'a) heat-making (Agneya) potency ? The question may be answered by stating that, since the Rasa lymph chyle fluid, and possessed of lubricating, vitalising, moistening, and nutritive (lit supporting) properties, it must be included within the class of Saumya (cooling) substances. The Rasa, though a Saumya fluid, obtains its characteristic pigment (Ragam) in its passage through the spleen and liver.

chapter: Chapter XIV: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of blood (Shonita-Varnaniya-mad hya'yam). The food human being, which usually composed of the five fundamental material principles, admits being classified under four different heads drinks and edibles, etc.]. has six different tastes or is of two [cooling or heat-making] potencies, consists eightfold properties, [viz. hot, cool, dry, expansive, slimy, mild, sharp, etc.] and variety other active efficacious virtues. english_text: Authoritative verses on the sub- ject:—The Rasa the lymph chyle, coloured through the effect of the healthy normal d5''eing heat the body, obtains the name of blood. The Rasa is transformed into the catamenial flow in women which commences at the age of twelve and ceases at fifty. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. XIV. Catamenial blood, though originating from Rasa which is of a cooling potency^, fiery heat-making (Agneya) its character and the fecundated impregnated o\'um (Garbha) is both cooling and heat- making in its properties on accoimt of its partaking of the nature of both the menstrual blood (ovum) and semen which are respectively possessed the two preceding virtues. Hence several authorities hold the blood to be identical with the life blood with the vital principle of a living organism, and being such, to be the product of the five fundamental material principles (Panchabhautikam). lYIctrical texts:— In blood the properties such as, a raw fleshy smell, fluidity, redness, lightness and mobility, which respectively characterise the fundamental principles (of earth, water, fire, air, and sky) are to be found thus representing those specific elements in its composition. The chyle produces blood. From blood is formed flesh. From flesh originates fat which gives rise bones. From bones originate marrow, which, its turn, germinates semen. The Rasa which is originated from the digested or assimilated food and drink pre-eminently strengthens all the fundamental principles of the body. The Purusha self-conscious personality is Chyl«- Chap. XIV. born in its origin, and hence an intelligent person should carefully preserve his bodily Rasa dymph chyle) by adopting a proper regimen of diet and conduct. The term Rasa is derived from the root "Ras", go, and the substance is so called from the fact its continually flowing through and permeating every vital principle of an animated organism. The Rasa is successively transformed into each of the six remaining fundamental principles of the body, and continues in the shape of each for the period three thousand and fifteen kalas five days according to our modern computation Thus the Rasa is converted into semen, into the menstrual blood 'ovum) in women, in the course of a month.* * The successive development the fundamental root principles of the body follows a distinct order. The essence of the assimilated food- matter under the heat of digestion goes towards the formation chyle, and is ultimately transformed into its excreted and effete residue being passed out of the organism in the shape stool, etc. The chyle thus produced is called the immature Rasa, or the Rasa its nascent stage.

The blood, thus newly generated, is merged into the fundamental organic principle blood and there the heat the latter again resolved into three factors, viz., its excreted portion transformed into bile, its thick condensed portion transformed assimilated into the fundamental organic principle blood, and its subtile essence metamorphosed into flesh. The flesh, thus newly formed, is merged into the fundamental organic principle flesh, and there, the native heat the latter, resolved into three I JO THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XIV. Authoritative verse on its com- putation:—In the present work, as well as in other works of recognised authority, a month is calculated to consist of eighteen thousand and ninety Kalas. The said Rasa courses through the whole body invisible currents of zigzag shape, like the waves of sound, upward direction) like flames of fire, or (in a downward direction s like rivulets of water. factors, vis, its excreted portion goes towards the formation of such excreta are found deposited the corners the eyes and inside the integuments of the prepuce, or about the region of the glans penis, its thick condensed portion is transformed into the organic principle flesh and its subtile essence metamorphosed into fat. The fat, thus newly generated, enters into the organic principle that name, and there, by the native heat the latter, resolved into three factors, ?7-, its excreted portion discharged through the pores of the skin in drops of perspiration, its condensed portion assimilated the organic principle fat, and its subtile portion metamorphosed into bone. Again the bone, its nascent stage, enters into the organic principle of bone, and there, by the inherent heat that principle, is resolved into three factors, v!~, its excreted portion goes towards the formation hairs, mustaches, etc, its thick condensed portion assimilated into the organic principle of bone, and its subtile portion metamorphosed into marrow. The marrow, its nascent state, enters into the organic principle that name and there matured under the native heat of that principle, is resolved into three factors, its excreted portion contributes towards the formation gelatinous matter deposited the corners of the eyes, and the oily secretions of the skin, its condensed portion is assimilated into the organic principle of marrow, and its subtile portion metamorphosed into semen. The semen again, its nascent stage, enters into the organic principle that name and there matured under its native heat is resolved into two factors, thick and thin.

english_text: The thick portion is assimilated into the organic principle of semen, the thin one being metamorphosed into (albumen). Semen, like gold a thousand times purified, casts off no dregs. Hence certain authorities hold albumen (protoplasmic matter) to be the eighth or the culminating principle of the body. Chap. XIV. 1 1 Now it may be asked, since the Rasa naturally transformed into semen in .the course of a month, what the use administering medicine which has stimulating effect upon the organs generation (Vajikaranam.) The answer is, that such medicines out their own specific potencies and virtue help the speedy conversion of Rasa into semen and its profuse emission [on the desired occasion] like purgatives aiding the drastic evacuation of the bowels. Again it may be asked, how that semen not found in an infant ? Since perfume in a flower- bud imperceptible the organ of smell you may as well ask whether there is any perfume it or not. But what does not exist in a thing can not be evoked in the subsequent course its development. the perfume flower-bud lies latent its earl}^ stage of growth but becomes patent only with the growth its seed organs, semen catamenial blood lies potential state male female child, and appears with the growth beards and mustaches, with the enlargement the breasts, uterus and vaginal canal and the appearance of pubic hair. The same Rasa, originated from the assimilated food, serves only maintain the vitality the old and spontaneously decayed subjects owing to an exhausted state of the inner vitalising principle, natural to old age. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. : Chap. XIV. The abovesaid principles (of Rasa, blood etc.) are called the root principles (Dhatus), inasmuch as they maintain the integrity the human organism and guard against its speedy dissolution). And since the strength weakness the abovesaid bodily principles absolutely depends upon the richness or poverty of blood, shall discourse on the latter condition of the blood. The blood, vitiated by the deranged bodily wind (Vayu), becomes thin, frothy, transparent, quick- coursing, and expansive, assumes vermilion black hue, and is divested of its slimy character ; whereas vitiated through a deranged condition of the bile (Pittam), assumes a blue, yellow, green, or brown colour, emits a fishy smell, becomes thin in its consistency and is shun flies and ants. Similarly, blood, vitiated by the deranged phlegm (Kapham), becomes cold, glossy and thick, assumes colour like that of the washings Gairika or that of a flesh tendon, takes time in secreting or in mnning down, and is marked by an increase its slimy character. The blood, vitiated through a concert- ed derangement of the three bodily humours, is marked features peculiar each of them, and assumes colour like that of Kanjika (sour gruel), and emits fetid smell. Similarly, the blood, vitiated through the joint action of any two of the (beforesaid) bodily humours, is characterised by features peculiar each of them. Cain XIV.

Hence certain authorities hold albumen (protoplasmic matter) to be the eighth or the culminating principle of the body. Chap. XIV. 1 1 Now it may be asked, since the Rasa naturally transformed into semen in .the course of a month, what the use administering medicine which has stimulating effect upon the organs generation (Vajikaranam.) The answer is, that such medicines out their own specific potencies and virtue help the speedy conversion of Rasa into semen and its profuse emission [on the desired occasion] like purgatives aiding the drastic evacuation of the bowels. Again it may be asked, how that semen not found in an infant ? Since perfume in a flower- bud imperceptible the organ of smell you may as well ask whether there is any perfume it or not. But what does not exist in a thing can not be evoked in the subsequent course its development. the perfume flower-bud lies latent its earl}^ stage of growth but becomes patent only with the growth its seed organs, semen catamenial blood lies potential state male female child, and appears with the growth beards and mustaches, with the enlargement the breasts, uterus and vaginal canal and the appearance of pubic hair. The same Rasa, originated from the assimilated food, serves only maintain the vitality the old and spontaneously decayed subjects owing to an exhausted state of the inner vitalising principle, natural to old age. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. : Chap. XIV. The abovesaid principles (of Rasa, blood etc.) are called the root principles (Dhatus), inasmuch as they maintain the integrity the human organism and guard against its speedy dissolution). And since the strength weakness the abovesaid bodily principles absolutely depends upon the richness or poverty of blood, shall discourse on the latter condition of the blood. The blood, vitiated by the deranged bodily wind (Vayu), becomes thin, frothy, transparent, quick- coursing, and expansive, assumes vermilion black hue, and is divested of its slimy character ; whereas vitiated through a deranged condition of the bile (Pittam), assumes a blue, yellow, green, or brown colour, emits a fishy smell, becomes thin in its consistency and is shun flies and ants. Similarly, blood, vitiated by the deranged phlegm (Kapham), becomes cold, glossy and thick, assumes colour like that of the washings Gairika or that of a flesh tendon, takes time in secreting or in mnning down, and is marked by an increase its slimy character. The blood, vitiated through a concert- ed derangement of the three bodily humours, is marked features peculiar each of them, and assumes colour like that of Kanjika (sour gruel), and emits fetid smell. Similarly, the blood, vitiated through the joint action of any two of the (beforesaid) bodily humours, is characterised by features peculiar each of them. Cain XIV.
title: Sushruta Samhita Volume 1

chapter: Chapter XIV: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of blood (Shonita-Varnaniya-mad hya'yam). The food human being, which usually composed of the five fundamental material principles, admits being classified under four different heads drinks and edibles, etc.]. has six different tastes or is of two [cooling or heat-making] potencies, consists eightfold properties, [viz. hot, cool, dry, expansive, slimy, mild, sharp, etc.] and variety other active efficacious virtues. The food fully digested with the help of the internal heat and ultimately assimilated in the system, giving rise to lymph chyle (Rasa) which is extremely thin attenuated its consistency and which forms the essence of the assimilated food.* The lymph chyle (Rasaj, though running through the whole organism, has its primary seat in the heart, whence it flows through the twenty- four vessels which branch off from the latter (heart) to the remotest parts and extremities of the bod5\ the aforesaid twenty-four vessels, ten are up-coursing, ten are down-coursing, and four have a lateral direction. The Rasa the is free from all sorts impurities such fecal matter, etc., and permeates the minutest vessels and capillaries
english_text: The blood in its healthy and natural state is possess- red colour like that of an Indragopa (Cochineal) insect, and neither too thin nor too transparent.* Cases where blood-letting is pro- hibited :—A person afflicted with oedematous swelling extending all over the body should deemed unfit for bleeding. An intumescence occurring in a weak and enfeebled patient owing excessive use of acid food or in a person suffering from jaundice or laid up with haemorrhoids or abdominal dropsy, as well in an enceinte, in a person suffering from Pulmonary consumption (Shosha), should not be bled. Blood-letting, with the help of a surgical instrument, may grouped under two distinct heads, according scarification (Prachchhanam) or venesection (Sira- Vyadhanam) is resorted to for the purpose. such case the knife or the instrument (Shastram) should be driven straight and speedily so as to make the incision straight, narrow, unextended, and equal and slight depth throughout, reach only the surface layer of the flesh and blood), and not injure any way the local veins, nerves, joints, and other vital parts. Bleeding performed on a cloudy day or done with a Additional texts :—Later on we shall have occasion speak the principles known as the life-blood (essential conditions vitality Sk. Jiva-Shonita) and of the process of blood-letting. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. Xiv wrong incision, or with full exposure to cold and wind, or performed on a patient not previously diaphorised, or on a patient with empty stomach, is attended . with little or no outflow of blood owing to the thickened condition of the blood. Authoritative verse the sub- ject :—Blood-letting surgically performed on a fatigued exhausted subject, person swoon, or anyway poisoned intoxicated, person suffering from extreme constipation of the bowels accom- panied by suppression of the flatus i Vayu) and urine, on a person of timid disposition, or on one overcome with sleep, is marked by the absence of any outflow of blood. The vitiated blood, failing to find out an outlet, gives rise itching, swelling, redness, burning, suppuration and pain the part which confined). the contrary, blood-letting performed on the body person excessively diaphorised heated, or by ignorant inexperienced surgeon, with injudiciously deep incision, is attended with haemorrhage, which may followed such dreadful results Shirobhitapa violent headache, blindness or loss of sight (Timria, Adhimantham (ophthalmia^ loss of vital principles the body (Dhatu-Kshaya), convulsions, paralysis (Ekanga Vikara), Hemiplegia (Pakshaghata), thirst, a burning sensation, hic-cough, cough, asthma, jaundice and even death. Chap. XIV. Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—Therefore blood-Jetting should performed on a patient not extremly hot cold season, neither one who too much heated properly diaphorised (before the act). The patient should be given gruel ( Yavagu) before the operation.

english_text: spontaneous cessation of red flow would indicate that there has been a free discharge of blood. An act of complete and successful blood-letting followed b}' a feeling of lightness and alleviation of pain in the affected part, by an abatement of the disease, and a general sense of cheerfulness. A person, accustomed to blood letting, enjoys a kind of immunity from all types skin diseases, sarcomata, aneurism, oedema, and diseases brought about vitiated condition of the blood such as, Ovarian tumour, Carbuncle, Erysipelas, etc. plaster composed Ela, Shitashiva, Kustha, Tagara, Patha, Agaradhuma, Bhadradaru, Vidanga, Chitraka, Trikatus, Ankura, Haridra, Arka, and Nakta- mala, three, four, many of them are available, pasted together and soaked in mustard oil saturated with common salt, should rubbed over the mouth of the incision. By this means the blood will fully come out. In a case of excessive flow hcem- orrhage, the mouth of the incision should be gently rubbed with a composition consisting of the powders of Lodhra, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. Xiv. Priyangii, Madhiika, Pattanga, Gairika, Sarjarasa, Rasanjana, Shalmali flowers, Shankha, Shukti, Masha, Yava and Godhuma, and firmly pressed with the tips of the fingers. As an alternative, the mouth of the incision should gently rubbed with the powdered barks Sdla, Sarja, Arjuna, Arimeda, Mesha-shringi, and Dhanvana, or the edges of the wound should be lightly dusted with the burnt ashes of a silk cord (a piece of silk rolled the form of a cord}, and firmly pressed with the tips of the fingers the mouth the wound should be lightly touched with the powders Laksha and Samudra-phena, and its edges should similarly pressed together as above. Then the wound should firmly tied (with piece silk linen; plastered over with paste of the substances mentioned in connection with the bandaging ulcers (Vrana). The patient should kept cool room, covered over with a wet sheet and constantly soothed with sprays of cold water. A medicinal plaster of a cooling virtue and a course of cooling diet should be prescribed for him. The wound should be cauterised with fire alkali, the vein should again opened point little below the seat the first incision case where the abovesaid measures should have failed check the flow of blood. The -patient should be made to drink a decoction compound of drugs of the Kakolyadi group, sweetened with sugar or honey ; and his ordinary' drink should consist of the [Chap. XIV. blood of the Ena or common deer, or of a sheep, hare, buffalo. diet composed boiled rice, soaked in or saturated with clarified butter, should be pres- cribed, and the complications should subdued according the nature the deranged bodily humours respectively involved therein.

english_text: Authoritative verses the sub- ject:—Excessive blood-letting is followed b}' im^paired appetite and agitated condition of the vital Vayu owing to the loss the fundamental principles of the body, and, accordingly, recoup the health of the the patient course diet should prescribed which light and not excessively heat-making, and which contains a fair amount of emollient and blood- making matter, and is marked by Kttle or no acid taste. The four measures indicated for the stoppage bleeding are known the Sandhanam (process contracting the affected part), the Skandanam (thickening congealing the local blood), the Pachanam (process setting suppuration the wound) and the Dahanam (process of cauterisation). Drugs astringent tastes are possessed of the property of bringing about an adhesion (contraction) of the wound. Cooling measures such as, applications of ice etc, tend to thicken the local blood alkalis and alkaline preparations produce suppuration such THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XIV. woimci or ulcer, whereas cauterisation has the property of .contracting a vein. Remedies and appHances possessed of the virtue bringing about an adhesion of such a wound should be used where applications for thickening congealing the local blood would fail ; whereas the suppurating measures should adopted the event the former (Sandhanam) proving ineffectual. With any of the three of these preceding measures a physician should try to check the outflow of blood incidental to an operation bleeding, and lastly the process of cauterisation should be resorted the event of the preceding ones having pro^-ed unavailing, is pre-eminently the best means of checking the bleeding. The least residue of the vitiated blood continuing the affected part may not aggi-avate the disease but prevent its perfect healing. In such case bleed- ing should not be again resorted but the derang- ed residue should be subdued by means of pacifying absorbing remedies. Blood the origin of the body. is blood that maintains vitality. Blood life. Hence it should be preserved with the greatest care. The Vayu person who has been bled, and which has been aggravated by constant cold applications Chap. XIV. may give rise swelling the incised* part characterised piercing pain, which should treated with an unguent of tepid clarified butter. Thus ends the fourteenlh Chapter of the Sutrasthan^m the Sushrula Samhit^ which treats of Blood. CHAPTER Now shall describe the Chapter which treats development and non-deA'elopment the humoral constituents . of the body and excrements (Dosha- Dhai:u-IVIaIa- Kshaya-Vriddhi Vijnaniya-madhya^am). Since the human body is constituted of humours, (Doshas), excretions (Mala\ and the fimdamental princi- ples (Dhatus. of blood, marrow, etc., hear me discourse on the features which are peculiar to each of them.

The Pittam.—Pigmentations coloiu-ing (Ragakrit), the digestion of food and metabolism of tissues (Paktikrit), the vitalisation and nutrition of the protaplasmic cells (Ojakrit), the origination and preser- * They are called Pr^na, Ud<ina, Samdna, \'y^na and Apina. Chap. XV. ] 1 2 vation of eye-sight (Teja-Krit), the germination df heat and maintenance of the temperature of the body (Ushma- Krit), and the origination of the faculty of intellection (Medha-Krit) should be regarded the functions the five kinds* of Pittam, which contribute to the preservation the body through its thermogenetic potency (Agni-Karma). The Shieshma'.—The function the five kindsf of Shieshma lubricate the interior of the joints (Sandhi-Samshleshanam), contribute the gloss of the body (Snehanam), to aid the formation of healthy granules in sores (Ropanam), add to the size of the body (Puranam', build fresh tissues (Vrimhanam), to impart a pleasant or soothing sensation the body (Tarpanam), increase its strength ' Valakrit , and to give firmness to the limbs 'Sthairya- krit), thereby contributing to the welfare of the body by supplying it with its watery element. The Rasa the lymph chyle exercises soothing effect upon the entire organism and tends * They are named Ranjaka. P^chaka, SSdhaka (Medh^krit and Ojakrit), Alochaka and BhrSjaka. t They are known as Shleshmaka, Kledaka, Vodhaka, Tarpaka, Avalamvaka. A^. B,—The V^yu, Pittam, andShleshmS, (Kaphham), though ordinarily translated as wind, bile and phlegm, differ their meaning from their usual English synonyms. reserve the treatment of these subjects for separate place another part the book when shall have occasion to deal with the essentials of Ayurvedic Physiology—Tr. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XV. contribute the increased formation blood. The blood, its turn, increases the healthful glow of the complexion, leads to the increased formation flesh and muscles and maintains vitalit}- in the organism. The flesh contributes towards the stoutness or rotundity the limbs and occasions the formation fatty matter the system. The fat gives rise the glossiness (formation of oily or albuminous matter) of the body and primarily contributes towards the firm- ness and growth the bones. The bones, their turn, support the body, and contribute to the formation of marrow. The marrow contributes towards the for- mation and increase of semen, and fills the internal canities of the bones, and fomis the chief soiu"ce strength, amorous feelings and hilarity.

The fat gives rise the glossiness (formation of oily or albuminous matter) of the body and primarily contributes towards the firm- ness and growth the bones. The bones, their turn, support the body, and contribute to the formation of marrow. The marrow contributes towards the for- mation and increase of semen, and fills the internal canities of the bones, and fomis the chief soiu"ce strength, amorous feelings and hilarity.

english_text: The semen gives rise to valour and courageousness, makes a man amor- ously disposed towards the female sex, increases his strength and amativeness, the sole impregnating principle in the male organism, and is possessed the virtue of being quickly emitted. The excreta or the fecal matters of a man are in- dispensably necessary for the preser\^ation of the body. They contain the wind and digestion .being primarily connected with the movements of the bodily Vayu and the feeling of hunger). The urine fills the receptacle the bladder, and is possessed of the property of washing or draining off the waste or refuse matter of the organism whereas perspiration tends to moisten the skin. Chap. XV. The Artavam (menstrual blood) is endued with the same properties as its arterial namesake, and is one of the essential factors in a woman which makes impregnation possible. The foetus impregnated matter (Garbha) serves to make patent the features characteristic pregnancy. The breast-milk its turn tends bring about expansion the mammae lof woman and maintains the life her child suppl}'ing it with the necessary and nutritive element food). These Vayu, etc. should duly preserved in their normal condition. Now shall describe the symptoms which attend the loss waste of any the foregoing principles of the body.* The loss of the bodily Vayu f nerve-force) is followed bv a state of languor, shortness speech, uneasiness absence hilarity, and loss of consciousness. The loss of fPittam) is marked b}' a dulness of complexion, diminution of the bodily heat and impaired state internal fire (digestive heat). The loss of phlegm (Kapham) is marked by dryness, a sensation of internal burning, a feeling of emptiness in the stomach and other * Such a loss perceptible deterioration of any of them should ascribed to the use of exce^^sive cleansing or cathartic (Samshodhanam) and pacifying (Samshamanam) measures, repression the natural urgings of the body, a course violent overfatiguing physical exercise, amorous excesses, the use unwholesome and unsuitable food, or ta grief, etc. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XV. cavities or chambers of the body, looseness of the joints (a feeling as if the joints were all broken), thirst, weak- ness, and insomnia. In such cases the medical treatment should consist of remedial agents which are capable directly contributing the growth formation of the humour so lost or deteriorated. Similarly the loss of lymph chyle is marked by pain about the region the heart, Angina Pectoris, with palpitation the heart, sensation emptiness gone-feeling the viscus, and thirst. The loss of blood is attended with such symptoms roughness the skin, and craving for acid food or drink. The patient longs cool place and asks for cool things, and the veins become loose and flabby. The loss of flesh marked by emaciation of the buttocks, cheeks, lips, thighs, breasts, armpits,* neck, and the calves the legs.

Now we shall describe the characteristic features the strength-giving principles the body, as well the symptoms that mark their loss or waste. The quintessence all the fundamental principles the body, starting with lymph chyle and ending with semen, is called the Ojas, which is identical with what termed "vital power." This view oneness vitality with protoplasmic albumen has been adopted in the present work* This Ojas (albumen) strength-giving principle serves to impart a firm integrity the flesh (and the muscles), exercises unbounded control over all acts of vitality, improves the voice and complexion, and helps both the external (operative") and the internal ^intellectual) sense organs, duly performing their natural functions. english_text: * It is further soft and shiny, and is possessed of the most efficacious virtue and should be regarded the most important element (seat) vitality. The whole body with its limbs and members permeated with Ojas, and a loss or diminution its natural quantity leads to the gradual emaciation (and ultimate dissolution) of organism. blow, persistent wasting disease, anger, grief, cares and anxieties, fatigue and hunger, are the causes to which should be ascribed the wasting disappearance of this strength-giving principle (albumen) of the body. The bodily albumen, through the agency of the above- said causes, is wasted through the channels carrying the different fundamental principles of the body. Albumen transformed into strength which radiates from the heart. A deranged or vitiated albumen (Ojas) is characterised firstly by its dislodgment from its proper seat or locality (Visransha), secondly, by a change or modification of its native virtues contact with the deranged humours or disordered organs (Vyapad) and thiidly, by wasting away (Kshaya\ Several editions read Rasam, meaning possessed of a sweet taste. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XV. The first the preceding properties (dislodgment) gives rise such symptoms looseness the bone-joints, numbness the hmbs, dislodgment the deranged humours from their respective recep- tacles and suppression of the (bodily and intellectual) functions. To the second of the foregoing properties, (change or modification its natural virtue through contact with the deranged bodily humours etc) should be ascribed such symptoms, as numbness and heaviness the limbs, dropsy due the action of the deranged bodily Vayu, discoloured or changed complexion, feeling of malaise, drowsiness and somnolence. The third pro- perty of the deranged albumen, loss or wasting), brings fits fainting, loss flesh, stupor, delirium and ultimately death. Numbness and heaviness the limbs, malaise, a discoloured complexion, drowsiness, Chap. XV. SUTRASTHA'NAM. somnolence and dropsical swelling brought about by deranged state bodily Vayu, should be considered natural consequences the Vyapad ^change the natural virtues of albumen through contamination). chapter: Chapter XIV: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of blood (Shonita-Varnaniya-mad hya'yam). The food human being, which usually composed of the five fundamental material principles, admits being classified under four different heads drinks and edibles, etc.]. has six different tastes or is of two [cooling or heat-making] potencies, consists eightfold properties, [viz. hot, cool, dry, expansive, slimy, mild, sharp, etc.] and variety other active efficacious virtues. The food fully digested with the help of the internal heat and ultimately assimilated in the system, giving rise to lymph chyle (Rasa) which is extremely thin attenuated its consistency and which forms the essence of the assimilated food.* The lymph chyle (Rasaj, though running through the whole organism, has its primary seat in the heart, whence it flows through the twenty- four vessels which branch off from the latter (heart) to the remotest parts and extremities of the bod5\ the aforesaid twenty-four vessels, ten are up-coursing, ten are down-coursing, and four have a lateral direction. The Rasa the is free from all sorts impurities such fecal matter, etc., and permeates the minutest vessels and capillaries

A patient who has lost all consciousness owing excessive loss waste albumen) should be given up by a physician as incurable\ The oily albuminous matter found within the components the other fundamental principles (Dhatu) the body metabolised the internal heat and regularly metamorphosed into the succeeding ones) should grouped under the head fiery thermogenetic (Agneya)) substances. This fatty matter iVas^'i predominates in the female organism and produces its peculiar softness, ^^^ SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XV. beauty and pleasing shape, causes the grovrth of scanty but soft hair on its surface. It strengthens the eyesight and increases the energy of the body, improves its power of digestion and heightens its glow and com- plexion. Fat is deranged by such acts as, an abuse astringent, bitter, cold, parchifying Vistambhi (indigestible food which remains stuffed the stomach^ substances, a voluntary repression of the natural urging for evacutions of the body, by excessive sexual in- dulgence, and fatiguing physical exercise, or by the draining action of any particular disease. An instance of dislodgment of fat from its proper seat or locality is attended by such symptoms as roughness of the skin, loss of the natural healthful glow of the body and a breaking or an aching pain in the limbs. Anaemia or a gradual emaciation of the body, impaired digestive function and slanting or downward course of the deranged humours, mark the case where the bodily fat has undergone a change in its natural properties through any foul contamination. A case of loss or waste of the bodily fat is marked by such S5aTiptoms as, impaired digestive function, dulness of sight, decay of strength and aggra- vation of the bodily Vayu, and always ends in death. The medical treatment in the latter case (loss of fat) should consist in the administration of oily or emollient drinks, use of medicated unguents lubrications, Pradeha (plasters oleaginous substances) and Chap. XV. 13^ Parisheka (washes) and a diet comprising light, cooling and well-cooked articles of food. lYIetrical texts person suffering from wasting any the constituent humours fundamental principles excrements the body, as well as one suffering from loss of Ojah (albumen) naturally craves for drink and food that tend con- tribute directly to the formation of the matter (or bodily principle) so lost or wasted. Conversely, the particular food drink longed for person suffering from a loss or waste of any of the abovesaid fluids principles, should looked upon possessed curative virtue that particular case.

english_text: Such person devoid consciousness and divested his bodily and intellectual functions through deranged state the bodih' Vayu ner\-e-force) and extremely weak and enfeebled owing to the loss of the vital fluid should be regarded as past all cure. Etiology of Obesity :— Obesity loss flesh (Karsha) should be ascribed to changes in the condi- tion of the lymph chyle. The lymph chyle derived from the assimilated food of a person, who is habituated to a course of diet which tends to promote the quantitv the bodily Kapham or is in the habit of pampering his belly even when a previous meal has not been thoroughly digested, or who is addicted to a habit of sleeping in the day, or leading a sedentary life, or is averse taking THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. t Chap. XV. any sort of physical exercise, continues in an immature State and is transformed into a serum of sweet taste which moves about within the body, engendering the formation of fat which produces excessive stoutness. person afflicted with obesity develops such symptoms as short- ness of breath, thirst, ravenous appetite, excessive sleepi- ness, perspiration, fetid odours in the body, wheezing sound in the throat during sleep or sudden suspension of breath, inert feeling in the limbs, dulness heaviness of the body, and indistinctness speech. Owing the softness of fat, a fatty person unntted for every kind of work. Capacity for sexual intercourse is dimi- nished (in such a one), owing to the obstruction of the passage semen phlegm and fatty deposits and the growth the rest the root-principles of the body such as, lymph chyle, albumen, semen, etc., considerably arrested owing the deposit fatty matter within the channels the internal passages of the body, thus seriously affecting his bodily strength. An obese or excessively corpulent person likely to be afflicted with any of the following diseases such as, urethral discharges, eruptions, boils, carbuncles, fever, fistula ano, or with any of the diseases which are caused by a deranged state of the bodily V^3ai and such attacks are invariably found terminate in death. Any disease in such a person is apt to develop into one of a violent and dangerous type owing the obstruction of the internal channels with deposits of fat. Chap. XV. ^2^7 Hence all things conditions which foster the growth of abnormal fat should be carefully avoided. Accordingly medicated compositions, consisting such drugs and substances Shilajatu, Guggulu, Go-Mutram, Triphala, Loharaja, Rasanjanam, IMadhu, Yava, Mudga, Koradusha, Shyamaka and Uddiilaka which are anti-fat their properties, or of remedial agents possessing the efficacy cleansing the internal channels, as well enematas liquefacient solutions technically known Lekhana Vastis and physical exercise should be prescribed.

english_text: Etiology of Karshyam :—Loss of flesh a gradual emaciation of the body should be ascribed to the partaking of food in the composition of which, matter which aggravates the bodily Vayu largel)' or excessively enters, to over-fatiguing physical exercise, sexual excess- es, over study, fright, grief anxiety, the keeping up of late hours, to unsatisfied hunger, insufficient food, and to astringent food which tends to dr}- up the lymph chyle. The chyle, thus parched up, moves about in the organism, but fails to impart to it the necessary nutritive element owing to its being insufficiently charged with thus causing the body to grow extremely emaciated. A patient suffering from extreme emaciation the body fails to bear the inclemencies of weather and the variations of terrestrial heat, and becomes apathetic all movements and does but imperfectly perform the THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XV. functions of vitality, and also incapable of enduring thirst or hunger. The bodily strength suffers a gradual diminution, and diseases, incidental to a deranged state of the bodily Vayu, make their appearance, and the patient has to meet his doom from any of the following diseases asthma, cough, Shosha (phthisis), enlarged spleen or liver, abdominal drops}^, dyspepsia, abdominal glands and haemoptysis. Any disease appearing in such a patient develops into one of a violent type owing to the loss or diminished condition of the bodily strength or protoplasm (Prina). Contrarily, conditions factors which produce obesity should be avoided. case of patent obesity should be checked with medicated compound, con- sisting of such drugs as, Payasya, Ashvagandha, Vidari, Vidarigandha, Shat^vari, Vala, Ativala, Nagavala and such other drugs of sweet taste. Diets consisting of thickened milk, clarified butter, ciu^d, meat, boiled Shall rice, Yasthika, wheat, barley, etc., should be prescribed in the case and sleep in the day, sexual indulgence, physical exercise, etc., should be prohibited. Enematas of nutri- tive substances can be likewise given with advantage. the other hand, the lymph chyle of a man, who partakes of food belonging to both the abovesaid classes, courses through his organism and strengthens the root- principles of his body, thus giving a middling or health- ful rotundity to his limbs owing to its properties being Chap. XV. SUTRASTHA'NAM. equipoised. A man possessed of such a body is capable of all kinds of work and movement. He can fairly stand the inclemencies of weather and the keenness of hunger and thirst, and will gain in strength and energy. Care should be always taken to have such well equipped body of moderate size. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject*:—Excessively corpulent and excessively lean persons are alike condemnable. A body which neither too stout nor too lean, but strikes the mean regards plumpness, the best. lean frame should have the preference to a stout one.

chapter: Chapter XVI: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of the piercing and bandaging of the lobules of ears (Kama- Vyadha-Vandha-Vidhimadhyaym). The lobules of the ears of an infant are usually pierced through for protecting (from the evil influences mali^ant stars and spirits) and for the purposes of ornamentation as well. The piercing should be performed on a day of bright fortnight marked by the auspicious lunar and astral combinations, and in the sixth the seventh month of the year reckoned from its beginning (Bhadra). The child should be placed on the lap its nurse, and benedictions should be pronounced over Then having soothed and lured with toys and playthings, the physician should draw down with his left hand the lobules its ears with a view to detect, with the help of the reflected sun-light, (the closed up) apertures that are naturally found exist those localities. Then he sliould pierce them straight through with a needle held in his right hand, or with an awl (Ara), or with thick needle where the appendages would be found to be too thick. The lobule of the right ear should be first pierced and then the left in the case of a male child, while the contrary should be the procedure in the case of a female. Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of the piercing and bandaging of the lobules of ears (Kama- Vyadha-Vandha-Vidhimadhyaym). The lobules of the ears of an infant are usually pierced through for protecting (from the evil influences mali^ant stars and spirits) and for the purposes of ornamentation as well. The piercing should be performed on a day of bright fortnight marked by the auspicious lunar and astral combinations, and in the sixth the seventh month of the year reckoned from its beginning (Bhadra). The child should be placed on the lap its nurse, and benedictions should be pronounced over Then having soothed and lured with toys and playthings, the physician should draw down with his left hand the lobules its ears with a view to detect, with the help of the reflected sun-light, (the closed up) apertures that are naturally found exist those localities. Then he sliould pierce them straight through with a needle held in his right hand, or with an awl (Ara), or with thick needle where the appendages would be found to be too thick. The lobule of the right ear should be first pierced and then the left in the case of a male child, while the contrary should be the procedure in the case of a female.

english_text: Out these, the process, known the Nemi- sandhanaka, should used cases where each the bifurcated lobes the ears would be found to thick, extended, and equal in size. The process, known the Utpala-Bhedyaka, should used cases where the severed lobes of the ears would be found to round, extended, and equal dimensions. The process, Valluraka should be resorted to in cases where the severed lobes of the ears would found to be short, circular and equal size. The process, known as the Asangima, should adopted cases where the anterior surface of one of these severed appendages would have a more elongated shape than the other. The process, known the Ganda-Karna, consists in slicing off a patch of healthy flesh from one the regions of the cheeks and in adhering it to one of the severed lobes of the ears which is more elongated on its anterior side than the other (Plastic-operations). the case of extremely short lobes, the flesh should cutoff from both the cheeks and adhered to them, the process being known as the Aharyaya. The lobes of the ears which have been completely severed from their roots are called Pithopamas. The process known the Nirvedhima should resorted in such cases piercing the two Putrikas (Tragus and Anti-tragus the ears. The process known the Vyayojima should made use cases where one the bifurcated Chap. XVI. lobes the ear should found to be dissimilar to the other regards its . thickness thinness. The process known as Kapata-Sandhika consists bring- ing about an adhesion, the posterior side, between one the bifurcated lobes and another, which elongated on the anterior side of the ear. The adhesion is so called from the fact of its resembling the closing of the two leaves door 'Kapatam), The process knoWn as the Ardha-Kapata-Sandhika consists in bring- ing about adhesion the anterior side between the shorter one the two parts of a bifurcated ear- lobe with the part, elongated on the posterior side, like a half- closed door. The ten aforesaid processes adhesion may successfully brought about and their shapes can easily pictured from the meanings their respective names. The remaining five sorts such as the Samkhiptam etc., are seldom attended with success and hence are called impracticable (Asadhayas'. The process Samkhiptam has its scope in the case where the auricle (^Shashkuli) has been withered up and one of the bifurcated lobes is raised, the other being reduced and shortened. The process of Hina-karna should adopted cases where the supporting rim of the lobe (pinna) has been entirely swept away and its exterior sides and the cheeks are sunk and devoid flesh. Similarly the adhesive THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XVI. process known the Vallikarna indicated cases where the lobes are short, thin and unequal. The adhesion known as the Yasthi Kama indicated in cases where the thin and severed ear-lobes are run across with veins and made of knotty or nodular flesh.

The lobules of the ears of an infant are usually pierced through for protecting (from the evil influences mali^ant stars and spirits) and for the purposes of ornamentation as well. The piercing should be performed on a day of bright fortnight marked by the auspicious lunar and astral combinations, and in the sixth the seventh month of the year reckoned from its beginning (Bhadra). The child should be placed on the lap its nurse, and benedictions should be pronounced over Then having soothed and lured with toys and playthings, the physician should draw down with his left hand the lobules its ears with a view to detect, with the help of the reflected sun-light, (the closed up) apertures that are naturally found exist those localities. Then he sliould pierce them straight through with a needle held in his right hand, or with an awl (Ara), or with thick needle where the appendages would be found to be too thick. The lobule of the right ear should be first pierced and then the left in the case of a male child, while the contrary should be the procedure in the case of a female. english_text: IVIetrical texts :—Then the above medicinal unguent should applied or rubbed over the lobe the affected ear, whereby all the disturbing or unfavour- able symptoms would subsided, thus favouring its firm and steady growth. Similarly a plaster composed of Yava, Ashvagandh^, Yashtyahva, and Tila, pasted together might be rubbed over the affected ear-lobe with advantage. Oil prepared and boiled with the essence of Shatavari, and Ashvagandh^, or Payasya, Eranda, Jivana and milk increases the growth of an ear-lobe. The lobe of an ear, which refuses to grow in size in spite of being fomented and lubricated above indicated^ should scarified with slight longitudinal incisions its anterior side (that is on the side nearest to the cheeks) Chap. XVI. and not on the posterior one, as such a procedure might be attended with dreadful results. An ear-lobe should not be tried to be elongated just after the adhesion its two severed parts, inasmuch as the centre the adhesion, still being raw, might cause them to fall off again. Thus ear-lobe under the circumstance should be gradually elongated, only when it would be found to be marked by the growth of hair on its surface, and the hole or the perforation has assumed circular look, and the adhesion has become firmly effected, well-dried, painless, even and level in its entire length. The modes bringing about adhesion of the two severed parts of an e;f\-lobe are innumerable and skilled and experienced surgeon should determine the shape and nature of each according the exi- gencies of a particular case.* Additional Text :—O Sushrula, again shall deal with diseases which affect the lobule of an ear under the circumstance described above The deranged bodily Vdyu, Pittam and Kaphani, either jointly or severally, give rise to several types of diseases which affect the lobule of an ear. The deranged V^yu produces numbness and an erysipelatous swelling and cer about the affected ear-lobe, while erysipelatous ulcer the locality accompanied by swelling, burning, suppuration, etc., should ascribed to the action of the deranged Pittam. Heaviness, numbness and swelling of the ear-lobe accompanied constant itching the affected locality mark the action of the deranged Kapham. The medical treatment in these cases consists in effecting a subsidence of the particular deranged humour means diaphoresis, lubrication, Parishekas (medicated plasters) or blood-letting as the case may be. These measures should moderately applied and a nutritive and invigorating food should pres- 1^2 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XVI. Rhinoplastic operations : -Now shall deal with the process affixing artificial nose. cribed for the patient. The physician who is well familiar with the actions of the deranged bodily humours as described above, should be looked upon as alone entitled to take in hand a case, which falls under the head of one of the preceding types.

english_text: Now I shall enumerate the names of the several diseases which affect severed lobe the ear and describe the sjTnptoms which each of them develops in succession. They are known as UtpStaka, Utputuka, Shyava, Bhrisam-kanduj^ta, Avamantha, Sakanduka, Akundaka, Granthika, J5m- vala, SrAvi and Dihavdna. Now hear me discourse the nature medicinal treatment to be adopted in each of them. Remedies plaster composed the drugs known as Apam£rga, Sarjarasa, Patala bark and Lakucha bark pasted togather, or medicated oil prepared and boiled with the preceding substances should applied in a case of the Utpataka type, wherea' a case of the Utputuka type would prove amenable to a medicinal plaster consisting of Shamp&ka, Shigru, Putika, the fat and marrow of a GodhS and the milk and bile of a she-deer, she-buffalo sow, pasted togather medicated unguent com- posed of the abovesaid substanees duly Lioiled with oil. Similarly, a medi- cinal plaster composed of the drugs known Gauri, Sugandhd, ShydmS, Anantd, Tanduliyakam, or an oil prepared and boiled with the extract the preceding drugs, would prove beneficial in a case of the Shyiva type of the desease. a case of the Vrisham-Sakundakam type, the affected part should rubbed lubricated with an unguent medicated oil prepared with the boiled extract PathA, Rasanjanam, Kshoudram, and warm Kdnjik5m. or a plaster composed of the same drugs and substances should be applied over the diseased locality. In a case of ulceration, the ulcerated ear-lobe should be rubbed with the oil prepared and boiled with the drugs known as Madhukam and Kshira- kSkoli, or with those which form the group known as the Jivakddi-Varga while in a case where Vringhanam measures are to be adopted, lard pre- pared from the fat of a Godhd, boar, or snake might be used with advantage. the Avamanthaka type the diseased ear-lobe should be washed and covered with a plaster composed of the drugs known as Prapaundarikam, Madhukam, Samanga and Dhavam, rubbed with oil prepared and boiled with the same drugs. Similarly, a case of Kandu-Juta (accompanied with itching) would yield a plaster composed the drugs known as SahadevA, Vishvadevd, and Saindhava salt pasted with goat's milk, or to the medicated oil boiled and prepared with the same drugs and substances. Chap. XVI.] 1 1;^ First the leaf creeper, long and broad enough fully cover the whole the severed or clipped off part, should be gathered and a patch of li\ing flesh, equal in dimension to the preceding leaf, should sliced off ;from down upward) from the region the cheek and, after scarifying with knife, swiftly adhered the severed nose. Then the cool- headed physician should steadily tie with bandage decent to look at and perfectly suited the end for which it has been employed (Sadhu Vandha).

chapter: Chapter XVII: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which deals with the mode of distinguishing between suppurating and non- suppurating swellings. Ama-pakkaishaniya- madhyayam. Diseases such Granthi (Aneurism), Vidradhi, (abscess) and Alaji (inflammation the edge of the cornea) etc. are ushered preliminary swelling which subsequently develops symptoms peculiar each of them. These diseases differ in their symptoms and outward shape. swelling which may appear at any part of the body, and round, elevated, even, uneven in its (surface) called Shotha (swelling). It restricts itself to the skin and flesh its locality and characterised the several concerted action of the deranged bodily humours. The Shothas (swelling) admit being divided into six different types according as they are caused by the action of the deranged Vayu, Pittam, Kapham or blood, are due the concerted action of the three fundamental humours of the bod)'', or are of traumatic origin. Now shall describe the symptoms which maT'k the respective actions the humours welling. english_text: The physician should make sure that the adhesion the severed parts has been fully effected and then insert two small pipes into the nostrils facilitate respiration, and to prevent the adhesioned flesh from hanging down. After that, the adhesioned part should dusted with the powders Pattanga, Yashti- madhukam and Rasanjana pulverised together and In a case of the Granthika type (accompanied by the formation knotty growths in its inside) the knotty growths or glandular formations should be first removed, and che affected locality should be bled with surgical instrument and dusted with powdered Saindhava salt. Likewise, case of J^mvala type, blood-letting should be resorted scarifying the seat of the disease, which should be then washed with a spray milk. The ulcer ild be healed after the perfect purification its internal morbid c^ Of ts. case the Srivi (secreting) type would readily vield to a inal plaster composed of the drugs known Madhuparni, snd Mad' ..alil, or of Madhukam pasted with honey, or to the medicinal oil Uprepared and boiled with the same drugs and substances. case the Jahyam^na (burning) tj'pe should be treated with plaster composed the drugs known as the five Kalkas and Madhukam pasted together and nixed with clarified butter, or with a pasted compound of the drugs which form the group of the Jivakadi Varga with quantity clarified butter added to it. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XVI. the nose should be enveloped Karp^sa cotton and several times sprinkled over with the refined oil of pure sesamum. Clarified butter should given the patient for drink, and he should be anointed with oil and treated with purgatives after the complete digestion of the meals he has taken, as advised (in the books medicine). Adhesion should be deemed complete after the incidental ulcer had been perfectly healed up, while the nose should be again scarified and bandaged in the case semi partial adhesion. The adhesioned nose should be tried to elongated where would fall short of its natural and previous length, or it should surgically restored its natural size in the case of the abnormal growth of its newly formed flesh. The mode bringing about the adhesion of severed lips is identical with what has been described connection with a severed nose with the exception of the insertion pipes. The physician, who well conversant with these matters, can be alone entrusted with the medical treatment of a King. english_text: The patient, as if stung by a scorpion, does not find comfort any place or position. The hue the local skin changed and the swelling goes increasing like inflated leather bag ; and fever, thirst, a burning sensation and aversion to food etc. gradually supervene. The suppurated stage is marked by an amelioration of the local pain and a yellowishness of the skin over the swelling, which cracks and seems too big, thus giving pu§ to folds in the integument. The swelling exhibits the uation under pressure and shows perceptible signs large cdinution. Moreover, yields pressure and 1^8 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XVII, reaches its former height when the pressure is removed. The pus or the suppurated matter changes its place, shifts from one part of the sweUing another under pressure hke water bloated leather bag. The distressing symptoms gradually subside the patient again evinces desire for food, and feels a constant inclination for scratching the affected part which characterised sort aching pain. Sometimes, cases of traumatic swelling or in those brought about by deranged condition of the Kapham, the suppurating process restricted the deeper tissues the affected part and hence fail exhibit its characteristic symptoms— a fact which often misleads a physician (surgeon) as regards the true state (lit whether suppurated or not) of the accompan3'ing swell- ing. But the knowledge that a process of suppuration, occurring in the deeper tissues of an affected part, accompanied by alleviation of the pain and swelling which becomes as compact as a stone and cold the touch, and the local skin resuming its natural colour, would unquestionably ward off all apprehensions for error of judgment. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject :—A physician (surgeon) who is fully conversant with the symptoms which are respectively exhibited by (an inflammatory) swelling in its unsuppurated, supT purating and suppurated stages, is alone worthy of the epithet the rest are but impostors. Since there can Chap. XVil. be no pain without the intervention of the deranged V^yu and suppuration can set without the action of the deranged Pittam nor pus, without the action of the deranged Kapham it is evident that suppurated swelling is marked the combined and simultaneous action of the three deranged humours the body. According certain authorities, the deranged Pittam gets the preponderance over the local Vayu and Kapham, and transforms the blood into pus out of its own preponderant energy. The incision or opening of a swelling its inflam- matory unsuppurated (lit. immature, unripe) stage is attended with the destruction of the local flesh, liga- ment, bone, vein, joint, and is usually followed by excessive haemorrhage. The incidental wound becomes extremely painful. Many distressing symptoms begin to manifest themselves succession and cavities are formed inside the wound which may lapse into case of Kshata-Vidradhi (a type of ulcerated abscess).

english_text: On the other hand, a fully suppurated swelling, left unopened for a long time out of fear or ignorance by the attending physician, is attended with symptoms which are fraught with dreadful consequences. The accumulated pus, unable to find an outlet, infiltrated and attacks the deeper tissues the affected part, and forms large cavities or sinuses in their inside, thus converting l6o THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XVII. the disease into one difficult incurable type. Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—The physician (surgeon) who opens an unsup- purated unripe swelling out of ignorance, as well as the man who neglects a fully suppurated one, should looked upon the vilest Chandala for his wrong or incorrect diagnosis. The patient should be provided with meal before the surgical operation, strong wine should be given him, if he is found to be addicted to the habit of taking any. The effect of a good meal under the circumstance will be to keep up the strength of the patient and to guard against his swooning during the operation, while the effect of wine will be to make him unconscious of the pain. The rule as regards the feeding and anaesthetising (wine giving) of the patient should strictly adhered since the internal vital principle of a man is invigorated by the strength of his body which is the product of lymph-chyle, the essence food, and the quintessence the five material principles. swelling, matter whether limited or extensive, spontaneously runs on to suppura- tion, if not medicinally treated, or left to nature. The base of such a swelling goes on extending. It becomes unequally suppurated and reaches an unequal elevation, thus affecting the deeper tissues of the part and swiftly running into one of an incurable type. A swelHng, which does not yield to the application of medicated plasters Chap. XVII. or to corrective or blood-letting measures, speedily and uniformly suppurates, and marked small and restricted base and a circular or conical elevation. blazing fire fed by gusts of favourable wind soon consumes a withered forest, so the incarcerated pus, the absence any outlet, attacks and eats away the healthy flesh, veins and nerves of an organism. Surgical acts in connection with an abscess (Shotha) may be divided into seven kinds such mutila- tion (Vimlapanam) the swelling massage, 2. Avashechanam (bleeding or application leeches) 3. Upanaham (poulticing) 4. Patanam (opening incision; 5. Shodhanam (purification the internal morbid matter incised boil with corrective medicines) 6. Ropanam (healing) and 7. Vaikritdpa- ham (restoring of the natural colour of the skin the cicatrix). Thus ends the .scvciUecnlh Chaplei ihc Suiiaslhfinam ttie Siisliiul;! A plaster of the Pradeha class pacifies the deranged Vayu and Kapham and tends to bring about the union, purification, and healing (of an ulcer), causing the As in the case of a Pidayitavya ulcer, described before, where the withdrawing or gathering of pus to a definite head is desired. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XVIII. subsidence of pain and swelling. Hence should used all types swelling whether ulcerated otherwise. medicinal plaster (Alepanam) applied over ulcer called the changed epithet of Kalka or Niruddha-Alepanam (arrestive astringent plaster). The function of such an Alepanam consists in arresting a local haemorrhage, in softening the ulcer, in withdrawing sloughing or putrifying flesh from its cavity, in checking the formation pus its inside, and in correcting the morbid matter deranged humours (that retard its union and healing).
