They are as follows Kshauma (cloth woven with the fibres Atasi plant), Avika (blankets made sheeps' wool), Dukulum (loom-silk), Kausheya (silk), the Patroma kind cloth made the fibres of Naga trees, which grow in the provinces of Paundra and Magadha), the Chinapatta (Chinese cloth), Antarvalkala (the inner bark fibres tree), Charma iskin), the Alfivu Shakala 'the skin gourd), the Lata-Vidala (half thrashed Shyama creepers), string cord, the cream milk, Tula-phalam (cotton seeds) and iron. These accessories should used considera- tion of the exigencies of each case and the time or the season of the year in which it occurs.! english_text: In a swelling or ulcer caused by the deranged and Kapham, the bandage should consist of a piece of thick cloth; whereas summer it should consist of thin linen. Similarly, a bandage, tied round anv deep or hollow part of the body, should consist of a piece of thick cloth. The contrary rule should be observed, when the seat of the bandage would at any flexible part of the body. Similarly, in the ca.se of a snake-bite, a ligature .should be- firmly tied above the punctured wound with a string or twisted cord cotton, while a fractured bone should be set right twisting bunches iif half-thrashed shydmS creeper (LatAvidala) round the seat of fracture. A local hemorrhage Chap. XVIII. SUTRASTHANA M. 1 67 Bandag'es :—The fourteen different forms bandage are named as the Kosha (a sheath or scabbard), the D^ma (a cord or chaplet , the Svastika (cross), the Anuvelhta twist), the Pratoli (a winding street or road), the Mandala (ring), the Sthagika (a betel -box), the Yamaka (double or twin), the Khatta (a bedstead), the China streamer), the Vivandha (noose), the Vitana (canopy) the Gophana (cow-horn), the Panchangi (five limbed). Their shapes can be easil}' inferred from the meanings of their names. Applications :—Out of these, the Kosha the sheath-shaped bandage should tied round the thumb and the phalanges of the fingers the Dama chaplet-shaped bandage, round the narrow or unbent parts of the body the Svastika or cross shaped, round the joints, round the articulations or the Marmas known the Kurchakas (Navicular ligaments) round the eye-brows, round the ears and round the region the breast. Similarly, the bandage, known as the Anu- vellita, should be used when the seat of the affection would be found to be situated at the extremities (hands and legs\ A bandage of the Protoli class should be tied round the neck the penis the Mandalam 'ring- should be arrested by binding the part with milk-cream, while the aflected part in a case of Ardita (facial paralysis) as well as a broken tooth should be bound with strings of iron, gold or silver. Warts, etc. should be bandaged with Ela (cardamom skins), while dried ^ourd-skins should used bandaging ulcers on the head (scalp). 1 68 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XVlil. shaped), round the circular parts the body the Sthagika (betel-box), round the glans-penis and the tips of the fingers ; the Yamakam, round the confluent contiguous ulcers the Khatta (bedstead-shaped), over and around the cheeks, cheek-bones, and the parts between the ears and the eye-brows ; the Vitdnam over the skull, the Gophana (horn-shaped), round the region of the chin and the Panchangi, round the part lying above the clavicles. In short, a bandage of any particular shape should be tied round the part of the body to which would found be most suited. Now we shall deal with the Yantranas (fastenings of bandages) which admit being divided into three different classes according they are fastened above, below, obliquely round an ulcer.

SUTRASTHANA M. 1 67 Bandag'es :—The fourteen different forms bandage are named as the Kosha (a sheath or scabbard), the D^ma (a cord or chaplet , the Svastika (cross), the Anuvelhta twist), the Pratoli (a winding street or road), the Mandala (ring), the Sthagika (a betel -box), the Yamaka (double or twin), the Khatta (a bedstead), the China streamer), the Vivandha (noose), the Vitana (canopy) the Gophana (cow-horn), the Panchangi (five limbed). Their shapes can be easil}' inferred from the meanings of their names. Applications :—Out of these, the Kosha the sheath-shaped bandage should tied round the thumb and the phalanges of the fingers the Dama chaplet-shaped bandage, round the narrow or unbent parts of the body the Svastika or cross shaped, round the joints, round the articulations or the Marmas known the Kurchakas (Navicular ligaments) round the eye-brows, round the ears and round the region the breast. Similarly, the bandage, known as the Anu- vellita, should be used when the seat of the affection would be found to be situated at the extremities (hands and legs\ A bandage of the Protoli class should be tied round the neck the penis the Mandalam 'ring- should be arrested by binding the part with milk-cream, while the aflected part in a case of Ardita (facial paralysis) as well as a broken tooth should be bound with strings of iron, gold or silver. Warts, etc. should be bandaged with Ela (cardamom skins), while dried ^ourd-skins should used bandaging ulcers on the head (scalp). 1 68 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XVlil. shaped), round the circular parts the body the Sthagika (betel-box), round the glans-penis and the tips of the fingers ; the Yamakam, round the confluent contiguous ulcers the Khatta (bedstead-shaped), over and around the cheeks, cheek-bones, and the parts between the ears and the eye-brows ; the Vitdnam over the skull, the Gophana (horn-shaped), round the region of the chin and the Panchangi, round the part lying above the clavicles. In short, a bandage of any particular shape should be tied round the part of the body to which would found be most suited. Now we shall deal with the Yantranas (fastenings of bandages) which admit being divided into three different classes according they are fastened above, below, obliquely round an ulcer.

The tow the Kavalika should placed thickly (on the seat of affection) and then the physician (surgeon; having pressed with his left hand should* place a piece of straight, soft, untwisted, Carefully examining whether ihe applied remedy had been unifurmly dislributed over the diseased surface and whether tlie contemplated pattern of bandage would l)e actually suited to the case. Chap. XVIII. SUTRASTHA'NAM. 1 69 and unfolded unshrivelled linen over it, and then firmly tie up the bandage manner not leave any knot over the seat of the ulcer, cause any discomfort to the patient. Introduction of lint :—A Visheshika (lint) saturated with hone}', clarified butter, and medi- cinal paste should inserted into the ulcer. Care should be taken not to introduce the lint extremely dry, or oily (oversoaked in a lubricating oily medi- cinal preparation), inasmuch as an over-lubricated lint would give rise excessive formation slimy mucus the ulcer, whereas, its parched substitute would bring about the friction and the consequent breaking the edges the ulcer, like one mis- placed or wrongly inserted. bandage should tied any the three ways Gadha, Sama and Shithila fastenings according the shape and seat the ulcer.* A tight bandage (Gadha-Vandha) should be tied round the buttocks, round the sides, round the arm-pits, round the inguinal regions, round the breast round the head. bandage the Sama pattern should fastened round the ears, round the extremities (hands Additional text :—A bandage, tightly tied round ulcerated affected part of the body without causing any pain discomfort the patient, is called a GAdha-Vandha, while the one which is loosely bound is called Shithila, the one neither too tight nor too loose being called a Sama- V'andha. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XVIII, and legs), round the face, round the throat, round the lips, round the penis, round the scrotum, round the back, round the belly and the chest. A loose bandaging (Shithila-Vandha) should be the rule the region the eyes and locations of important joints or unions. ulcer, brought about characterised the symptoms of the deranged Pittam and occurring at a place where a tight bandaging indicated, should fastened with one of the Sama-Vandha class, and with a Shithila bandage where one of the Sama type would be indicated whereas it should not be bandaged at all in the event of a loose bandage (Shithila-Vandha) being indicated. The same rule should observed the case of an ulcer caused through a diseased contami- nated state of the blood. Similarl}'', in the case of an ulcer produced through a deranged condition of the Kapha m, a loose bandaging, otherwise enjoined adopted, should substituted for one the same pattern.

english_text: A tight bandage should give place lighter one under the same circumstances, and such a procedure should be deemed holding good even the case ulcer caused by the action the deranged Vayu. In summer and autumn, the bandage of an ulcer, due to the vitiated blood or Pittam, should be changed twice a day ; while the one tied round ulcer of the deranged Vayu or Kapham, should be changed on each Chap. XVIII. third day in spring and Hemanta. Similarly, ulcer, marked by the action of the deranged Vayu, should be bandaged twice a day. " Thou shalt exercise thy own discretion, and vary or adopt the preceding rules bandaging according to the exigencies of each case." medicated lint fails have any efficacy but rather tends to augment the local pain and swelling where a bandage, enjoined to be loosely bound, or bound up with moderate and uniform steadiness (Sama-Vandha), is replaced by tight deep fastened one (Gadha- Vandha,. loose bandage, injudiciously used case where tight moderately firm bandage should have been used, would cause the medicine to fall off from the lint and give rise the consequent friction and laceration the edges the ulcer. Similarly, moderately firm and steady bandage (Sama-Vandha; fastened in a case where a light or loose bandage should have been used, would fail produce any effect. proper bandage would lead the subsidence of pain, and the softening the edges the ulcer, thus bringing about a purification of the local blood. Evils non- bandaging :~An ulcer, left uncovered and untied with suitable bandage, soon assailed by gnats and flies. moistened sweat and cold wind, etc. and stands in danger being irritated deposits of many foreign matters THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XVIII. such the particles bone, dust, weeds, etc. Moreover, a constant exposure to heat or cold brings on varied pains, the ulcer develops into one malignant type, and the applied medicinal plasters are dried, encrusted and speedily fall off. IVIetrical Texts :—A smashed, lacerated, frac- tured, dislocated, displaced bone, or a vein or a ligament similarly jeopardised, may be soon healed set right with the help surgical bandage. The patient enabled by such a means to lie down, stand move about with ease. And increased facility rest or movement leads to speedy healing. Cases where bandaging is prohibit- : —Ulcers should not bandaged at all that are due the deranged condition blood Pittam, the effects of a blow or of any imbibed poison, and characterised by a sucking, burning pain, redness, or suppuration, as well as those which are incidental to burns, or to the applications actual potential cauteries marked by a sloughing or phagedenic character. Metrical Texts :—An ulcer due to a scald in a leper or a carbuncle diabetic patient (Pidaka) as well as fleshy condylomata due bite from venomous rat, or any other poisonous ulcer should not be bandaged at all. The same rule should be observed Different reading :— Pricking, burning pain. Chap.

english_text: XVIII.] 1 73 the case of a dreadful suppuration about the anus, or in that of a sloughing ulcej. An intelligent physician, familiar with the specific features ulcers, should observe the shape the one under treatment, and prognosticate the result from its seat or locality and the nature of the deranged bodily humours involved in the case. The season of the year in which ulcer first seen to appear also determines the nature the pfognosis. Bandages may be tied up either from above, below, from the sides of a diseased locality. Now I shall fully describe the process of bandaging an ulcer.* First the Kavalika or tow should thickly laid over the seat of the ulcer and after that piece soft and unshrivelled linen should placed upon it, and the bandage should be loosely or tightly tied up according to the directions laid down before.* The lint and the (inserted) medicine should not over-lubricated and must not be inordinately oily in much as such a lint or medicine would give rise the formation of excessive and abnormal slimy mucous the ulcer. On the other hand, extremely dry lint would set friction and laceration of the edges the ulcer, like the one wrongly improperly inserted Several authorities such as GayadAsa, Brahmadeva, etc. hold this portion the text to be an interpolation. Both Dallana and ChakrapSni have included it within their commentaries with nearly the same remark. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap, xviii. into its cavity, causing numbness, excessive exudation and unevenness its surface. lint, properly saturated with a medicinal plaster and rightly inserted into the cavity of an ulcer, leads to its speedy healing. All secreting measures connection with ulcer should be continued or stopped according its condi- tion, whereby the nature and shape of the bandage should be determined well. ulcer, due either the deranged condition blood the Pittam, should be dressed and bandaged once a day which may be extended to a number of times the case ulcer brought about by the deranged Kapham and Vayu. The pus or the local morbid matter should secreted by pressing the base or the bottom of an ulcer and gently moving the hand along it in a contrary' direction (down, upward ; and all bandages around joints and Gudasandhis) should be duly tied up. The rules laid down under the head of adhesioning the parts of a bifurcated ear-lobe would hold good case of severed lips as well. The measures amply dis- cussed in the present Chapter should extended by means inference, analogy and judgment to apply mutatis mutandis the bandaging of fractured or dislocated bone. An ulcer, properly bandaged, has greater chance of not being affected by lying down, sitting up, any other movement of the patient, nor by the joltings Chap. XVIII. SUTRASTHA'NAM.

chapter: Chapter XIX: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats the management nursing of a patient with an ulcer etc. (Vranito-pa^saniya-madhyaryam). First of all a suitable chamber should be sought and selected for a patient, suffering from an ulcer. It should be roomy and spacious and situated a commend- able site. IVIetrical Text :—Diseases, which are physical, mental or traumatic their origin, can never attack a person who dwells in a clean and spacious chamber, protected from excessive heat, and strong gusts of wind. The bed should spread clean, ample and comfortable, with the head the beadstead turned towards the east, and provided with some kind of a weapon. rVIetrical Texts :— In spacious and well- spread bed, an ulcer-patient can toss about and move his limbs with the greatest comfort. The reason for the head being turned towards the east that the patient may easily make obeisance the (demons and) celestial spirits, who inhabit that quarter of the sky. Thus the patient shall lie in comfortable posture, attended upon by his sweet-talking friends and relations
english_text: 1 7^ of a conveyance he may ride carried ulcer affecting a vein ligament or the skin (super- ficial) the flesh the bones cannot healed without bandaging An ulcer situated in any of the internal chambers (cavities) of the bod}', or occurring at any junction of the limbs organs, etc. having its seat in bone and whether deep, superficial, malignant, or corrosive character, cannot be brought to a successful termination without the lielp of a bandage. Thus ends the eighteenth Chapter of the Sutrasth^nam in the Siishruta Samhita which treats of the dressing and bandaging of ulcers. CHAPTER XIX. Thus the patient shall lie in comfortable posture, attended upon by his sweet-talking friends and relations. Chap. XIX. 1 77 Metrical Text :—The friends and relations of a patient shall alleviate , the pain his ulcer with pleasant and interesting topics, and solacing him with the prospect speedy recovery. ulcer- patient should not sleep the day time, tends aggravate the pain, swelling and redness the ulcer, increases its exudations, and gives rise to itching and heaviness of the limbs. The patient must carefully protect the ulcer when moving any his limbs, such standing sitting down, or turning on his sides, or while moving about, or speaking in a loud voice. IVIetrical Text ulcer-patient, even feels himself strong and capable, should avoid a standing or sitting posture, well locomotion, and day-sleep.* These acts done excess, or a long confinement to bed would aggravate the bodily Vayu, thus causing pain in the ulcer. He should studiously avoid the company and touch of, and even conversation with, women with whom can legitimately have intercourse. english_text: Prohibited diet :—A diet consisting newly harvested Dhan3''am, Masha pulse, Sesamum_, Kalaya, Kulattha, and Nishpaba should avoided by an ulcer-patient. The pot-herbs known as Haritaka- shaka, acid, saline or pungent substances, treacle and its modifications, cakes, dried meat, dried pot-herbs, goat's flesh, mutton, meat animals which are amphi- bious in their habits or which live close to water, lard, cold water, Krishara composition prepared with sesamum, Masha pulse and rice), P^yasa sweetened preparation of rice, milk and sugar boiled together), curd, milk and whey should be regarded as unwholesome. iVIctrical Texts :—Vegetables and articles which belong the groups commencing from the one technically known the Nava-Dhanya-Varga, and ending with the one known as the Takra-Varga, should be understood as possessed the property ' increasing the pus in an ulcer and of aggravating the deranged bodily humours. the habit taking wine, an ulcer-patient will well avoid the use spirituous liquors, such as Mairaya, Arishta, Asava, Sidhu, Sura and its varieties.* ulcer may develop The species of wine which are made of the expressed juice of grapes and are antacids in their virtues, as well as those mentioned under the head of Haemoptysis, may be given to an ulcer-patient. Chap. XIX. SUTRASTHA'NAM. into one of malignant type through the use wine which is acid in its taste, sharp, dry and heat-making its potency, followed almost instantaneous intoxication. An ulcer-patient should avoid all things that retard the progress of a rapid cure, such as wind, dust, smoke, exposure heat and cold, over-eating, unpleasant sounds and sights, envy, humiliation, fear, anger, grief, scheming, keeping of late hours, sitting or lying in uneven posture, fasting, garrulousness, physical exer- cise, leaping standing posture, locomotion, posure to cold winds, ingestion of unwholesome, compatible indigestible substances, and flea-bites on the affected locality. IVIctrical Texts :—The food, partaken of by a weakened and emaciated ulcer-patient, not fully digested owing the above mentioned, and other multifarious causes. The undigested food violently disturbs and aggravates the bodily humours, which move about in the body and give rise swelling, secretion, burning pain and suppuration in the ulcer. ulcer-patient should always clad clean and white garments, have his hair and nails closely clipped and pared off, and live humble devotion to the Br^hmans, to the gods and the preceptors. The rites of benediction and divine peace should done unto him. Wherefore Because the monsters and j8o the SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XIX. demons of mighty prowess, who are the attendants the gods Pashupati, Kuvera and Kum^ra, roam about quest prey, and visit the bedside ulcer-patient out of their fondness for flesh and blood, being attracted thereto by the smell of the secreted and morbid matter in the ulcer.

chapter: Chapter XIX: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats the management nursing of a patient with an ulcer etc. (Vranito-pa^saniya-madhyaryam). First of all a suitable chamber should be sought and selected for a patient, suffering from an ulcer. It should be roomy and spacious and situated a commend- able site. IVIetrical Text :—Diseases, which are physical, mental or traumatic their origin, can never attack a person who dwells in a clean and spacious chamber, protected from excessive heat, and strong gusts of wind. The bed should spread clean, ample and comfortable, with the head the beadstead turned towards the east, and provided with some kind of a weapon. rVIetrical Texts :— In spacious and well- spread bed, an ulcer-patient can toss about and move his limbs with the greatest comfort. The reason for the head being turned towards the east that the patient may easily make obeisance the (demons and) celestial spirits, who inhabit that quarter of the sky. Thus the patient shall lie in comfortable posture, attended upon by his sweet-talking friends and relations
english_text: These evil spirits come take away the life a patient in a case which is doomed to terminate fatally, while successful case their advent is due to the desire extorting sacri- ficial oblations from him. Authoritative verse the sub- ject : —These honour-seeking evil spirits should be worshipped and propitiated with the whole heart and offerings of burning incense sticks, edibles and sacrifices, etc. should made to them with the greatest humility. The evil spirits, worshipped and propitiated above, spare the life a self-controlled patient ^out of compassion Hence he shall be kept chamber furnished with flowers, garlands, weapons, fried paddy, and lamps kept continuously burning. His friends and relations should regale him with fond and loving topics to drive away the feeling of sleepiness with the prospect of a speedy cure. Metrical Texts :—A patient, constantly cheered with the suggested prospects speedy Chap. XIX.] recovery, and beguiled with pleasant and congenial discourses, soon gets rid of his complaint. Morning and evening, the physicians and the Brdh- mans should perform the rites of benediction, over him^ as laid down in the Rik, Yajuh, Saman and the Atharva Vedas. IVIetrical Texts :— For ten consecutive days, the room of the patient should be diligently fumigated, morning and evening, with the fumes mustard, Arishta-leaves, clarified-butter and salt made into kind of incense stick. Drugs such as Chhatra, Atichhatra, Languli, Jatil^, Bramhacharini, Lakshmi, Guh^^ Atiguha, Shata-viryaya, Sahasra-viryaya and white mustard seeds should placed on the head of the patient. Metrical Texts :—The patient should fanned with blowing chowries that the ulcer may not any way thrashed lacerated during the fanning. The ulcer should not scratched pressed. The patient should be carefully watched, while asleep. Demons, that get abroad in the night, fly from the presence of an ulcer-patient pro- tected as above, as herds of deer fly from the forest where lions are found. Regimen of diet and conduct :— An ulcer-patient living diet consisting old and igo THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XIX. boiled Shall rice, not extremely liquefied, and treated with clarified-butter, and taken with the cooked meat of animals of the J^ngala species, soon gets rid of his disease. A diet consisting of boiled rice, the pot-herbs known the Tanduliyakam, Jivanti, Sunishannaka, V^stuka, immatme Mulaka, Vartaku, Patola and K^ravella, fried with Saindhava (rock-salt) and clarified-butter, and seasoned with the expressed Juice of Dhadima and Amalakam, of Mudga soup treated as above, should be prescribed for the patient. Barley powder, Vilepi, Kulm^sha and boiled water, should likewise given the patient for food and drink. Fatigue physical exercise causes the ulcer swell, while the keeping late hours increases the local redness. sleep during the day under the circumstance would give rise to pain the affected part, while a coitus may bring the death of the patient.

chapter: Chapter XX: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of the salutary and non- salutary effects regimen, etc. (H ita'h itiya- madhyayam) According to certain eminent medical authorities, an article or a substance which is beneficial derange- ments of the bodily Vayu may prove positively injuri- ous Pittaja affection hence impossible to name an article substance which absolutely universally wholesome (^irrespective of the nature and type disease, and the deranged bodily humours involved therein}. But we cannot subscribe the foregoing pothesis, since nature combination, things (substances are, or become endued with properties, which prove absolutely beneficial unconditionally harmful or exert mixed virtue (both beneficial and injurious) according to the difference in the natu^ and type of the disease in which they are employed. Things articles such clarified- butter, water, milk and boiled-rice, etc. may denominated absolutely beneficial owing their congeniality natural suitableness to the human organism. Similarly, substances such as fire, alkali and poison, may be designated as unconditionally harmful in virtue
english_text: ulcer-patient, not given sleep the day, and ^ing a room protected from gusts wind, and strictly following the instructions of his physician, (surgeon) is healed in the course of very short time and will enjoy a long life through the observance the abovesaid regimen of diet and conduct. This is the dictum of Dhanvantari. Thus ends the nineteenth Chapter of the SutrasthSnam in the Sushruta SamhitS which treats of the nursing or management of an ulcer-patient. CHAPTER XX. Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of the salutary and non- salutary effects regimen, etc. (H ita'h itiya- madhyayam) According to certain eminent medical authorities, an article or a substance which is beneficial derange- ments of the bodily Vayu may prove positively injuri- ous Pittaja affection hence impossible to name an article substance which absolutely universally wholesome (^irrespective of the nature and type disease, and the deranged bodily humours involved therein}. But we cannot subscribe the foregoing pothesis, since nature combination, things (substances are, or become endued with properties, which prove absolutely beneficial unconditionally harmful or exert mixed virtue (both beneficial and injurious) according to the difference in the natu^ and type of the disease in which they are employed. Things articles such clarified- butter, water, milk and boiled-rice, etc. may denominated absolutely beneficial owing their congeniality natural suitableness to the human organism. Similarly, substances such as fire, alkali and poison, may be designated as unconditionally harmful in virtue THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XX. their burning, suppurating lit boiling) and fatal effect upon the organic bodies in general. A substance, which is innocuous by nature, may prove equally injuri- ous any active poison through injudicious incompatible combination whereas a substance or an article, which proves beneficial in a derangement of the Vayu, ma}^ prove otherwise in a disorder of the Pittam. Articles or substances which may be safel}'- included within the food stuffs of all human beings are the mem- bers of the group Varga) known as the red Shali, the Shastika, the Kanguka, the Mukundaka, the Panduka, the Pitaka, the Pramodaka, the Kalaka, the Ashanaka, the Pushpaka, the Karddamaka, the Shakunahrita, the Sugandhaka, the Kalama, the Nivara, the Kodrava the Uddalaka, the Shy^maka, the Godhuma and the Venn, etc., as well as the flesh of the Ena, the Harina (copper coloured deer), the Kuranga, the Mriga, the Mriga- matrika, the Shvadanstra, the Karala, the Krakara, the Kapota (pigeon), the Lava, the Tittiri, the Kapinjala, the Varttira, and the Varttika, and such like beasts and birds. The varieties pulse which form the articles of human food are known the Mudga, the Vana-Mudga, the Makushtha, the Kalaya, the Masura, the Mangalya, the Chanaka, the Harenu, the the Adhaki and the Satina.

english_text: Similarly, the different species of pot-herbs, which may be safely used by a man to give a greater relish to his food, are named the Chap. XX.] 1 85 Chilli, the V^stuka, the Sunishannaka the Jivanti, the Tanduliyaka, and the Mandukaparni, etc. Clarified- butter, the salt known the Saindhava, and the luscious juice of the pomegranate and the Amalakam, should be generally deemed the most wholesome articles of food. Similarly, the practise of self-control, residence in a room protected from the strong gusts of wind, sleeping only at night, tepid water, and moderate physical exercise should regarded as absolutely conducive to a better preservation of health. have already enumerated the names sub- stances which are absolutely beneficial uncondi- tionally injurious to human health. Things which are both wholesome and injurious are those, which, for example, may prove beneficial distemper the bodily. Vayu though otherwise Pittaj a affec- tion. The Valli fruit, the Karaka, the Karira, the Amla-phala, the salt, the Kulattha, the Pinyaka, curd, oil, Virohi, cakes, the dried pot-herbs, goat's flesh, mutton, wine, the Jamboline fruit, the Chilichima fish, the flesh of the Godha, and the Varaha (wild boar) being eaten simultaneousl)^ with milk, furnish an example of articles which may act deadly poisons through incompatible combinations. Metrical Texts:—An intelligent physician, considering the nature of the disease, the strength and 1 86 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XX. temperament of the patient, and the state of his diges- tion well the seat of the affection, the physical features of the country and the then prevailing season of the year, should prescribe a diet which thinks the most proper and suitable the requirements the case. Since the conditions infinitely vary in the different types of diseases and even the same conditions do not obtain one and the same type,* physicians generally prescribe a diet of their own selection, one determined with regard to its general effect on health, in preference one that has been laid down in books of medicine. If asked prescribe either milk poison healthy person, a physician would naturally prescribe the former, and thereby, prove the absolute wholesome- ness of milk and unconditional harmfulness of poison. Thus verified, Sushruta, the correctness of the tum, that things such water, etc., are absolutely and unconditionally wholesome or otherwise, by virtue of their respective natural properties. Things which are unwholesome through combination :—Now shall enu- merate the names of substances which become positively unwholesome through incompatible combinations. The The propriety and improprietyof a particular diet should be deter- mined with a full regard the antecedent and attending circumstances of a particular malady. Chap. XX.

english_text: 1 87 flesh any domestic (Gramya) aquatic (Anupa) beast or bird, as well the flesh of those which live in marshy ground (Audaka), should not be eaten with boiled rice prepared from paddy which has com- menced sprouting, with lard, honey, milk, treacle Masha-pulse. The pot-herbs,, known the Rohini and the J4tu-shaka, should not partaken combination with milk and honey ; nor the flesh of a heron, eaten simultaneously with Kulm^sha and the spirituous liquor known V^runi. Maricha (black pepper) and Pippalis should not eaten combination with the pot-herbs known as the Kakam^chi. The pot-herbs known as the Nadima and Siddhi should not be simultaneously eaten with curd, and the flesh a cock. Honey should not be taken immediately after drinking warm water, nor meat and bile should simultaneouly eaten. Sura (wine), Krishara and Payasa should not be taken in combination. Similarly, Souviraka and sesamum paste, fish and modifications of sugarcane juice, treacle and Kdkam^chi, honey and Mulakam, treacle and the flesh wild boar, honey and boar's flesh should not taken combination. Similarly, milk and Mulakam, mango fruit and Jamboline fruit and the flesh of Godh^, Porcupine and hog should not be eaten together. All fish, specially those of the Chilichimi species, should not be taken with 1 88 I'HE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XX. milk, nor the fruit of a plantain tree should be simulta- neously eaten with Tila fruit, milk or whey. The fruit known as Lakucha should not be taken with milk, curd or meat soup, nor with honey and clarified-butter, nor immediately before or after the drinking of milk. I ncompatible preparations of food:— Now shall enumerate the names sub- stances, which become unwholesome through incom- patible preparations. Flesh of pigeon fried with mustard oil should not eaten. The flesh of a Kapinjala, Myura (peacock, L^va, Tittira, and Godha, boiled with castor oil and fire the twigs castor plants, should not eaten. Clarified-butter, kept in a vessel Indian bell metal for ten consecutive days, should rejected unwholesome. Honey should not used combination with article substance heated fire, nor the seasons spring and autumn. The pot-herbs known the Kakam^chi, boiled in a bowl which fish ginger had been previously boiled prepared, should be rejected as positively injurious. Similarly, the pot-herbs known the Upodika should not be eaten by boiling them with the levigated paste of sesamum. The flesh heron prepared with hog's lard should not taken with the pulp of the cocoanut fruit. The flesh of a Bhasa bird, roasted on a spit over a charcoal fire, should not be eaten. Chap. XX. chapter: Chapter XX: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which treats of the salutary and non- salutary effects regimen, etc. (H ita'h itiya- madhyayam) According to certain eminent medical authorities, an article or a substance which is beneficial derange- ments of the bodily Vayu may prove positively injuri- ous Pittaja affection hence impossible to name an article substance which absolutely universally wholesome (^irrespective of the nature and type disease, and the deranged bodily humours involved therein}. But we cannot subscribe the foregoing pothesis, since nature combination, things (substances are, or become endued with properties, which prove absolutely beneficial unconditionally harmful or exert mixed virtue (both beneficial and injurious) according to the difference in the natu^ and type of the disease in which they are employed. Things articles such clarified- butter, water, milk and boiled-rice, etc. may denominated absolutely beneficial owing their congeniality natural suitableness to the human organism. Similarly, substances such as fire, alkali and poison, may be designated as unconditionally harmful in virtue
english_text: Incompatible tastes, potencies and chemical actions :—Now shall describe the substances enumerated in couples, and possessed different tastes, which prove incompatible each other through their respective tastes, potencies and chemical actions Vipaka). Sweet and acid tastes, or sweet and saline tastes should deemed incom- patible to each other in respect of their potencies and inherent properties. Sweet and acrid tastes are incom- patible to each other in all the above three respects. Similarly, sweet and bitter, or sweet and astringent things should be deemed incompatible to each other in respect of their tastes, and chemical action. Acid and sahne things are incompatible to each other regards their flavours. Acid and acrid things are incompatible regards flavour and chemical action. Acid and bitter, or acid and astringent things, are incompatible each other, both as regards their respective flavours, potencies, and digestive chemical transformations. IQO THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XX. Saline and pungent things are incompatible to each other regards their respective flavour (Rasa) and digestive (chemical) transformation. Similarly, saline and bitter things saline and astringent things are incompatible each other in respect of all the three abovesaid relations and cate- gories. Pungent and bitter tastes are incompatible each other respect flavour and digestive transformation, whereas substances pungent and astringent bitter and astringent tastes are compatible one another regards their spective potencies, flavours and digestive chemical) action or transformation. Degrees incompatibility : -Sub- stances that are incompatible with, or antagonistic to, the system through difference of degree or intensity, as well as things which bring about extreme dryness the organism, those which are extremely oily in their composition are characterised extreme cold or warmth, should be categorically rejected. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject :—Things or substances which are incompatible one another in their respective tastes, potencies and reactionary transformation should denied abso- lutely unwholesome, while the rest should be consi- dered possessed of mixed virtues ^wholesome Chap. XX. SUTRASTHA'NAM. injurious under certain circumstances' described before. By taking substances which are incompatible to one another as regards their tastes, potencies and digestive transformation, greed}^ and intemperate person becomes afflicted with disease and weakness the sense-organs, and ultimately meets with his doom. Anything, which being taken enrages or agitates the bodily humours without causing the assimilated food ( effete matter) to be evacuated out of the bowels, or possessed of a taste contrary to, or other than what necessary for the purposes vitalization, should looked upon as the primary source all bodily dis- tempers.

chapter: Chapter XXI: Now we shall discuss the Chapter which investigates the nature bodily humours, exciting causes ulcers (Vrana-prashna-madhyayam). The Vayu, Pittam and Shleshma should be considered as the primary and the most essential factors in the con- stitution of human organism. These fundamental and vital humours, occupying respectively the lower, middle, and upper parts of the body, maintain its integrity. The human body supported the three fundamental humours the same way dwelling house propped up by three supporting poles or stays from which fact, the body is called the three-supported one (Tristhunam) by certain authorities. deranged con- dition of these three fundamental humours may bring about its dissolution death, while their con- tinuance in a normal state depends the vitality the organism. These three humours, in combination with a fourth, the principle blood, determine the origin, preservation, and dissolution of animated organism and permeate with their respective properties till the moment of death. Authoritative verse the sub- ject :—There can organism without Vdyu, Pittam, Kapham and blood, which are necessary

The terms Vata (Vayu), Pittam and Shleshma (Kaphami are respec- tively derived from the roots to move or smell, ' Tapa,' to burn or to heat, and " Shlisha," to embrace, with the suffix 'Ta' thereto added.* Seats of the bodily humours :— Now we shall describe the locations of the foregoing vital hu- mours. The Vaj'^u may be briefly described located in the regions of the pelvis (Shroni), and the rectum (Guda The Pittam has its seat in the region between the stomach 1 Amashaya) and the intestines (Pakvashaya) which above the pelvis and the rectum and below the umbilicus, while the Kapham is ensconced within the cavity of the stomach (Amashaya. Xow we shall divide the locations each the vital humours into five parts :—The five localities of the Vayu will described under the head Vata- Vyadhis (nervous diseases), while those of Pittam are the liver and the spleen, the heart, the pupils the eyes, the skin and the intestines (Pakvashaya\ The Kapham is located in the region of the breast, the throat, the head, the joints and the stomach (Amashaya). The fore- going regions are the seats of the vital humours in their normal state. * From this shiiuld he infenetl that niotion and sim-ll arc nalural allriljutes ihe viud heal and ImininL; arc lliosc uf I'iUam, and union and inlegraliun arc iho.sc of Kapham. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. xxi. IVIetrical Texts :—The vital humours ^Vayu, Pittam and Kapham maintain the integrity the animated organism creating, assimilating and diffusing strength in the same way as the moon, the sun, and the winds maintain the integrity the terrestrial globe.* The Pittam :—Now it may be asked whether the Pittam is identical with the elemental fire, or is something other than that The question may answered by stating that the Pittam is the same fire.

english_text: The stomach (Amashaya), which is the seat of Kapham, occupies the same position as regards its location that Pittam as the sun holds in relation to that the moon. And since the stomach (Amashaya; situated above the pancreas (Pittashaya^-, and is endowed with a property (cooling) contrary to the primary virtue (heating) of Pit- tam, and, since the heat emitted by the receptacle of Pit- tam is naturally radiated in an upward direction, the four kinds of food, brought in to the stomach (Amashaya), are boiled and transformed into a soft placid mass (chyme;, like rice boiled a bowl full of water placed over a Chap. XXI. SUTRASTHA'NAM. 1 99 burning oven. The food, thus brought down into the stomach, easily moistene'd, disintegrated and digested coming into contact with the oily secretions of the stomach (Amdshaya). IVIetrical Texts :—The Kapham originated through the sweet, slimy, watery, exudating character of the food brought into the stomach (Amashaya) and hence the Kapham becomes endued with similar attributes. The Kledakam :—The Kapham, even though principally located the stomach, permeates its four other distant localities with its peculiar watery humid essence in virtue of its inherent attributes. The Avalamvaka :—The Kapham, located the region of the chest, protects the joints of the arms, the neck and the sternum, and enables the heart to perform its natural functions with the help the lymph-chyle derived from the assimilated food and its own intrinsic potency. The Vodhakam :—The Kapham, situated the throat and at the root of the palate, lends its aid to the perception tastes by maintaining the moist humid character of the tongue. The Tarpakam :—The Kapham, situated the head, cools and bathes the different sense organs THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXi. with its own humid essence, virtue its natural humid attributes. The Shimcshakam :—The Kapham, situated the joints, keeps them firmly united, protects their articulation and opposes their separation and disunion. Metrical Texts :—The Kapham white, heavy, oily, slimy and cool. its normal state, possessed sweet taste, which followed saline one in its reactionary transformation (chemical reaction when deranged or vitiated. Seats of blood :—The seats blood are in the liver and the spleen, as stated before, whence helps its other receptacles serve their proper functions. Metrical Texts :—The blood red, oily glossy, a little warm, and is possessed of attribute similar to something of a sweet taste. It is heav)', and it emits a fleshy smell and resembles the Pittam in its reactionary process, other words, those factors, which derange the Pittam, vitiate the blood as well. These are the locations the deranged humours, which are respectively accumulated in them on account of the aforesaid causes. The deranged humours exhibit such symptoms as, fullness and stuffedness of the abdo- men, or of any of the viscera (due to the action of the Chap. XXI. SUTRA8THANAM.

english_text: deranged Vayu yellowness of the affected part (due the action of the deranged Pittam , and diminution of the bodily heat, heaviness of the limbs, and sense of languor .^due to the action of the diseased Kapham), and natural repugnance for causes (factors) which lead their respective aggravations or accumulations. The medical treatment should be commenced soon as the symptoms, peculiar to their accumulation, would become manifest. Humours and their aggravations :— Xow we shall enumerate the causes which agitate and (aggravate) the deranged humours. The bodily Vayu aggravated by such factors (conduct, practices and diet, etc.) wrestling with wrestler superior strength, violent gymnastic exercises, sexual excesses, excessive study, a headlong plunge into water or a leap from inordinate height, running, a violent pressing blow, leaping over ditch, a bounding gait, swimming, keeping of late hours, carrj'ing of heavy loads, excessive riding, walking long distance and the partaking food into the composition which pungent, astringent, bitter, light or parchifying articles, sub- stances of cool potency, largely enter. Diets consisting dried pot-herbs, Vallura, Varaka, Uddalaka, Kara- dusha, Shyamaka, Xiv^ra, Mudga, Masura, Adhaki, Harenu, Kalaya, and Nishpava tend aggravate the bodily V^yu. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. XXI. Fasting, unequal irregular meals, over-eating, voluntary suppression of urine, semen, and tears, or of the mucous secretions from the nose fluent coryza, forced stoppage defecation, eructation sneezing are the factors, which may set down as the aggravating causes of the bodily Vayu. Metrical Text :—The bodily Vayu is naturally aggravated cold, cloudy or windy day, in winter, during the rains, the morning and evening and especially at the close of digestion. Symptoms of aggravated Pittam :— The Pittam is aggravated by anger, grief, fear, fatigue, fasting, acid transformation (reaction) of the assimilated food, deficient gastric digestion, unnatural sexual indulgence, partaking of a food consisting pungent, acid or saline, keen, heat making or light substances, well those whose digestion followed reactionary acidity. aggravated by the use sesamum oil, or of sesamum paste. Kulattha, Sarshapa, Atashi, the pot-herbs known Haritaka, fish, the flesh a Godha or goat or mutton may lead to its aggravation, if taken iniudiciously. Similarly, the use curd, whey, Kurchika, spissated milk), Sauviraka, different kinds wine, Amla-phala (sour fruits), or Katvara i.curd mixed with oil) and excessive exposure to the sun, may be followed by the same consequences. Chap. XXI. SUTRASTHA'NAM. Metrical Texts :—In addition all these, the Pittam is spontaneously and abnormally aggravated summer, autumn, noon, mid-night and during the process digestion, well the partaking of hot or warm substances. Symptoms the deranged pham :—The deranged Kapham aggravated sleep in the day time, the following lazy sedentary habits.

The use food grains, which are called the Hayanaka, the Yavaka, the Naishadha, the Itcata, the Masha, the Mahamasha, the Godhuma, the Tilam, or of rice cakes ma}-- lead to its aggravation. Curd, milk, the Krishara, the Payasha (sweetened rice porridge^ the various preparations of cane-sugar are things which produce the same result. The flesh beasts and birds that are aquatic in their habits or live in swampy lands, as well as lard, have the same effect, used as food. The use bulbs and lotus stems Kasheruka, Shringataka, Madhura-phala, Valli- phala as well eating before digestion the par- taking of food consisting of both wholesome and wholesome substances may aggravate this bodily humour. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXI. lYIetrlcal Texts :—The Kapham naturally and spontaneoush' aggravated the morning and evening, in Hemanta, and specially Spring, and just after a meal. Likewise, it is aggravated by the use of cold food or drink, etc. Symptoms the aggravated blood:—[Owing to a natural similarity between blood and the Pittam, and through a natural affinity between their attributes], causes, which tend aggravate the deranged Pittam, tend to aggravate or agitate the blood well. Moreover, frequent meals or repeated use of food, into the composition of which cool, liquid and heavy substances largely enter, are followed by a dis- turbed or aggravated condition of the blood. Sleep the day time, anger, exposure the glare of the sun fire, over- fatiguing labour, an external blow, ingestion of indigestible or incompatible substances, and eating before the full digestion of previous meal, may well be set down causes which tend aggravate blood. IVIetrical Texts:—As the bodily humours are never aggravated independently of the blood, their aggravation goes together with a disturbed or agitated condition of the blood. The aggravated condition of the humours gives rise to pain and moves the wind A'ayu) the bowels further occasions acid eructations, thirst, burning sensations, aversion food, vomiting Chap XXI.] and nausea. Any of these symptoms should be regard- ed as the second occasion which calls for medical aid. Expansion the deranged mours :—Now shall describe the expansion (Prasaram) of the deranged humours. The deranged humours, aggravated b}- the above mentioned causes, expand and overflow the limits their respective localities the same manner cakes, soaked any ferment enzyme and kept standing over night, ferment and rise through the acquisition new and unseen attributes. The V^yu, which possessed of locomotion or extreme mobility, should looked upon the cause their expansion or over-flowing.

XXI. the bladder), Mutrakrichchhra (stricture of the urethra) and Mutraghata 'retention urine), and diseases affecting the renal secretion, etc. Restricted the penis they tend to bring in syphilis, Xirudha-prakasha (phymosis and the local inflammatory diseases known the Shuka-dosha, etc. Similarly, lodged the region of the anus, these deranged and expanded humours beget fistula in ano, hccmorrhoids and polypus growths about that locality. Confined the region the scrotum, they give rise to hydrocele and other types of scrotal tumours, etc. Restricted the region above the clavicles, these humours originate diseases peculiar to that locality, while erysipelas, cutaneous affections (Kushtha . and other minor diseases supervene, when they restrict themselves to the flesh and the skin (lymph- chyle) and blood. Affecting only the fat, these humours tend to originate Granthi (Aneurism), Apachi (scrofula), Arvuda (tumour', Galaganda (goitre) and Alaji (inflammation of the eye at the edge of the cornea.* Lodged the lower extremities, they bring on elephantisis, Vata-Rakta kind leprosy Vata- Kantaka, etc. Permeating the whole organism, they irive rise such diseases fever, SarAangaroga, etc. which invade the entire system. * Additional text:—Reaching down and confined in the bone -systems of ihe body, ihey produce Vidradhi (abscesses), Anushayi, cte. Chap. XXI. The aggravated and expanded humours, thus firmly ensconced in the different parts of the body, exhibit the premonitor}'' symptoms diseases which will be fully dealt with under their respective heads. The manifestation of these premonitory symptoms should considered the fourth occasion for medical treatment. Disease- Its Development :— Now shall deal with the full development manifestation of a disease.The full manifestation of a disease, such as a swelling, tumour, aneurism (Granthi), Vidradhi (abscess) and erysipelas (Visarpa) etc., fever or dysentery, signifies the complete development of the characteristic symp- toms, which should be regarded as the fifth occasion for medical treatment. The sixth occasion for the calling in of medical aid should be considered to have arisen when swelling (abscess, tumour, etc.) would burst and exhibit the charac- teristic symptoms open ulcer. persistent lingering or continuance of a fever or dysentery, etc., should be considered as marking, or forming one of its particular stages, and which may run into one of an incurable type, if neglected or not sufficiently cared for at the outset. chapter: Chapter XXII: Now shall discuss the chapter, which treats secretions from boils ulcers different types. (Vranasra'va-Vijna'niaya- madhya'yam). A boil or an ulcer has its seat generally one the eight following components principles the body such as, the bone, the skin, the flesh, the veins, the ligaments, the joints, the viscera and the Marmas (vital parts of the body). boil ulcer of any type may crop appear in any one of the above mentioned localities. A boil or an ulcer, which confined onl)' the skin, readily yields medical treatment, while the remaining types, as well as those, which spontaneoush' suppurate and bursty are hard cure. A boil or an ulcer usually assumes a shape which is either diffused^ rectangular, spheroidal triangular while those, which are irregular or indefinite in shape, (or have forms other than the preceding ones , should looked upon as belonging to types which can cured only with the utmost difficulty. Any Vrana (burst incised abscess) in a patient, who observes a strict regimen, and who, from the outset, is placed under the medical treat- ment of an experienced physician (surgeon), will speedily healed while an ulcer, affecting a person
english_text: tion (Sanchaya), disturbance or aggravation rPrakopa}, expansion ( Prasaram), and differentiating traits of the deranged humours (Bheda), and is well conversant with the specific localities in which they are respectively confined in the course of their expansion (Sthana- samshrayam), and with the symptoms which they respec- tively exhibit in connection with the incidental disease (Vyakti), is alone worthy of that epithet. The deranged humours, checked or subdued in their accumulating stage, fail to exhibit any further or subse- quent development, but, left unremedied, they gain in strength and intensity in the course of their further development. The humours, deranged either singly, or in couples, or in a triple combination as regards one or two of their virtues, push on, follow and blend with humours similarly deranged regards their qualities and com- binative numbers. The medical treatment in a case, where two or all (three) of the deranged humours are involved, consists in conquering the strongest one in the combination, but so as not to enrage or aggravate the minor or the weaker humours the group and specially so in a case Sannipata." * THl- cuniliinaiinii nfany iwnot ihc IkkIIIv liuiiKiuis wiih the viiiaied hlond niav liki'wisf Uc iiiicrpiL-tL-d signify SAnnip^tika (tiihuninuial) conil.iTiaticin. Chap. XXI. A concourse of deranged humours, affecting and pearing in a particular part of the body, is called a boil ulcer (Vrana) which derived from the root "Vri" cover and so called from the fact its covering particular part of the body from its leaving a cicatrix which remains the whole life-time of the patient. english_text: irregular habits and treated by a quack or an ignorant physician, will dcNelop into one of a malignant type, which can be healed only with the greatest difficulty, account of it becoming aggravated b}' the deranged bodily humours involved therein. Symptoms of Dushta-Vranas :—Malig- nant ulcers (Dushta Vranas) are known by the following indications :—They are either too narrow or too wide- mouthed. They feel either extremely hard or soft to the touch and present either raised elevated or a de- pressed aspect. They are of either a black or red, yellow white colour, and are characterised extremes of temperature. Exhibiting strange and unusual fea- tures, they are checkered with networks veins, hgaments, etc., and are filled with putrid and sloughing flesh and fetid pus. Indefinite and irregular shape, they are found exude sort of dirty, fetid pus, which runs into fissures and cavities, following oblique upward course. They have cadaverous look and smell and are characterised by extreme pain and burning sensation, attended with swelling, redness, itching and suppuration. Pustules crop up round these ulcers, which largely secrete vitiated blood, and linger unhealed for an inordinate length of time. These ulcers may be divided into six classes [accord- ing they are severally caused the deranged bodily humours fVayu, Pittam and Kapham), are ^^^ SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXli. due their concerted action '^Sannip^ta), to the effects blow (traumatic) vitiated blood.], and should be medically treated according to the nature of their respective exciting factors. Secretions from ulcers :—Now we shall describe the characteristic secretions from all types ulcers. Secretions from a contused or lacerated skin, well from an ulcer confined only to it), whether spontaneously bursting surgically opened, are thin and watery in their consistency. They are character- ised by a raw (fleshy) smell and yellowish colour. An ulcer, affecting the flesh, exudes slimy, thick and white secretion like clarified-butter. A copious quantity of blood flows out a vein recently cut, while the incidental ulcer, its suppurating stage, secretes a copious secretion, like water flowing out of a hydrant, which moreover detached, thin, pendent (ropy), and slimy its character and has a brown or frosty hue. ulcer, confined only to a ligament, secretes a sort of cold and thick secretion, like expectorated mucous, though sometimes marked with streaks blood. A bone, mjured, tractured, suddenly cracked by idiopathic causes (derangement of the bodily humours), loses its internal marrow and appears as if washed (loses its natural gloss\ It assumes the colour of an oyster shell, whereas the secretions from an ulcer, which seated in Chap. XXII. 1 SUTRASTHA'NAM. bone, are cold and marked b}^ streaks of Mood and lumps of marrow.

english_text: An ulcer, 'situated in an}' of the bone- joints, does not exude any secretion under pressure, but secretes a sort of slimy, pendent, frothy and blood- streaked pus, when the affected limb or part flexed, expanded, raised lowered, in running (moving about), sitting or standing erect, or at defecation. An ulcer, seated in the abdominal cavity (Koshtha), exudes secretion, which mixed with urine, fecal matter, pus blood, and thin or watery (serous) fluid. The secretions from an ulcer, affecting any vital part of the body, need not be separately described, such a part naturally involves the organic principles skin, flesh, etc. ; and hence ulcer, invading it, must necessarily exude secretion, which is peculiar to any of the aforesaid bodily principles (skin, flesh, etc.) that has become affected. The deranged V^yu makes the secretions from ulcer, seated of the seven abovesaid principles such as, the skin, flesh, veins, ligaments, bones, joints and the abdomen, respectively coarse, and rough the touch, brown, grey, frosty, or white like the cream of curd, and coloured like the washings of an alkali, like that of meat or paddy husks. Similarly, the action of the deranged Pittam should be inferred from the secre- tions assuming the colours Gomedha (a species bluish yellow agate;, that the urine 2,6 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XXII. a cow, or that of water saturated with the burnt ashes conch-shells that of Kashaya water that of the wine known the Madhvika that oil, according as the skin, flesh, etc. are respectiveh^ affected. The action the deranged blood, changing the nature of the secretions of ulcers in the seven above- sajd locations, identical with that of the deranged Pittam with the exception, that the secretions are characterised by an extremel}'' fishy smell. epidermic (confined onl}' the epidermis part) superficial ulcer the action the deranged Kapham manifests itself by imparting butter-like Kasisha (sulphate iron) colour the secretions. They have lard-like hue colour like that of rice paste, that of water tinged with sesamum, colour like that of the internal juice or water of a cocoanut, colour like that of hog's lard, according the flesh, vein, a ligament, bone joint is attacked. the other hand, through the combined action of all the three deranged humours the body (Sannipata), those secretions become coloured like the water tinged with the soakings sesamum seeds, the internal sap water of a cocoanut, or the juice of the Ervaruka or the transparent surface layer of rice gruel, or the washings of the Aruka fruit, or the water tinged with the fruits of the Priy^ngu, or like the liver or the Mudga pulse. Chap. XXII. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject ulcer, situated the cavity of the ab- domen and secreting exudation resembling paddy husks in colour, as well as one located the viscera of blood (spleen liver—Raktasha^'am) and exuding secretion like alkaline water, should deemed incurable.

english_text: Similarh', ulcer having its seat the cavity the stomach (Amashaya), the region the Trika, (articulation the clavicle with the intraclavicular notch) and exuding thin, watery secretion, coloured like the washings of Kalaya pulse, should regarded belonging to the same type (incurable). A physician should only take in hand the treatment of an ulcer-patient after having examined the abovesaid nature of the discharges. Pain and its character :- Now we shall describe all the different kinds pain, which are experienced the several types of Vrana (ulcers) described before. Vartaja pain :—Pains pricking, piercing, thrashing, cutting, expanding, gnawing, churning, shoot- ing, tingling, burning, breaking, bursting, pinching, uprooting, uplifting, quivering, aching different types, shifting, stuffing, benumbing, indurating, contract- ing, and pains spasmodic character are usually felt in ulcers. pain, which comes vanishes without any apparent cause, or is varied and shifting 'I'HE SUSHKUTA SAMHITA. f Chap. XXII. its character, should ascribed the effects the deranged Vayu. Pittaja pain :—A sensation of burning is felt in the ulcer accompanied sort of sucking pain. feeling of inhaling heat or vapour, and a burning sensa- tion running through the whole body, should be looked upon the resultant of the deranged Pittam. the same time the body seems had been strewn over with bits glowing charcoal. The heat (the temperature of the affected locality) shows steady rise, and pain like the one incidental to the application of alkaline water (caustic solution is experienced in the ulcer. Raktaja pain :—The pain and other specific features ulcer due the vitiated condition of the blood are identical with those developed one of the Pittaja type. chapter: Chapter XXIII: Now shall discuss the chapter which deals with the Prognosis of an ulcer (Kritya'kritya- Vidhi-madhya'yam). A boil or an ulcer appearing patient who young, muscular (in frame\ strong, or is possessed of an indomitable courage and fortitude, proves readily amen- able to healing measures and applications how much more so when one appears patient in whom all these four conditions simultaneously obtain. ulcer young patient speedily healed owing to the fresh and vigorous vitalizing principles of the body ; whereas the one, which appears in a person of strong and muscular build, finds a speedy and success- ful termination owing to the inability the incising instrument to cut deep into the hard and tough muscles of the affected part and to reach down, in any way destro}" the underl3nng veins and nerves, etc. A strong and vigorous patient can easily endure a considerable amount of burning pain, etc. and does not feel distressed strict regimen diet. A man of stupendous endurance and fortitude can sustain the fatigue and worry of even the most painful surgical operation. Accordingly, a boil or an ulcer, appearing in a patient of the above said description, is easily and speedil)' healed

Boils ulcers, which appear in the regions of the buttocks (Sphik), or about the anus, and the organs of generation, the back, forehead, cheek, lips, the region the external ears, the testes the abdomen, the cavity of the mouth, about the nape the neck, above the clavicles, can easily healed. Those, that are seated in the eyes, the gums, the nostrils or the exterior angle of the e3^e, or in the cavity of the ears, abdomen or the umbilicus, or about any suture of the body, hips, ribs, arm-pits, chest, breasts, sides, or the joints, as well as those, that secrete frothy blood or pus with a gurgling sound, or contain any foreign matter em- bedded in their inside, are healed only with the greatest difihcult3\ Similarl}^ an abscess ulcer appearing in the nether region the body and pointing upward, the one appearing on the extremity of scalp (Romanta) or about the end finger-nail, an)" of the vulnerable parts the body, well the one affecting either of the thigh bones (femurs), should looked upon equally hard cure. Likewise abscess ulcer affecting bone the pelvis 'Shronikanda- Acetabulum), well fistula THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap, xxill. ano opening inward should regarded hard to cure. Authoritative verse the sub- ject :—An ulcer (Vrana) appearing in a leper (Kushthi person suffering from diabetes iMadhu- meha from Shosha (lit : pulmonary- consumption) from the effects poison, well the one appearing in a pre-existing ulcer, should be looked upon as curable only with the greatest difficulty.

english_text: Ya'pya ulcers :--An ulcer incidental to, and affecting the seat any the following diseases, viz. Avap^thika ( paraphimosis Niruddha-Prakash (phimosis), Sanniruddha-guda (constriction the anus), Jathara abdominal-dropsy), or Granthi (glandular inflammation), and characterised the germination parasites its interior, well the one appearing in the cavity the abdomen affecting the mucous linings of the intestines, or brought about by the corrosi>'e secretions nasal catarrh (Pratishyaya), and infested with parasites, should considered as onh- admitting of a palliative treatment. Similarly palliation is the only remedy the case an ulcer which appears in a patient suffering from any morbid secretion from the urethra (Prameha) from any form of cutaneous affections, marked by worms in its inside. Likewise case gravel Sharkara urinary Chap. XXIII. SUTRASTHA'NAM. calculi Shikata ) in which the urine found charged with concretions, or leaves a deposit of sandy sediment, can not be radicalh' cured by medicine alone. A case of V^ta-kundalika, Asthila, Upakusha, Kantha- saluka, Danta-sharkar^, Danta-veshta, ^'isarpa, Asthi- kshata, Uru-kshata, or Vrana-Granthi, may not perfectly yield to medicine alone. In an inflammation of the gums resulting from the use of poisonous twigs brushes for teeth Xishkoshana) a temporary amelioration all that can expected from good and efficient treatment. IVIetrical texts :— In patient neglecting disease its preliminary stage, otherwise not observing a strict regimen) even a curable malady ma}' speedily develop into one which admits only pallia- tive measures, while a disease of the last named type is soon transformed into incurable one. curable disease under the circumstances speedily finds a fatal termination. A patient laid up with a disease, which only admits of a palliative treatment, lives so long as the course of the medical treatment is continued, and will die almost simultaneously with its discontinuance. Just as a prop or a pillar can prevent the collapse tumbling edifice, palliative measures, judiciously^ applied skilful physician, may keep off the inevitable in a disease which knows no radical cure. Incurable diseases :—Now we shall '^^^ SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap, xxili. scribe the types of diseases which are usually held as in- curable. ulcer (\'rana) cropping up like fleshy tumour, painful and containing pus in its inside, and which is characterised by a copious secretion, with its edges raised like those of the genital of a mare, should be understood as belonging to the incurable type. condylomatous (papillomatous ) ulcer which is soft and raised like the horn cow, the one which moderately raised or elevated at its base, and secretes an exudation of vitiated blood, or a thin slimy secretion, should likewise regarded incurable. ulcer with an embossed or heaved up centre, and one dipped or fissured at its extremity should be regarded as past all remedy.

english_text: An ulcer covered over with shreds of ligaments, and looking if studded with loose shreds of hemp, should given incurable. Similarly, ulcer due to the deranged condition of any of the funda- mental humours, and secreting an exudation composed coagulated blood, fat, marrow and brain-matter should be deemed incurable. Likewise, an ulcer, in a weak and emaciated person, which is located within the cavity of the abdomen, (Koshtha: and which assumes either a black or yellow- ish colour, and exudes a secretion composed urine, pus, blood and fecal matter, which finds its outlet both through the upward and downward fissures of the body (the mouth and the anus) making a rumbling, gurgling Chap. XXIII. SUTKASTHANAM. sound, or which simultaneously secretes pus and blood through both the channels, should be regarded as belong- ing to the incurable class. An ulcer emaciated patient, which is situated either on the head or in the throat, and which is narrow-mouthed and tra\ersed network capillaries, and studded with fleshy or papillomatous eruptions, should be regarded as incurable. A distinctly audible sound report heard these ulcers which are found to be charged with wind. ulcer emaciated patient, which secretes blood and pus, and is attended with indigestion, cough, painful respiration and non-relish for food, well as a case fractured skull, attended with cough, dyspnoea, secretion of brain-matter, and symptoms peculiar to the concerted action of the three deranged humours of the body, should be given up as past all remedy. Authoritative verses the sub- ject traumatic ulcer, which exudes secretion of fat, marrow brain- matter, may prove amenable to medical treatment, whereas a humoural ulcer under the circumstance will prove incurable. ulcer appearing an}' part of the body other than a vital one (Marma), and which is found to invade its successive elements though without affecting any vein, bone, joint, etc. should be regarded incurable. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap, xxili. Just as a tree, that has grown old and that has spread its roots deep into the soil, can not be uprooted, so a disease can not be eradicated that has gained in strength and maturity with the process of time, and that has gradually invaded the different essential principles the body. disease, which, having been neglected at the outset, has run one of lingering or persistent type invading the successive elements of the bod}', and has thereby gained strength and intensity, baffles medicines, tested and marked eflicac)^, just as malignant astral combinations tend nullify potent incantations. Symptoms cleansed Fiealthy ulcers ulcer, not belonging any of the above said types, may prove easily amenable to the curative efficacies of medicines. In other words, an ulcer of recent origin is easily uprooted like a tender sapling of recent gi^owth.

english_text: An ulcer, which is unaffected by any of the three deranged bodily humours, and which assumes a dark brown hue along its edges, and is characterised the absence any pain, pustular eruptions secretions, and which of an even of an equal elevation throughout its length, should be regarded cleansed (asepsised healthy), and divested all morbid matter or principle (Shuddha-Vrana). Symptoms Healing Ulcers:—An ulcer, which is dove-coloured (yellowish dusky), and is Chap. XXIII. SUTRASTHA'NAM. not lardaceous at its base, and is further characterised by the absence of any muco-piirulent secretion along its margin, and which has become hard and surrounded shreds dead skin, and presents symptoms healthy granulation, should be looked upon course of healing. Symptoms of Healed Ulcers:—An ulcer, with its edges firmly adhered and characterised by the absence of any pain and swelling and not appearing knotty or glandular to the touch and that has left cicatrix of the same hue with the surrounding skin, should be considered as perfectly healed. Causes, such as mental excitements, as excessive grief and ecstacies of joy, anger or fright, as well as an exter- nal blow, or excessive physical exercise, or an abnormal excitation any the deranged humours, impaired digestion, may tend reopen ulcer recently adhered and healed. Accordingly such acts and conditions should be avoided by an ulcer-patient. Thus ends the twenty-third Chapter the Sutrasth^nam the Sushruta Samhita, which treats of the prognosis of ulcers. C H A P i E R XXIV. Now we shall discourse on the Chapter^ which deals with the classification diseases according their specific nature (Vya'dhi-Samudcihcshiya- madhya'yam). Diseases may grouped under two broad sub- divisions, such Surgical, and Medical, that is those that yield to the administration of purgatives, emetics, oils, diaphoretics, and unguents. The use or administration of medicated oils and unguents, etc., is not prohibited in surgical disease, while case, which exclusively medicinal its character, does not admit of the adoption of any surgical remedy. Onl}^ general outline the nature and S3miptoms all diseases will be found to have been briefly laid down in the present work. This work includes within its scope subject matters which have been fully dealt with other books (having only general bearing upon all the several branches of the science of medicine). It has been stated before that anj^thing that afflicts either the body the living personality—self, both, called disease. This pain affliction Chap. XXIV. either physical* its character (Adhyat- mikam), due disturbance the physical environments of a man (Adhibhautikam), the acts God (Adhidaivikam) etc. This three- fold pain may ultimately transformed into the seven kinds of diseases such as, the Adi-vala-pravritta, Janma-vala-pravritta, Dosha-vala-pravritta, Sanghata- vala-pravritta, Kala- vala-pravritta, Daiva-vala-pravritta and Svabhava- vala-pravritta.

SUTRASTHA'NAM. not lardaceous at its base, and is further characterised by the absence of any muco-piirulent secretion along its margin, and which has become hard and surrounded shreds dead skin, and presents symptoms healthy granulation, should be looked upon course of healing. Symptoms of Healed Ulcers:—An ulcer, with its edges firmly adhered and characterised by the absence of any pain and swelling and not appearing knotty or glandular to the touch and that has left cicatrix of the same hue with the surrounding skin, should be considered as perfectly healed. Causes, such as mental excitements, as excessive grief and ecstacies of joy, anger or fright, as well as an exter- nal blow, or excessive physical exercise, or an abnormal excitation any the deranged humours, impaired digestion, may tend reopen ulcer recently adhered and healed. Accordingly such acts and conditions should be avoided by an ulcer-patient. Thus ends the twenty-third Chapter the Sutrasth^nam the Sushruta Samhita, which treats of the prognosis of ulcers. C H A P i E R XXIV. Now we shall discourse on the Chapter^ which deals with the classification diseases according their specific nature (Vya'dhi-Samudcihcshiya- madhya'yam). Diseases may grouped under two broad sub- divisions, such Surgical, and Medical, that is those that yield to the administration of purgatives, emetics, oils, diaphoretics, and unguents. The use or administration of medicated oils and unguents, etc., is not prohibited in surgical disease, while case, which exclusively medicinal its character, does not admit of the adoption of any surgical remedy. Onl}^ general outline the nature and S3miptoms all diseases will be found to have been briefly laid down in the present work. This work includes within its scope subject matters which have been fully dealt with other books (having only general bearing upon all the several branches of the science of medicine). It has been stated before that anj^thing that afflicts either the body the living personality—self, both, called disease. This pain affliction Chap. XXIV. either physical* its character (Adhyat- mikam), due disturbance the physical environments of a man (Adhibhautikam), the acts God (Adhidaivikam) etc. This three- fold pain may ultimately transformed into the seven kinds of diseases such as, the Adi-vala-pravritta, Janma-vala-pravritta, Dosha-vala-pravritta, Sanghata- vala-pravritta, Kala- vala-pravritta, Daiva-vala-pravritta and Svabhava- vala-pravritta.
