chapter: Chapter XXIII: Now shall discuss the chapter which deals with the Prognosis of an ulcer (Kritya'kritya- Vidhi-madhya'yam). A boil or an ulcer appearing patient who young, muscular (in frame\ strong, or is possessed of an indomitable courage and fortitude, proves readily amen- able to healing measures and applications how much more so when one appears patient in whom all these four conditions simultaneously obtain. ulcer young patient speedily healed owing to the fresh and vigorous vitalizing principles of the body ; whereas the one, which appears in a person of strong and muscular build, finds a speedy and success- ful termination owing to the inability the incising instrument to cut deep into the hard and tough muscles of the affected part and to reach down, in any way destro}" the underl3nng veins and nerves, etc. A strong and vigorous patient can easily endure a considerable amount of burning pain, etc. and does not feel distressed strict regimen diet. A man of stupendous endurance and fortitude can sustain the fatigue and worry of even the most painful surgical operation. Accordingly, a boil or an ulcer, appearing in a patient of the above said description, is easily and speedil)' healed
english_text: A'di -vala-pravritta :—The disease termed Adi-vala-pravritta ascribed any inherent defect in the semen or the ovum of one's parent, which forms one of the original and primary factors of " being " and includes leprosy (Kushtham), hemorrhoids, phthisis etc. This type may divided into two subdivisions, according as the disease is generated by the deranged paternal or maternal factor at the time of incubation. J anma-vala-pravritta :—The Congenital the Janma-vala-pravritta type usually follows such causes improper conduct the part the mother during the period of gestation, etc., and embraces such defects maladies (congenital) blindness, deafness, dumbness, nasal-voice, and such monstrous aberrations of nature as congenital cretinism, * Certain commentators interpret the term "Atman" in "AdhyStmikam" to mean body only, and accordingly designate all phenomena that may manifest in the body as AdhyAtmikam, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXIV. and the births dwarfs and pigmies. This type, its turn, admits of two sub-divisions, according the disease is due to the action of the deranged lymph- chyle (Rasa-krita), ungratified desire of the mother during gestation, her gratification any improper longing conduct during pregnancy (Dauhridyam). Dosha-vala-pravritta -.—The Dosha-vala- pravritta (idiopathic) type due the action any of the fundamental bodily humours deranged by an improper diet, or resulting from the dynamical energies of the mind, such (Rajas and Tamas, etc). This type may be classified under two sub-heads, according as the disease is found to have its origin in the Amashaya (stomach), or in the Pakvashaya (intestines), and each of these again may further divided into two main sub-divisions such as the physical and the mental. The three preceding kinds of diseases include within their category disorders which are called mental or psychical (Adhyatmikam). Samghala-vala-Pravritta :—The Trau- matic type (Samghatha-vala-pravritta) includes diseases that are caused external blow are due to wrestling with antagonist superior strength. They may sub-divided into minor divisions, according the disease due external wound, the bite from any fierce beast Chap. XXIV. SUTRASTHA'NAM. poisonous reptile, etc. These types belong to the Adhi- bhautikam t5'pe, (brought about physical causes). Ka'Ia-vala-pravritta:—The Periodical type (K^la-vala-pravritta) includes diseases that are brought about by the variation of atmospheric heat or humidity with the change of the seasons, and admits of being grouped under two different sub-heads, according as the seasons, which usher these changes exhibit natural or contrary features. Daiva-vala-pravritta :—The Providential ('Daiva-vala-pravritta^ type includes diseases that are the embodiments of curses, divine wrath displeasure, are brought about through the mystic potencies of charms and spells, as described the Atharva-Veda.

english_text: This type may be divided into two minor divisions according the disease is due to such acts of God as when man is struck by lightning, etc., or the malignant influences of demons and monsters, and these may further grouped under two main sub-heads, according as the disease assumes a contagious character (epidemic), or purely accidental, and restricts itself isolated cases (sporadic). Svabha'va-vala-pravritta:—The Natural the Spontaneous (Svabhava-vala-pravritta) type includes such natural organic phenomena THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XXIV. decrepitude, death, hunger, thirst, sleep, etc. These phenomena are either Kalakrita (timely) Akalakrita (untimely). They are called Kalakrita when the}^ occur at the proper time persons who strictly observe the rules of health, and Akalakrita, when they appear the improper time (morbid premature) as the effects of unhealthy living. These diseases belong to the Providential or Adhi-daivikam typet. Thus we have classified diseases into their several types. The deranged bodily humours such as, Vayu, Pittam and Kapham should looked upon as the primary sources of all diseases, inasmuch as symptoms charac- teristic of each of them may be detected in the case of a disease whatsoever type, (which usually abates with their corresponding subsidence), and also because the Shastras have ascribed to them the fatherhood of all maladies that assail the human frame. As the three qualities of Sattva, Rajas and Tamasi are inherent and inseparable from, all the pheno- menal appearances in the universe whicli are, in reality, Accord. ng certain aulhorilies "Death" may also mean death of tissues. Several authorities on the other hand include such diseases thirst, hunger etc., within the Adhy^tmika class inasmuch as they are but the indications the want certain vital principles the body and appear the mental plane (Adhy^tmika) only longings for water, food, ttc X The Sattva :— Illuminating psychic principle. Rajas :—Prin- ciple of Action and Co-hesion. Tanias -.—Principle of Nescience or Illusion. Chap. XXIV. SUTRASTHA'NAM. but modifications of their own qualities, the three fundamental bodily hum'ours underlie the root of, and run through, the course of all known forms bodily distemper. The deranged bodily humours (Dosha" contact with the different elements, Dhathu) and excrements (Mala) of the body, together with the difference their locations and pathological effects, give rise the different forms of disease.* The nomenclature of a disease depends upon where the affection of the several elementary principles of the body by the deranged bodily humours lies, and which is accordingly styled as it is seated in the lymph- chyle, or in the blood or the flesh, or it is in fat, bone, or in the semen.

english_text: Rasaja Distempers :—Distempers such aversion and loss of relish for ' food_, indigestion, aching in the limbs, fever, nausea and a sense of reple- tion even without food, heaviness of the limbs, diseases affecting the heart, jaundice, constriction of any in- ternal passage of the body (Margo-parodha), emaciation of the body ^cachexia), bad taste the mouth, weak feelings in the limbs, premature whiteness and falling off of the hair, and s)^mptoms indicative of senile decay, should be regarded as having their seat in the deranged l5"mph- chyle (Rasa). This answers the question, "how can the deranged bodily hmnours bring about a disease of the Adhi-vala-type—a disease which is specifically due to the derangement of the innate and primary factors of life." THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XXIV. Raktaja Diseases :— Maladies such as Kush- tha (cutaneous affections in general Visarpa (erysipe- las), Pidaka (pustular eruptions, Mashaka, Nilika, Tilakalaka (specks), Nachhya (tans), Vyanga (stains), Indralupta alopecia), enlarged- spleen, Vidradhi scess*, Gulma abdominal glands), Vata-shonita kind of leprosy), Arsha (piles), Arvuda (tumours), aching of the hmbs^ menorrhagia, h£emopt3'sis, etc. well suppuration in the regions the anus and the penis should deemed having their origin the blood Raktaja contaminated by the deranged bodily humours. IVSa'nsaj a- Diseases :—Similarly Adhi-mansa, Arvuda, Arsha, Adhi-jihva, Upa-jihva, Upakusha, Gala-sunthika, Alaji, Mansha-sanghata (condylomatous growth), Astha-prakopa, Gala-ganda, Garjda-mala (scrofula), etc. should regarded diseases having their seat in the flesh, vitiated by the deranged bodily humours. IVledaja- Distempers :— Diseases, such Granthi, \'riddhi, Gala-ganda, Arvuda, and Ostha-prakopa are due to tlie action of the deranged fat. Madhu-meha (diabetes), obesitv and abnormal diaphoresis, etc. should regarded having their origin the humour- deranged fat of the body. Asthija- Disease :— Adhyasthi, Adhi-danta, Asthi-toda, Asthi-shula and Ku-nakha, etc. are the Chap. XXIV. diseases which should be regarded the outcome the deranged bodily humours affecting the bones. IVIaJJadoshaJa Diseases :—The vanishing of sight, epileps}'; vertigo, conjunctivitis and the appear- ance broad-based ulcer about the Parva- sthanam and a sense heaviness the thighs and knee-joints should be regarded as having their seat the deranged marrow. Shukra-doshaja :—Diseases such potency, entire aversion to sexual intercourse, Shukra- shmari fseminal concretions). Spermatorrhoea, and other seminal affections, should be regarded having their seat in the deranged semen. Cutaneous affections, constipation or looseness of the bowels, and diseases impeding or arresting the proper functions of the sense-organs or in an}" bringing about their aberrations, should be regarded as respec- tively located the receptacle of the faeces and the sense organs.

chapter: Chapter XXV: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which deals with the eight different forms of surgical operations (Ashtavidha-Shastra- Karmanya - ma- dhya'yam). Metrical Texts :—Bhagandara, Shlaishmika Granthi, Tilakalaka, Arvuda, Arsha, Charma-Kila, Jatumani, Mansa-Samgh^ta, Gala-Sunthik^, Valmika, Vrana-Vartma, Shataponaka, Adhrusha, Upadansha, Mansakandha, Adhimansaka, as well as ailments due to the lodgment of a foreign body in the flesh a bone, and a sloughing of ligaments, flesh or veins are the diseases in which incision i Chhed)^am should be made. Bhedyam : -Excision (Bhedyam) should resorted to in the following diseases, viz., Vidradhis, the three types of Granthi other than the Sannipatika one, Visarpa due either to the deranged Vayu, Pittam Kapham, Vriddhi, Vidarika, Prameha-pidaka, swellings general, diseases affecting the mammary organs, Avamanthaka, Kumbhika, Anushayi^ Nadi, the two types Vrinda, Pushkarika, Alaji, Kshudra-roga (all minor cutaneous pustular diseases), the three types of Puppata, Talu-puppata, and Danta-puppata Tundukeri, Gil^Cyu, and the diseases which are caused by suppuration in the local flesh or any soft part of the
english_text: Thus we have briefl}' enumerated the names diseases, the specific nature and symptoms of which will full)' discussed later on under their respective heads. Authoritative verse the Sub- ject :—The deranged and aggravated humours, freely coursing through the body, give rise to a disease at the :J36 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. xxiv. place which they are incarcerated owing obstruction in their natural passage. Now it may be again asked, whether the relation of a disease, such as fever, etc. with the deranged bodily humours is constant and inseparable, or otherwise. All human beings would danger perpetually falhng ill in the event of the said connection relation; being constant and unseparable but case of their separate existence, it but natural that their charac- terstic symptoms should separately manifest them- selves instead being simultaneously present with fever, etc. they are found to be in reality. And accordingly the theor)', that diseases (such as, fever, etc.) and the deranged bodily humours have separate existence, and are not pritna facie intimately co-related with one another falls the ground. the other hand, the assumption of their separate existence invalidates the incontestable conclusion, that diseases such as, fever, etc. are fathered by the deranged humours of the body. Hence it may be safely asserted that no disease can occur without the direct mediation intervention the deranged bodily humours. Yet the connection (relation) which exists between the two neither constant nor separable. As the physical phenomena of lightning, storm, thunder and rain can not happen independently the sky (cloud) and yet they Chap. XXIV. sometimes do or do not occur with the presence of a cloud again bubbles^ though reality but the modifications the underlying water, not swell its surface all times, the connection between a disease and the bodily humours is neither universally separable or inseparable. Authoritative verse the sub- ject shall describe the nature, intensity and quality of diseases with their complications and give the number their different t3'pes. Diseases [omitted to be mentioned in the chapter Xidanam (aetiology)] will be found fully dealt with in the sup- plementary part of the present work (Uttara-tantram). Thus ends the Iwenty-fourlh Chapter the SulrasthSnam the Sushruta SamhitA which treats of the classification of diseases according to their specific nature. CHAPTER XXV. And accordingly the theor)', that diseases (such as, fever, etc.) and the deranged bodily humours have separate existence, and are not pritna facie intimately co-related with one another falls the ground. the other hand, the assumption of their separate existence invalidates the incontestable conclusion, that diseases such as, fever, etc. are fathered by the deranged humours of the body. Hence it may be safely asserted that no disease can occur without the direct mediation intervention the deranged bodily humours. Yet the connection (relation) which exists between the two neither constant nor separable. As the physical phenomena of lightning, storm, thunder and rain can not happen independently the sky (cloud) and yet they Chap. XXIV. sometimes do or do not occur with the presence of a cloud again bubbles^ though reality but the modifications the underlying water, not swell its surface all times, the connection between a disease and the bodily humours is neither universally separable or inseparable. Authoritative verse the sub- ject shall describe the nature, intensity and quality of diseases with their complications and give the number their different t3'pes. Diseases [omitted to be mentioned in the chapter Xidanam (aetiology)] will be found fully dealt with in the sup- plementary part of the present work (Uttara-tantram). Thus ends the Iwenty-fourlh Chapter the SulrasthSnam the Sushruta SamhitA which treats of the classification of diseases according to their specific nature. CHAPTER XXV. Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which deals with the eight different forms of surgical operations (Ashtavidha-Shastra- Karmanya - ma- dhya'yam). Metrical Texts :—Bhagandara, Shlaishmika Granthi, Tilakalaka, Arvuda, Arsha, Charma-Kila, Jatumani, Mansa-Samgh^ta, Gala-Sunthik^, Valmika, Vrana-Vartma, Shataponaka, Adhrusha, Upadansha, Mansakandha, Adhimansaka, as well as ailments due to the lodgment of a foreign body in the flesh a bone, and a sloughing of ligaments, flesh or veins are the diseases in which incision i Chhed)^am should be made.

english_text: of the mammae), Vidarika, Shaushira, Gala- Shaluka, Kantaka, Krimi-dantaka worm-eaten teeth), Danta-veshta (inflammation of the gums), Upakusha, Shit^da, Danta-puppata, diseases of the lips originated through the action of the deranged blood, Pittam Kapham, and a variety of other diseases passing under the denomination of Kshudra-Roga (minor ailments\ Sccvyam :—Suturing rSeevya* should resorted the case open ulcer due the action the deranged fat after its vitiated contents (morbid matter) had been fully scraped out, well as in the case of an uncomplicated (curable"^ Sadya- Vrana (wound instant ulcer) any of the joints Chap. x.w. which are connected with the acts of movement or loco- motion. Conditions of Suturing:—An ulcer cidental to the application of fire (canter}'), or any kaline preparation (caustic), or treated with any poison- ous drug or substance, or from whose inside the bedded Shalyam (foreign matter) has not been removed, should not sewed without being thoroughly cleansed and purified asepsised = inasmuch as any foreign matter, whether a hair, nail or a particle of dust or bone, lying embedded in its cavity, might set up an abnormal suppuration, accompanied by extreme pain and excessive secretion. Hence such ulcers should thoroughly cleansed (and all foreign indigenous morbid matter should be extracted therefrom) before being sewed up. Mode of Suturing :—Then having pressed the ulcer into its proper position, should sutured with strings of any of the following kinds, viz. thin cotton thread, the fibres the Ashman- taka tree or hemp plants, the Atasi, Murva Guduchi, with strips leather, plaited horse- hair animal sinews, into any the officinal shapes suturing) known the Gophana, Tunna- Sevani and Riju-Granthi, etc. suited the shape and position of the ulcerated part. The margin the ulcer should gently pressed close with the fingers during suturing. round needle THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XXV. the length of two fingers' width should be used in sew- ing up an ulcer occurring about any joint or in a part of the body where the flesh is thin and scanty. A needle a triangular body tri-hedral), and measuring three fingers' width in length, is recommended in the case of ulcer appearing any flesh}' part the body. semi-circular bow-shaped needle should be used case where the seat of the ulcer would be found to be on the scrotum, or on the skin of the abdomen, or about any of the Marmas (vital parts). Needles of these three shapes should be so construc- ted as to be fitted with sharp points capable being handled with the greatest ease, having girth equal that of the stem of a Malati flower. The needle should not be pricked into a part too near, or too remote from the fissure, or the mouth of an ulcer, as there might be the danger of the suture being broken off (at the least pressure or movement) the first instance and genesis pain the second.

english_text: xAn ulcer, thus properly sutured, should covered over with cotton and dusted over with pulverised compound consisting the powders Priyangu, Anjanam, Yasthyahva and Rodhra, or with the ashes of a burnt piece of Kshauma cloth, or with the powders of the Shallaki fruit. Then the ulcer should be properly bandaged, and measures and rules regarding the regimen of diet, and conduct previously laid down in the chapter Chap. XXV. on the nursing of an ulcer- patient (Ch. XIX. ) should be adopted and observed. The eight kinds of surgical operations have thus been briefly described. They will be dealt with later the Chikitsitam. Defective Surgical Operations:—These eight forms of operations may be attended with dangers four different kinds such those arising from an insufficient over performance, from the slanting or oblique deviation (of the knife the instrument), or from act of self-injury the part the physician. A physician ( surgeon ; making a wrong operation on the body his patient either through mistake, through the want of necessary skill knowledge, out of greed, fear, nervousness haste, conse- quence ofbeing spurned or abused, should be condemned as the direct cause of many new and unforeseen maladies. A patient, with any instinct of self-preservation, would do well to keep aloof from such a ph\sician, or from one who makes a wrong injudicious application the cautery, and should shun his presence just as would shun a conflagration or a cup of fatal poison. On the other hand, a surgical operation, carried excess, (or a surgical instrument inserted deeper than what necessary attended with the danger THE SUSHRUTA SAiMHlTA. L Chap. xxv. cutting or destroying a vein, ligament, bone, joint, any vital part of the body. surgical operation an ignorant surgeon brings about, most cases, the instantaneous death of the patient, or consigns him the pangs of a life-long death. The symptoms which generally manifest themselves in connection with the injudicious hurting of any of the five vital parts principles of the body (such the joints, bones, veins, ligaments, etc.) are vertigo, delirium, loss of bodily functions, semi-insensibility ( comatose state), incapacity of supporting oneself, cessation mental functions, heat, fainting, looseness of the limbs, difficult respiration, excruciating pain or pain peculiar to the deranged Vayu, secretion of blood or a thin watery secretion like the washings of meat from the injured part, or the organ, with coma or inoperativeness all the senses.

chapter: Chapter XXVI: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which treats of the exploration of splinters lost or deep-seated in the organism (Pranashta-Shalya-Vijna'niya'- madhysryam). Definition :—The term Shalyam derived from the root "Shala" Shvala" (to go swiftly) joined to the Unadi affix "Yat." Shalyas may be divided into two kinds according as they are extrinsic Agantuka) or idiopathic (Sharira) in their origin. A Shalyam usually serves to act as an impeding obstructing agent to the entire organism, and, hence, the science which deals with its nature and characteristics is called the Shalya-Shastram (Surgery). An idiopathic (Sharira) Shalyam may be either a hair, nail, embohsed blood (Dhatus)*, etc., excrements (Mala), or deranged humours the body (Dosha), while extrinsic Shal3'am should be regarded one which afflicts the body and is originated from source other than any of the preceding ones, including particles iron and bone, stems of grass, scrapings of bamboo, and bits of horns, etc. But an Agantuka (extrinsic) Shalyam specifically denotes an article of iron, inasmuch as * Embolism and Thrombosis have been included within Shalyam by the Ayurvedic Pathologists

Similarly, an injured ligament gives rise to a crooked- ness or bending of, as well as to a gone feeling the Other than ihc one silualcd any the abovesaid vital parts the body. Chap. XXV. SUTRASIHA'NAM. injured limb or organ, attended with pain and loss function, and the incidental ulcer takes a long time to heal. An abnormal increase in the local swelling, together with excruciating pain, loss strength, breaking pain in the joints, and in-operativeness of the affected part, mark the wounding flexible or immovable joint. Similarly, in the case where bone is hurt or injured in the course of a surgical operation, the patient tormented with indescribable pain, and night, and finds comfort any position what- soever. Pain and swelling specifically mark the affected locality, and thirst and inertness of the limbs add to the list of his sufferings. A case of any injured Sira-Marma ( vital venal arterial combination plexus exhibits the same symptoms which characterise the hurting single vein, as previously described. Loss of actual perception (anaesthesia^ and a yellowish colour of the skin mark the case where the injury confined the vital principle of the flesh. A patient, who discreet, and not special hurry to end his earthly sojourn, would do well shun the presence bungling, unskilful surgeon, who can not even keep himself unhurt the course of a surgical operation. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. LChap. xxv. The evils, which attend the obhque insertion of a surgical instrument, have been described before ; and accordingly care should be taken not to leave any room for the occurence of those evils connection with surgical operation. The patient, who may mistrust his own parents, sons and relations, should repose an implicit faith in his own physician, and put his own life into his hands without the least apprehension of danger ; hence ph5'sician should protect his patient as his own begotten child. surgical case may yield to a single incision, or may require two, three, four or more than that number effect a cure. By doing good to humanity with his pro- fessional skill, a physician achieves glory, and acquires the plaudits of the good and the wise in this life, and shall live in Paradise in the next. Thus ends the twenly-fiflh Chapter of the Sulrasthimim in the Sushruta Samhita which tieats of the eight forms of Surgical operations. CHAPTER XXVI. The patient, who may mistrust his own parents, sons and relations, should repose an implicit faith in his own physician, and put his own life into his hands without the least apprehension of danger ; hence ph5'sician should protect his patient as his own begotten child. surgical case may yield to a single incision, or may require two, three, four or more than that number effect a cure. By doing good to humanity with his pro- fessional skill, a physician achieves glory, and acquires the plaudits of the good and the wise in this life, and shall live in Paradise in the next. Thus ends the twenly-fiflh Chapter of the Sulrasthimim in the Sushruta Samhita which tieats of the eight forms of Surgical operations. CHAPTER XXVI. Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which treats of the exploration of splinters lost or deep-seated in the organism (Pranashta-Shalya-Vijna'niya'- madhysryam).

chapter: Chapter XXVI: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which treats of the exploration of splinters lost or deep-seated in the organism (Pranashta-Shalya-Vijna'niya'- madhysryam). Definition :—The term Shalyam derived from the root "Shala" Shvala" (to go swiftly) joined to the Unadi affix "Yat." Shalyas may be divided into two kinds according as they are extrinsic Agantuka) or idiopathic (Sharira) in their origin. A Shalyam usually serves to act as an impeding obstructing agent to the entire organism, and, hence, the science which deals with its nature and characteristics is called the Shalya-Shastram (Surgery). An idiopathic (Sharira) Shalyam may be either a hair, nail, embohsed blood (Dhatus)*, etc., excrements (Mala), or deranged humours the body (Dosha), while extrinsic Shal3'am should be regarded one which afflicts the body and is originated from source other than any of the preceding ones, including particles iron and bone, stems of grass, scrapings of bamboo, and bits of horns, etc. But an Agantuka (extrinsic) Shalyam specifically denotes an article of iron, inasmuch as * Embolism and Thrombosis have been included within Shalyam by the Ayurvedic Pathologists

An idiopathic (Sharira) Shalyam may be either a hair, nail, embohsed blood (Dhatus)*, etc., excrements (Mala), or deranged humours the body (Dosha), while extrinsic Shal3'am should be regarded one which afflicts the body and is originated from source other than any of the preceding ones, including particles iron and bone, stems of grass, scrapings of bamboo, and bits of horns, etc. But an Agantuka (extrinsic) Shalyam specifically denotes an article of iron, inasmuch as * Embolism and Thrombosis have been included within Shalyam by the Ayurvedic Pathologists. ITHE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. 1 Chap xxvi. pre-eminently sqvvqs the purpose of killing and is the most irresistible of all metals. Since any amount sharpness can be imparted to the point of an article made iron and since can easily discharged from a distance, iron the metal exclusively chosen in the construction of darts or arrows. Classification of Shafts : — Arrows (Shara) may be divided into two classes according as they are feathered or unfeathered ; and their barbs are usually constructed in the shape trees, leaves, flowers, fruits, or are made to resemble the mouths of birds and wild and ferocious animals. Flights of arrows:—The flights direc- tions of an arrow (Shalyam) may divided into five different kinds, such as the upward, the downward, the backward (coming from the back), the oblique and the straight. Either through its diminished momentum, through any external resistance, an arrow may drop down and penetrate into the skin, arteries, any internal channel of the body, into any bone its cavity, causing a wound or an ulcer (Vrana) at the spot its penetration. Symptoms :—Now hear me describe the symp- toms which are exhibited in connection with an arrow- wound (Shalya*-Vrana). These symptoms may * An arrow or an iron barb, from "Shala" to kill. Chap. XXVI. grouped under two sub-heads, such as the specific and the general. The general characteristics are follows :—The ulcer, which marked pain and swelling and presents raised bloated aspect like a water bubble, assumes a dark brown hue and appears soft to the touch. The seat of the ulcer seen studded over with pustular eruptions and a constant bleeding sets in from its inside. The specific symptoms, which mark a Shalyam lodged in the skin, are the hard- ness and extended character of the local swelling and the darkness (discolouring) of its skin.

A flow of red and frothy blood with gurghng sound, accompanied thirst, nausea, and aching the limbs, sets when the arrow lodged artery (Dhamani). Similarly, pain and swelling of diverse kinds mark a case where the shaft embedded bone. The appearance goose flesh the skin, stuffed sensation inside the cavity of the affected bone, and violent piercing bone-ache, mark a case where the shaft has found lodgment inside the cavity of a bone. pierced joint exhibits the same symptoms described connection with arrow-lodged bone, with the exception that the patient incapable flexing and expanding the affected joint. case where the shaft (Shalyam) has lodged the abdomen (Koshtha), the bowels become constipated ; the abdomen becomes distended with a rumbling in the testines and the suppression of flatus and urine ; and ingested food matter, as well urine and feces are found to ooze out of the fissure or mouth of the ulcer. Symptoms, similar those above described, manifest themselves when the arrow lodged any of the vital parts (Marmas'i the body. The preceding symptoms are but faintly exhibited in a case of super- ficial penetration. An ulcer incidental to the penetration of an arrow Chap. XXVI.] (Shalyam"), along the direction of the local hair, in* the throat, in any internal channel of the body, or in a vein, the skin, or a muscle, or into a cavity of the bone, and not in any way affected by the action of the deranged bodily humours, may speedily and spontaneously heal but it may break open and become painful afresh the bodily humours become deranged and aggravated by a blow or physical exercise. Localisation :—The exact position of a shaft (Shalyam) embedded in the skin should ascertained by applying plaster composed of clay, Masha-pulse, Yava, Godhuma and cow-dung over the injured limb part. The part (hmb) should duly lubricated with oil, and diaphorised (by fomenting or applying heat to its surface) before the plaster is applied.

The embedded shaft (Shalyam) then exactly located the spot where, owing the heat of the affected part, the clarified butter, earth, sandal paste would found to have melted, or dried up. Similarly, the mode of localising a shaft (Shalyam), So as not to obstruct the coursing the blood serum the locality. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. ; Chap. XXVI. embedded in the flesh is as follows :—First, the patient should be duly lubricated and diaphorised with medi- cinal agents suited the requirements of his case. Then, the part or the limb having been thus reduced with depletive measures, the shaft would found have been dislodged from its seat and to moving about (^within the deeper tissues of the affected part giving rise to pain, redness and swelling. In such a case the exact location of the shaft should be fixed at the spot where the pain and swelling, etc. would occur. The same measures should be adopted in the case of a shaft (Shalyam) which lies embedded the cavity the abdomen (Kostha), or in a bone, or joint, or muscle the case of a Shalyam lodged in a vein, in an artery, any external channel (Srota) of the body, or in a ligament, the patient should be made to ride carriage with a broken or lopped oif wheel and dragged up and down in it on an undulating road The pain and swelling, etc. incidental to the jolting, would occur at that part his body, where the shaft (Shalyam} embedded. the case shaft Shalyam) lodged bone, the affected bone should lubricated and diaphorised with oil and heat respectively, after which it should be firmly pressed and bound up. The seat of the pain or swelling, caused by such a procedure, would mark the exact locality the embedded Shalyam. Chap. XXVI. Similarly, in the case of a shaft (Shalyam) lodged joint, the same lubricating, diaphorising, compressing, and expanding measures should be adopted, and the painful swelling caused thereby would indicate its exact locality.

english_text: definite method can laid down regards ascertaining the exact location of a Shalyam lodged in any of the vital parts of the body (Marma inasmuch as they are co- existing with (the eight different locations ulcers, such the skin_, the flesh, the bone, etc.)* General rule A painful swelling, occurring at any part of the body and incidental to such physical natural endeavours of the patient, riding on elephant or on horse-back, climbing a steep hill, bendr ing bow, gymnastic exercises, running, wrestling, walking, leaping, swimming, high-jumping, yawning, coughing, singing, expectorating, eructating, laughing, practising of Pranay^ma (regulating the breath prelimi- nary to the practice of Yoga), or an emission of semen, urine or flatus, defecation, would clearly indicate the exact location of the embedded shaft (Shalyam). Authoritative Verses on the Sub- ject :—The part of the body, which is marked by pain and swelling, or which seems heavy and is marked Accordingly measures enjoined to be adopted in connection with shaft (Shalyam) lodged in any one of them should be applied wufati.-: mutandis cases which these Marmas would be found to be similarly affected. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XXVi, by complete anaesthesia, or the part which the patient repeatedly handles, or constantly presses with his own hand, which exudes any sort of secretion, and marked by sort of excruciating pain, which he involuntarily withdraws from, constantly guards against imaginary painful contact), should regarded clearly indicative of the exact location of the embedded Shalyam. A physician, having tested with a probe the cavity of the incidental ulcer or the interior the affected locality, and found it to be characterised by little pain and absence of any aching discomfort unfavour- able symptoms and swelling, after a course of proper treatment, and after having been satisfied its healthy look and the softness of its margin, and after having ascertained that any remnant of the embedded arrow can not be perceived with the end of the director by moving it to and fro, should pronounce free from any embedded foreign matter (Shalyam), which would be further confirmed by the full flexion and expansion of the affected limb or organ. A particle of soft bone, horn iron, an}'wise lodged in the body, assumes an arched shape whereas bits of wood, grass-stems, chips of bamboo-bark, under the same circumstances, putrify the blood and the local flesh, if not speedily extracted from their seats 1 odgment. Bits of gold, silver, copper, brass, zinc, or Chap. XXVI. lead, anj'how inserted into a human organism, are soon melted the heat of the Pittam and are assimilated and transformed into the fundamental principles of the body. Metals or substances kindred softness, and which are naturally cold, are melted and become amal- gamated, under such circumstances, with the elements of the organism.

chapter: Chapter XXVII: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which deals with the modes of extracting sphnters (Shalyar- pa n iya- mad hyaryam) There are two kinds Shalyas. Shalya either loose firmly fixed to its seat within the body. We shall presently speak of the fifteen different modes of extracting a loose Shalyam, which are as follows, viz. Extraction by natural expulsive functions of the body (Svabhaba), by suppuration or putrefication (Pachanam), excising (Bhedanam), by bursting Daranam), pressing Pidanam), by rubbing i^Pramarjananr, b)'' blow- ing with the mouth of medicinal powders into the affect- part (Nirdhmapanam), the administration emetics (Vamanam), exhibition purgatives (Virechanam) by washing (Prakshalanam friction with the fingers (Pratimarsha), straining the time defecation (Pravahanam), sucking (Achushanam), by applying a magnet (Ayaskanta) and by exhilarating ; Harsham). embedded foreign matter usually expelled from the eyes, etc, by inducing lachrymation, sneezing, eructation, coughing, micturition, defecation, and the emission of flatus. Shalyam, or any other foreign matter which has
english_text: hair, particle of hard bone, wood, stone, bamboo scraping, cla}^ which remains lodged in the body Shalyam, does not melt, nor undergo any change or deterioration. The physician, who fully conversant with the five different courses or flights of an arrow 'Shalyam), whether feathered or unfeathered, and has minutely observed and studied the symptoms due to its lodg- ment in any of the eight different seats of ulcers (Vrana) in the human organism such as, the skin, etc.), is alone worthy of attending on kings and nobles. [Chap, xxvil. should evacuated administering purgatives (Virechanam). The pus or any other morbid matter found within the cavity of an ulcer should removed by washing while incarcerated flatus, obstructed scybala retained urine, or obstructed foetus, should be borne down and expelled by means of straining. Any deranged Vayu or watery secretion incarcerated anv part the body, as well as poisoned blood or vitiated breast-milk, should be sucked off with the mouth, or with a horn. A loose, unbarbed arrow, lodged a wound with broad mouth and lying an Anuloma direction, should withdrawn applying magnet its end. A shaft of grief, driven into the heart by any of the multifarious emotional causes, should be removed by exhilaration and merry-making. shaft (Shalyam), whether large small, may withdrawn from its place either the two ways known the Anuloma and Pratiloma. The Anuloma consists withdrawing Shalyam through a way other than that its penetration, while the contrary is called the Pratiloma. A Shalyam lodged place lying close the spot its penetration (Arvacheenam) should extracted through the wav bv which has entered Chap. XXVII. (Pratiloma). On the other hand, a shaft Shalyam, piercing deep into any part of the body, but not coming out by the other side (Par^cheenam), should drawn out through way other than that of its penetration (Anuloma). A shaft, piercing deep into any part of the body reach the other side the wounded hmb part, (but not cutting out clean through it owing the diminution its original momentum), and remaining protruded the heaved flesh, should extracted through a channel other than that which has originally penetrated (Anuloma), and by stirring striking with the hand hammer. The heaved up flesh should be opened with incision, when found possible of being opened, and the embedded Shalyam should be drawn out by stirring or striking it with the hand as laid down before. A Shalyam, lodged in any soft part of the abdomen, chest, arm-pits, inguinal regions or ribs, should not cut open or struck with hammer, but should be tried removed with the hands through the wa)- of its penetration (Pratiloma), in failure whereof the Shalyam should be extracted with surgical appliances (Shastra) or any other surgical instruments ( Yantras), Authoritative Verse on the Sub- ject patient, fainting away (during the course of such surgical operation), should be enlivened by 26o THR SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. : Chap. XXVli. dashing cold water over his face. He should be solaced with many a hopeful and cheering word, and a nourish- ing diet such as, milk, etc. should be given him, and his vital parts should be protected.

english_text: Then having extracted the Shalyam, the incidental wound or ulcer, the blood having been wiped of, should be fomented with lieat or by apph'ing warm clarified butter to its surface in the event of it being found fit to be so treated i.e., 'devoid of pain and unattended with further bleeding). Cauterisation should be resorted where the condition of the wound would indicate such a measure. After tliat, the wound should be plastered (Pradeha) with honey and clarified butter, and bandaged with a piece of clean linen and directions the diet and nursing the patient should given previously laid down). A Shalyam, lodged in a vein or a ligament (Snayu), should be extracted with the help probe. The shaft (Shalyam), lodged the body and lying buried under the incidental swelling, should extracted firmly tying blades Kuslia grass around its body. A shaft (Shalyam;, lodged in a spot situated anywhere close to the heart, should be withdrawn the way by which it has entered and the patient should enlivened with sprat's of cold water, etc. during the operation. Chap. XXVII. SUTRASTHA'N'AM. Shalyam, lodged any other part of the body and that is difficult to extract, and that produces pain and local inflammation, should be removed by cutting the part open. In the case of a shaft 1 Shalyam) which has pierced into the cavity of a bone, the surgeon should firmly press the affected bone with his legs, and pull out the embedded shaft with all his might by gripping with surgical instrument, failure whereof strong man should be asked firmly catch hold the patient, and the Shalyam should pulled out with the help of a gripping surgical instrument as before. As an alternative, the bottom of the shaft should tied the string of a bow, strung and fully bent down ; and the Shalyam should ejected with the means full twang. alternative, a horse should harnessed the fashion known the Panch^ngi-vandhanam (lit. bound in the five parts of the body), and the end of the Shalyam should be bent down and tied the bridle. Then the horse should so whipped as to raise its head first, thus pulling out the embedded shaft .Shalyam) from its seat of lodg- ment the jerk its head. As an alternative, high and tough bough of a tree should be lowered down and tied the bent end the shaft the preceding case. The bough should be then let loose, thus pulling out the shaft (Shalyam / with its rebounding force. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XXVII. A shaft (Shalyam), lodged in a bone and lying pro- truded the heaved up local flesh (situated in a place other than the inguinal regions, abdomen, or arm-pits, etc.), should stirred by striking it on the head with an Asthila a round stone,—a short hammer according to certain authorities), or with a stone or hammer, and should be taken out by the way of its penetration.

english_text: The feather of a barbed shaft, lying embedded bone situated at part the organism where the existence of such foreign matter calculated not to create any special discomfort, should be first crushed putting pressure the heaved or protruded flesh, and the shaft then should gently pulled out its seat of lodgment. the case of a bit of shellac being accidentally pricked into the pharynx, a metal tube should first inserted into the passage, and then a heated metallic rod should be reached down to the obstructing shellac through its inside. The shellac, thus melted by the heat of the inserted rod, would naturally stick fast it, which should be then condensed by an injection of cold water poured down through the aforesaid tube after that the rod should be withdrawn thus carrying away the melted shellac at its end. According to certain authorities, any other obstruct- ing foreign matter accidentally introduced into the pharynx should be withdrawn with the help of a rod, Chap. XXVII. SUTRASTHA'NAM. soaked in melted wax or shellac, and then inserted into that passage, all other procedure being the same the preceding instance. In the case of a bone Shalyam (such the bone fish etc.) having accidentally stuck fast the throat, bundle hair, tied to a string of thread, should inserted into the gullet of the patient, the physician holding the other end of the string in his hand. Then a copious quantity water, any other liquid substance, should be poured down into his throat, so as to entirely fill his stomach. After that some kind emetic should given to the patient, and the string should be pulled out soon the bundle of hair would be felt to have struck below the obstructing bone Shalyam, which would naturally come out with the pull. alternative, the top end of a soft twig, generally used in cleansing the teeth, should be bruised into the shape of a brush, and the thorn or the Shalyam should be removed with its help. The incidental wound should be treated by making the patient lick compound clarified butter and honey, of the powders of the Triphal^, saturated with honey and sugar. The body of the patient should be pressed or rubbed, or he should be whirled round by the ankles, or general- measures, calculated induce vomiting, should be adopted in a case where he would be found have THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXVIt. swallowed a stomachful of water (as in a case of drown- ing). As an alternative, he should be buried under the ashes up to his chin. Strong wine should be given the patient, should be slapped on the shoulders, so as cause him to suddenly start in a case where a morsel of food would be found to have obstructed and stuck fast in his gullet. A tight gripe about the throat of a person with a creeper, rope or the arm of an antagonist, tends enrage the local (Kapham), which obstructs the cavity the passage (Srota) producing salivation, foaming the mouth and loss consciousness.

chapter: Chapter XXVIII: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter, which deals with the fa\ourable or unfavourable prognosis of an ulcer. ( Viparitarviparita - Vrana - Vijna^niya - madhyaryam ). IVIetrical Text :— Certain fatal or unfavourable symptoms (Arishtas)* unmistakably presage the death ulcer-patient, flower, smoke and cloud respectively herald a fruit, fire and rain. In most cases, the ignorant cannot interpret aright these fatal symp- toms owing to their extremely subtile nature, or out of ignorance or stupidity, or because such symptoms are very closely followed by the death of the patient. These fatal indications serve sure precursors death patient, unless warded off the blessings hoi}' Brahmanas, who are free from low desires animal propensities, and are also accustomed practise the Yo^a and other religious penances or death may be averted with the help men who are initiated into the mystery of concocting life-giving elixirs (Rasayanam". The symptoms which are developed by the deranj^ed bodily humours in the organism of a man at time when they have passed beyond all medical cure, and when the body serves as a mere passive back-ground for those phenomena, awaiting its impending dissolution, are called Ariahtas,
english_text: The remedy in such cases consists in lubricating and diaphorising the body of the patient with oil and heat, and in administering strong errhines (Shiro-Virechanam), and the juice extract of meat which possessed of the virtue subduing the deranged Vayu. Authoritative Verses on the Sub- ject:—An intelligent physician should remove Shalvam with due regard to its shape, location and the adaptability of the different types of surgical instru- ments to the case under treatment. A physician should exercise his own discretion extracting feathered shafts (Shalyas) from their seats of lodgment, as well as those that are difficult of extraction. A physician is at liberty exercise his own skill and wisdom, and to devise his own original means for Chap. XXVII. SUTR.ASTHA'NAM. the extraction of a Shalyam with the help of any surgical instruments when the abovesaid measures would prove abortive. A Shalyam, not removed from the bod}'- and left in its place of lodgment, brings swelling, suppuration, mortification of the affected part, and a sort excruciating pain, and may ultimately lead to death. Hence a physician should spare no pain to extract a Shalyam from its seat of lodgment. Thus ends the twenty-seventh Chapter ihe Sutraslh^nain the Sushruta SamhitS, which treats of extraction nf Shalvam. CHAPTER XXVIII. Man}'' such indications do not prove instantly fatal but bring death in course of time, just as diseases, supposed b}' some to be due to the influence of malig- nant planets, take time before they become patent out of their incubative stages.

A physician should exercise his own discretion extracting feathered shafts (Shalyas) from their seats of lodgment, as well as those that are difficult of extraction. A physician is at liberty exercise his own skill and wisdom, and to devise his own original means for Chap. XXVII. SUTR.ASTHA'NAM. the extraction of a Shalyam with the help of any surgical instruments when the abovesaid measures would prove abortive. A Shalyam, not removed from the bod}'- and left in its place of lodgment, brings swelling, suppuration, mortification of the affected part, and a sort excruciating pain, and may ultimately lead to death. Hence a physician should spare no pain to extract a Shalyam from its seat of lodgment. Thus ends the twenty-seventh Chapter ihe Sutraslh^nain the Sushruta SamhitS, which treats of extraction nf Shalvam. CHAPTER XXVIII. Now we shall discourse on the Chapter, which deals with the fa\ourable or unfavourable prognosis of an ulcer. ( Viparitarviparita - Vrana - Vijna^niya - madhyaryam ). IVIetrical Text :— Certain fatal or unfavourable symptoms (Arishtas)* unmistakably presage the death ulcer-patient, flower, smoke and cloud respectively herald a fruit, fire and rain. In most cases, the ignorant cannot interpret aright these fatal symp- toms owing to their extremely subtile nature, or out of ignorance or stupidity, or because such symptoms are very closely followed by the death of the patient. These fatal indications serve sure precursors death patient, unless warded off the blessings hoi}' Brahmanas, who are free from low desires animal propensities, and are also accustomed practise the Yo^a and other religious penances or death may be averted with the help men who are initiated into the mystery of concocting life-giving elixirs (Rasayanam". The symptoms which are developed by the deranj^ed bodily humours in the organism of a man at time when they have passed beyond all medical cure, and when the body serves as a mere passive back-ground for those phenomena, awaiting its impending dissolution, are called Ariahtas, Chap. XXVIII. Man}'' such indications do not prove instantly fatal but bring death in course of time, just as diseases, supposed b}' some to be due to the influence of malig- nant planets, take time before they become patent out of their incubative stages.

chapter: Chapter XXVIII: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter, which deals with the fa\ourable or unfavourable prognosis of an ulcer. ( Viparitarviparita - Vrana - Vijna^niya - madhyaryam ). IVIetrical Text :— Certain fatal or unfavourable symptoms (Arishtas)* unmistakably presage the death ulcer-patient, flower, smoke and cloud respectively herald a fruit, fire and rain. The symptoms which are developed by the deranj^ed bodily humours in the organism of a man at time when they have passed beyond all medical cure, and when the body serves as a mere passive back-ground for those phenomena, awaiting its impending dissolution, are called Ariahtas,
english_text: An attempt to cure a doomed patient is only repaid by failure and the ridicule of the world, and hence an intelligent physician should make it worth his while to carefully observe and study these fatal indications. A contrariety of the natural smell, colour, taste, (sensation, sound, touch, etc.) of an ulcer indicates a near and fatal termination of the disease. An ulcer emits a pungent, sharp, or fishy smell under the respective influences of the deranged Vayu, Pittam and Kapham. ulcer, deranged the action the vitiated blood, emits smell like that of iron (Loha-gandhi), while one, originated through the concerted action of the deranged humours, emits smell characterised by the distinctive features of each of them. On the other hand, an ulcer, due to the joint action '^of the deranged Vayu and 'Pittam), emits a smell like that of fried paddy one, due to the action the deranged Vayu and Kapham, emits smell like that of linseed oil ; whereas one, brought about the action of the deranged Pittam and Kapham, smells like sesamum oil. All those odours, marked somewhat fishy character, should be deemed the natural odours of ulcers, and any other smell should be held as a contrary or unnatural one. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XX\ 111. ulcer emitting a sweet smell like that of wine, fragrant aloe wood (Aguru), clarified-butter, Jati flower, Champaka, sandal, lotus or any celestial flower (Divyagandha), should be regarded as the precursor death. Similarly, a smell like the one which character- ises a dog, horse, mole, crow or a bug, like the one emitted by dry, putrid meat, or resembling the smell of earth or slime, should be likewise deemed unfavourable or fatal in an ulcer. A physician should give up a case where ulcer, though it has assumed a blackish, saffron Kankustha colour (a sort of mountain earth) through the action of the aggravated Pittam, divested of the burning, sucking and drawing pain, which is peculiar that morbiferous diathesis. Similarly, an ulcer, which, though brought about through the action the deranged Kapham, has become cold, hard and whitish natural in one of the Kaphaja type, should be given up as soon is marked by a burning pain. Likewise ulcer, due to the action of the deranged V^iyu, and characterised blackish hue and a thin secretion, and which found to invade the vital principles of the body, should be abandoned by a physician, whenever found entirely devoid of pain. An ulcer, which makes a gurgling or groaning sound, one which characterised by an extreme burn- ing sensation, oris confined to the skin and the flesh, Chap. XXVIII. SUTRASTHA'NAM. and is marked by the emission of wind with loud report, is sure to have fatal termination. Likewise, one, which characterised extreme pain, though not otherwise seated about any of the vital parts of the body, or which cold on the surface, though attended with an extremely burning sensation its inside and vice versa, should be deemed the precursor death.

chapter: Chapter XXIX: Now we shall discourse the Chapter, which treats favourable unfavourable prognosis diseases, as known from messengers, omens and dreams etc. (Viparitarviparita-Duta-Shakuna- Svapna- Nidarshaniya-madhyaryam). IVIctrical Texts:—The favourable or unfavour- able termination of a disease may be predicted from the appearance, speech, dress and demeanour of the messenger sent call physician, from the nature of the asterism and the lunar phase marking the time his arrival, from the direction of the wind (Anila) blowing at the time, from the nature of omens (Shakuna) seen by him on the road, or from the posture, temperament speech the physician himself. A messenger belonging the same caste the patient* should regarded auspicious omen, whereas one from a different caste would indicate fatal or an unfavourable termination of the disease, eunuch, husband of many wives, a messenger * A P^shanda messenger should despatched to call in a physician where a member of the same community would fall a householder, in the case of a patient of the same social order ; a BrShmana, the case of a BrShmana patient, and while infringement of the rule would be looked upon as an evil omen
english_text: Similarly, an ulcer should be regarded fatal, that shaped like the barb of a spear, Kunta kind of barbed dart spear), like banner, chariot, horse, or an elephant, or like a cow, temple, or a palace. A wise physician, witli any regard to his own repu- tation, should abandon a patient laid up with an ulcer which appears to have been dusted over with a sort pulverised crust, or who has been suffering from one companied by loss of flesh and strength, cough, difficult respiration and aversion food. ulcer, which occurring at any of the vital parts of the body secretes copious quantity of pus and blood, and refuses be healed even after a course of proper and persistent medical treatment, is sure to have a fatal termination. Thus ends the twenty-eighth Chapter the SutrasthSnam the Sushruta SamhitS, which deals with the favourable and unfavourable prognosis of ulcers. CHAPTER XXIX. Now we shall discourse the Chapter, which treats favourable unfavourable prognosis diseases, as known from messengers, omens and dreams etc. (Viparitarviparita-Duta-Shakuna- Svapna- Nidarshaniya-madhyaryam). IVIctrical Texts:—The favourable or unfavour- able termination of a disease may be predicted from the appearance, speech, dress and demeanour of the messenger sent call physician, from the nature of the asterism and the lunar phase marking the time his arrival, from the direction of the wind (Anila) blowing at the time, from the nature of omens (Shakuna) seen by him on the road, or from the posture, temperament speech the physician himself. A messenger belonging the same caste the patient* should regarded auspicious omen, whereas one from a different caste would indicate fatal or an unfavourable termination of the disease, eunuch, husband of many wives, a messenger * A P^shanda messenger should despatched to call in a physician where a member of the same community would fall a householder, in the case of a patient of the same social order ; a BrShmana, the case of a BrShmana patient, and while infringement of the rule would be looked upon as an evil omen. Chap. XXIX. StJTRAStHANAM. sent on a different errand and incidentally calling physician's house, or one who has quarrelled on the road, messengers who come riding on camels, donkeys or in carts, or on foot one unbroken line, should looked upon as inauspicious messengers. Similarly, messengers, who call at the house of a physician, holding their hands rope, club, any other weapon, or who come dressed blacky red, yellow, wet, dirty or torn garments, or with the upper sheets placed arranged their right shoulders (Apasavya), or clad in single cloths without such upper sheets on, as well as those, who are possessed of addi- tional or smaller number of limbs, or look disturbed and agitated, or whose bodies are in any way mutilated such, as look fierce and haughty, speak rough and harsh tone, utter any term implying death, should be regarded as augurs of evil.

english_text: Likewise^ a messenger, tearing off a blade of grass or chip of wood with his fingers, or handling the tip of his nose or the nipples of his breast, pulling the ends his cloth hair, the ring-finger his hand, brushing his nails and hair, standing with his fingers his ears nostrils, waiting with his hands placed his cheeks, chest or head, about the regions the arm-pits, well one, who has arrived the house of the physician with bits of human skull or stone, or with ashes, bones, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXIX. paddy husks or charcoal in the palms of his hands, one, who digs into the earth with his toe-nails, wantonly breaks stones brickbats, while waiting the physician's house, should regarded messenger of evil augury. A messenger, who at the time of visiting a physician for his professional help comes smeared with oil, or with red sandal paste or mud, and carries a red garland or a ripe but sapless fruit, or any other thing of like nature in his hand, or brushes together the nails of his fingers or touches his legs with the hand, carries a shoe in his hand, or who appears to have been suffering from a foul loathsome disease, further one, who breathes heavily, or weeps or behaves contrarily, or stands with the palms of his hands united and his face turned waids the south, waits one leg on an uneven ground with the other raised and placed higher support, should looked upon the precursor evil. A messenger, reporting his errand to the physician while he is facing the south, or who in an unclean state of the body, engaged in kindling a fire or in killing an animal, or is remaining in a nude state, or found to be lying on the bare floor of his chamber, or performing afiection after attending call nature, anointing himself with oil, or perspiring, sitting with his hair dishevelled, or in a state of mental [Chap. XXIX. sOtrasthanam. 27; perturbation, to be looked upon as a messenger fore- boding evil. A messenger, seeking the interview of physician M'hile he is engaged in offering oblations to his departed manes, or to the gods, or one who calls on him at noon or at midnight, at morning or at evening, or during the happening of any abnormal physical phenomenon, or at an hour under the influence of any the following asterisms (lunar mansions), viz. the Ardra, the Ashlesa, the Maghd, the Mula, the two Purvas, and the Bharani, on the day of the fourth, ninth, or the sixth phase of the moon (whether on the wane or on the increase), as well as on the last days of months and fortnights, should be considered as a messenger of evil augury. messenger, hot and perspiring from being seated near a blazing fire, and calling upon a physician in the midday, should deemed inauspicious one the case Pittaja distemper whereas messenger of similar description should be looked upon as foreboding the favourable termination of a disease, due to the action of the deranged Kapham.

Similarly in a case of haemoptysis, dysentery or any morbid discharge from the urethra (Prameha j, the first interview between a messenger and a physician near a reservoir of water is an omen of happy augury, learned physician shall tluis determine the ominous character messenger connection with other diseases as well. IVIessengers of happy augury :—A fair and handsome messenger, who is clad in clean and white garments, and belongs the same caste spiritual clan (Svagotraj as the patient himself, forebodes the suc- cessful termination of the disease (for which the medical aid is needed). A messenger, calling on a physician either on foot or in bullock cart, and who contented, intelligent, capable of acting according to the rules of decorum, time and circumstances, and is independent and original in his thoughts and ideas, and carries ornaments, and other auspicious articles about his person, alone capable of rendering the best services connection with the calling in of a physician. A messenger, for the first time, interviewing a physician, when the latter complacently seated with his face towards the east, and clean and even ground, should be regarded as a messenger of happy augury. IVIctrical Texts:—The favourable or unfavour- able termination of a disease may be predicted from the appearance, speech, dress and demeanour of the messenger sent call physician, from the nature of the asterism and the lunar phase marking the time his arrival, from the direction of the wind (Anila) blowing at the time, from the nature of omens (Shakuna) seen by him on the road, or from the posture, temperament speech the physician himself. A messenger belonging the same caste the patient* should regarded auspicious omen, whereas one from a different caste would indicate fatal or an unfavourable termination of the disease, eunuch, husband of many wives, a messenger * A P^shanda messenger should despatched to call in a physician where a member of the same community would fall a householder, in the case of a patient of the same social order ; a BrShmana, the case of a BrShmana patient, and while infringement of the rule would be looked upon as an evil omen
english_text: Similarly, harmonious melodies birds chirping the boughs of healthy Kshira trees, bent under the weight of fruit, and looking gladsome with their dowry beautiful blossoms and foliage, or notes of birds perched the terraces of palace towers the tops of banner poles singing melodiously, or birds following the messenger with their songs or singing seated from the auspicious quarters of the heavens, or following him on his left, should be equally regarded as sights and notes of happy foreboding. bird, seated on the withered trunk of a blighted 2/6 THR SUSHRUTA SAMHITA 1 Chap. xxix. or thunder-blasted tree, or on thorny knoll covered over with creepers, or ashes stones, amidst ordure husks grain, dried skeletons, and singing harsh voice with its head turned towards the blazing inauspicious quarter the sky, should be deemed as portending evil. Similarly, birds, which are possessed of names masculine terminations are happy omens if seen the left by physician his way the house of a patient, while birds, similar occasion, whose names have feminine endings, are auspicious seen him the right. dog or jackal, seen run- ning from the right the left, hapjn- omen, and so is a mongoose a Chasha bird if seen on the left. hare, serpent, or an owl, seen on either side of the road, inauspicious sight. The sight and the sound Godha Krikal^sha an animal of of the lizard species) are both inauspicious. a man, other than messenger inauspicious character but possessed of features alike unfavourable, should happen cross the way a physician, just starting on a professional call, should be regarded equallv indicative evil. The sight vessel full of Kulutha pulse, or of husks grain, or of stone, ashes, clay charcoal, oil, inauspicious. Similarly, the sight of a vessel filled with red mustard witli wine other than whicli clear and Chap. XXIX. (Prasanna) should deemed omen evil augury. Similarly, the sight of a parched corpse, of a withered tree or Pal^sha branch, is equally inauspicious. A physician, meeting a member of any of the vile or degraded castes or a blind or indigent person, or man inimically disposed towards him, should consider the character of the disease to be unfavourable. gentle, cool and fragrant breeze, blowing from the direction of his destination, should regarded auspicious omen by a physician. wind, which is hot, dry, and charged with the fetid exalations of putrid matter, and which blows from the direction his starting point, should regarded an evil omen. The word "cut," used by another and accidentallv heard a physician fon his wa^O to the bed-side of a patient laid with Granthi (aneurism) Arvuda (tumour), should be regarded as a good omen ; while the term "open", heard under similar circumstances and connection with a case of Vidradhi (abscess), or Gulma (abdominal gland\ or Udara (ascites), should be regarded equally auspicious portent. Similarly, the term "stopped" is commended case dysenter}' haemoptysis.

english_text: Thus the physician should interpret the auguries according to the nature of each individual case. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XXIX A curse, imprecation, or wailing like "woe to as well as sobs, groans, reports of defecation vomit- ing, the brayings of an ass, the frightened sound a camel, obstacle impediment the path physician, sudden breakage, collapse, the falling any article from cupboard, and sad or dejected spirit of the physician without any assign- able cause, should regarded evil omens the time of his starting. These omens should observed attended at the time first entering the house patient, the threshold within its walls, but not after the physician has once commenced the medical treat- ment. The sight of a knot of torn hairs, ashes, bones, wood, stone, husks of grain, cotton, thorns, a bedstead with its legs upturned, wine, water, fat, oil, sesamum, dried grass, straw, a eunuch, a deformed person or one with a broken limb, a nude man, or one with a clean shaved head, or clad black garment, should regarded as evil omens by a physician, whether noticed b}' him at the time starting after getting into a sick room. Pots or utensils placed in pendent brackets, and found to be spontaneously moving about without any definite cause, as well as any other fallen articles digged smashed thrown out the sick-room a ph3^sician sitting dejected and gathered his seat, and the patient sitting with a down- cast face, or pricking his body or the bed clothes Chap. XXIX. SUTRASFHANAM. while talking with the physician, or shaking his hands, back or head, or taking hold of placing the hands of the physician in his own, or on his breast, or interro- gating the physician with up-turned face, pressing his own limbs, when interrogated the physician return, should considered unfavourable signs. The patient, whose house physician is not duly honoured, can never rally. The due honouring of a physician leads to a speedy recoAery. messenger good omen forebodes the favourable termination disease, while the contrary indicated messenger of the opposite type. Hence physician shall carefully observe the ominous character of a messenger (despatched to seek his aid Dreams :—Now shall describe the dreams, which either being dreamt the patient, by his relations, portend fatal successful close the malady. The patient, who dreams going towards the south the back of an elephant, or on that of any carnivorous animal, riding boar buffalo, sees himself carried towards the quarter by dark woman with dishevelled hair and clad in a blood- red garment—laughing and dancing, soon meets his doom. A dream by a patient that members of vile castes have been drawing him southward, that ghosts or anchorites have been embracing him, that 28o THE SUSHRUtA SAMHITA. Chap. xxix. savage beasts with diabolical faces have been smelling his head, predicts that his earthly days are numbered, while such dreams occurring healthy subject indicate an impending disease.

IVIctrical Texts:—The favourable or unfavour- able termination of a disease may be predicted from the appearance, speech, dress and demeanour of the messenger sent call physician, from the nature of the asterism and the lunar phase marking the time his arrival, from the direction of the wind (Anila) blowing at the time, from the nature of omens (Shakuna) seen by him on the road, or from the posture, temperament speech the physician himself. english_text: Similarly, the patient, who dreams of drinking oil honey, or of diving into a bed of dank oozy slime, or of laughing and dancing mud-besplattered, is at the threshold of death. dream ha^*ing entwined wreath of red iiowers round one's head, though other- wise nude stripped clothes, seeing reeds, bamboos, or palm trees growing on his chest, portends the impending death patient. the other hand, such dreams, occurring healthy subject,, forebode the advent of disease. I.ikewise, the patient, who dreams being eaten up b}' fish, or who fancies himself again entering into the womb of his mother, or thinks he is falling from the summit of a mountain or into a dark and dismal cave, or as being carried away by the current of a river, or assailed and overwhelmed by a pack of crows, is already a doomed being. The dream of a clean shaved head, or of falling stars, or of dying lamp light, or of the extraction of one's own eyes, or of shaking divine images, or of earthquakes, purgings, vomit- ings or falling out one's own teeth, is always fatal. The patient, who dreams of climbing a Shalmali, Kinsuka, Pari-bhadra tree, or of ascending an ant-hill funeral pyre, or of witnessing himself bound to sacri- Chap. XXIX. ficical stake, receiving eating, cotton, levigated sesamum paste, . iron, salt, sesamum, boiled rice, or drinking oil or wine (Sura), as the case may should consider himself as a doomed being, while such dreams healthy subject indicate the impending attack of a disease. dream should regarded as ineffectual which is quite in conformity with the physical temperament of the dreamer (such one scaling the heavens a person of Vataja temperament one of seeing blazing fire, flash lightning, or a meteor-fall a man of Pittaja temperament ; and one wit- nessing reservoirs of water, etc. by a man of Kaphaja temperamentj as well as one which has been forgotten or followed by another of an auspicious type or is the outcome of premeditated thought like one dreamt the da}' time. A fever patient dreaming of friendship with a dog, consumptive one dreaming of making friends with monkey or a monster ; a hysteric patient who dreams of making friendship with a ghost ; a Prameha or dj'sentery patient dreaming of drinking water ; a leper dreaming of drinking oil, or a Gulma patient dreaming of a tree grow- on his belly, should count his days as numbered. A person afflicted with any disease of the head, and dreaming of a tree growing on his head, one suffering from vomiting and dreaming eating sesamum cakes THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XXIX. asthma patient, person, afflicted with thirst, dreaming of making journey on foot or a jaundice patient dreaming eating food prepared with turmeric ; or a person suffering from hecmoptysis and dreaming of drinking blood, should be considered as about to depart this life.

chapter: Chapter XXX: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which treats of the prognosis that can be obtained from the perverted functions the five sense organs (Panehe n- d riyartha- Vi prati pattf - madhyaryam) Metrical texts ;—A perversion or contrariety of the functions the mind or brain (Shilam), and of the organs sense-perception, is called Arishtam unfavourable symptom foreboding death). Now hear me describe, brief detail, the symptoms which are called Arishta (fatal indications). The man, who hears variety divine sounds even tlie absence of any the celestial beings, (such as, the Siddhas, the Gandharvas etc.), thinks that hearing the uproar city, the moanings the sea, the rumbling rain cloud, without their actual presence or proximity, who incapable catching their sounds even when they are actually present and sounding, assigns them causes other than the actual ones, should regarded as a doomed being. The person, who interprets the uproar of a city the rustling forest sounds emanating from other sources, or rejoices at the voice his enemies, and annoyed that his own devoted friends, or who suddenly loses the faculty
english_text: A patient having had any the aforesaid dreams, under the circumstances, should get up in the morning and make a gift Masha-pulse, sesamum, iron and gold the Brahmanas, and repeat the blessed Tripada Gayatri (Mantras Having dreamt a bad dream in the first watch of the night, a person should meditate upon a holy or auspicious subject, and then lie down again with all his senses fully controlled, and repeat the Mantras sacred to any of the gods. An evil dream should not be related to another. The dreamer the dream should reside in a holy temple for three consecutive nights,, and worship the deity with the most fervent devotion, whereby its evil effects would become nullified. Now shall describe the dreams, which are auspicious nature. Members the twice born castes, gods, cows, bullocks, kings, one's own living friends and relations, a blazing fire, a Brahmana, or a sheet of clear water seen in a dream by a healthy person predict or predicts to him a pecuniary gain in the near future, while such dreams occurring in a diseased person indicate a speed}' recovery of the disease he has been Chap. XXIX. SUTKASTHANAM. 28" suffering from. Similarly, dreams of meat, fish, garlands of white flowers, cloths and fruit predict a gain speedy cure, as the case may be. Dreams ascending the terrace royal palace, of climbing tree hill, or of riding elephant predict similar results above. A dream one's sailing over river, pool or sea of turbid water predicts money gain cure, according as one healthy diseased. dream having been bit or stung by a serpent, by leeches, or by a bee, indicates bliss cure, according to one's good bad health at the time. The man, who usually gets such auspicious dreams, should looked upon as a long-lived man, and may unhesitatingly taken under medical treatment by a physician. Thus ends the Uventy-nintli Chapter the Sutiasthinam the Sushruta SamhitS, which deals with favourable unfavourable prognosis from messengers, birds, omens etc. CHAPTER XXX. english_text: The person, who interprets the uproar of a city the rustling forest sounds emanating from other sources, or rejoices at the voice his enemies, and annoyed that his own devoted friends, or who suddenly loses the faculty Chap. XXX. hearing without any manifest tangible reason, should be deemed as already on the threshold of death. The man, who feels cold when touching hot warm substance, and, vice versa, complains a burning sensation even when suffering from boil, postule the Kaphaja type (characterised numbness, shivering, etc, shivers when the tem- perature his body is felt to be considerably high, should looked upon already the point death. The person, who has lost the faculty of touch, and does not feel any pain any part the body when struck amputated, feels his body had been strewn over with particles dust, or suffers from discoloration of the skin which becomes marked with blue red stripes, and who is harassed by hosts of blue flies after a bath anointment, should be regarded as one who has already passed the confines of life. Similarly, the man whose body emits a fragrant smell without having been rubbed with any kind of perfume, or to whom a sweet thing tastes acid, and an acid tastes sweet, or who exhibits symptoms of a general per- version of the faculty of taste, or in whom (articles different tastes (administered their officinal order of enumeration') tend to aggravate the deranged bodily humours, bring about their pacification and a dulness appetite if partaken of the inverted THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXX. order, should regarded departed soul, like the one who has lost the faculty of taste. The man, who deems a fetid odour to be fragrant perfume, one fragi'ant to be fetid, or one who does not feel any discomfort even at the smell of a burning lamp wick that has just been extinguished, or who has entirely lost the faculty smell, should looked upon as a dead man. The man, to whom the twin attributes of heat and cold, pleasure and pain, as well as the peculiarities of weather storm, drought, snowfall, etc.), and the different quarters of the sky appear to be reversed inverted ; one who has lost all distinctions (of joy and misery, storm and sunshine, heat and cold, etc.), whom the specific attributes things appear to be contrary and reversed, should be regarded the point of death.
