chapter: Chapter XXX: I. Now we shall discourse the Chapter, which deals with the prognosis gathered from the altered condition features (Chhd'yar-Viprati- patti madhy^yam).* IVIetrical Text:—The man, whose complexion suddenly assumes a brown, red, blue yellow shade, should be regarded as already gathered to his rest. The man, who has lost all sense of modesty or propriety, and whose complexion, and whose strength (ojah) and memory have suddenly undergone discolouration extreme deterioration, should be counted with the dead. Little chance there the life patient whose lower lip hangs down while the upper one drawn or turned and both of them have assumed black colour like that a jamboline fruit. The patient, whose teeth fall out or which have assumed a reddish Physicians of the Ayurvedic School, however, observe dislinclion between Chhiyd (shade complexion), PrabhA (healthful glow the complexion), and Varna (natural colour of the complexion) itself. The Chh%d the shade one's complexion may easily distinguished as clear, rough or cool, etc. and can detected only on close view. The Prabhd, on the other hand, visible from distance and admits being divided into seven different types, such red, yellow, while, brown, greenish, pale, and black. The Varna the natural colour of the complexion of a man is found either fair, black, dusky leaning towards the fair, according his race and habitation. The term also includes natural modestv, look and ease

A wise physician should abandon a patient, characterised the cold- ness of his breath and extremities and a hurried and intermittent respiration, or who is found breathing with his mouth open, or lips separated. Similarly, a patient affected with kind stupor insomnia and remaining drowsy, all day long, or fainting at the least attempt speaking, should be counted with the dead. The patient, who licks his upper lip, troubled with eructations, or holds conversa- tions with the departed, should be deemed already entered into the region of the dead. A man, spontane- ously bleeding through the roots of his hairs (pores of the skin) otherwise than in case of poisoning, should be deemed as dying on that day. (Similarly) a limb or part of the body, hanging down from its natural position, or becoming raised or twisted round, or cast obliquely from its natural seat, or dislocated, or protruded, or drawn inward, or suddenly becoming light heavy without any definite assignable cause, sudden eruption coral- coloured rash or Vyanga, should be regarded as indicat- ing speedy dissolution of the patient in whom they are exhibited. Likewise, the appearance of veins the region the forehead, or an eruption of postules on the ridge of the nose, perspiration on the forehead in the morning, copious lachrymation without any ocular complaint, sense being dusted with dried and pulverised cowdung over the face, or the flying of pigeons, Kankas, etc, over one's head, excessive micturitions motions of the bowels from empty stomach, suppression of urine or feces even after hearty meal or draught, is fatal. So also, pain and aching about the breast and the chest, emaciation the extremities and an oedema of the middle part of the trunk, and vice versa oedema the upper trunk and emaciation of the lower part, and vice versa or an oedema of the left half of the body and emaciation of the right, and vice versa ; or hoarseness, huskiness, or loss of voice, discolouring of the teeth, nails or of the skin, eruption of white patches on the chest, etc, of the Chap. XXXII. body, should be deemed signs which forebode the approaching dissolution of an individual. (Similarly) a limb or part of the body, hanging down from its natural position, or becoming raised or twisted round, or cast obliquely from its natural seat, or dislocated, or protruded, or drawn inward, or suddenly becoming light heavy without any definite assignable cause, sudden eruption coral- coloured rash or Vyanga, should be regarded as indicat- ing speedy dissolution of the patient in whom they are exhibited. Likewise, the appearance of veins the region the forehead, or an eruption of postules on the ridge of the nose, perspiration on the forehead in the morning, copious lachrymation without any ocular complaint, sense being dusted with dried and pulverised cowdung over the face, or the flying of pigeons, Kankas, etc, over one's head, excessive micturitions motions of the bowels from empty stomach, suppression of urine or feces even after hearty meal or draught, is fatal. So also, pain and aching about the breast and the chest, emaciation the extremities and an oedema of the middle part of the trunk, and vice versa oedema the upper trunk and emaciation of the lower part, and vice versa or an oedema of the left half of the body and emaciation of the right, and vice versa ; or hoarseness, huskiness, or loss of voice, discolouring of the teeth, nails or of the skin, eruption of white patches on the chest, etc, of the Chap. XXXII. body, should be deemed signs which forebode the approaching dissolution of an individual. Moreover the patient, whose semen, or expectorated or fecal matter does not float on water, or who sees the distorted or bifurcated images of objects, or whose hair shines with gloss if anointed with oil, finds his relief in death. weak dysentery patient with complete aversion food, one who tormented with thirst even when suffering from cough, a man suffering from chronic catarrh with complete loathing for food, from gastritis ^Sula) with aphonia, and vomiting frothy blood and pus, should be regarded as past all cure. patient, enfeebled and emaciated through fever, cough and an oedematous swelling of the face and the extre- mities, and showing the greatest aversion food, and the muscles of whose calves, shoulders and thighs have grown loose and flabby, should considered awaiting the call of death. A patient, suffering from fever, cough, and vomiting, or passing with the stool, the evening, undigested food matter eaten in the morning, would die of asthma. The patient, who falls the ground bleating hke a goat, and exhibits such symptoms as a rupture of the testes, numbness of the penis, drooping of the neck and introsusception of the penis, should be considered as past all cure. chapter: Chapter XXXII: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which treats of the prognosis based on the perversion of the external appearances the body (Svabha'va-viprati- patti - madhyaryam) A contrariety of the natural features of any part member of the body should be looked upon as a fatal indication. The blackness of a limb part which naturally white,* or the whiteness of a blackf part, or a naturally red| part, or member, etc. assuming any other colour, hard§ part becoming soft, and vice versaW, movableU part suddenly becoming fixed, and vice versa** or the contraction (flexion) of an extended part, the extension expansion contracted (flexible; part, shorttt part suddenly becoming elongatedji, and vice versa, sudden hanging down of a part or member of the body which does not naturally §§ hang down, and vice versa\\\\, or a sudden increase or decrease of natural temperature of any part, member, organ of the body, well as its sudden glossiness, roughness, numbness, discolour- ation, weakness, or weariness, should be looked upon as fatal symptoms. The teeth and the cornea, f The iris, J The tongue and the palate, etc. § Bones, teeth, etc. Soft parts such as the flesh, fat, etc. Joints, etc. ** Nose, ears and flesh, etc. ft Head and forehead, etc. Pupils, etc. Hair, nails, etc. Perspiration, urine and feces, etc

Similarl}', a disease, due to the influence of a malig- nant planet occupying, either through its retrogade or zigzag movement, an inauspicious position in relation to the natal asterism of the patients, is sure terminate in death. A man, struck by lightning or a falling meteor, baffles all medicinal skill. Similarly, a disease due the fact one's own house, wife, bed, seat, con- veyance, riding-animal assuming any ill-omened features, or a disease originated through the use of gems, utensils, garments, etc. forbidden inauspicious character usuall}^ ends in death (Aristam). Authoritative verses on the Sub- ject disease, appearing enfeebled and emaciated subject and refusing to yield to course proper medicinal treatment, and which becomes rather aggravated by the administraticn of proper medicinal remedies or antidotes, necessarily portends the death of the patient. Chap. XXXII. A Mahavyadhi* (lit :—a deep seated disease) sud- denly abating person whom nourishment fails to produce any perceptible effect forebodes a fatal termination. The ph3'sician, who can detect and full}^ interpret these fatal indications, is honoured by the king for determining the curable or incurable nature of a disease. * Any deep seated disease, which seriously affects the vital principles of a man, is called MahAvj-^dhi. Diseases such Prameha, V^tavyadhi, Shosha, etc. have also been included within the category in the Chapter on ICciya-chikitsh^. general amelioration recovery in these cases being natural, account of their deep-seated character, sudden abatement is usually fraught with fatal consequences. (Arishtattl.) Thus ends the thirty-second Chapter of Sutrasthanam in the Sushrula- S^mhita which deals with the prognosis based perversion the natural appearances of the body. CHAPTER Now we shall discourse on the Chapter, which treats of incurable diseases (A'varaniya-madhyayam). IVIetrical texts :—Hear me describe, Oh child, the diseases which being attended with many distressing and supervenient symptom, and being treated without rejuvenating and restorative medicines, speedily assume incurable character. The following eight diseases, viz Maha-Vata-vyadhi (paralysis diseases affecting the nervous system general), Prameha morbid discharges from the urethra) , Kushtha, Arsha (piles), Vagandara fistula ano\ Ashmari (stone the bladder), Mudha-garbha (false presen- tations) and the eight kinds of Udari (abdominal drops)'-) are, by their very nature, extremely hard to cure.

chapter: Chapter XXXIV: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which treats of the mode of preserving the life of a king whose soldiers are on march (J ucta-Scniya-madhy^- yam). Metrical Texts :— I shall presentl}^ describe the measures, which a physician in the king's service should adopt with a view to protect the life of his royal master, specially from acts of secret poisoning, while mobilizing his armies invade the territor}^ neighbouring monarch accompanied by his chiefs and ministers. common practice the enemy under such circumstances is to poison the wells on the roadside, the articles of food, the shades of trees (shadowy places) and the fuel and forage for cattle ; hence incumbent physician marching with the troops, to inspect, examine and purify these before using any of them, in case they be poisoned. The symptoms and medical treatment will be fully described and discussed later on in the part, entitled the Kalpa Sthanam. A person laid up with an attack of Vidradhi (abscess) and exhibiting such fatal symptoms distension of the abdomen, retention urine, vomiting, hic- cough, thirst, pain of a varied character (such as aching, excruciating, etc.) and dyspnoea, should regarded THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap, XXXIII. as to have approached the goal of his life. patient suffering from attack jaundice chlorosis marked yellowness of the teeth, nails, and of the conjunctivae, and seeing everything yellow, not expected to long survive the occurrence of the attack. A person laid with an attack of Haemoptysis, largely vomiting blood, and viewing everything red blood-coloured with his blood-shot eyes, should regarded as about to depart this life. A person, insane, extremely enfeebled and emaciated, and sitting up sleep- less in the night, or with eyes constantly lifted upward cast down, would soon relieved of his earthly suffering. Men, learned in the lore the Atharva Veda, hold that death may attributed to a hundred and one different causes, (lit deaths of a hundred and one kinds) of which one (which that old man THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XXXIV. natural!}'- and spontaneously expiring) called natural, while the rest are unnatural traumatic their origin. Physicians conversant with the curative virtues of drugs and minerals, and priests well versed the Vedic Mantras, should jointly protect the king from death, whether due idiopathic (Doshajai extrinsic causes. The god Brahma disclosed to the world the Atharva Veda together with the eight allied branches Vedic hterature and the science medicine. And since a priest (Brahmana) is well-versed in the aforesaid branches of study, a physician should act subserviently and occupy a subordinate position the priest. The death king usually leads political revolu- tion popular disturbances and brings about confusion among the vocations of the different orders society. The growth population markedly suffers through such catastrophies. As the external features of a king resemble those of a common person, while his (king's) commanding majesty, sacrifice, forbearance and fortune are super-human (in their nature and intensity), therefore a man should, who prudent and seeks his own good, think reverentially his king, and propitiate him with tokens loyalty and allegiance as if he were a deity. physician, fully equipped with a supply of medicine, should live camp not remote from the royal Chap. XXXIV. SUTRASTKANAM. pavilion, and there the persons wounded shafts ot arrows other war projectiles, suffering from the effects any imbibed poison, should resort him (the physician), conspicuous like a tri- umphant ensign for his fame and professional success. physician, well versed his own technical science, and commanding fair knowledge other allied branches study well, glorified his king and the Brahmanas, and is like a banner of victory ennobling ornament to the state. The physician, the patient, the medicine, and the attendants (nurses are the four essential factors of a course of medical treatment.

chapter: Chapter XXXV: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which deals with clinical observations (A'turopakramaniya- madhyaryam). physician should first observe the vital condition (Ayu) the patient before commencing the medical treatment. After that, the nature the disease, the country and season of the year in which it has made its appearance, as well as the state of digestion, age, body, strength, disposition, habit, previous medicine, natural temperament and the power endurance the patient, etc. should be observed and carefully examined. Characteristic features long lived man :—Men, the dimensions whose hands, legs, sides, back, nipples the breast, teeth, face, shoulders and forehead exceed the average, well those whose eyes, arms, phalanges and fingers are longer than the ordinary ones should be regarded as going live long. Authoritative verses the sub- ject man, with deep-set bones, ligaments, and veins, and tough and thick-set limbs, and firm and unflinching sense organs, well as one whose body gradually develops more and more symmetrical shape, should be looked upon as a long-lived man The man, who has not ailed for a single moment even from the day his birth, and has been getting more and more strong- limbed every day through the culti- vation his inborn sense and better knowledge of the laws of health, is sure to live to a good old age in the full enjoyment of his senses and intellect. 3IO THE SUSHRTTTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXXV. IVIadhyama^uh :—Now, hear describe the bodily features a man a mean aver- age duration life (Madhyamayuli;. We shall now give the exact measures of the different limbs and members the body for the better ascertainment the duration life patient under investigation.—The legs, the arms, and the head are called the limbs of the bod}*-, while their com- ponent parts are called the members (Avayavas). The great toe of a man, or the one next to it, measured Chap, xxxv.] with his own fingers should measure two fingers' width in length, the lengths of the other toes (the third, fourth, and small ones) successively diminishing by a fifth part of that of his middle finger ( Pradeshini). The fore-sole and the sole proper respectively should measure four fingers' width in length and five fingers' width in breadth. The heel of the foot (Parshni) should measure five fingers' width in length and four fingers' wadth breadth. The foot itself should measure fourteen fingers' width in length. The girth of the foot, well as the circumference the middle parts thighs and knee-joints, respectively should measure fourteen fingers in width. The part the leg between the ankle and the knee-joint should measure eighteen fingers' width length, while the part between the joint of the waist and the knee-joint should measure thirty-two fingers' width length, the entire leg thus measuring fifty fingers' width in all. The length the thigh the same as that of the part lying between the heel and the knee-joint (Jangha The scrotum, the chin, the (two rows teeth, the exterior line the nostrils^ the roots the ears, and the intervening space between the eyes, should respectively measure two fingers' width in length. XXXV. the ears, and the forehead, and the space intervenmg between the pupils the eyes measure four fingers' width in length. The entire length of the vaginal canal should measure twelve fingers' width. The space l3nng between the penis and the umbihcus,as well as the one intervening between the chest and the upper end the throat (lit neck), like the one h'ing between the tips the two nipples of the breast, should measure twelve fingers' width in length. The length the entire face should measure twelve fingers' width. The girth round the wrist and the fore-arm of a man should measure twelve fingers. XXXV. SUTRASTHA'NAM. 31^ the absolute good, should be regarded as a man of Satvasara (psychic or illumined) temperament. man with glossy, white and close-set bones, teeth, and nails and who has beootten large family children, and shows a marked amative ten- dency, should be looked upon as a man in whom the prin- ciple of semen decidedly preponderates. A man with a thin and sinewy bod}', and who exibits traits of excessive strength, and possesses a deep resonant voice, and a pair large and handsome eyes, and who successful ever}' walk of life, should be looked upon one whom the principle of marrow preponderates. A man with large head, and large pair shoulders, and having firm teeth, bones, cheek-bones, and finger-nails, should considered as one in whom the principle of bone preponderates. A man with large and bulky body, and who capable enduring large amount fatigue physical exertion, and who naturally talks soft and melodious voice, and whose bodily secretions such urine and perspiration are characterised by coldness should be regarded as one a fatty tempera- ment. A man with an erect and upright frame, and deep-set bones, and joints thick layers flesh, should be regarded as one in whom the principle flesh predominates. A man, whose finger nails, eyes, tongue, palate. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Clmp. xxxv lips, palms of hands and soles of feet are glossy, and tinged with a shade of red, should be looked upon as one in whom blood forms the essential and predominant principle. A man with a soft, smooth and pleasant skin and hair should considered one in whom serum fTvak) forms the essential principle the body. respect worldly success and longevity, men each the aforesaid types should successively judged inferior men belonging the one pre- ceding it in the above order of enumeration. Authoritative verse the sub- ject A quabfied physician should examine the dura- tion of life in a patient with the help of the aforesaid measures of limbs and the essential bodily principles, before proceeding to take up his medical treatment, and his professional success should be decidedly increased thereby. All the diseases, whose names have been specifically enumerated before, grouped under any of the three different heads the curable, the suppressible (Yapya) and the incurable (lit fit to be pronounced as hopeless). Each of these different types, in its turn, should carefully observed so as to determine whether primar}' an independent disease, merely accessory or sympathetic one, or the premonitory indica- tion of an incipient distemper in its incubative stage. text: A man, who is possessed of a good retentive memory, and is intelligent, valorous and cleanly in his habits, and whose mind graced with such rare and excellent virtues as, purity of thought, and fervent and unflinching devotion gods and the reverend, and who exerts himself for the furtherance Chap. XXXV. SUTRASTHA'NAM. 31^ the absolute good, should be regarded as a man of Satvasara (psychic or illumined) temperament. man with glossy, white and close-set bones, teeth, and nails and who has beootten large family children, and shows a marked amative ten- dency, should be looked upon as a man in whom the prin- ciple of semen decidedly preponderates. A man with a thin and sinewy bod}', and who exibits traits of excessive strength, and possesses a deep resonant voice, and a pair large and handsome eyes, and who successful ever}' walk of life, should be looked upon one whom the principle of marrow preponderates. A man with large head, and large pair shoulders, and having firm teeth, bones, cheek-bones, and finger-nails, should considered as one in whom the principle of bone preponderates. A man with large and bulky body, and who capable enduring large amount fatigue physical exertion, and who naturally talks soft and melodious voice, and whose bodily secretions such urine and perspiration are characterised by coldness should be regarded as one a fatty tempera- ment. A man with an erect and upright frame, and deep-set bones, and joints thick layers flesh, should be regarded as one in whom the principle flesh predominates. A man, whose finger nails, eyes, tongue, palate. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Clmp. xxxv lips, palms of hands and soles of feet are glossy, and tinged with a shade of red, should be looked upon as one in whom blood forms the essential and predominant principle. A man with a soft, smooth and pleasant skin and hair should considered one in whom serum fTvak) forms the essential principle the body. respect worldly success and longevity, men each the aforesaid types should successively judged inferior men belonging the one pre- ceding it in the above order of enumeration. Authoritative verse the sub- ject A quabfied physician should examine the dura- tion of life in a patient with the help of the aforesaid measures of limbs and the essential bodily principles, before proceeding to take up his medical treatment, and his professional success should be decidedly increased thereby. All the diseases, whose names have been specifically enumerated before, grouped under any of the three different heads the curable, the suppressible (Yapya) and the incurable (lit fit to be pronounced as hopeless). Each of these different types, in its turn, should carefully observed so as to determine whether primar}' an independent disease, merely accessory or sympathetic one, or the premonitory indica- tion of an incipient distemper in its incubative stage. Chap. XXXV. SUTRASTHA'XAM.

chapter: Chapter XXXV: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which deals with clinical observations (A'turopakramaniya- madhyaryam). physician should first observe the vital condition (Ayu) the patient before commencing the medical treatment. After that, the nature the disease, the country and season of the year in which it has made its appearance, as well as the state of digestion, age, body, strength, disposition, habit, previous medicine, natural temperament and the power endurance the patient, etc. should be observed and carefully examined. Characteristic features long lived man :—Men, the dimensions whose hands, legs, sides, back, nipples the breast, teeth, face, shoulders and forehead exceed the average, well those whose eyes, arms, phalanges and fingers are longer than the ordinary ones should be regarded as going live long. Those who have broad chests, broad eye-brows with broader spaces intervening between the muscles the breasts, and who take deeper inspirations of breath, will be long lived. Those whose necks, thighs, and generative organs are shorter than those of the average type, or those whose voices and umbilical cavities -are deep, and whose breasts are unraised an d thick-set, and external ears broad, fleshy and haiiy
english_text: ^17 An A upasargika (sympathetic) disease merely symptom developed the course of an original primary malady, and which has its foundation the very nature component factors the pre-existing distemper. A disease, Avhich manifests itself from the commencement a case and is neither accessor}' symptom, nor a premonitory indication of any other distemper, called Prak-kevalam (primary original) one. A disease which indicates the advent future or impending malad}' is called a Purvaru- pam (premonitory stage or indication of a disease). The medicinal remedy administered any particular case should be selected with an eye the curative ^•irtues of each of its components, so as not clash with the nature (cause) the disease and its accompanying symptoms, and to prove simultaneously soothing to both of them. the contrary, violent unfavourable symptom should be first attended to and checked in a case where it would be found have grown stronger and more distressing or dangerous than the original malady course of which has been developed. primary independent malady, unattended with any of the distressing or unfavourable symptoms, should be treated according its indications and the nature the deranged humours involved therein, while in incubative disease the treatment should THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. Chap. XXXV. consist subduing a premonitory s^'mptom as soon as it would make itself manifest. Authoritative Verse the sub ject there not a single disease, which can make its appearance without the participation of any of the deranged bodil)' liumours, a wise physician joined to administer medicines according to the specific features of the deranged humours involved in a disease whose nature and treatment have not even been scribed in any book on medicine. The different seasons of the year have been described before. IVIetricai Texts :—In the cold season, disease should be treated with measures and remedies endued with the virtue destroying or warding off cold, while in summer the medicinal treatment should consist of measures and applications capable of alla^n'ng the heat. The medical treatment disease should be connnenced just the opportune moment, which should not be allowed expire in vain under anycircumstances whatsoever. A course of medical treat- ment commenced at an inopportune moment, or not resorted to at the advent of its proper time, as well as over or insufficient medication, proves abortive even a curable type of disease. The proper medical treat- ment (of disease) that which successfully copes with the malady under treatment, and arrests the recrudescence of a fresh one by way of sequel, and not Chap. XXXV. that, which, though subduing particular distemper, is immediately followed by a new one. It has been demonstrated before that the food of a man is digested only with the help of the digestive fire or heat (Pachakagni), which may be divided into four ditferent kinds (states).

english_text: One of these kinds is due not being in any way affected by the deranged humours of the body, while the other three are respec- tively ascribed to the fact of their becoming so deranged. The digestive fire heat becomes irregular fitful (Vishamagni) through the action of the deranged Vayu, becomes keen, through the action the deranged Pittam, and dull sluggish, through the action the deranged Kapham. The fourth kind (Sama) continues state unaffected by any of the morbid humoural constituents the body owing to their maintaining the normal equilibrium. Samargni and Vishamei'gni :—The diges- tive heat, which l\illy digests the ingested food the proper time without the least irregularit}', thus reflecting the continuance of the bodily humours in their normal state, called Samagni. The digestive heat which is irregular in its action, and which sometimes helps the process complete digestion, and produces distension of the abdomen, colic pain, constipation the bowels, dysentery, ascites, heaviness of the limbs. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. Xxxv. rumbling in the intestines, and loose motions (diarrha;a) at other times, is called Vishamagni. Tikshna'gni :- -The digestive heat, which helps the digestion of even a heavy meal within an incredibly short space of time, is called " Keen " (Tikshnagni) and which becoming abnormally augmented begets excessive voracious appetite (Atyagni), helps glutton digest his frequent meals, and produces a parched throat, palate and lips, heat and other dis- comforts. Wlanda'g'ni :—The digestive fire heat which causes the tardy digestion even of a scanty meal, and produces heaviness of the abdomen and head, cough, difficult breathing, water-brash, nausea, and weariness of the limbs simultaneously with the taking thereof, is called dull or sluggish (Mandagni). Metrical Texts :— The digestive fire the Vishama kind brings diseases characterised by the derangement of the Vayu. A keen (Tikshna) digestive fire brings on bilious (Pittcija) affections, while a sluggish (Manda) fire gives rise diseases marked deranged state of the Kapham. Endeavours should be made keep the digestive fire the Sama type normal regular appetite*; in an unimpaired state. TIktc is a (lifk'iencc Ijclwccn "Agni" and "appclile." Ayni includo liile and pancrcalic sccrcliuns, and hence inchcales llie stale of ones diges- tion. Appetite, though not iinening indicator of the ]:)r(jcess, is the eliecl of Agni. Chap. XXXV. The one known as Vishama 'irregular) should cor- rected by a diet consisting' of emollient, acid or saline substances. a case of abnormally keen digestive fire, the medical treatment should consist prescrib- ing purgatives and diet the composition which sweet, cooling, and fatty albuminous matters largely enter. The same treatment should be adopted in (Atyagni) as marked cases of voracious appetite, and a diet consisting of buffalo-milk, or its curd (Dadhi) and liquid buffalo- butter should be prescribed for the patient in addition.

IVIetrical Texts :—The fire, that burns within a person, is godly its subtle essence, and possesses the divine attributes of atom-like invisibility, weight- lessness, etc., and the digestant of food. takes the lymph chyle different tastes for the purpose digestion, and invisible owing its extremely subtle essence. The three vital Vayus known Prana, Apana and Samana, located in their own spheres within the organism, feed it and keep burning. The three stages of man may be roughly described infancy or childhood, (2) youth or middle age, and (y old age or dotage. Childhood extends up to the THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. xxxV. sixteenth year of life, and children may be divided into three different classes, according they are fed milk, milk and boiled rice boiled rice alone. A child lives exclusively on milk up to the first year its life, it is fed on milk and boiled rice (hard food) the second year, and thenceforward nourished with boiled rice (hard food). The middle age of a man extends from the sixteenth to the seventieth year of his life, and exhibits the traits of growth, youth, arrest of de^'elopment and decay. The process of growth building goes the twentieth year life, when youth the age of maturity sets and holds sway over the body of a man up to the thirtieth year of his life,—the strength, semen, and all the organs and vital principles of the body attain (their full maturity the age of forty. Thenceforth decay gradually sets the seventieth year life. After that the strength and energy of a man dwindle day by dav. The organs and virility grow weak and suffer deterioration. The hair turns to a silvery white, the parched skin looks shrivelled and becomes impressed with marks of dotage (crow's feet-marks). The skin hangs down and becomes flabby, the hair begins to fall off, and symptoms of alopecia mark the smooth, sheen and balded pate. The respira- tion becomes laboured and painful. The body, worn out like an old and dilapidated building, shakes with fits Chap. XXXV. distressing cough. Such a man is incapable all acts, and does but imperfectly perform all bodily functions. He has grown old. The dose of medicine should be increased with the age of a patient till the age of decay, and reduced after the expiry the seventieth year the quantity ( which is usuall}' prescribed for an youth of sixteen). Authoritative verses on the Sub- ject :—Kapham increased during the years childhood and Pittam in middle age ; while an increase of VcCyu (nervous derangement^ marks the closing years life. The use strong drastic purgatives, and cauterisation are alike prohibited cases of children and old men. They should be used only weakened or modified forms if found indispensably necessary. has been stated before that the body person either stout, thin average (middling) bulk.

We have already discoursed on the strength of the body. Now in a particular case under treatment, primarily incumbent the physician enquire whether the patient is naturally weak, or has become THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XXXy, so through a deranged condition of the bodily humours or old age. And since the strength of a patient which makes all remedial measures (such cauterisa- tion, etc.) possible, should regarded the grandest auxiliary to a medical treatment of whatsoever nature it may be. lYIetrical Texts : —There are some men who are strong though thin while others are weak, though stout ; and accordingly a physician should deter- mine the bodily strength patient by enquiring about the capacity his physical endurance and labour. Sattvam or fortitude denotes a kind of (stoic) indifference of one's mind sensations and sources of pleasure or pain. A man of strong fortitude (Sattvika temperament) capable of enduring everything, or any amount of pain by repressing his mind with the help of his will or intel- lect. A man of a Rajasika turn of mind (strong, active, energetic) may be made to patiently submit to a course painful medical treatment by means of persuasive counsels and the logic of the inevitable, whereas a man of a Tamasika temperament worldly cast of mind characterised Nescience) is simply overwhelmed at the prospect of bodily pain. Later on, we shall have occasion deal with the different types of physical treatment and of remedial agents in general. particular country, season Chap. XXXV. ] ^25 of the year, particular disease or a peculiar mode living, any particular kind physical labour exercise, or the specific properties of the water of any particular locality, or day sleep, or a juice of any parti- cular taste, is or are said to be congenial (Satmya) to man, or a man is said to be naturalised to these condi- tions and environments, when they fail to produce any injurious effect on his health, though naturally unwhole- some to others. Metrical Texts :—A thing of any taste what- soever, any kind of habit physical exercise is said to be congenial to a man which, instead of in any way telling on his health, contributes his positive pleasure and comfort. Features of an Anupa country:— country may be classed either as an Anupa, Jangala or a Sadharana one, according its distinctive physical features. Anupa watery swampy) country contains large number of pools, and is wooded and undulated with chains lofty hills traversing its area, and which is impassable owing its net- works of rivers and sheets of accumulated rain-water rippling before the currents the gentle, humid air. inhabited race stout, shapely and soft- bodied men, susceptible to Vatala and Kaphaja diseases.

Anupa watery swampy) country contains large number of pools, and is wooded and undulated with chains lofty hills traversing its area, and which is impassable owing its net- works of rivers and sheets of accumulated rain-water rippling before the currents the gentle, humid air. inhabited race stout, shapely and soft- bodied men, susceptible to Vatala and Kaphaja diseases.

chapter: Chapter XXXV: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which deals with clinical observations (A'turopakramaniya- madhyaryam). physician should first observe the vital condition (Ayu) the patient before commencing the medical treatment. After that, the nature the disease, the country and season of the year in which it has made its appearance, as well as the state of digestion, age, body, strength, disposition, habit, previous medicine, natural temperament and the power endurance the patient, etc. should be observed and carefully examined. Characteristic features long lived man :—Men, the dimensions whose hands, legs, sides, back, nipples the breast, teeth, face, shoulders and forehead exceed the average, well those whose eyes, arms, phalanges and fingers are longer than the ordinary ones should be regarded as going live long. Those who have broad chests, broad eye-brows with broader spaces intervening between the muscles the breasts, and who take deeper inspirations of breath, will be long lived. Those whose necks, thighs, and generative organs are shorter than those of the average type, or those whose voices and umbilical cavities -are deep, and whose breasts are unraised an d thick-set, and external ears broad, fleshy and haiiy

chapter: Chapter XXXVI: Xow we shall discourse the Chapter, which treats miscellaneous remedies for swellings, etc. (lYI ish raka-m ad hyayam) . * Metrical Texts :—A medicinal plaster, com- posed of Matulanga, Agnimantha, Devadaru, Mahaush- dham, Ahinsra, and Rasna pasted together and applied to the seat of the affection, leads to the resolution of a swelhng, due to the action of the deranged Vayu. A plaster composed of Durva, Xalamulam, Madhu- kam, and Chandanam, as well as plasters composed drugs of cooling properties,t brings about the resolution inflammatory swelling the Pittaja type, and proves similarly beneficial to a traumatic swelling, or to one which has its origin in the vitiated condition of the blood. Measures, laid down in connection with swelling resulting from the effects of poison, would lead the resolution of a Pittaja swelling as well. The nomenclature the chapter based, according to certain authorities, on the fact its jointly treating of eight principal processes of absorption, suppuration, spontaneous bursting, etc. of a swelling ; while some there are who hold that the name of the chapter derived from the fact its containing remedial measures commonly (Mishrakam beneficial to swellings and ulcers. Belonging the groups ( Gana of medicinal herbs, which go by the names of their first components, such the Kakalyadi group (Gana), the Utpalidi group etc
english_text: measure, failing produce any tangible effect, may repeated quick succession difficult dangerous disease, empirically found beneficial in the case under treatment. The intelligent physician, ^vho, considering the nature of the season, etc., fully conforms to the abovesaid rules of medical treatment, conquers the bodily distempers and dispels the gloom of Death from the world with his medical skill. Thus ends the Thirty-fifth Chapter of the Sulrasthinam in the Sushrula Samhila, which treats of clinical oliservalions. CHAPTER XXXVI. Xow we shall discourse the Chapter, which treats miscellaneous remedies for swellings, etc. (lYI ish raka-m ad hyayam) . * Metrical Texts :—A medicinal plaster, com- posed of Matulanga, Agnimantha, Devadaru, Mahaush- dham, Ahinsra, and Rasna pasted together and applied to the seat of the affection, leads to the resolution of a swelhng, due to the action of the deranged Vayu. Measures, laid down in connection with swelling resulting from the effects of poison, would lead the resolution of a Pittaja swelling as well. The nomenclature the chapter based, according to certain authorities, on the fact its jointly treating of eight principal processes of absorption, suppuration, spontaneous bursting, etc. of a swelling ; while some there are who hold that the name of the chapter derived from the fact its containing remedial measures commonly (Mishrakam beneficial to swellings and ulcers. Belonging the groups ( Gana of medicinal herbs, which go by the names of their first components, such the Kakalyadi group (Gana), the Utpalidi group etc. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXXVI. plaster, composed of Ajagandha, Ashvagaiidha, Kala, Asarala, Ekaishika, and Ajashringi pasted together, and applied the spot, leads the resolution Kaphaja swelling (appearing any part the body). A plaster, composed of the components of the above- said groups of medicinal drugs and Lodhram, Pathya, Pinditakam, and Ananta, brings about the resolution of a swelling due the simultaneous derangement the three fundamental humours the body (Sannipatikam). A medicinal plaster, prescribed for swelling due to the deranged ^'ayu, should be applied mixing with a little rock salt, acid (Amla), and oil clarified butter. Similarly, a plaster, prescribed for the resolution of a Pittaja swelling, should be applied cold, and with a little quantity of milk added plaster for the resolution of a Kaphaja swelling should applied warm to the affected part, and with the addition of a considerable quantity of an alkali and cow's urine.

english_text: Parehana Plasters :—A piaster composed of the seeds of Shana, Mula, Shigru, Tila and Sarshapa, Yava-powder, Kinva (enzyme), and linseed pasted together, or one consisting of thermogenetic drugs (such as Kustha, Aguru, etc.), would establish suppuration a swelling. Chap. XXXVI.] Darrana Plasters :^A plaster composed of Chiravilva, Agiiika, Danti, Chitraka, Hayamaraka and the dung of pigeons, vultures and storks (Kanka) pasted together, would lead to the spontaneous bursting of a swelling. An alkali, or its ingredients should garded as a powerful auxiliary bringing about the spontaneous bursting of a swelling. Pidana Plasters :—A plaster composed the roots and bark slimy trees (Shalmali, Shelu, etc.), or of barley, wheat, and Masha pulse powdered together, would increase the secretion pus from an ulcer, or a swelling that has burst.* ShOdhana Plasters :—A Kashayat decoc- tion) Shankhini, Ankota, Sumanah, Karavira, and Suvarchchala, of drugs belonging the group (Ganas) known the Aragvadadi-Varga, should used in washing and purifying (asepsising) the contents of an ulcer, or a secreting swelling. ShOdhana Varti :—A lint saturated with plaster of Ajagandha, Ajashringi, Gavakshi, Langalahva- Putika, Chitraka, Patha, Vidanga, Ela, Renuka, Tri-katu, Yavakshara, the five kinds of salt, Manahshila, * The plaster should be applied all round the swelling, leaving its head free and exposed. A decoction with one part of a drug mixed with four, eight or six- teen parts of water, the whole being boiled down to a quarter pan of the entire quantity. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XXXVI Kasisa, Trivrita, Danti, Haritala and the Saurashtra- mrittikjl, and inserted into ari ulcer or an open swelling, brings about the purification of its interior, and these drugs and substances should be regarded the ingre- dients of Shodhana Vartis (aseptic plugs). Shodhana Kalka :—A kalka (aseptic paste), composed of the preceding drugs and substances, possesed of the virtue purifying the interior of an ulcer, or open swelling. Oil or clarified butter prepared with the aforesaid Ajagandha, Ajashringi, etc, and Kasisa, Katurohini, Jatikanda, and the two kinds of Haridra, and applied to ulcer open swelling, purifies its interior. The medicated Ghritam prepared with the expressed juice of Arka roots, Uttama, the milky juice Snuhi plants, drugs abounding in alkalis, Jati-roots, the two kinds Haridra, Kasisa, Katurohini and the aforesaid plug-drugs (Sodhana-Varti) pasted together, should be regarded as possessed of a virtue similar to the preced- ing one. medicated oil prepared with Mayuraka, (Apang), Rajabriksha, Ximva, Kosh^taki, Tila, Vrihati, Kantakari, Haritala, Manahshila, and the afore- said plug-drugs (purgative drugs according to others), should be used for the purpose of purifying the interior of an ulcer. A pulverised compound consisting of Kasisa, Saindhava, Kinva, Vach.1, the two kinds Haridra, Chap. XXXVI.] SUTRASTHA'NAM.

english_text: and the component drugs the aseptic plug powdered together, should used for the puri- fication the cavity ulcer. For the same purpose a condensed extract (Rasa-Kriya)* should made of the essence the drugs belonging the Salsaradi, Patoladi, and Triphaladi groups. Dhupanam :—A wise physician should fumigate (Dhupanam) an ulcer with the fumes compound consisting Sriveshtaka, Sarjarasa, Sarala, Devadaru, and the drugs belonging the Salsaradi group, pulverised together and made into an raseptici fumigat- ing compound. cold infusion (Shhita-Shritam) trees (Vata, Audumvara, Ashvattha, etc.) which are cooling and astringent in their virtue, should be used healing setting up a process of granulation in an ulcer. The Ropana-Varti :—Plugs of drugs such as Soma, Amrita (Gulancha), and Ashvagandha, or of those belonging to the Kakolyadi group, the sprouts of milk-exuding trees (Kshirivrikshas such Vata, Audumvara, etc.) and inserted into ulcer tend help its granulation (Ropana). A paste (Kalka) of Samanga, Soma, Sarala wood, Soma-Valka, (red^ The process consists in mixing the drugs wiih water weighing eight or sixteen times their combined weight, and then boiling them down an eighth or sixteenth part of the entire quantity. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap, xxxvi. Chandana, and drugs belonging the Kakol)'adi group, is recommended for the healing of an ulcer. medicated Ghritam, prepared with the Prithak- parni, Atmagupta, Haridra, Daruharidra, Malati, Sita, and drugs belonging to the Kakolyadi group, is renowned for its healing properties. medicated oil prepared with Kalanusari, Aguru, Haridra, Daru- Haridra, Devadaru, Priyangu, and Lodhra, is possesed of a similar efficacy. pulverised compound consisting Kanguka, Triphala, Lodhra, Kasisam, Shravana and the barks of Dhava and Ashvakarna powdered together, possessed of a similar healing property. The use of a pulverised compound consisting of Priyangu, Sarjarasa, Pushpa-kasisa, Tvaka, and Dhava powdered together is commended for the healing of an ulcer. A condensed extract ( Rasakriya ) the bark milk-exuding trees ( such Vata, Ashvattha etc. ) and the drugs known the Triphala, should be successively used for the healing of an ulcer. Utsardanam :—The drugs known as Apam^rga, Ashvagandha, Talapatri, Suvarchhala and those belong- ing the Kakolyadi group, should used for the growth of flesh in an ulcer ( Utsadana Avasa'danam :—A compound consisting K^sisa, Saindhava ( rock salt ), Kinvam, Kuruvinda, Chap. XXXVI.] SUtRAStHi^NAM. Manalishila, the shell of a hen's egg, the blossoms of Jati flowers, the seeds of .Shirisha, and Karanja, and powders of the abovesaid metals ( Dhatus ) mixed gether, should be used in destroying the fleshy super- growths of an ulcer { Avasadanam ). A wise physician should use all the drugs and sub- stances as have been enumerated in connection with the healing or establishing of suppuration, etc. in ulcer, or as many of them as would be available at the time.

Utsardanam :—The drugs known as Apam^rga, Ashvagandha, Talapatri, Suvarchhala and those belong- ing the Kakolyadi group, should used for the growth of flesh in an ulcer ( Utsadana Avasa'danam :—A compound consisting K^sisa, Saindhava ( rock salt ), Kinvam, Kuruvinda, Chap. XXXVI.] SUtRAStHi^NAM. Manalishila, the shell of a hen's egg, the blossoms of Jati flowers, the seeds of .Shirisha, and Karanja, and powders of the abovesaid metals ( Dhatus ) mixed gether, should be used in destroying the fleshy super- growths of an ulcer { Avasadanam ). A wise physician should use all the drugs and sub- stances as have been enumerated in connection with the healing or establishing of suppuration, etc. in ulcer, or as many of them as would be available at the time.

chapter: Chapter XXXVII: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter, which deals with the distinctive traits of the different classes of soil commended for the growth culture of medicinal herbs (Bhumi-Pravibha'ga-Vijna'niya- madhyaryam). These are the general features of a ground which recommended for the culture of medicinal plants herbs. A plot of ground, whose surface is not broken rendered uneven by the presence of holes, ditches, gravel and stones, nor is loose in its character, and which not disfigured by ant-hills, nor used for the purposes of a cremation or execution ground, and which does not occupy the site of a holy temple, is favourable for the growth of medicinal herbs. A ground which possesses a soil which is glossy, firm, steady, black, yellowish red and does not contain any sand, potash or any other alkaline substance, and is favourable to the germination of plants and easily pervious the roots of plants growing thereon, and which supplied with the necessary moisture from a close or adjacent stream or reservoir of water, is recommended for the growth of medicinal plants and herbs. Plants should regard- ed as partaking of the virtues of the ground they grow upon. plant, growing in such a commendable site, should be examined as to its being infested with worms

chapter: Chapter XXXVII: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter, which deals with the distinctive traits of the different classes of soil commended for the growth culture of medicinal herbs (Bhumi-Pravibha'ga-Vijna'niya- madhyaryam). These are the general features of a ground which recommended for the culture of medicinal plants herbs. A plot of ground, whose surface is not broken rendered uneven by the presence of holes, ditches, gravel and stones, nor is loose in its character, and which not disfigured by ant-hills, nor used for the purposes of a cremation or execution ground, and which does not occupy the site of a holy temple, is favourable for the growth of medicinal herbs. Plants should regard- ed as partaking of the virtues of the ground they grow upon. plant, growing in such a commendable site, should be examined as to its being infested with worms
english_text: hills cropping intervals thereon, and which covered with growths of weeds and under-shrubs, and is endued with a dark soil, kept moist and sappy the percolation of invisible (subterranean) water, should be looked upon as permeated with the essential properties of sky (Akashaguna). According to certain authorities, the roots, leaves, bark, milk\^ exudations, essence and fruits (seeds) of medicinal plants and herbs, should be respectively culled in the early part of the rains (Pravrit) and in the rainy season proper (Varsha\ autumn, (Sharat), fore- winter (Hemanta), spring (Vasanta) and summer (Grishma). But we cannot subscribe to that opinion inasmuch the nature or essential temperament of the earth is both cool (Saumya) and hot (Agneya). Accordingly drugs cooling virtues should be culled during the cold seasons of the year, and the heat- making ones in the hot season, as they do not become divested of their native virtues at those seasons of the year. Medicinal plants of cooling virtues, which are grown on a soil of cool temperament and are culled during the cool seasons of the year, become intensely sweet, cooling and glossy. These remarks hold good of other medicinal plants and herbs. Herbs of purgative properties, which are grown on soil permeated with the specific virtues of water or earth matter, should be culled as the most effective of their kind. Similarly, herbs of emetic virtues should Chap. XXXVII.] SUTRASTHA'NAM. 3.^9 be culled from a ground permeated with the essential virtues of fire, sky and air. Herbs exercising both purgative and emetic virtues should culled from ground exhibiting features common both the two aforesaid classes soil. Similarly, herbs possessed soothing properties (Sanshamanam)* are found to exert stronger action in the event of their being reared on a soil permeated with the essential properties of sk5\ All medicinal herbs and substances should be used fresh as possible, excepting Pippali, Vidanga, Madhu, Guda, and Ghritam, fwhich should be used in a matured condition i.e. not before a year;. The milky juice or sap of a medicinal tree or plant should be regarded as strong and active under all circumstances. Herbs and drugs, that had been culled or collected within the year, might be taken and used in making up a medicinal recipe in a case where fresh ones would not be available. Authoritative Verses on the Sub- ject—Medicinal herbs and plants should recognis- ed and identified with the help of cowherds, hermits, huntsmen, forest-dwellers, and those who cull the fruits and edible roots of the forest. definite time can be laid down for the culling of the leaves and roots of * Herbs drugs, which virtue of their own essential properties soothe subdue disease without eliminating the morbid humours or without exercising any emetic or purgative action. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap, xxxvil.

chapter: Chapter XXXVIII: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which deals with the general classification of drugs according their therapeutical properties (Dravya-Sangra- haniya-madhyaryam). These drugs are usually made into thirty-seven different groups (Gana) which are as follows The Vidarigandha'cli Croup:—The drugs known as Vidarigandha, Vidari, Sahadeva, Vishvadeva, Shvadanstra, Prithakparni, Shatavari, Sariva, black Sariva, Jivaka, Rishavaka, Mahasaha, Kshudra-Saha, Vrihati, Kantakari, Punarnava, Eranda, Hansapadi, Vrishchikah, and Rishavi, form the group known as the Vidari-gandhadi. IVIetrical Text :—The present gi-oup of drugs subdues the action the deranged Vayu and Pittam and proves beneficial in phthisis ;Shosha~^, Gulma, aching of the limbs, Urdha Shvasa and cough. The A'ragvadha'di Group :—The drugs known Aragvadha, Madana, Gopaghonta, Kutaja, Patha, Kantaki^ Patala, Murva, Indrayava, Saptaparna, Ximva, Kuruntaka, Dasi-kuruntaka, the two kinds Karanja, Patola, Kiratttikua, Guduchi, Chitraka, Sh^ngshta, and Susha^•i form the group known the Aragvadhjidi
english_text: medicinal plants, etc., such as are used in compounding the recipe, which is called the Patra-lavanam, and which covers, within its therapeutic range, diseases, which are peculiar the entire organism (such as Vata-vyadhi, etc).* soil admits of being divided into six different classes according its smell, colour, taste, etc. the sap of a medicinal plant may assume an}' of the six different tastes through its contact with the peculiar properties of the soil it grows Tastes such sweet, etc., remain latent in water, which imparts them to the soil in a patent or perceptible condition. A plot of ground, exhibiting traits peculiar to all the five fundamental material principles (such as the earth water, fire, etc.), said possessed soil general character (Sadharani Bhumi), and medicinal plants and herbs partake of the specific virtues of the soil the}' grown on. Drugs, whether fresh or old, and emitting a contrary smell, or in any way affected as regards their natural sap juice, should not be used for pharmaceutical purposes. The virtues of such medicinal drugs and substances such Vidanga, Pippali, Madhu, and Guda, improve ITence the doctrine, as regards the culling of the difterent parts of a medicinal plant such the leaves, roots, etc., in the different seasons of the year, naturally falls to the ground. Chap. XXXVII. SUTRASTHA'NAM. (after year. Accordingly all drugs and n^.edicinal herbs, excepting the preceding ones, should be used fresh and unsoiled, or uninjured by insects. Blood, nails, or hair etc., of animals, '^officinally laid down to be used in our Pharmacopoeia), should be taken from young and healthy animals, and the ordure, urine, or milk of an animal, (enjoined to be used for medicinal purposes), should collected a time after it has completed its digestion. The pharmacy and the medicinal store of a physician should occupy commendable site and an auspicious quarter of the sky (Xorth or East), and the collected medicines should be kept tied in pieces of clean linen, or stored earthen vessels and hollow tubes of wood, or suspended on wooden pegs. Thus ends the thirty-seventh Chapter the in the Sushruta Samhit^ wliich treats the Classification of grounds for the culture of medicinal plants and herhs, etc. CHAPTER XXXVIII. Chap. XXXVIII. Metrical Text : — The group under discus- sion destroys the deranged Kaphani and the effects poison and proves beneficial in cases of Meha (^morbid discharges from the urethra), Kushtha, fever, vomiting and itching the body and acts purifying (aseptic) agent in the case of an ulcer. The Varuna'di Group : —The drugs known Varuna, Artagala, Shigru, Madhu-Shigi-u, Tarkari, Mesha-Shringi, Putika, Xaktamala, Morata, Agni- mantha, the two kinds Sairiyaka, Vimvi, Vasuka, Vasira, Chitraka, Shatavari, Mlva, Ajashringi, Darbha, and the two kinds Vrihati form the group known as the Varunadi. IVIetrical Text : —The group is possessed of the efficacy reducing the deranged Kapham and fat and proves efficacious in cases of cephalaegia, Gulma and internal abscesses. The Viratarva'di Group :—The drugs known as Virataru, the two kinds of Sahachara, Darbha, Vrikshadani, Gundra, I\'ala, Kusha, Kasha, Ashma- bhedaka, Agnimantha, Morata Vasuka, Vasira, Bhalluka, Kuruntaka, Indivara, Kapotavanka, and Sh^adanstra enter into the composition of the group known as the Viratarvadi. The Sa^Iasara'di Group :—The group medicinal drugs, known as the Salasaradi, consists Salasara, Ajakarna, Khadira, Kadara, Kalaskandha, Kramuka, Bhurjja, Meshashringi, Tinisha, Chandana, Kuchandana, Shinshapa, Shirisha, Asana, Dhava, Arjuna, Tala, Shaka, Xaktamala, Putika Ashvakarna, Aguru and Kaliyaka. IVIctrical Texts -The group the drugs, known the Salasaradi Gana, destroys the germ of Kushtha, absorbs the deranged fat and Kapham and proves beneficial in morbid discharges from the urethra (Meha\ chlorosis or jaundice (Pandu). The Rodhrardi Group:—The group of medi- cinal drugs known as the Rodhradi consists Rodhra, Savararodhra, Palasha Kutannata, Ashoka, Phanji, Katphala, Elabaluka, Sallaki, Jingini, Kadamva, Sala and Kadali. IVIetrical Texts :—The group antidotal the deranged Kapham and fat, astringent its properties, removes vaginal and uterine disorders, neutralises the effects poison (anti toxic) and acts stj'ptic and purifying agent in a case of ulcer and arrests all secretions and excretions of the body. The Arkardi Group :--The drugs known the Arka, Alarka, the two kinds Karanja, Chap.

english_text: XXXVIII.] Nagadanti, Mayuraka, Bhargi, Rasna, Indrapiishpi, Kshudrashveta Mahashveta, Vrishchikali, Alavana and Tapasha-Vriksha, enter into the composition the group known as the Arkadi Gana. Metrical Texts:—The group known the Arkadi destroys Kaphani, fat, and the effects poison. It acts as a vermifuge and specific aseptic agent the case of an ulcer and proves curative in diseases of the skin. The Surasa'di Group :—The drugs known Surasa, white Surasa, Fainjjhaka, Arjaka, Bhustrina, Sugandhaka, Sumukha, Kalamala, Kashamarda, Kshavaka, Kharpushpa, Vidanga, Katphala_, Surasi, Nirgundi, Kulahala, Indurakarnika, Phanji, Prachi- vala, Kakamachi and Vishamushtika form the group known as the Surasadi Gana. IVIetrical Texts :—The group acts vermifuge and is an aseptic agent. subdues the deranged Kapham and proves beneficial catarrh, non-relish for food, asthma and cough. The lYIushkaka'di Group:—The group medicinal drugs known as the Mushkakadi consists Mushkaka, Palasha, Dhava, Chitraka, Madana, Shinshapa, Vajra-Vriksha and Triphala. Metrical Text :—The present group possessed of the therapeutic virtue o-f destroying fat and THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. XXXVIII. removing the defects of semen. Meha, piles, jaundice, chlorosis, gravels and urinary calculi the bladder are the diseases which yield Lo its curative efficac)'. The Pippalya'di Group: -The group medicinal drugs known the Pippalyadi consists Pippali, Pippali mulam, Chavya, Chitraka, Shringavera, Maricha, Hasti-Pippali, Harenuka, Ela, Ajamoda, Indrayava, Patha, Jiraka, Sarshapa, Maha-Nimva-Phala, Hingu, Bhargi, Madhurasa, Ativisha, Vacha, Vidanga and Katurohini. IVIetrical Text :—The present group acts good appetiser and is an absorbant of intestinal mucous and unassimilated lymph chyle. The range of its thera- peutical application includes catarrh, deranged Kapham and Vatam, non-relish for food, abdominal glands, colic and gastralgia. The Elardi Group :—The group of medicinal drugs known as the Eladi-Gana consists of Ela, Tagara, Kushtha, M^nsi, Dhyamaka, Tvaka, Patra, Naga- pushpa, Priyangu, Harenuka, Vyaghranakha, Shukti, Clianda, Sthauneyaka, Shriveshtaka, Chocha, Choraka, Valaka, Guggulu, Sarjarasa, Turushka, Kunduruka, Aguru, Sprikka, Ushira, Bhadradaru, Kumkuma, Punnaga and Keshara. IVIetrical Text :—The therapeutic virtue the group consists in subduing the action of V^yu and Chap. XXXVIII.] Kapham and in neutralising the effects of poison. It is cosmetic and arrests* the eruption pimples and other vegetations on the skin such as rash, urticaria etc. and checks the itching sensation incidental thereto. The Vach2rcli and Haridrardi Groups :- The groups known the Vachadi and Haridradi Ganas, respectively consist of Vacha, Musta, Ativisha, Abhaya, Bhadradaru, Nagakeshara (Vachadi), Haridra, Daruharidr^, Kalashi, Kutaja seeds and Madhuka (Haridradi).

english_text: IVIetrical Text:—These two groups are the purifiers breast milk and specifically act the assimilators of the deranged humours of the body, their curative properties being markedly witnessed cases of mucous dysentery (Amatisiira). The Shy^mardi Group : -The drugs known Shyama, Mahd-Shyam^, Trivrit, Danti, Shan- khini, Tilvaka, Kampillaka, Ramyaka, Kramuka, Putrashroni, Gavakshi, Rajavriksha, the two kinds of Karanja, Guduchi, Saptala, Chhagalantri, Sudh^ and Suvarnakhiri, form the group known the Shyam^di Gana. Metrical Text :—This group possessed the therapeutic virtue curing abdominal glands and acts as an anti-toxic. proves beneficial An^ha THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXXVIII. 'epistasis), abdominal dropsy and diarrhoea and one of the most reliable purgatives cases obstinate constipation the bowels with suppression urine and distention of the abdomen (Udavarta). The Vrihatya'di Group :-^The drugs known Vrihati, Kantakarika, Kutajaphala, Patha and Madhuka combinedly form the group known as the Vrihatyadi Gana. IVIctrical Text :—The group is a good digestant or assimilator the deranged humours. It subdues the deranged Vata, Pitta and Kapham and proves efficacious in cases of nausea, water-brash, d^'suria and non- relish for food. The Patola'di Group:— The drugs known Patola, Chandana, Kuchandana, Murva, Guduchi, Patha, and Katurohini form the group known the Patoladi Gana. IVIetrical Text :—The group is a febrifuge and anti-toxic, and its therapeutic action consists in destroy- ing the action of the deranged Pittam and Kapham. restores the natural relish the patient for food, removes vomiting, and proves beneficial ulcers, and itching erythematous eruptions. The Ka'kolya'di Group :—The drugs known as Kakoli-Kshira-Kakoli, Jivaka, Rishabhaka, Mudga- parni, Mashaparni, Meda, Mah^meda, Chhinna-ruha, Chap. XXXVIII.] SUTRASTHA'NAM. Karkata-Shringi, Tugakshiri, Padmaka, Prapaundarika, Riddhi, Vriddhi, Mridvika, Jivanti and Madhuka, com- binedly form the group known as the Kakolyadi Gana. IVIetrical Text :—The group medicinal drugs under discussion subdues the action the deranged Pittam, blood and Vayu. increases the quantity of milk the breast f galactagogue) and favours the accumulation of phlegm (Kapham) in the body. It is a restorative and an elixir and is endued with the therapeutic virtue of augmenting the virile potency of a man. The Ushaka'di Group :—The medicinal drugs and substances known as Ushaka (alkaline earth) Saindhava salt, Shilajatu, the two kinds of Kasisa, Hingu and Tutthaka enter into the composition the group known as the Ushakadi Gana. IVIetrical Text :— It destroys kapham mucous), absorbs the fat of the body and proves curative in cases stone gravel the bladder (urinary calculi), dysuria and abdominal glands fGulma\ The Sa'riva'di Group :—The drugs known as Sariva, Madhuka, Chandana, Kuchandana, Padmaka, Kashmari phala, Madhuka-pushpa and Ushira_, com- binedly form the group known as the Sarivadi Gana.

Metrical Texts :—An attack of hasmoptj-sis readily 5n'elds to the curative virtue of the group under discussion. It is anti-toxic in its character and allays the internal burning sensation of the body. The Parushaka'di Group:—The gioup known as the Parushakadi Gana consists of Parushaka, Dr^ksha, Kat-phala, Dadima, Rajadana, Kataka-phala Shaka-phala and Triphala. lYIetrical Text subdues the deranged Vayu, allays thirst, acts cordial, increases one's relish for food 'and cures the diseased or abnormal com- ponents of urine or its defects. The Priyangvardi Group:— The group medicinal drugs known as the Priyangvadi Gana consists of Priyangu, Samang^, Dhataki, Naga-pushpa, Chandana, Kuchandana, Mocharasa, Rasanjana, Kumbhika, Srotohnjana, Padma-keshara, Jojanvalli, and Dirghamula. The Amvashtha'di Group:—Drugs known Amvashth^, Dhataki flowers, Samanga, Katvanga, Chap. The Nya'grodha'di Croup:—The drugs known as Nyagrodha, Audumvara, Ashvattha, Plaksha, Madhuka, Kapitana, Kakubha, Amra, Koshamra, Chorakapatra, the two sorts of Jamvu, Piyala, Madhuka (Maula), Rohini, Vanjula, Kadamva, Vadari, Tinduki, Sallaki, Rodhra, Savara-Rodhra, Bhallataka, Palasha, and Nandi-Vriksha, combinedly form the group known as the Nyagodhradi Gana. Metrical Texts :—This group proves bene- ficial cases ulcer, cures all disorders the uterus and vagina, favours the adhesion fractured bones and all sorts secretions the body addition to its astringent properties (Sangrahi) and proves curative a case of haemoptysis. anti-fat and assuages the burning sensation the body. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap, xxxviii. The Guduchya'di Group :—The drugs known Guduchi, Ximva, Kustumvuru, Chandana, and Padmaka, combinedly form the group known the Guduchyadi Gana. The lYIusta'di Group :—The group drugs known Mushtadi Gana composed Musta, Haridra, Daru-Haridra, Haritaki, Amlaki, Vibhitaka, Kushtha, Haimavati, Vacha, Patha, Katu- rohini, Sharngashta, Ativisha, Dravidi, Bhallataka and Chitraka. Chap. XXXVIII.] ^53 Metrical Text :—The group under discussion destroys the deranged Shleshma, cures uterine and vaginal disorders, purifies the breast milk of a mother, and acts as a good digestant (Pachana). The Triphalar Group :- The drugs known Haritaki, Amlaki and Vibhitaka, constitute the group known as the Triphaladi Gana. Metrical Text :—The present group destroys the action of the deranged Vayu, Kapham and Pittam and proves curative in Meha, and in diseases of the skin (Kushtham). good appetiser, improves the eyesight and proves beneficial chronic intermittent fever (Vishama-jvara). The Trikatu Group :—The Trikatu group consists of Pippali, Maricha and Shringavera. Metrical Text :—It destroys fat and Kapham, proves curative cutaneous affections, leprosy (Kushtha), and morbid discharges from the urethra, and is possessed of the virtue curing abdominal glands, catarrh, dullness of the appetite and indigestion. The A'mlakya'di Group :—The group known as the Amlakyadi Gana consists Amlaki, Haritaki, Pippali and Chitraka. Metrical Text :—The present group of medici- nal drugs acts as a general febrifuge and may used ^54 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap, xxxviil. with advantage in fevers of whatsoever type. Moreover, it is an aphrodisiac and acts as a general tonic resto- rative and appetiser, destroying the deranged Kapham and improving the eyesight. The Trapvardi Group :—Tlie group known as the Trapvadi Gana consists of Trapu, Sisa, Tamra, Rajata, Krishna-Lauha, Suvarna and Lohamala. lYIctrical Text :—The present group regarded as a good vermifuge and possessed of the virtue of neutrahsing the effects of chemical poison originated through incompatible combinations. Its therapeutic range covers jaundice, chlorosis, Melia (morbid secre- tions from the uretlira), Hridroga (heart disease), thirst and maladies incidental to the effects of poison. The La'ksha'di Group :—The drugs known as the Laksha, Arevata, Kutaja, Ashvamara, Katphalam, Haridra, Daru-Haridra, Ximva, Saptachchhada, Malati, and Trayamana form the Lakshadi Gana. Metrical Text :—This consists astringent, bitter and sweet taste (Rasa) and acts good vermifuge and purifying (aseptic) agent cases of bad, malignant or indolent ulcers.

chapter: Chapter XXXVIII: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter which deals with the general classification of drugs according their therapeutical properties (Dravya-Sangra- haniya-madhyaryam). These drugs are usually made into thirty-seven different groups (Gana) which are as follows The Vidarigandha'cli Croup:—The drugs known as Vidarigandha, Vidari, Sahadeva, Vishvadeva, Shvadanstra, Prithakparni, Shatavari, Sariva, black Sariva, Jivaka, Rishavaka, Mahasaha, Kshudra-Saha, Vrihati, Kantakari, Punarnava, Eranda, Hansapadi, Vrishchikah, and Rishavi, form the group known as the Vidari-gandhadi. IVIetrical Text :—The present gi-oup of drugs subdues the action the deranged Vayu and Pittam and proves beneficial in phthisis ;Shosha~^, Gulma, aching of the limbs, Urdha Shvasa and cough. The A'ragvadha'di Group :—The drugs known Aragvadha, Madana, Gopaghonta, Kutaja, Patha, Kantaki^ Patala, Murva, Indrayava, Saptaparna, Ximva, Kuruntaka, Dasi-kuruntaka, the two kinds Karanja, Patola, Kiratttikua, Guduchi, Chitraka, Sh^ngshta, and Susha^•i form the group known the Aragvadhjidi
english_text: Diseases due the deranged Kapham and Pittam prove amenable to its curative properties, which extend to cases of cutaneous affections (Kushtham) as well. Now we shall describe Chap. XXXVIII.] the five groups medicinal roots (Mulam), each consisting of similar number of components. The Svalpa Panchamulam Croup :— The group known the mmor group five roots (Svalpa-Pancha-Mula) consists of the roots of medicinal plants known the Trikantaka, the two species Vrihati, Prithakparni, and Vidarigandha. lYIetrical Texts :—The compound possesses a taste blended astringent, bitter and sweet. It is a tonic and aphrodisiac, subdues the deranged Yayu and proves soothing to the deranged Pittam. The IVlahat Panchamula Croup:— The one known as the great or the major group five medicinal roots (Mahat-Pancha-Mula) consists the roots such trees Vilva, Agnimantha, Tuntuka, Patala and Kashmari. Metrical Texts :— It bitter taste and subdues the deranged Kapham and Vatam. light (easily digestible) and appetising, and acquires sub- sequent sweet taste in its reaction (Anurasa). The Dashamula Croup :—The two preceding groups in combination form the one techni- cally known as the Dasha-Mulam (the ten roots), which is possessed of the virtue destroying the deranged Vata, Pittam and Kapham. It proves beneficial in cases of asthma and difficult respiration. acts good THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. XXXVIII. digestant in respect of undigested lymph chyle, etc and is used with satisfactory results in all types of fever. The Valli-Panchamulam Group:— The group consisting of the roots of the five medicinal creepers known Vidari, Sariva, Rajani, Guduchi and Aja-Shringi, is called the Valli-Panchamulam. The Pancha-kantakam Groups— Simi- larly, the group consisting the five medicinal (thorny) shrubs known Karamradda, Trikantaka, Sairiyaka, Shatavari, and Gridhranakhi, called the Pancha- Kantaka. Metrical Texts :—The two preceding groups prove curative Hcemoptysis and all the tliree types of anasarca or cedema (Shopha). Moreover, it has the incontestable virtue of arresting all sorts of urethral discharges and is a potent remedy in all cases of seminal disorders. The Pancha-Trina Group :- The group consisting of the five medicinal herbs the grass species) and known Kusha, Kasha, Nala, Darbha, Kandekshuka, is called the Pancha-Trina. Metrical Texts :—Cases of Haemoptysis, renal defects or of uninary diseases are found to speedily yield the curative efficacy of the compound internally administered through the medium of cow's milk. Metrical Texts -.—The first two of the afore- Chap. XXXVIII.] SUTRASTHA'NAM. said groups of Panchamulas (viz., the Svalpa and the Vrihat Panchamulas) are possessed the virtue destroying the deranged Vata, while the one standing in the bottom of the list (Trina-Panchamula) is endued with the property of killing the deranged Pittam. Those stand- ing third and fourth in order of enumeration (the Valli and Kantaka Panchamulas) subdue the deranged Kapham.

chapter: Chapter XXXIX: Xow we shall discourse on the Chapter which treats medicinal drugs possessed cleansing (cathartic') or soothing effects (Samshodhana-Samsha- maniya-madhyaryam). Emetic Drugs :—The drugs known Madana fruits, Kutaja, Jimutaka, Ikshvaku, Dhamagarba, Krita-vedhana, Sarshapa, Vidanga, Pippali, Karan- jaka, Prapunnada, Kovidara, Korvudara, Arishta, Ashva- gandha, Vidula, Vandhujivaka, Shveta, Shanapushpi, Vimvi, Vacha, Mrigervaru and Chitra, etc. are possessed of emetic properties. Out of these the fruits (seeds) plants preceding Kovidara the abovesaid list (from the ]\Iadana fruits those of the Prapunnada) and the roots of plants from Kovidara its close, should be used. Purgative Drugs :—The trees, plants, herbs and creepers, etc. known Trivrita, Shyama, Danti, Dravanti, Saptala, Shankhini, Vishanika, Gavakshi, Chhagalantri, Snuk, Suvarnakshiri, Chitraka, Kinihi, Kusha, Kasha, Tilvaka, Kampillaka, Ranwaka, Patala, Puga, Haritaki, Amalaka, Bibhitaka, Xilini, Chatur- angula, Eranda, Putika, Mah^vriksha, Saptachchhada, Arka, and J3''otishmati, etc. are possessed of purgative properties. english_text: The groups of medicinal drugs and roots have thus been briefly described, which will be more elaborately dealt with later on in the chapter on Therapeutics. intelligent physician should prepare plasters, decoctions, medicated oils, Ghritas (medicated clarified butter) or potions, according to the exigencies of each individual case.* The groups enumerated above should be therapeutically used according to the nature the deranged humours involved each individual case. Only two, three or four drugs the same medicinal group, or a similar number of drugs chosen from the the different groups, or a group of medicinal drugs in its entirety, or combination with another, should used according to the indications of any particular case, as the physician, in his discretion, would determine. Additional Text :—These drugs may be duly culled in all seasons of the year, and should be stored in a room protected from smoke, blasts cold, wind and rain. Thus ends the thirty-eighth Chapter of the Sutrasth^nam in the Sushruta Samhit5, which deals with the classification of drugs according their therapeutical use. CHAPTER XXXIX. Xow we shall discourse on the Chapter which treats medicinal drugs possessed cleansing (cathartic') or soothing effects (Samshodhana-Samsha- maniya-madhyaryam). Emetic Drugs :—The drugs known Madana fruits, Kutaja, Jimutaka, Ikshvaku, Dhamagarba, Krita-vedhana, Sarshapa, Vidanga, Pippali, Karan- jaka, Prapunnada, Kovidara, Korvudara, Arishta, Ashva- gandha, Vidula, Vandhujivaka, Shveta, Shanapushpi, Vimvi, Vacha, Mrigervaru and Chitra, etc. are possessed of emetic properties. Out of these the fruits (seeds) plants preceding Kovidara the abovesaid list (from the ]\Iadana fruits those of the Prapunnada) and the roots of plants from Kovidara its close, should be used. Purgative Drugs :—The trees, plants, herbs and creepers, etc. known Trivrita, Shyama, Danti, Dravanti, Saptala, Shankhini, Vishanika, Gavakshi, Chhagalantri, Snuk, Suvarnakshiri, Chitraka, Kinihi, Kusha, Kasha, Tilvaka, Kampillaka, Ranwaka, Patala, Puga, Haritaki, Amalaka, Bibhitaka, Xilini, Chatur- angula, Eranda, Putika, Mah^vriksha, Saptachchhada, Arka, and J3''otishmati, etc. are possessed of purgative properties. Of these the roots of plants, which precede Chap. XXXIX.] StJTRASTHANAM. Tilvaka in the above list, should be used for purgative purposes. The barks of trees from Tilvaka Patala in the same list should used for similar purposes. The pollens or dust of the Kampilla seeds, and of the fruits trees from Eranda Puga, the leaves of Putika and Aragvadha. and the milky exudations of the remaining members of the list, should be similarly used. The expressed juice of Koshataki, Saptala, Shankhini, Devadali, or Karavellika is both emetic and purgative. The Errhincs :—The following drugs, viz.

english_text: Pippali, Vidanga, Apamarga, Shigru, Siddharthaka, Shirisha, Maricha, Karavira, Vimvi, Girikarnika, Kinihi, Vacha, Jyotishmati, Karanja, Arka, Alarka, Lashuna, Ativisha, Shringavera, Talisha, Tamala, Surasa, Arjaka, Ingudi, Meshashringi, Matulungi, Murangi, Pilu, Jati, Shala, Tala, Madhuka (Maula), Laksha and Hingu, together with such substances rock-salt, spirits, cow's urine and watery exudation of cow dung should regarded errhines (Shirovirechanam The fruits (seeds) of plants from Pippali to Maricha enumerated in the above-said list, the roots of plants commencing with Karavira and ending with Arka, the bulbs of those whose names precede Talisha in the same list, the leaves of those commencing with Talisha and ending with the Arjaka therein, the barks Ingudi and Meshashringi, the flowers Matulungi, Murungi, Pilu and Jati, the essence (Sara) of Shala, Tala THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap, xxxix. and Maduhka (Maul) trees, the gummy exudation (Niryasa) of Hingu plants and Laksha trees, as well as salts which are but the saline modifications of earth, Madya (wines) which are but the modified products Asava (fermented liquors), and secretions of cowdung, or cow's urine which should be understood to mean the animal excrements, in their crude or natural state, should be used where errhines are indicated. Samshamaniya Drugs :—Now shall enumerate the names of drugs and substances which soothe or pacify the deranged humours principles the body involved any particular disease (Sanshamanani\ Va'ta-Samshamana-Varga:—The follow- ing drugs, viz. Bhadradaru, Kustha, Haridra, Varuna, Meshshringi, Vala, Ativala, Artagala, Kachhura, Sallaki, Kuverakshi, Virataru, Sahachara, Agnimantha, Vatsa- dani, Eranda, Ashmabhedaka, Alarka, Arka, Shata- vari, Punarnava, Vasuka, Vasira. Kanchanaka, Bhargi, Karpasi, Vrishchiaali, Pattura, Vadara, Yava, Kola, Kulattha, etc. and the drugs forming the group of Vidari- gandhadi-Gana, as well as those belonging the first two groups of Panchamula (Mahat and Svalpa), are possessed of the general virtue of soothing (restoring to its normal state) the deranged (Vaym Vata. Pitta-Samshamana-Varga —The drugs known Chandana, Kuchandana, Hrivera, Ushira, Chap. XXXIX.] StiTRASTHANAM. Manjishtha, Payasya, Vidari, Shatavari, Guiidra, Shaivala, Kahlara, Kiimuda, Utpala, Kadali, Kandali^ Durva, Miirva, etc. and the drugs forming the groups Kakolyadi, Sarivadi, Anjaiiadi, Utpaladi, Nyagro- dhadi, and Trina-Panchamula groups generally prOve soothing to the deranged Pittam. Shicshma'- Samshamana- Varga :— The drugs known Kaleyaka, Aguru, Tilaparni, Kushtha, Haridra, Shitashiva, Shatapushpa, Sarala, Rasna, Prakiryya, Udakiryya, Ingudi, Sumanah, Kakadani, Langalaki, Hastikarna, Munjataka, Lama- jjaka, etc.
