Kanchanaka, Bhargi, Karpasi, Vrishchiaali, Pattura, Vadara, Yava, Kola, Kulattha, etc. and the drugs forming the group of Vidari- gandhadi-Gana, as well as those belonging the first two groups of Panchamula (Mahat and Svalpa), are possessed of the general virtue of soothing (restoring to its normal state) the deranged (Vaym Vata. Pitta-Samshamana-Varga —The drugs known Chandana, Kuchandana, Hrivera, Ushira, Chap. XXXIX.] StiTRASTHANAM. Manjishtha, Payasya, Vidari, Shatavari, Guiidra, Shaivala, Kahlara, Kiimuda, Utpala, Kadali, Kandali^ Durva, Miirva, etc. and the drugs forming the groups Kakolyadi, Sarivadi, Anjaiiadi, Utpaladi, Nyagro- dhadi, and Trina-Panchamula groups generally prOve soothing to the deranged Pittam. Shicshma'- Samshamana- Varga :— The drugs known Kaleyaka, Aguru, Tilaparni, Kushtha, Haridra, Shitashiva, Shatapushpa, Sarala, Rasna, Prakiryya, Udakiryya, Ingudi, Sumanah, Kakadani, Langalaki, Hastikarna, Munjataka, Lama- jjaka, etc.

english_text: and the drugs belonging the groups of Valli and Kantak Panchamulas and those composing the Pippalyadi-Varga, Brihatyadi-Varga, Mushkadi- Varga, Vachadi, Surasadi and Aragvadhadi groups are generally possessed the efficacy of restoring the deranged Shleshma to its natural state. The choice medicine whether for cleansing soothing purposes should determined by the consideration of the strength (intensity) of the disease, and the stamina and the digestive function the patient under treatment. A medicine soothing or Samshamanam efficacy), which stronger than the disease has been applied combat with, not only checks with its own soothing virtue but usually gives rise fresh malady, on account its surplus energy being not requisitioned into THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XXXIX. action, nor its being used up by the weakened and conquered origuial distemper. It is thus stored the organism for the working fresh mischief. medicine, which proves stronger than the digestive function of a patient, impairs his digestion, takes unusually greater length of time digested and assimilated into his organism. A medicine, which is stronger than the physical stamina of a patient, may bring on a feeling of physical languor, fits of fainting, loss of consciousness, delirium, etc. Similarly, an over- dose of cleansing (cathartic) medicine .may work similar mischief. the other hand, medicines inadequate potencies, and accordingly unequal to the strength disease, well medicines adequate doses fail produce any tangible effect. Hence medicines of adequate potencies should be alone administered in adequate doses. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject :^A prudent physician should prescribe purgative for patient enfeebled by the action of the deranged and accumulated bodily humours and laid up with a disease in which such a cleansing (cathartic) or emetic remedy is indicated. The same rule should hold good in the case of a patient enfeebled through causes other than physical distempers, and whose bowels are easily moved, and whom the fecal matter, etc. are found have been dislodged Chap. XXXIX.] SUTI^ASTHANAM. from their natural seats locations. Decoctions (including extracts and cold infusions medicinal herbs) doses of four Palas weights, and pastes and powders doses two Palas weights, should prescribed in a disease of ordinary intensit}'. Corrective medicines (Purgatives and lilmetics) may be safely exhibited even in a weak patient with loose uncon- stipated bowels, if they are found to be stuffed with spontaneous accumulation fecal matter (Dosha) etc. inspite of such looseness or easy motion. Thus ends ihe thiity-ninlh Chapter of the Sutrasth^nam in the Sushiuta SamhitS, which treats drugs of cleansing (corrective) and soothing properties. CHAPTER Now shall discourse the Chapter, which treats of drugs and their flavours, virtues, potencies and chemical actions. (Dravya-Rasa-Guna- Virya-Vipa'ka-Vijnaniya-madhya'yam).

english_text: Certain professors the Aj'urveda hold that medicinal drug or substance is pre-eminently the most important niatter with which the science of medicine is concerned. First because, a drug, as a substance, has definite and continuous existence/ which its attributes (such as, taste, etc.,) do not possess. for example the tastes, etc., which characterise a fruit its unripe stage, are not perceived in its ripe or matured condition. Secondly because, a drug is real (Xitya) and invariable, whereas its attributes are but transitory and accidental at the best. for instance the real character of a drug cannot destroyed whether powdered or pasted. Thirdly because, a drug or a substance never can lose its own generic character. for example, drug possessed attributes peculiar the fun- damental matter, earth, can never transformed into one watery attributes—a truth which does not hold good its attributes. Fourthly because, drug substance object all the five senses of a man, whereas its attributes of tastes, etc. Chap. XL. ^5^ are respectively accommodated the faculty special sense organs. Fifthly because, drug substance is the receptacle of the attributes of taste, etc., whilethe latter are the things contained. Sixthly because, a dictum of medicine can be commenced with the name drug substance. for example, it is quite natural to say that the drugs such as Vidari Gandha,etc., should be pressed and boiled. But it sounds preposterous to utter that the sweet taste should pulverised and boiled. Seventhl)' because, the greater importance a drug or substance has been laid down in the Shastras of medicines inasmuch medical recipes have been described by the names their component ingredients such Matulunga, Agnimantha, etc., and not described as the tastes Matulunga, Agnimantha etc. Eighth!}' because, the attributes tastes, etc., depend upon the drugs and substances (of which they are the attributes) for their progressive maturity. for example, the taste of a drug or substance varies with its growth and different its raw (immature) and ripe (mature) conditions. (Hence drug is more portant than its attributes taste, etc.) Ninthly because, a drug may prove curative through the eflicacy of one of its component parts principles the case Mahavriksha, the milky exudations of which are possessed therapeutical virtues, which cannot be said of its taste. Hence a drug or a substance (Dravyam) is the most THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XL. important factor (which the science of medicine has got to deal with). A substance or drug necessarily implies action and attributes with which is intimately con- nected and of which is the primary cause, or to put more explicitly, these attributes have inseparable inherence and are intimately associated with the substance by way cause and effect (Samavayi- Karanam). Others, the contrary, who not endorse the above opinion, accord the highest importance the attribute taste (Rasa) drug sub- stance.

english_text: Firstly because, laid down the Agamas (Vedas), which include the science of medicine (Ayurveda Shastram) well, and inasmuch such statements " Food primarily contingent its tastes and food depends life " occur therein. Secondly because, the essential importance taste may be inferred from such injunctions instructions the professors medicine " sweet, acid and saline tastes soothe or pacify the deranged bodily Vayu." Thirdly because, a drug or a substance is named after the nature of its taste, as a sweet drug, a saline sub- tance, etc. Fourthly because, its primary importance based the inspired utterances of the holy sages (Rishis) which form the sacred hymns and verses of the Vedas, and such passages as " sweets to be collected for the purposes religious sacrifice," etc, are to Chap. XL. StJTRASTHANAM. found them. Hence taste the most important factor in the science of medicine and forms the primary attribute of a medicinal drug. But, later on, we shall have occasion to speak of that. Certain authorities however, (who reject the two aforesaid theories), hold the potency (Viryam) drug to be the most important factor in medicine inas- much its therapeutic action, whether purgative, emetic, both, cathartic, pacifying, astrin- gent, appetising, pressing (drawing definite head) liquefacient, constructive, tonic (vitalising) aphrodisiac, inflammatory, absorbing, caustic, or bursting, or intoxicating, soporific, killing or antitoxic, depends upon its potency. The potency drug either cooling heat-making owing to the two- fold (hot and cool) nature the temperament the world. According several authorities the potency medicinal drug may be classed as either hot cool, emollient expansive slimy, keen, embrace the eight differ- ent attributes in all. These potencies of medicinal drugs serve their respective functions overpowering their (drugs') tastes with their specific strength (inten- sity) and virtues. for example the decoction the roots belonging the group the Maha- Panchamulam, though possessed astringent taste which subsequently transformed into a bitter THE SUSHRUTA SAMHiTA. [Chap. XL. one, acquires the virtue pacifying the deranged Vayu out its heat-making potenc3^ Similarly, the pulse known Kulattha though possessed astringent taste, and onion though endued with , pungent one, respectively soothe the same deranged humour the body through the oleaginous character their potencies. the other hand, the expressed juice sugar-cane, though possessed sweet taste, tends augment aggravate the deranged Vayu owing its cooling potency. The drug Pippali, though pungent substance itself, proves soothing to the deranged Pittam, owing its and cooling potenc}'. Similarly, an Amalakam fruit, though acid in taste, and Saindhava, though saline, respectively tend pacify the deranged Pittam. The drug Kakamachi, though of a bitter taste, and fish, though sweet, respectively aggravate the Pittam, owing their thermogenetic potency.

english_text: Similarly, Mulakam (Radish), though pungent, increases the Kapham of the body, account its emollient potency ; and Kapittham, though acid, soothes ; and honey, though sweet, tends pacify the deranged Kapham owing to the dry character its potency. The aforesaid instances have been cited by way illustration. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject :—Tastes, which are possessed dry, light or Chap. XL. expansive potencies, fail to pacify the deranged Vayii, though otherwise they may prove soothing that deranged humour. Similarly, tastes, which are dinarily reckoned as pacifiers the deranged Pittam, fail to produce that effect in the event their being endued with keen, light heat- making potency. Likewise, tastes, which are commonl}' found to soothe the deranged Kapham, tend aggravate the event their being possessed of potencies which are respectively heavy, cool and emollient their character.* Hence the potency of a drug is the most important factor in the science of medicine. But certain authorities dissent from the above-said view, and attach the highest importance the process digestive (chemical) reaction (Vipaka) for the reason, that all ingested food, properly or improperly digested the stomach, proves wholesome or other- wise to the body. Certain authorities the subject hold that digestion develops all the several tastes. According others, tastes such sweet, pungent and acid, follow upon the completion the process digestion (b}^ way of reactionary result or transformation). Flavours such sweet, acid and saline, subdue the deranged \"5yu. Tastes such as, sweet, bitter and astringent are antiljilious in their efficacy, while those, which are pungent, bitter and astringent, are antiphlegmagogic in their virtues. t The process of digestion is followed by a reactionary taste, which may be either sweet, pungent, acid, astringent, bitter or saline. chapter: Chapter XLI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter, which treats of the specific properties drugs (Dravya- Vishcsha-Vijna'niya-madhya'yam). The five fundamental principles* such as the earth (Kshithi), water (Apa), fire (Teja), air (Marut) and Sky (Vyoraa) enter into the composition all substances in the world, and the predominance of any of them particular substance determines its character. Accordingly a thing is denominated substance of dominant earth principle, one marked by pre- dominance of fire, air or ether. Parrthiva Drugs thing substance, which is thick, pithy, compact, dull, immobile, rough, heavy (hard to digest^ strong smelling and largely has a sweet taste marked by a shade of astringent, called a substance of dominant earth (Parthivam) matter. • Such a thing increases the firmness, strength, hardness and rotundity of the human body, and possessed of gravity (the virtue of moving the bowels). A'pyam Drugs :—Similarly, thing or subs- tance, which is cold, moist, glossy, devoid of keenness, takes time digested, mobile, compact, soft, These may be translated as Solid, Liquid, Gas, Ether, and Etherioil in the parlance of modern science

A taste and substance are correlative categories from the time their origin, like body and embodied self the plane organic existence. Since attribute per se can not possessed of another attri- bute, the eight kinds potency (properties) can only appertain substance and not taste, which is an attribute in itself. Substances are digested organic body and not the six tastes simply for the reason their being invisible and intangible themselves. Hence substance the greatest of all tlie aforesaid five factors substance, taste, virtues, etc.) and the attributes lie inherent the substance. Unscrutable and unthinkable are the virtues drugs (medicines), which are above all rules syllogism ; and hence drugs (medicines), which have been observed to be efficacious from time immemorial, well those laid down the scriptures medicines, should alone used the course medical treatment. A learned physician should think sacrilege logically dispute the efficacy medicine tested virtue, and which has been adopted after generations of careful observation and is instinc- tively pronounced b}-^ men beneficial remedy. ;74 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XL. No amount logic will alter the nature of things, nor persuade the drugs of the Amboshtha group exercise a purgative virtue. Hence an intelligerrt physi- cian should adhere to the officinal recipes given in the books medicine, and not introduce innovations, however logical probable, into the realms applied or practical Therapeutics. Thus ends the fortieth Chapter of the Sutrasthinam the Sushruta SamhitS, which deals with drugs and their flavours, virtues, and digestive (chemical) transformation. CHAPTER XLI. Such a thing soothes and imparts a glossy character th2 body, keeps moist, favours the adhesion of its parts, and increases its liquid contents. Taijasam Drugs :—A thing substance, which heat-making, pungent and keen, subtle its essence, permeates the minutest capillaries, and is dry, rough, light, and non-slimy in its character and has strong properties and a taste which is largely pungent marked by a shade of saline, is called a substance of the dominant principle fire (Taijasam). Such thing naturally evinces an up-coursing tendency in the body, produces burning sensation in its inside, helps the process digestion and spontaneous bursting abscesses), increases the temperature of the body, strengthens the eyesight, improves the complexion and imparts healthful glow to Varyaviyam Drugs :—A thing or substance, which is subtle in its essence, and is dry, rough, light, cold and non- slimy, increases tactual sensation and is endued with a largely astringent taste marked shade of bitter, is called a substance of the dominant principle of air ( Vayaviyam). Such a thing removes the slimy character of the internal organism, produces light- Chap.-XLI. ness, diyness and emaciation of the body, and increases the speculative or contemplative faculty of the mind. Aka'shiyam Drugs :—A thing sub stance, which is smooth, unctuous, and subtle its nature, soft or pliant in its consistency, expansive 'in the internal organism), porous, soundy and non- slimy its character without any definite taste, is called substance the dominant principle sky (Akashiyam). Such a substance produces softness, light- ness and porosity of the body. It may be inferred from the foregoing illustrations that there is not a single substance in the world but endued with certain curative virtues. Drugs substances, used in specific combinations and according to the indications of a disease under treatment, prove curative in virtue of their native virtues and potencies. The time, during which a drug or a medicine exerts its curative virtues, called its Kala the period action. That which immediately results from the use or application of a medicinal remedy is called its Karma or physiological action. The principle, in virtue of which the action is performed, is called its potenc)' or Viryam. That, in which the action takes place, called its receptacle or Adhikaranam. The means by which it effected is called its agency or Upaya, while that what it accomplishes is called its therapeutic effect or Phalam. Of these the drugs of purgative virtue are possessed THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap.XLi. attiibutes which specifically belong the earth and water. Earth and water are heavy, and natur- ally gravitate downward owing their heaviness.

english_text: Hence it is inferred that purgative drugs are largely endued with the specific attributes of earth and water, in virtue of which they are more strongly attracted towards the centre of the earth (gravity). Drugs endued with emetic properties are possessed of attributes which form the characteristics fire and air. Fire and air are light, and naturally ascend upward owing their lightness. Hence inferred, that emetic (Vamana) drugs are largely possessed attributes, which are upcoursing in their nature. Drugs or substances endued with both emetic and purgative virtues are charac- terised by attributes belonging both the aforesaid elements (earth and fire). Drugs, which soothe the deranged bodily humours, are permeated with, qualities which specifically belong the principle the sky. Astringent (Sangrahaka) drugs are endued with attributes, which specifically belong to the air owing to the drying character of the latter element. Appetising (Dipana) drugs are largely possessed of attributes which belong to the material principle of fire. Lekhana (Liquefacient) drugs or sub- stances are endued with attributes which belong to fire and air. Constructive or restorative (Vringhanam) drugs or substances are endued with attributes which speci- Chap. XLI. fically belong earth and water. These inferences should carefully remembered the time of pre- scribing medicines. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject ;—The deranged bodily Vayu readily yields to the curative efficacies drugs possessed attributes, specifically belonging to the earth, fire and water, while the deranged Pittam is speedily soothed or restored to its normal state by drugs having attributes, specifically belonging to the earth, water and air. Similarly, the deranged Kapham pacified by drugs possessed attributes which characterise the sky, fire and air. The bodily Vayu is increased by the use drugs possessed of attributes which specifically belong to the sky and air, while the Pittam increased by the use of those which are largely endued with the specific attributes fire. The bodily Kapham of the body is increased by the use drugs which are largely endued with the specific attributes the earth and water. Thus having ascertained the dominant attributes of drugs, a physician should use them for the pacification of two or more of the deranged humours of the body according to the exigencies of a case. the eight-fold potencies drug, such cooling, thermogenetic, oleaginous, heavy, parchifying, plastive, keen and slimy, keenness and thermogenetic 38o THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. XLI.

chapter: Chapter XLII: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter, which treats the specific properties flavours (Rasa- Vishcsha-Vljnariiya-maclhyaryam). The properties of sky (Akasha), air (Pavana), fire (Dahana), water (Toya) and earth (Bhumi) are sound touch, colour, taste and smell, each of the preceding elements possessing properties less one than those of the one immediately succeeding in the order of enumeration.* [Since matter designated after the name the preponderant natural element, which enters into its composition], taste is said to be a water-origined prin- ciple. All material elements are inseparably connected with one another, and there is a sort of interdependence among them, each one contributing to the continuance of the other and jointly entering, a more or less extent, into the composition of all material substances. This water-origined flavour (Rasa), which becoming mo- dified through its contact with the rest of the material put it more explicitly the property of sound belongs to the sky (Akdsha). The'properties of sound and touch appertain to the air (V^yu). The properties of sound, touch and colour form the characteristics of Fire (Teja). Sound, touch, colour and taste form the specific properties water (Toya). Sound, touch, colour, taste and smell mark the earth matter (Bhumi)*
english_text: potency should ascribed the attributes fire coohng potenc)- and that engendering slime, to the predominance the attributes of water drug or substance ; oleaginousness, to the predominance of the attributes of the earth and water ; plastive potenc)^, to the predominance of the attributes of water and the sky ; parchifying potency, to the excess of the attributes of air non-slimy potency (Vaishadyam), to the predo- minance of the attributes of earth and air ; and heavy and light digestion, to the same cause. Of these, oiliness and thermogenetic potency prove curative in respect of the deranged Vayu, while cooling potency, plastive potency, and that of engendering slime subdue the deranged Pittam. Keenness, parchifying and non-slimy potencies conquer the deranged Kapham. chapter: Chapter XLII: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter, which treats the specific properties flavours (Rasa- Vishcsha-Vljnariiya-maclhyaryam). The properties of sky (Akasha), air (Pavana), fire (Dahana), water (Toya) and earth (Bhumi) are sound touch, colour, taste and smell, each of the preceding elements possessing properties less one than those of the one immediately succeeding in the order of enumeration.* [Since matter designated after the name the preponderant natural element, which enters into its composition], taste is said to be a water-origined prin- ciple. All material elements are inseparably connected with one another, and there is a sort of interdependence among them, each one contributing to the continuance of the other and jointly entering, a more or less extent, into the composition of all material substances. This water-origined flavour (Rasa), which becoming mo- dified through its contact with the rest of the material put it more explicitly the property of sound belongs to the sky (Akdsha). The'properties of sound and touch appertain to the air (V^yu). The properties of sound, touch and colour form the characteristics of Fire (Teja). Sound, touch, colour and taste form the specific properties water (Toya). Sound, touch, colour, taste and smell mark the earth matter (Bhumi)*
english_text: The properties of sky (Akasha), air (Pavana), fire (Dahana), water (Toya) and earth (Bhumi) are sound touch, colour, taste and smell, each of the preceding elements possessing properties less one than those of the one immediately succeeding in the order of enumeration.* [Since matter designated after the name the preponderant natural element, which enters into its composition], taste is said to be a water-origined prin- ciple. All material elements are inseparably connected with one another, and there is a sort of interdependence among them, each one contributing to the continuance of the other and jointly entering, a more or less extent, into the composition of all material substances. This water-origined flavour (Rasa), which becoming mo- dified through its contact with the rest of the material put it more explicitly the property of sound belongs to the sky (Akdsha). The'properties of sound and touch appertain to the air (V^yu). The properties of sound, touch and colour form the characteristics of Fire (Teja). Sound, touch, colour and taste form the specific properties water (Toya). Sound, touch, colour, taste and smell mark the earth matter (Bhumi)* Chap. XLII.] elements, admits of being divided into six different kinds, such as sweet, acid, saline, pungent, bitter and astringent. These, in their turn, being combined with one another, give rise sixty- three different kinds. A sweet taste is largely endued with attributes whi{;h specifically appertain the material principles earth and water. An acid taste is pre-eminenth' possessed of attributes, which belong to the elementary principles of earth and fire. A sahne taste is mostly endued with attributes which characterise the elements water and fire. A pungent taste largely possessed attributes, which mark the elementary principles air and fire. The specific attributes of air and sky predominate in a bitter taste. The specific properties of earth and air should be regarded as dominant in an astringent taste. Tastes such as sweet, acid and saline are endued with the virtues of subduing Vayu. Tastes such sweet, bitter and astringent are possessed of the virtue subduing the deranged Pittam. Tastes such pungent, bitter and astringent tend subdue the deranged Kapham. The Vayu is a self-origined principle in the human organism. The Pittam owes its origin to the bodily heat (Agneya), while the origin of Kapham is ascribed the presence of watery (Saumya) principle the body. Tastes such sweet, etc. are augmented 3^4 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLII. causes which they have their origin, and prove soothing pacifying respect of causes other than those which produce them. According to certain authorities, there are only two kinds of tastes, owing the two- fold (hot and cold) nature of the temperament of the world. these the tastes such sweet, bitter and astringent are cold in their properties, while the pungent, acid and saline ones exercise fiery or heat making virtues.

Coldness, dryness, lightness, non-sliminess, suppress- ion (of the urine ordure) form the characterstic properties of the Vayu. astringent taste should be considered possessed the same properties as the Vayu, and hence (astringent taste) increases the coldness, dryness, lightness, non-sliminess and arres- tiveness of the latter with its specific coolness, dryness, lightness, non-sliminess and arrestiveness. Heat, pungency, dryness, lightness, and non-slimi- ness form the specific properties of the Pittam. A pun- gent taste, which is possessed of the same properties the Pittam, respectively increases the heat, pungency, Chap. XLII ^8^ dryness, lightness and non-sliminess of the latter with the help of similar properties of its own. Sweetness, oiliness, heaviness, coldness and slimi- ness form the specific properties of Kapham. A sweet taste, which is possessed of the same properties as the Kapham, respectively increases the sweetness, oiliness, heaviness, coldness and sliminess the latter with the help similar properties of its own. A pungent taste endued with properties which are contrary to those of the Kapham, hence the sweetness, oiliness, heaviness, coldness and sliminess the latter, are respectively destroyed the pungency, dryness, lightness, heat and non-sliminess of the former. These have been cited only by way of illustration. Characteristics of Tastes :—Now shall describe the characteristics tastes. taste, which pleasant, proves <"omfortable to, and contributes the life-preservation man, keeps his mouth moist, and increases the quantity bodily Kapham, is called Sweet (Madhura). taste, which produces tooth- edge and increased salivation, and increases the relish for food, called acid (Amla). taste, which imparts a greater relish to food, pro- duces salivation and softness of a part, is called saline (Lavana). A taste, which produces a burning sensation at the tip of the tongue attended with a tingling of the part and headache, and instantaneously followed THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. Specific virtues of tastes : -Now we shall describe the specific virtues of tastes. Pungent taste :—A pungent taste is endued with appetising, resolving (Pachana"! and purifying properties in respect of ulcers etc.), and destroys obesity, languor, deranged Kaphani and intestinal parasites. antitoxic its character, proves curative Chap. XLII. .589 cases of Kushta (skin diseases) and itches, and removes the stiffness of the ligaments. It acts sedative and reduces the quantity of semen, milk and fat. pungent taste, tliough possessed of the aforesaid virtues, may bring vertigo, loss of consciousness, dryness of the throat, palate and hps, burning sensation and high temperature of the body, loss of strength, tremor, a sort of aching or breaking pain, and a neuralgic pain (Vata Shula) in the back, sides and the extremities, etc. in the event of its being largely partaken of in exclusion of all other tastes. Bitter taste bitter taste serves restore the natural relish of a person for food and brings on a sense of general languor. is a good appetiser, and acts as a good purifying agent (in respect of ulcers, etc.), and proves curative itches and urticaria. removes thirst, swoon and fever, purifies mother's milk, and is possessed of the virtue of drying up urine, ordure, mucous, fat and pus, etc. bitter taste, though possessed of the aforesaid properties, may bring on numbness of the limbs, wry-neck, convulsions, facial paralysis, violent headache, giddiness, and an aching, cutting and breaking pain, as well as a bad taste in the mouth in the event its being largel}^ partaken of in exclusion of all other tastes. Astringent taste :—An astringent taste possessed astringent, healing, styptic (Stam- THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. XLII. bhana), purifying, liquefacient, drying and contracting virtues. It lessens secretions from mucous membranes. astringent taste, though possessed the above- said properties, may bring the peculiar type heart disease known (Hridroga) parchedness the mouth, distention of the abdomen, loss of speech, wry-neck (Manya Stambha), throbbing quivering and tingling sensations in the body with contraction of the limbs and convulsions, etc. Now we shall make a general classification of the drugs according to their taste. lYIad hura-Groups :—The drugs forming the groups known the Kakolyadi-Gana, well thickened milk. Ghee, lard, marrow. Shall and Shashtika rice, Yava, Godhuma, Masha pulse, Shringataka, Kasheruka, Trapusha, Erv^ruka.
title: Sushruta Samhita Volume 1

chapter: Chapter XLII: Now we shall discourse on the Chapter, which treats the specific properties flavours (Rasa- Vishcsha-Vljnariiya-maclhyaryam). The properties of sky (Akasha), air (Pavana), fire (Dahana), water (Toya) and earth (Bhumi) are sound touch, colour, taste and smell, each of the preceding elements possessing properties less one than those of the one immediately succeeding in the order of enumeration.* [Since matter designated after the name the preponderant natural element, which enters into its composition], taste is said to be a water-origined prin- ciple. All material elements are inseparably connected with one another, and there is a sort of interdependence among them, each one contributing to the continuance of the other and jointly entering, a more or less extent, into the composition of all material substances. This water-origined flavour (Rasa), which becoming mo- dified through its contact with the rest of the material put it more explicitly the property of sound belongs to the sky (Akdsha). The'properties of sound and touch appertain to the air (V^yu). The properties of sound, touch and colour form the characteristics of Fire (Teja). Sound, touch, colour and taste form the specific properties water (Toya). Sound, touch, colour, taste and smell mark the earth matter (Bhumi)*
english_text: Alavu, Kalaukata Ankalodya,_ Piyala, Pushkara, Vijaka, Kashmarya, Madhuka (Moula), Draksha, Kharjura, Rajadana, Tala, Narikela, modifications the expressed Juice Ikshu (Sugarcane), Vala, Ativala, Atmagupta, Vidari, Pyash5^a, Gokshuraka, Kshiramorata, Madhulika, and Kushmanda etc. are generally included within the Madhura gi'oup. Acid Groups :—The fruits known Dadima, Araalaka, Matulanga, Amrutaka, Kapittha, Karamarda, Vadra, Kola, Prachina-Amalaka, Tintidhi, Chap. XLII.] Koshamra, Bhavya, Paravata, Vetraphala, Lakucha, Amla-Vetash, Dantashatha and curd, whey, Sura, Shukta, Sauvira, Tushodaka and Dhanyamla, etc. are generally included within the acid group. Saline Group :—The different kinds salt such Saindhaba, Sauvarchala, Vida, Fakya, Romaka,. Samudraka, Paktrima, Yavakshara (nitrate potash), Ushara and Suvarchika collectively form the Saline group. Pungent Group :—The component drugs which form the groups known the Pippalyadi and the Surasadi-Ganas and Shigru, Madhu-sigru, Mulaka, Lashuna, Sumukha, Shitashiva (camphor, Kushtha, Devadaru, Harenuka, Valguja-phalam, Chanda, Guggula, Mustha, Langalaki, Shukanasa and Pilu etc. and the components of the group known as Salasaradi gana collectively form the pungent group. Bitter Group :—The component members the groups of medicinal drugs known as the Aragva- dhadi-Gana and the Guduchyadi-Gana together with Mandukparni, Vetra-karira, Haridra, Daruharidra, Indra-yava, Varuna, Svadu-kantaka, Saptaparna, Vrihati, Kantakari, Shankhini, Dravanti, Trivrit, Kritavedhana, Karkotaka, Karavellaka, Vartaka, Karira, Karavira, Sumanah, Sankha-pushpi Apamarga, Trayamana, Ashoka, Rohini, Vaijayanti, Suvarchal^, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. I Chap. XUI. Punarnava, Vrishikali and Jyotishmati, etc. collectively constitute the bitter group. Astringent Group :—The component mem- bers of the groups known as the Nyagrodhade-Gana, the Amvashtadi-Gana, and the Priyangvadi and the Rodhradi Ganas, Triphala, Shallaki, Jambu, Amra, Vakula, Timduka fruits, Katakha fruits, Shaka fruits, Pashanabhedaka, the fruits trees known the Vanaspatis (lit lords of the forest, such as the Vata, the Ashvattha etc.) and most of the component members of the group known as the Salasaradi Gana, as well as Kuruvaka, Kovidaraka, Jivanti, Chilli, Palanka and Sunishanuaka, etc. and grains and pulse the Nevara and Mudga species, collectively form the astringent group. These .tastes, groups of different combinations, number sixty-three all for example, fifteen, computed b}" taking two at a time ; twenty, computed by taking three at a time fifteen, computed by taking four at a time ; six, computed by taking five at a time and six, being severally computed, thus making up an aggregate of sixty-three. Authoritative verse the sub- ject :—The man, who gradually habituates him- self the use each of the six aforesaid tastes, Chap. XLII.

chapter: Chapter XLIII: Xow we shall discourse the Chapter, which treats the mode administering emetics (Vamana-Dravya- Vikalpa-Vijna'niya- madhya'yam). all emetic fruits the Madana (seeds) should deemed the best (most active). Madana fruits should dried the sun and powdered. Then a Pala weight (eight tolas'^ of the powder should be stirred in a decoction of Pratyakpushpi, Sada-Pushpi, or Nimva, and given the patient with honey and Saindhava salt, for emesis. As an alternative, a potion consisting of the powders of raw Madana fruits, stirred in a decoction of Vakula and Ramyaka, and heated in fire, should be' administered with the addition of honey and rock-salt. gruel, consisting of sesamum rice and powders of green Madana fruits, boiled together, should be given to the patient. Likewise matured though not ripe Madana fruits should be stored in a box made of the blades of Kusha grass. The box should be plastered over with a composition of cowdung and clay and kept buried in a bushelful of Yava, Tusha, Mudga, Masha pulse Shali rice for eight consecutive nights. Then having extracted them, fully burst out, with the heat the covering grain, their kernels should be separated from
english_text: enjo5''s a sort of immunity from their injurious action in the same manner as, a strong man, who makes him- self successively accustomed the action the three deranged humours his bod)", not easily affected by their pathogenetic properties. Thus ends ihe forty-second Chapter of the Sutrasthinam in the Sushruta SamhitS, which treats of the specific properties of flavours. CHAPTER XLIII. Xow we shall discourse the Chapter, which treats the mode administering emetics (Vamana-Dravya- Vikalpa-Vijna'niya- madhya'yam). all emetic fruits the Madana (seeds) should deemed the best (most active). Madana fruits should dried the sun and powdered. Then a Pala weight (eight tolas'^ of the powder should be stirred in a decoction of Pratyakpushpi, Sada-Pushpi, or Nimva, and given the patient with honey and Saindhava salt, for emesis. As an alternative, a potion consisting of the powders of raw Madana fruits, stirred in a decoction of Vakula and Ramyaka, and heated in fire, should be' administered with the addition of honey and rock-salt. gruel, consisting of sesamum rice and powders of green Madana fruits, boiled together, should be given to the patient. Likewise matured though not ripe Madana fruits should be stored in a box made of the blades of Kusha grass. The box should be plastered over with a composition of cowdung and clay and kept buried in a bushelful of Yava, Tusha, Mudga, Masha pulse Shali rice for eight consecutive nights. Then having extracted them, fully burst out, with the heat the covering grain, their kernels should be separated from Chap. XLIII. SUTRASTHA'NAM. their seeds and dried in the sun. Then having pasted them with curd, hone}' and the levigated paste sesamum they should again dried the sun, after which they should be kept clean vessel. Pala weight of the aforesaid prepared powder should be pounded in a decoction of Yastimadhu or of any of the drugs of the Kovidaradi group over night, and given to the patient the following morning, through the medium of honey and Saindhava salt. The patient should take it looking towards the north or the east, and the following benedictory Mantra should be recited on the occasion. Metrical texts : -"May tlie gods Bramha, Daksha, Ashvis, Rudra, Indra, the earth goddess, the moon, the sun, the fire, the wind, the concourse of holy sages (Rishis) and the material elements with the curative properties of drugs they originate and nourish, preserve thee. May the potion prove wholesome to you, the elixirs prove wholesome to the Rishis, the nectar to the gods, and ambrosia to the good Xagas." This emetic medicine should be specially employed in cases of catarrhal fever, catarrh, and internal abscess. In case of insufficient unsatisfactory action of the potion, the drugs known as Pippali, Vacha, and a paste of Gaura-Sarsapa and Saindhava salt should be added to It should be administered warm and repeated doses until the symptoms emesis would fully THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLIII. appear.

english_text: In the alternative, powders of Madana kernels soaked and saturated with their decoction and subsequently dried, should administered through the same vehicle (decoction of the Madana kernels) milk cream boiled with the kernels jVIadana fruits should be administered with hone}' or a barley gruel made with milk prepared as above should be prescribed for the purpose. This emetic measure should resorted to in cases of Haematemesis or in Haemorrhaee from the bowels generative organs and burning sensation in the heart due to the action of the deranged Pittam. Milk, boiled with the kernel of a Madana fruit, should be curdled, and the cream of the curd or the curd itself so prepared should be used for emetic purposes cases of water-brash, vomiting, syncope and dyspnoea. The essence (Rasam) the seed pulps of Madana fruits should be pressed out and condensed the manner indicated connection with the extraction oil (Sneha) of Bhallataka, and the patient should be made lick that condensed essence in cases where the Pittam would found have shifted into the natural seats of Kapham. Sun-dried and pulverised Madana fruits^ mixed with a decoction of Jivanti, may be administered in its stead. A decoction of the kernels of Madan seeds (Majja), saturated with powders of Pippali, Yadi or a potion consist- Chap. XLIII. >97 ing of the same powder mixed with a decoction of Ximva or Riipika, should be prescribed cases of lymphatic (Kaphaja) diseases due acts of Santarpanam (use emollient remedies), the emesis such cases should effected with decoction Madhuk'am, K^shmari and Draksha saturated with the aforesaid powder. Thus the emetic remedies made of Madana fruits are discharged. Pulverised Jimutaka flowers may used in the same manner and through the same medium or with the same adjuvants and for same purposes, the preceding (Madana fruit). Jimutaka fruits should pulverised their raw unripe state and dried in the sun, and gruel made with milk boiled with the same powder should be given patient for emesis or milk-cream, boiled with the powder of Jimutaka fruits (lit :—flowers*) powdered their mature or hardened (Romesha) state, should be given or the surface cream of milk boiled with the powders of full grown Aromasha), greenish yellow Jimutaka fruits, or a Sura (wine) made of their decoction should be prescribed. These emetic remedies should be used cases of disincHnation for food through the action of deranged Kapham (lymphatic derangements), cough, dyspnoea, jaundice and in phthisis as well, like the compounds of Madana fruits described Fruits include flowers. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLIII. before. Mature Kiitaja Kritavedha.na fruits may used for emetic purposes similar combinations and through similar mediums the Madana fruits described before.

chapter: Chapter XLIV: Now we shall discourse the Chapter, which treats the choice purgatives (Vircchana- Dravya-Vikalpa - Vijiiariiya - madhya'- yam.) Metrical Texts :—Of the purgative roots, the roots of reddish Trivrita should deemed the most effective. The barks of Tilvaka and the fruit known Haritaki are the most potent all purgative barks and fruits. Similar!}-, the oil castor seeds (Erauda-Taila), the expressed juice Karavellika and the milky exudations of Sndha plant are the most effective all such purgative oils, pressed juices and milky exudations plants, etc. These drugs or substances form the principal purgative remedies (in our pharmacy), and we shall discuss the mode of their administration in successive order. A purgative remedy consisting the sound and matured roots Tri\Titam, soaked the expressed juice of the principal purgative drugs and subsequently pulverised and mixed with a considerable quantity Saindhava salt and powdered Magara, should administered through the medium curd sour rice gruel, etc. patient, suffering from a disease due the derangement the bodil)' Vayu
english_text: Powders of Ikshaku fruits (Kushuma), similarly prepared with milk, curd, etc., may be used for emetic purposes in cases of cough, asthma, vomiting and non-relish for food due to the action of deranged Kapham (lymphatic disorders). The emetic compounds Dhamargava flowers are identical with those the kernels Madana fruits, the former being regarded as specifically indicated in cases of chemical poisoning, Gulma (internal gland), abdominal drops}', cough, asthma, as well as in diseases due the action of deranged Kapham (lymphatic disorders). The pulps or kernels of Kritavedhana seeds should soaked the expressed juice emetic drugs and subsequently reduced powder. The powder, prepared, should strewn over Utpala or any other flower and the patients should made to smell it in the case where the Kapham would found have changed its seat with the bodily Vayu. Likewise, in cases of excessive derangement the bodil)' humours, the patient should given stomachful of barle}' gruel and then made eject the contents of his stomach by causing him to smell such medicated flower. Sternutatoric (Shiro-virechanam) emetic or purgative drugs prove most efficacious after Chap. XLIII. SijTRASTHA'NAM. being soaked in or saturated with the expressed juice of their own. Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—Thus general outhne the leading characteristics of the emetic drugs in general has been given. intelligent physician should choose emetic remedy consideration of the season of the year and the strength of the disease, and should tr}- to set in the process of ejection in a patient either with the help of the expressed juice, paste or powder of the prescribed drug duly administered through the medium of an article of food or drink, through an electuary. Thus ends llie forly-third Cliapler of the Sulraslhanain llie Sushiula Sanihila which deals wiih ihe choice and mode of administering emetics. CHAPTER XLIV. Now we shall discourse the Chapter, which treats the choice purgatives (Vircchana- Dravya-Vikalpa - Vijiiariiya - madhya'- yam.) text: Powders of Ikshaku fruits (Kushuma), similarly prepared with milk, curd, etc., may be used for emetic purposes in cases of cough, asthma, vomiting and non-relish for food due to the action of deranged Kapham (lymphatic disorders). The emetic compounds Dhamargava flowers are identical with those the kernels Madana fruits, the former being regarded as specifically indicated in cases of chemical poisoning, Gulma (internal gland), abdominal drops}', cough, asthma, as well as in diseases due the action of deranged Kapham (lymphatic disorders). The pulps or kernels of Kritavedhana seeds should soaked the expressed juice emetic drugs and subsequently reduced powder. The powder, prepared, should strewn over Utpala or any other flower and the patients should made to smell it in the case where the Kapham would found have changed its seat with the bodily Vayu. Likewise, in cases of excessive derangement the bodil)' humours, the patient should given stomachful of barle}' gruel and then made eject the contents of his stomach by causing him to smell such medicated flower. Sternutatoric (Shiro-virechanam) emetic or purgative drugs prove most efficacious after Chap. XLIII. SijTRASTHA'NAM. being soaked in or saturated with the expressed juice of their own. Authoritative verses the sub- ject :—Thus general outhne the leading characteristics of the emetic drugs in general has been given. intelligent physician should choose emetic remedy consideration of the season of the year and the strength of the disease, and should tr}- to set in the process of ejection in a patient either with the help of the expressed juice, paste or powder of the prescribed drug duly administered through the medium of an article of food or drink, through an electuary. Thus ends llie forly-third Cliapler of the Sulraslhanain llie Sushiula Sanihila which deals wiih ihe choice and mode of administering emetics. CHAPTER XLIV. english_text: aiap.XLIV] The same powder mixed with modifications of sugar- cane juice (such treacle, sugar etc.), with decoctions of drugs belonging to the Madhuradi group (Kakolyadi-gana), or with milk, should be prescribed for patient laid with Pittaja distemper. diseases due the deranged action of the Kapham the same powder should administered with a decoction of Guduchi, Arishta or Triphala, with the addition of pulverised Vyosha and cow's urine. One part of the same powder (Trivrit), mixed with one part of old treacle and the drugs known as Trivarnaka, and Tryushana, should be administered for purgative pur- poses disease (due to the concerted action of the deranged Viyu and Kapham). alternative, Prastha mea sure (four seers) cf the decoction the Trivrit roots, mixed with a Kudava measure (half a seer) their paste, and a Karsha (two tolas) weight Saindhava salt and Xagara, and boiled together, and then formed into a condensed compound should be used one part of the paste of the same roots, mixed with half part each of rock-salt and powdered Nagara, should be administered through the vehicle of cow's urine. A compound consisting of one part of each of the following drugs viz., powdered Trivrita roots, Nagara and Haritaki, and a half part of each of such drugs powdered Maricha, Devadaru, Vidanga and ripe Puga nuts, mixed with rock-salt, and administered through THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. Xliv the medium of cow's urine, should be considered as an effective purgative (in diseases of whatsoever type). Powders purgative drugs taken adequate measures and soaked their own juice should boiled with their roots and made into pills (Gutikd) with clarified butter and administered occasion would arise. alternative, powders pur- gative drugs pasted with clarified butter boiled with their roots should be made into boluses, and the intelligent ph5^sician should administer them through the medium clarified butter, prepared above, whenever necessary. quantity* of treacle should be kept boiling over an oven, and a (halt part) of the pu'verised purgative roots should be cast into little before it is completely boiled. Then the basin should be taken off the fire, and powders aromatic drugs known as Trij^ta strewn over it, and the compound sub- sequently made into boluses (Gutika) of adequate size according to the requirements of the case under treatment. One part of any of the pulverised purgative drugs (such as the Trivrit roots, etc.) should boiled with four parts of their own decoction, and one part of pow- dered wheat steamed in the fumes of a separate quantity of a similar boiling decoction, should be pounded with a quantity of clarified butter boiled and prepared with * The quantity of old treacle should be equal to the aggregate weight of the other drugs in the compound under similar circumstances. Chap. XLIV. the same decoction.

Similar!}-, the oil castor seeds (Erauda-Taila), the expressed juice Karavellika and the milky exudations of Sndha plant are the most effective all such purgative oils, pressed juices and milky exudations plants, etc. These drugs or substances form the principal purgative remedies (in our pharmacy), and we shall discuss the mode of their administration in successive order. english_text: Then having cooked a quantity of thin treacle a separate utensil, powders of wheat and purgative drugs prepared above, should cast into immediately before being completely cooked, and the vessel should taken down from the oven and allowed to cool. Then this confection (Modaka) should perfumed with aromatic drugs and regarded as ready for use. short, this purgative Modaka is good food as well. Purgative preparations lYIudga, etc.:—The soup of Mudga pulse saturated with the decotion of a purgative drug, and taken with clarified butter and rock salt, acts as a good purgative well. Similarly, soups of other pulses (such the Musara, etc.) soaked a decoction of any the purgative drugs and drunk with the aforesaid adjuvants, exert purgative virtues. Drugs possessed of emetic properties may be used through the preceding media pulse- soups as well. A bit of sugar-cane should be longitudinally split, and then paste of Tribhandi should placed its middle ; then it should be tied up (with the blades Kusha grass), and plastered over with a coat of clay, and inserted gentle fire of dung cake. After that, should be taken out of the fire, fully roasted the juice squeezed out and cooled, would prove a good purgative to a patient laid up with a Pittaja distemper. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XLIV. A compound consisting of equal parts of sugar and powdered Ajagandha, Tvakkshiri, Vidari and Trivrit and licked with honey and clarified butter, proves curative in a fever with thirst and burning sensation of the body. A compound consisting of one part pulverised Trivrit and a quarter part each the drugs known as Tvak, Patram and Maricha, and administered with an adequate quantity of honey and sugar, should regarded as a good purgative for delicate persons. A Pala weight of sugar should be boiled with a half Kudava weight of hone)', and Trivrita powders to the weight of a quarter part the combined weight of honey and sugar) should added the boiling compound the later part the cooking. The remedy should be administered cool, and looked upon as a good purger of Pittam. A compound consisting equal parts pow- dered Trivrit, Shyama (Vriddha-Daraka), Yavakshara, Shunti and Pippali, and taken with honey, acts one of the most effective purgatives diseases due to the action of the deranged Kapham. Over-ripe Pathya, Kdshmari, Dhatri, Dadima and Kola fruit taken with their seeds stones, should boiled (with quantity water weighing sixteen times their combined weight). The decoc- tion thus obtained should boiled with (castor) Chap. XLIV. oil, and the juice of Amlaphala etc. (to the weight of a quarter part thereof), should added The whole should boiled together until reduced to considerable consistence. The powders of the three aromatic drugs (Trisugandha) and Trivrit* should then added to which should be administered patient electuary with hone)". This remedy will prove a good purgative respect of a delicate person of Kaphaja temperament.

english_text: A compound, consisting of one part of powdered Nili fruit, one part of powdered Tvak and Ela, and two parts pulverised Trivrit, and mixed with adequate quantity sugar, and taken with honey and the juice of Amlaphalam, should be regarded as a purgative remedy possessed of the virtue of destroying the concerted action the three deranged humours of the body. compound, consisting of equal parts of powdered Trivrit, Shyama (Vriddhadaraka), Pippali and Triphal^ and made into a confection ( Modaka) (with the addition of honey and sugar), should be regarded one of the most potent cures (purgatives) for S^nnipata (simul- taneous derangement the three vital humours), haemoptysis and fever. A compound consisting three parts Trivrit, * The weight of honey and pulverised Trivrit should equal to a fourth part of the entire quantity of medicine taken at a time* 4o6 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap XLIV. one part Triphala, one of Yavakshara, one Krishna (Pippali), and one Vidanga, pounded together and used electuary with the addi- tion honey and clarified butter, or made into bolases (Gutika) with treacle, proves curative cases of enlarged spleen, in Gulmas due to the action of the deranged Kapham and Vayu, in Halimaka .Chlorosis), well cases of abdominal dropsy, etc. The present remedy (purgative) is one of the most harmless purgative compounds our pharmacopoeia}. A pur- gative compound consisting of Shyama (Vriddhadaraka), Trivrit, Xili, Katvi, Musta, Duralabha, Chavya, Indra- yava and Triphala, administered through the vehicle of clarified butter, essence of meat, or water, com- mended to persons of dry temperament. Preparations Purgative Asavas (Wines):—All purgative drugs* should duly boiled water. Three parts the decoction thus prepared should mixed with two parts cold powdered barley (Phanitam) and again boiled over fire. Then after boiling it should taken down from the oven, cooled and poured into pitcher previously coated inside with a special plaster.t Then according to the difference the season (cold or hot), * Several authorities exclude the plant known as Sudh^ (ManasS), while others stick to Trivrita alone in exclusion of all other drugs. t A new earthen pitcher first washed with water and dried in the shade. Then its inside coated with a plaster of honey and powdered Pippali and is fumigated with the fumes of Aguru (Eagle wood). Chap. XLIV. the pitcher should kept buried heap paddy for month, fortnight. should taken out and understood ready for use soon would emit a winy fermented odour. Asavas (fermenting liquours) of animal urines and alkaline substances should likewise prepared in the foregoing manner. Preparations of purgative rice Surar (Wines) etc. :—Quantities of Masha pulse and Shali rice should be respectively first soaked and washed in decoction of purgative roots.

english_text: Then they should be dried and pounded together and made into balls, which should be subsequently dried in the sun and again pulverised.* After that separate quantity Shali rice steamed the vapours the aforesaid decoction, and kept apart, should be made into cakes. Then three parts of these cakes should be mixed with one part of the aforesaid powdered ball. The compound thus obtained should soaked adequate quantity that purgative decoction previously kept apart in an earthen pitcher of the plastered type, described before. The Sura should be deemed ready for use, soon would emit the peculiar honey-like smell. Suras of emetic drugs should be likewise made in the same manner. Preparations of purgative Souvira- kas (Barley Wines) ;~-Trivrit roots and drugs * For imparting to liie necessary Enzyme, 4o8 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLiv. belonging the groups of Vidarigandha and major Panchamulam, as well Murva, Sh^rngashta, Sudha, Haimavati, Triphala, Ativisha and Vacha should mixed together and then set apart two equal parts. A decoction should be made of one them, while the other should reduced to a state of powder. After that, quantity of well thrashed and huskless barlej^ should be soaked in the aforesaid decoction for seven days, and should subsequently dried and fried little. Then three parts the latter and one part of the aforesaid powder (powdered Trivrita roots etc.) should be mixed together and soak- the aforesaid cold decoction those drugs. The mixture should then kept into earthen pitcher of the foregoing type and administered ade- quate doses as soon the characteristic winy smell of the mixture (Jatarasa) would be detected. The pre- paration is called the purgative Sauvirakam. Preparations of purgative Tusho- dakam (fermented liquors of barley with husks):—The drugs enumerated connection with the foregoing preparation should be mixed together and di^•ided in halves and kept in two separate vessels. One half the mixture should be well-thrashed and tied up in piece clean linen with quantity unthrashed barley in husks and should be boiled with a decoction of Ajashringi in a separate basin. Then barley in husks Chap. XLIV. should be separated from the rest of the components of the mixture after it has been thoroughly cooked. Then three parts of these barley grains subsequently thrashed should be again soaked in their decoction, and fourth part of the aforesaid pulverised drugs 'such as the roots of Trivrit, etc.) should be added and the entire mixture should kept earthen pitcher the before mentioned type. This preparation called Tushodakam (lit: Washings of husks and should be used as soon as the characteristic smell of fermentation (Jatarasa) would be emitted from the pitcher. The processes of preparing Sauvirakam and Tushodakam have been described. They should be used after the expiry six seven nights from the date of their being in the pitcher.

Then three parts the latter and one part of the aforesaid powder (powdered Trivrita roots etc.) should be mixed together and soak- the aforesaid cold decoction those drugs. The mixture should then kept into earthen pitcher of the foregoing type and administered ade- quate doses as soon the characteristic winy smell of the mixture (Jatarasa) would be detected. The pre- paration is called the purgative Sauvirakam. Preparations of purgative Tusho- dakam (fermented liquors of barley with husks):—The drugs enumerated connection with the foregoing preparation should be mixed together and di^•ided in halves and kept in two separate vessels. One half the mixture should be well-thrashed and tied up in piece clean linen with quantity unthrashed barley in husks and should be boiled with a decoction of Ajashringi in a separate basin. Then barley in husks Chap. XLIV. should be separated from the rest of the components of the mixture after it has been thoroughly cooked. Then three parts of these barley grains subsequently thrashed should be again soaked in their decoction, and fourth part of the aforesaid pulverised drugs 'such as the roots of Trivrit, etc.) should be added and the entire mixture should kept earthen pitcher the before mentioned type. This preparation called Tushodakam (lit: Washings of husks and should be used as soon as the characteristic smell of fermentation (Jatarasa) would be emitted from the pitcher. The processes of preparing Sauvirakam and Tushodakam have been described. They should be used after the expiry six seven nights from the date of their being in the pitcher.

english_text: The rules and processes regarding the preparation of Tri\Tit compounds hold good in cases of similar pre- parations made the rest of purgative drugs (such as, Danti, Dravanti, etc.) The roots of Danti and Dravanti should first pulled up and collected, after which they should be dried in the sun. After that, they should be mixed with honey and pasted Pippali and placed in a box of Kusha grass firmly tied up and plastered with a layer of clay. The box should be put into a fire of dried cowdung cakes. The compound inside the plastered grass box should be cooked according to the process of Putapaka, 4IO THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. XLIV. and should taken out and used in diseases due to the action of the deranged Kapham and Pittam and combination and through vehicles described in connec- tion with the purgative compounds of Trivrit. Pastes (Kalkas' and decoctions of Danti and Dra- vanti should be boiled with clarified butter, and Chakra Tailam (sesamum oil pressed in an oil mill). The clarified butter, thus cooked and prepared, would prove curative in cases of Erysipelas, Kaksha, burning sensation of the body and Alaji, while cases of Meha, Gulma, retention flatus, (kapham) and obstruction the bowels would prove amenable the oil above described. Diseases due the retention urine, semen and Vavu or fecal matter readily yield to one of the four oily substances (Chatuh-sneha, oil, clarified butter, lard and marrow) cooked and prepared with the paste and decoction of Danti and Dravanti. A compound consisting of Danti, Dravanti, Maricha, Kanakahvaya, Yavasaka, \'ishs^a-veshaja, Mridvika, and Chitraka powdered together and successi^"ely soaked in cow's urine for seven days, should be administered for purgative purposes, through the medium of clarified butter. A diet of powdered barley, stirred honey, should be given to the patient after the assimilation of the abovesaid medicine. english_text: Trikatu, Trijata, Musta, Vidanga and Amalaka taken in equal parts, and eight parts of Trivit, and two parts of Danti roots should be separately pulverised and sieved through a piece of thin linen. The powders thus prepared should be pounded together and mixed with six parts of sugar and a little quantity of honey and rock salt.* Cold water should be given to the patient after he had taken the medicine, which proves curative * The term little (Ishat) in the present instance stands for quarter part. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLIV. in colic pain in the bladder (Vasti-Shula), thirst, fever, vomiting, anasarca (Shotha), chlorosis and vertigo. does not entail any strict regimen of conduct like other purgatives and acts as a good eliminator of poison. The compound is called the Trivridashtakam and is specially recommended Pittaja affections. Persons, suffering from diseases due to the action the deranged Pittam and Kapham, should take the medicine through the vehicle milk. The medicine should prescribed for rich persons, owing to its dietetic character. Purgative barks :—The external skin of the Lodhra bark, to the exclusion of its inner lining, should be taken and pulverised. The powder, thus prepared, should be divided into three equal parts, two of which should be soaked in a decoction of the same (Lodhra) bark and filtered twenty- one times according to the process laid down in connection with the preparation of alkalis. The remaining third part of the powders should soaked the aforesaid filtered decoction and subsequently dried in the sun, and again soaked decoction the drugs, which coUectiveh' the name of Dashamulam. The medicine should prescribed forms (wines, electuaries, etc.) pre- viously described connection with the Trivrit compounds. The mode ol preparing and administering purgative medicines out of barks endued with similar virtues has Chap. XLIV.] been described. shall presently deal with those made with purgative fruits. Fruit Purgatives :—Sound and stoneless Haritakis administered the Trivrit com- pounds prove curati^'e all forms disease and in malignant sores and internal abscesses. Thev are the best elixirs and improve the intellectual faculties. Haritaki and Vidanga, as well as rock salt, Xagaram, Trivrit and Maricha mixed equal parts and taken with cow's urine, act good purgatives. Similar!}', powders of Haritaki, Bhadra-daru, Kushtham, Puga-phalam, Saindhava salt and Shringaveram taken through the medium of cow's urine, act as good purga- tive. For purgative purposes, a man should lick a com- pound consisting of the powders of Xilini fi-uits, Nagara, Abhaya and treacle and subsequently drink good draught vrarin water. compound composed Haritakis pasted with a decoction of the drugs con- stituting the group Pippalyadi and bit Saindhava salt, exerts an instantaneous purgative action. Haritakis eaten with Nagaram or treacle and with a bit of rock salt added to is an excellent stomachic.

english_text: The specific virtue of Haritaki consists in restoring the normal condition the bodily Vayu (laxative), rejuvenating used exhausted frame, and in soothingly in^•igorating the sense organs. Haritaki destroys all diseases, which are due the use THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLIV sweet or richly cooked dishes (Santarpanam) such thirst, etc. Amalakam is cooling, and refrigerent it sub- dues Pittam and Kapham and is antifat in its virtues. Vibhitakam is cooling it subdues Pittam and Kapham. The- group medicinal fruits known as the Triphala consists of Haritaki, Amalakam and Vibhitakam, which are collectively marked by an acid-astringent taste with a shade bitter and sweet. Powdered Triphala regularh' taken with clarified butter of a three quarter part its own weight acts regular panacea and is endued with rejuvenating virtue. All fruits possessed purgative properties, should be used in the manner described in connection with Haritaki with the exception of Chaturangulas. The Chaturangula fruit should be collected in the proper season, and then kept buried for a week in a bed of sand. After that, they should be unearthed and dried in the sun, and their stones or seeds (lit. marrow) should taken out. Then the essential oil the seeds should be extracted by pressing them in an oil-mill like the seeds of sesamum, or boiling them with water (hot expression). The oil good purgative for child up to its twelfth year. Hot water taken after having licked compound consisting Castor oil saturated with powdered Kushtha and Trikatus, acts as a good purgative. Castor oil taken with a decoction of Triphalas, double its own Chap. XLIV. SUTRASTHA'NAM. 41^ measure, or with milk extract of meat, acts good purgative, which should be prescribed for infants, old men, persons debilitated from the effects ulcer cachexia, or of delicate constitution. I have finished describing the preparation and appli- cation fruit purgatives. Now hear me, O Sushruta, discourse similar milky exudations of plants and trees, etc. which are possessed of purgative properties. The milky juice of a Sudha plant the strongest all purgatives, which being imprudently used medical ignoramus, may attended with dangerous consequences, while the same in the hands of a judicious physician proves strong enough dis- integrate mighty accumulation of deranged humours and successfully combat man}' an irremediable dis- temper. One part of the decoction each of the drugs constituting the gi'oup major Panchamulam and Vrihati, etc. should be mixed with one part of the milky juice of a Sudha plant (thus forming an eighth part of the whole compound). After having boiled over charcoal fire, the compound should be taken with two Tola (kola) weights of any acid liquid (such as wine, sour rice gruel, cream of curd, etc.) the manner Trivrit compounds. A gruel made of rice saturated with the milky exudation of a Mahavriksha, or a sweetened, porridge-like preparation of the same substance (Utka- THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap.XLIv.

Thus ends the forty-fourth Chapter of the Sutrasthinam in the Sushruta SamhitS, which treats of the choice of purgatives. CHAPTER XLV. Now we shall discourse the Chapter, which deals with the rules to be observed respect of liquid substances general (Drava-Dravya-Vidhi- madhyaryam). Certain authorities maintain that heavenly atmospheric water having fallen on a red, brown, grey, yellow, blue or white coloured soil, respectively assumes a sweet, acid, saline, pungent, bitter or an astringent taste. But the theory not soTind one as much the comparative predominance of the attributes the five material principles particular soil determines the taste the water contained therein. Water, contained collected soil marked by predominance the attributes earth-principle, acquires acid and saline taste. Water, contained in a soil marked by a predominance of the attributes fire, acquires a bitter and pungent taste. Water, contained in soil marked a pre- dominance the attributes air, acquires astringent taste. The sky devoid of all tastes, and hence, the water contained soil, which is largely possessed of the specific attributes that element, characterised by the absence of any taste whatever. Only the last named kind should be used for drinking purposes where atmospheric water would not available. Atmospheric water (Antariksha Jalam), its turn, may divided into four classes such rain water, hail water, frost water dew, and snow water, which the first the best for its lightness. Rain water may divided into two classes such the Gangam and the Samudram, ^20 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLV. according the rain- cloud charged with vapours evaporated from the bosom of the Ganges the sea. Gangetic rain generally descends the month Ashvina, but both kinds should subjected test; The test the case of Gangam rain water consists in exposing for Muharta (forty-eight minutes), a quantity of undiscoloured Shali rice silver bowl which is not extremely softened by boiling. To ascertain whether it is Gangetic rain water or not, Gangetic rain water should ascertained from the fact of the aforesaid Shali rice not being in any way affected its colour whereas change its colour under exposure, well the fact its being formed into shredd}' or seedy balls mixed with slimy secretions, would indicate that the rain water had been formed of the vapours of the sea ; Samudram), and should be regarded extremely unwholesome.

english_text: Rain water from a cloud entirely formed of sea-vapours and collected the month of Ashvina, wholesome what technically known as Gangetic rain water, but the latter is the best of the several kinds of atmos- pheric water. The means collecting atmospheric (rain) water follows broad piece clean and white linen should be hung out in the open air, (with a stone placed across the middle dip its centre gravity). The rain water thus collected should kept in a Chap. XLV. SUTKASTHANAM. vessel. alternative, rain water flowing from the waterspouts house should collected clean receptacle, and subsequently poured into golden, silver or an earthen vessel. The water thus collected can taken all times, and ma}^* be substituted any other terrestrial water the event of its not being available at the time. Terrestrial water generally marked pre- dominance the specific properties of the sky, and admits of being grouped (under seven sub-heads such well-water, river-water, lake-water, tank- water, fountain water, spring (Artesian) water, and Chunti (well unprovided with masonry steps) water. Atmospheric spring water should be used for their high efficacy during the rains (Varsha). All kinds of water may be used Sharat on account of their clearness. Lake tank water should be used in Hemanta ; well and foun- tain water, spring* (Vasanta) and summer and Chunti water, as well as all water not of recent origin, nor due excessive down-pour inundation, should be used during Prdvrit. Metrical Texts falls an easy victim to internal and external diseases (cutaneous affections), etc., who drinks bathes pool water, * The " rains " in the present passage should interpreted to mean the end of the rainy season or the month of Ashvina, and not the month of Bh^dra, as its use is specially forbidden in that month. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLV. which full poisonous worms, saturated with urine fecal matter, defiled with germs of vermin decomposed animal organisms, covered over with the growths aquatic plants, is stiewn over with withered and decomposed leaves, which any way rendered poisonous and contaminated, as well he, who drinks and bathes the freshly collected water of a pool or a reservoir during the rains. sheet water, which entirely covered over with the growths aquatic plants such moss, zoophytes, water weeds, lotus leaves, etc., which looks turbid owing oozy mire, or is not exposed to the currents of fresh air, nor illumined the sun the moon, and possessed of a definite smell, colour, and taste, should regarded contaminated defiled (V3'apannam). Water may be affected with regard the six categories of touch, sight, taste, smell, potenc}^, and chemical transformation or re-action (lit digestion). Roughness, sliminess, warmth, and the production shivering sensation (lit : tooth-edge) are the tactual defects (Sparsha-Dosha) of defiled water, whereas a varied colour, and the presence of mire, sand, and shreds of moss are the defects, which mark its look or appearance (Rupa-Dosha).

english_text: A distinct taste marks the water, which is affected as regards its taste (Rasa-Dosha), while unpleasant smell the characteristic Chap. XLV. the water, which aiEFected regards its smell (Gandha-Dosha). The water, which being taken gives rise thirst and to a sense of heaviness of the limbs, colic, and a fluent coryza, is said to be affected vitiated its potency (Virya-Dosha whereas that, which takes long time to be digested, or is retained the stomach for inordinate length of time, said affected regards digestion chemical transformation (Vipaka-Dosha). Atmospheric water is free from the abovesaid defects. The defiled contaminated water should be purified by boiling heating the sun, immersing a red-hot iron, or hot sands or stones the same, and its smell should be removed by perfuming it with the Nageshvara, Champaka, Utpala, or Patala flowers, etc. IVIetrical Texts :—Water should drunk perfumed golden, silver, copper earthen goblet, or in a bowl made of bell metal precious stones. Contaminated water, well rain water accumulated improper season, should never used for drinking purposes, inasmuch tends derange the fundamental humours the body, and is positively injurious to the human" system. The man, who drinks, or bathes in, any contaminated water without previously purifying it as before directed, incurs the risk of being speedily affected with oedema, jaundice, cutaneous affections, indigestion, dyspnaa, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLV. cough, catarrh, colic pains, abdominal glands, ascites or any other dreadful disease. There are seven modes purif3'ing water such b}- immersing the Kataka fruits, the gems known the Gomedha, the roots lotus plants, of aquatic mosses, a piece of linen, or a pearl, or a crystal pitcher or vessel containing it. The bottoms of a water pitcher are made of five different shapes such as, the Phalakam (rectangular wooden stool), the Try^ashtakam (octagonal wooden tripod), the Manju Valayam (ring made of the blades of Manju grass), the Udaka-Manchika (wooden scaffold for pitcher) and the Shiky (pendent bracket\ There are seven ways cooling water, such exposing water pitcher currents of air, immersing a water pitcher (tied round with a piece of wet cloth) neck-deep in a vessel full water, churning it with a stick, by fanning, or siphoning it by means of a piece of linen, or b}' burying a water pitcher underneath a bed sand, keeping suspended in a pendent bracket. IVIetrical Texts :—The water, which is devoid all smell taste, and pure, cool, limpid, transparent, refrigerent and pleasant, should be regarded as possessed of all the commendable traits. The water rivers, (which drain the J^ngala countries) and flow into the western sea, light, and therefore wholesome. The water rivers, which traverse Chap. XLV. .35 the marshy (Anupa) countries and empty themselves into the eastern sea, heav}' and therefore not com- mended to be used.

english_text: The water of rivers, which run into the southern sea, is neither too heavy nor too light owing the fact its traversing countries which have a Sadharana character. The water of rivers, which have their sources in the Sajhya mountains, begets cutaneous affections ; while the water of those, which rise from the Vindhya mountains, produces Kushtha and Jaundice. The water of rivers, which rise on the mount Mala)'a, begets worms and in- testinal parasites, while the water those, that have their sources in the Mahendra mountain, begets elephantisis and abdominal dropsy. The water of rivers, which rise on the Himalaya, produces angina pectoris, (Hridroga), anasarca, diseases of the head, elephantisis, or goitre in persons using for the purposes life. Similarh% the water rivers, which drain the eastern portion of the country of Avanti, flow through its western part, begets piles while the water of those, which rise on the mount Paripatra is wholesome, strength -giving, and conducive to health. IVIetrical Texts :—The water clear and swift- running rivers light, while the water of those, which are sluggish their course and are covered with mosses and other aquatic plants, is heavy. The water of rivers, which run through Marudesha (Modem THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLV. Marwar) possessed of a bitter saline taste, endued with a sweet taste blended with a shade of the astringent, and is easil}" digestible and strength-giving its properties. Every kind of terrestrial water should collected early in the morning, since obtained the clearest and coolest at that part of the day and since these two attributes by far form the most commendable traits in water. lYIetrical Texts :—The water, which gets the light of the sun in the day and reflects the moon in the night, and which, moreover, neither produces Kapham nor a parched condition in the body, should be regarded as one in virtue with the atmospheric water. Atmos- pheric water, collected in a good and proper receptacle, has the virtue of subduing the three deranged humours the body, and acts pure tonic and elixir, its virtue varying with the excellence the vessel which it is contained. The cool and limpid washings of the gem known as the Chandrakanta Mani (the moon- stone) should be regarded as possessed the mystic virtue of warding off the attacks monsters and demons, and of subduing the deranged Pittam. They are beneficial fever and in cases of poisoning marked by a burning sensation of the body, etc. Cold water usually proves beneficial in epileptic fits, Chap. XLV.l in hot seasons, and in a burning sensation the body due to the deranged action the Pittam, in blood- poisoning, ha3moptysis, abuse wine (Mad^tya), loss consciousness, fatigue exhaustion, vertigo, Tamaka and vomiting.
