River water pro- duces Vayu and a parched condition in the body, and is light, stomachic and (Lekhana) liquefacient. the contrary, that which is heavy, comparatively denser its consistency, sweet, and cooling, brings on catarrh. The water of lake ( Sarasam) quenches thirst and strength-giving, light, sweet and astringent. The water pond or a tank (Tadaga) produces V^yu, and is sweet, astringent, and pungent in digestion. The water from a Vapi (a large tank) subdues the deranged Vayu and Kapham, and generates Pittam, and is pungent in taste and is found to charged with a solution of alkali. The water from a Chunti is a good digestant, sweet, and parchifying, though it does not give rise Kapham in the system. The water from a well (Kupa) generates Pittam and appetising. It subdues the deranged Kapham, and light and alkaline. The THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. Chap. XLV. water fountain is light, appetising, and pleasant, and destroys Kapham. The water accumulated open field, or fallow land, heavy digest and tends augment the deranged humours of the body. chapter: Chapter XLV: Now we shall discourse the Chapter, which deals with the rules to be observed respect of liquid substances general (Drava-Dravya-Vidhi- madhyaryam). Water Group :—Atmospheric, rain water possessed non-patent taste. ambrosial its nature, pleasant and beneficial life. enlivening,* invigorating strength-giving, f frigerent, frigorific, antipyrotic_, anti-hypnotic, and conquers vertigo, drowsiness and fits fainting. most wholesome the human body. After having fallen upon the surface of the earth it acquires one the six different tastes according the nature its receptacle such river, or a Xada (a river with a masculine name), a pond, tank (Vapi) Kupa Chunti fountain, Artesian spring Vikira 1, fallow land (Kedar), a pond covered over with growth aquatic plants Enlivens ihe body during fits of fainting and such like cases. + Imparts strength to the exhausted or emaciated frames. tank large well with its sides protected by buttresses masonry work. § A well with flights of masonry steps descending to its bottom. II An ordinary well, unprotected by buttresses and unpro\-ided with steps. If A flow of subterranean water dug out of a bed of sand
english_text: Water boiled and subsequently cooled down should given person suffering from an 3^ the diseases due to an abuse of wine or to Pittam, or from a complaint brought about through the concerted action the three deranged humours. The water found inside the shell cocoanut is heavy,* demulcent, cool, pleasant and appetising etc. diuretic, (Vasti-shodhaka) spermatopoietic, and subdues Pittam and thirst. The use of water boiled and subsequently cooled down is recommended in dysentery, burning the skin, haemoptysis, diseases due to the abuse of wine, or to the effects of any imbibed poison, as well as in thirst, vomiting, catarrh, vertigo and loss of consciousness. Water should taken little possible by a person suffering from any of the following diseases viz., loss of relish for food, catarrh, water-brash, oedema, any of the wasting diseases, impaired digestion, abdominal dropsy, cutaneous affection, fever, diseases affecting the eyes, ulcer and diabetes (Madhumeha, etc), Light according to Jejjada. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLV, The lYIilk Group :—The milk of a cow, she- goat, she-camel, ewe, she-buffalo, mare, she-elephant, or of a woman, is what generally comes to the use of man.* The milk is the white fluid essence of drugs and cereals, which enter into the food of the aforesaid milk- giving animals, and is therefore the best of all nutritive substances (literally life-giving). heavy, sweet, slimy, cold, glossy, emollient, laxative and mild. Hence proves congenial to all sentient animals. And since milk is kindred in its nature to-the essential principles of life and so very congenial to the panzoism all created animals, its use may unreservedly recommended to all, and not forbidden in diseases due the deranged action of (Vayu) or Pittam, or in ailments affecting the mind (Mansa), or the vascular system of man. Its beneficial and curative efticac}' ma}' be witnessed cases of chronic fever, in cough, dyspnoea, phthisis and other wasting diseases, Gulma (abdominal glands), insanity, ascites, epileptic fits, vertigo, delirium, burning sensation of the bod}^, in thirst, diseases affecting the heart and the bladder, in chlorosis and dysentery, in piles, colic and obstinate constipation, Grahani, Pravahika, * From the construction of the present sentence in the original texts, we are warranted to include the milk of a doe, or of she-mule, or of a cow- rhinoceros in the list, as they sometimes prove beneficial for external appli- cations. Chap. XLV.l SUTRASTHAiNAM. miscarriage and other diseases peculiar to the female reproductive organs, and ha^'moptysis. refrigerant and acts as a bracing beverage after physi- cal exercise. It is a sacred, constructive, tonic, spermato- poietic, rejuvenating and aphrodisiac. It expands* the intellectual capacities of a man, brings about the adhe- sion of broken or fractured bones (Sandhana) rejuvenates used and exhausted frames, forms an excellent enemata, increases the duration of life, and acts as a vitaliser.

english_text: It is an emetic and a purgative remedy, and imparts a healthy rotundity to the frame, and which through its kindred or similar properties augments the quality of bodily albu- men (Ojah) and is the most complete and wholesome diet for infants, old men and persons suffering from cachexia witnessed in cases of ulcers in the chest, as well as for persons debilitated from insufficient food, sexual excesses or excessiAC, physical labour. Metrical Texts :—Cow-milk demulcent, and does not set up or increase the normal quantity of slimy secretions in the internal channels of the body. It is heavy and is a good elixir, and proves curative in hajmoptysis. It is cold, and sweet both taste and chemical reaction. It subdues both Vayu and Pittam and is accordinglj' one of the most efficient of vitalising agents. The milk of a she-goat is possessed of properties similar to those of a cow, and specially beneficial to THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. XLV. persons suffering from phthisis.* is light, astringent, appetising (Dipana), and is efficacious in dyspnoea, cough and haemoptysis (Amlapitta—A. The milk of a she-goat proves curative in all diseases owing to the smiillness of her limbs and her agile habits, as well as for the fact of her drinking comparatively a less quantity of water and living upon bitter and pungent herbs. The milk of a she-camel is parchifying, heating, hght, palatable and possessed of a little saline taste. proves curative in oedema, abdominal glands, ascites, piles, intestinal worms and Kushtha, and a good antitoxic agent. The milk of a ewe is sweet, demulcent, heavy and proves aggravating in disorders Pittam and Kapham. forms good diet Kevalavata and in cough due to the deranged condition of the bodily Vayu. The milk of a she-buffalo is sweet taste, tends to impair digestion and increases the slimy secretion the organs. heavy, soporific, cooling, and contains more latty matter than cow's milk. The milk of a she-animal with unbifurcated hoofs (Ekashapha) such the mare, etc., tonic, light, parchifying, sweet and acid in taste, leaving a saline after-taste, and proving curative in cases of rheumatism restricted to the extremities. It has been recently discovered by a German physician thai tuber- culosis bacilli do no not thrive in goat's- blood—Translator. Chap. XLV. The milk of a woman is cold and sweet, leaving an astringent after-taste. It proves beneficial as an errhine and acts as a good wash in eye diseases. is whole- some, vitahsing, light and appetising. The milk of a she- elephant is sweet though it leaves an astringent after-taste. spermatopoietic, heavy, demulcent, cooling and tonic. It invigorates the e5'esight. The milk of a she-animal, milched in the morning, is heavy, cold and takes a long time digested owing to her entire repose (literally want physical exercise locomotion) during the night, when cooling attributes preponderate.

The milk, which emits a fetid smell, or has become discoloured and insipid, or has acquired an acid taste and looks shreddy and curdled, or tastes saline, should be regarded as Unwholesome and injurious. The Curd-group :—There are three kinds of curd such as, the sweet, the acid, and the extremely acid curd. Milk curd generally leaves astringent after-taste. demulcent and heat-making its potency, well spermatopoietic, vitahsing and auspicious. It proves curative in Pinasa (nasal catarrh), intermittent fever (Vishama Jvara, dysentery, non- relish for food, difficult urination, and general cachexia. Acid curd deranges the Pittam and the Kapham^, while the extremely acid curd vitiates the blood. Curd, which has been not perfectly curdled (Mandajatam) acid its (digestive) chemical reaction, acts inordinately strong purgative and diuretic agent, and deranges the three fundamental humours the body. Curd prepared with the milk of a she-elephant, light digestion, subdues Kapham, and is heat-making in its potency. It impairs digestion, leaves an astringent after-taste and increases the quan- tity fecal matter. Of all the preceding kinds curd, the one prepared with cow's milk should regarded as the best in virtue and quality. This curd well filtered through a piece of clean linen, imparts a relish the food, whereas the curd, which had been prepared with boiled milk, should deemed the most efficacious. The cloth-filtered curd subdues the deranged Va^ai. is demulcent and restorative, though tends to increase the Kapham without bring- ing about similar augmentation the Pittam. The curd prepared with boiled milk subdues the deranged V^yu and Pittam, imparts a relish to the food, and acts as a good stomachic remedy. It increases the strength and the root principle life. The cream of curd heavy and spermatopoietic. It subdues the deranged Vayu, impairs digestion and phlegma- gogic and aphrodisiac. Curd made without cream is parchifying, astringent and arrests stool and urine (Vistambhi), It increases the bodily Vayu. is appe- tising and is comparatively lighter, a little astringent in taste, and imparts a rehsh to food. IVIetrical Texts :—A compound made of equal parts of curd and water and subsequenth' churned so as have the contained cream butter completely skimmed off, and which neither too thick nor too thin, called Takram. possesses taste blended the sweet, acid and astringent. Waterless curd, churned with the entire butter creamy sub- stance inherent called Gholam kind whey). The use of Takram prohibited the hot season, nor should "it be given to a weak person, nor to one suffering from an ulcer, or laid up with an attack hccmoptysis, or to one suffering from epileptic fits, vertigo (Bhrama), or from a burning sensation the body. The use of Takram is recommended during the cold months of the 3'ear, as well as to persons suffering from diseases due to the action of the deranged Kapham, from suppression of stool urine, etc., from the effects of the deranged Vayu. Again sweetened Takram soothes the deranged Pittam and aggravates the Kapham. Acid Takram subdues the Vayu and produces Pittam. IVIetrical Texts a case of deranged disordered V^yu, acid Takram should be drunk mixed with rock-salt, and with sugar in disorders of the Pittam, Chap. XLV. while in a case of deranged Kapliam should be mixed with Yavakshara and the powders of the drugs known as Vyosha. TakrakurcMka (Inspissated milk) astrin- gent (^Gr^hi), parchifying and hard to digest. It produces Vayu. The Manda or the residuary sediment of a«com- pound made the aforesaid Kurchika and Dadhi Takram fcurd-whey) is lighter than whey. Kilata* heavy, hypnotic, spermatopoietic and subdues V^yu. Similarly, Morathat and Piyusha? are sweet the taste and restorative and aphrodisiac in their properties. Fresh butter (Navanita'i is an albuminous substance, and is light, sweet, cooling, demulcent, pleasant, appe- tising, slightly acid and astringent. subdues the deranged Vayu and Pittam. spermatopoietic, antacid in its reaction, and conduces the improve- ment one's memory and intellectual capacities. proves beneficial cases consumption, cough, d3'spnoea, ulcer, piles and fecial paralysis. Butter few days standing) heav)\ increases the quantity of fat and Kapham, and imparts strength and rotundity to the body, and proves especially wholesome children. Clarified butter matured from eleven hundred years called the Kumbha Gritam (Pitcher clarified butter), while that, which older than the one the preceding kind, called the Maha GLritam (the great clarified butter). They are flesh-making and strength-imparting, and increase the firmness of the body. They are possessed of eye- invigorating virtues, and are anuretic, liquefacient (Lekhana), bitter and stomachic (P^chana). They cure V^yu and Kapham. They are vermifuge and pro- duce slight Pittam, leaving an astringent after-taste. They relieve aching pain in the head, ears, and the female organs of generation (Yoni), act purifying agents respect of the uterus, and prove curative in urticaria. The use of sesamum oil is recommended in cases of cut, cleft, punctured, severed, lacerated, blistered, thrashed or contused wounds and ulcers, and burns Chap. XLV. and scalds whether due the application heat or any vesicant alkaline solution, well bites of wild beasts and* birds, etc., and act beneficially baths, unguents and lubrications. Metrical Texts :— Oil should used snuffing, and as enematas (Vasti), eye-drops, ear-drops, as well as in seasoning soups, curries and cordials, etc. It pacifies the bodily Vayu. Castor Oil is sweet, hot in its potency\ irritating and appetising. It leaves pungent astringent after-taste, and is subtile. It acts as a cleansing agent in respect of the internal channels of the body, and is wholesome to the skin. spermatopoietic, sweet digestion (Vipaka), and rejuvenating. purifies the semen, vagina, and removes vaginal and uterine disorders, and contributes the preservation sound health. improves the memory, complexion and intellect (of its user), subdues the bodily Vayu and Kapham, and cleanses the system from all injurious principles inducing purging. Oils obtained from the seeds of Nimba, Atasi, Mulaka, Jimutaka, Vrikshaka, Kritavedhana, Arka, Kampillaka, Hastikarna, Prilhvika, Pilu, Karanja, Ingudi, Shigru, Sarsapa, Suvarchala, Vidanga or Jyotishmati seeds, are irritating, light, non -heat-making their potency, and pungent in taste and digestion. They act as a good laxative, and prove curative diseases due the THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLV. deranged V.-Cyu, or Kapham, as well cases Kushtha, Prameha, head disease, and intestinal parasites. IVIetrical Texts :—Ksliaiima (Linseed) oil sweet. It subdues the bodily V^yu and strength- giving, and pungent in digestion. Devoid of an}' eye- invigorating properties, is hot tliough demulcent, and heavy. It increases the Pittam. Mustard oil light, and acts as a vermifuge. proves curative itch and cutaneous affections, reduces V^yu, Kapham and fat, and is pungent, appetising and Lekhana (liquefacient). Oil obtained from the seeds of Ingudi is a vermifuge, and is light, and slightly bitter in taste. It proves curative in Kushtha and parasitic disorders, and affects the strength, semen and the eyesight of its user.

chapter: Chapter XLV: Now we shall discourse the Chapter, which deals with the rules to be observed respect of liquid substances general (Drava-Dravya-Vidhi- madhyaryam). Water Group :—Atmospheric, rain water possessed non-patent taste. ambrosial its nature, pleasant and beneficial life. enlivening,* invigorating strength-giving, f frigerent, frigorific, antipyrotic_, anti-hypnotic, and conquers vertigo, drowsiness and fits fainting. most wholesome the human body. After having fallen upon the surface of the earth it acquires one the six different tastes according the nature its receptacle such river, or a Xada (a river with a masculine name), a pond, tank (Vapi) Kupa Chunti fountain, Artesian spring Vikira 1, fallow land (Kedar), a pond covered over with growth aquatic plants Enlivens ihe body during fits of fainting and such like cases. + Imparts strength to the exhausted or emaciated frames. tank large well with its sides protected by buttresses masonry work. § A well with flights of masonry steps descending to its bottom. II An ordinary well, unprotected by buttresses and unpro\-ided with steps. If A flow of subterranean water dug out of a bed of sand
english_text: The oil obtained from Kushnmbha flowers is pungent in digestion and leads to the derange- ment of all the bodily humours. It is irritating, and acid its reaction (Vidahi). It is devoid of any eye-cleans- ing property and brings on haemoptysis. The Oils obtained from the Kirata-tiktaka, Atimuktaka, Vibhitaka, N^rikela, Kola, Akshoda, Jivanti, Piydla, Karvudara, Surjavalli, Trapusa, Erv^ruka, Karkaru, and Kushm^nda seeds, etc. are sweet in taste, potency and digestion, and tend to pacify the deranged V4yu and Pittam. Cooling in their potency, they increase the slimy secretions of the organs, impair digestion, and help the copious evacuation of stool and urine. Chap. XLV. The Oils of the Madhuka (Maula), Kishmarya, and Palasha seeds are sweet and astringent. They pacif}^ the deranged Kaph^m and Pittam. The oils the Tuvaraka and Bhallataka are heat-making, sweet and astringent, and leave a bitter after-taste. The}^ ptove curative in diseases due to the action of deranged V^Cyu and Kapham, as well obesity, Meha, cutaneous affections, and intestinal worms, and cleanse the system both b}' their emetic and purgative actions. The Oils obtained from the piths (Sara of such trees as, Sarala, Devadaru, Gandira, Shiushapa and Aguru, are bitter, pungent and astringent their tastes, and act purifying agents in respect of bad ulcers. The}' prove curative in skin diseases and destroy the deranged Va)^!, Kapham, and intestinal worms. The Oils obtained from the seeds Tumvi, Koshamra, Danti, Dravanti, Shyama, Saptala, Nilika, Kampiilaka, and Siiankhini, are bitter, pungent and astringent in their tastes. They serve to cleanse the system from all impurities and baneful principles through their purgative properties. They act as purifying agents in respect of malignant ulcers, and prove curative diseases due the deranged V^yu and Kapham, as well as in skin-diseases (Kushtha), and parasitic complaints. Yavatikata-oil tends to subdue all the deranged humours, is slightly bitter, and acts as a good elixir. appetising, acid, and liquefacient. It is holy and wholesome (Pathyam), and serves improve the memory its user. The THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. xLv. Oil From Ekaishika seeds sweet, and extremely cooling. It subdues the Pittam, increases the Kapham and aggravates the V^yu. The Oil of the seeds mango stones is slightly bitter taste, and extremely aromatic. subdues the and Kapham. parchifying, sweet and astringent, palatable, and not highly Pittam-making. IVIetrical Texts :—The therapeutic properties of the oils from the seeds of fruits, which have not been specifically described in the present chapter, should considered identical with those the fruits seeds of which they have been so pressed out. All the vegetable oils (Sneha) described above should regarded possessed the virtue of subduing the bodily Vayu, and they possess some of the properties, which specifically belong Besainum oil.

english_text: Sesamum oil the most commendable all oils inasmuch the very word, which signifies oil (Tailam), etymologically derived from Tilam (sesamum). The oil, myosin (Vasd), fat, marrow, and Ghritam obtained from animals, which live in villages (Gramya), or frequent the marshy swamps (Anupa), or are aquatic (Audoka) in their habits, are heavy, heat- making in their potency, and sweet in taste. They subdue the bodily Vayu, while those obtained from J^ngala (such deer, etc.) carnivorous animals, from those possessed of unbifurcated hoofs, are light, cool their Chap. XLV. SUTRASTHA'NAM. potenc}', astringent in taste, and prove curative in cases of haemoptysis. The fat, marrow, etc. of animals of the Pratuda and Vishkira species (doves, pigeons^ etc.) reduce the bodily Kapham. Of clarified butter, oil, myosin (Vasa), fat and marrow of animals eacli heavier digestion, and possesses a greater power of subduing the bodily Vayu than the one immediately preceding it in the order of enumeration. The Honey Group :—Honey sweet, and leaves an astringent after-taste. It is parchifying, cold, stomachic, cosmetic, tonic, light, softening, palatable, liquefacient (Lekhanan), and fermenting (Sandhanam). It acts as a purifying and healing agent respect ulcers and eyes, is aphrodisiac, astringent, and tends to permeate all the minutest channels and capillaries of the organism. antifat and pacifies the deranged Pittam and Kapham, and proves curative hiccough, Meha, dyspnoea, cougli, dysentery, vomiting and thirst. vermifuge, antitoxic and demulcent, and influences the subduing of the three deranged humours. Owing to its lightness it subdues the deranged Kapham, and proves a good antidote the deranged Vayu and Pittam owing to its sliminess, sweetness and astringent taste. IVIetrical Texts :—Eight different kinds honey are commonly used such the Pauttikam, Bhr^maram, Kshaudram, Mdkshikam, Chh^tram, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XLV. Argh3'am, Auddalakam and Dalam.* these the honey obtained from the hives of bees, known the Puttikas, hot and parchifying owing to their habit of sucking the juice or sap of flowers and plants without eliminating therefrom other foreign or poisonous matter, that might have become naturally accidentally mixed with This kind of honey is intoxicating and acid in its re-action, and tends to aggravate the Vayu, blood and Pittam. acts liquefacient dis- cutient agent. Honey, known as the Bhramaram, is extremely heavy owing its extremely sweet taste and slimy character, while the one known the Kshaudram extremely cool, light and liquefacient. Honey known as the Makshikain lighter, dryer and more efficacious than the honey of the preceding class (Kshaudram), and proves specially benencial cases of dyspnoea, etc. Honey, known the Chhatram, (l) The kind of honey obtained from hives large, yellow bee*, is called the Pauttikam. (2) The kind of honey obtained from hives of bees of the Bhramara species is called Bhramaram.

english_text: (3) The kind of honey ol^tained from hives of small, tawny brown bees is called the Kshaudram. (4) The kind of honey obtained from the hives of large, brown bees of the jNIakshik^ species is called Makshikam, (5) The kind of honey obtained from the umbrella shaped hives of bees of the Chhatra species is called Chhdtram. (6) The honey obtained from the hives of thin-mouthed bees the Argha species often found in ant-hills is called Arghyam. (7) The kind of honey obtained from the hives of small brown bees the Uddiilakam species is called Auddalakam. (8) The kind of honey found accumulated leaves honey-bearing plants is called D^lam. Chap. XLV. sweet in digestion, heav}", cooling and slimy. acts as a vermifuge, proves curative in haemoptysis, psoriasis and Meha, and is* possessed of a high efficacy. Honey known the Arghyam possessed properties highly beneficial to the eye. It is a gi^eater subduer of Pittam and Kapham than any other kind of honey, astringent taste, and pungent digestion. bitter tonic and does not generate Vayu the system. Honey known the Auddalakam improves the voice and relish for food. It is antitoxic, and proves curative in cutaneous affections. It is heat-mak- ing its potency, and acid and astringent taste. generates Pittam, and pungent digestion. Honey, known as the D^lam, is parchifying and proves beneficial cases vomiting and Meha. Fresh honey constructive and aphrodisiac, acts a mild laxative, and to a small extent subdues the deranged Kapham. Old honey astringent and liquefacient, and reduces fat and obesity. Honey, that has attained thickened condensed state course of time (Pakka Madhu), tends subdue the three deranged humours, while thin and im matured honey (Ama Madhu) possessed contrary properties, and tends agitate the three fundamental humours of the body. In conjunction with many other drugs and medicinal remedies, honey proves curative various diseases, and partakes of the virtues of the drugs or substances with which is so used (Yoga-Vahika). 4^2 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [ Chap. XLV. Honey not collected from the flowers of any particular species. the other hand, the honey- making bees cull it from the sap and juice of flowers and plants, which are incompatible with one another respect their nature, taste, virtue, potency and re-actionary (chemical) effect. For these reasons, and further from the fact being prepared poisonous bees, honey becomes positively injurious after contact with heat fire, and accordingly the u^e of hot or boiled honey is forbidden. Metrical Texts :—On account of its poisonous contact in its origin honey exerts similar injurious virtue. Used boiling heated state, hot country, during the hot season the year, heated state the body, honey sure to prove fatal like poison. Honey specially made injurious by hot contact owing its placidity and coolness, and further for the reason its being collected from the sap variety flowers and plants.

english_text: Atmospheric water (rain-water), like heat, serves impart injurious character all kinds honey (except the one known as the Arghyam Madhu). Wletrical Texts :—For emetic purposes honey may administered with any other hot substance, inasmuch intended such case that the imbibed lioney, instead being retained Chap. XLV. digested in the stomach, should be ejected immediately after its use.* Undigested honey is more painful, or gives rise to a greater discomfort than all other undigested substances the stomach, more so because internal fermenfation and use of hot water, which are usually resorted a case of deranged digestion, can not be used in case of undigested honey retained in the stomach, owing the poisonous nature of the chemical change (Vip^ka) undergoes therein contact with hot substances in general. Hence, undigested honey is as fatal any poison. The Sugar-cane group :- Sugar cane sweet in taste and digestion, heavy, cool, demulcent, strength-giving, spermatopoietic, and diuretic. produces Kapham in the bod}", and proves remedial haemoptysis, and helps the germination of worms in the intestines. Metrical text :—There are many species sugar-cane such as, the Paundraka, Bhiruka, Vanshaka, Shataporaka, K^ntara, Tapasekshu, Kastekshu, Suchi- patraka, Naipala, Dirghapatraka, Nilapora, and Koshakrit. Now we shall deal with the specific Though the use of honey with hot substances not forbidden such cases, still many an experienced physician the Ayurvedic scliool thinks safe refrain from its use, lest it might retained the stomach for a considerable time, or find out a downward outlet and pass off with the stool. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLV. virtues each them. The Sugar-cane the Paundraka and Bhiruka t^-pes is cooHng, sweet, demul- cent and constructive. It produces Kapham and laxative without giving rise to imperfect gastric diges- tion. is heavy and spermatopoietic. The Sugar- cane of the Vanshaka species possessed of similar properties the two foregoing ones, though little alkaline its constitution, while that the Shatapora species is a little more heat-making than that of the preceding class, and found to subdue the deranged Vayu. The Sugar-cane of the Kdntara and Tapasa species possessed of the same virtues that of the Vanshaka class. The Sugar-cane the Kastekshu species identical its properties with that of the aforesaid Vanshaka class, though tends agitate the bodil)" Va3'u. The Sugar-cane of the Suchipatra, Nilapora, Naipala and Dirghpatra species produces Vayu the system, and subdues the Kapham and Pittam. slightly astringent taste and indigestible (gives rise to acidity after digestion). The Sugar-cane of the Koshakara species heavy digestion), cooling and proves curative cases of haemopt5^sis and wasting diseases in general. Sugar-cane is extremely sweet about the roots, sweet at the middle, and saline at the tops and joints. The juice sugar-cane when' eaten raw not marked any acid reaction after digestion. Chap.

Water Group :—Atmospheric, rain water possessed non-patent taste. ambrosial its nature, pleasant and beneficial life. enlivening,* invigorating strength-giving, f frigerent, frigorific, antipyrotic_, anti-hypnotic, and conquers vertigo, drowsiness and fits fainting. most wholesome the human body. After having fallen upon the surface of the earth it acquires one the six different tastes according the nature its receptacle such river, or a Xada (a river with a masculine name), a pond, tank (Vapi) Kupa Chunti fountain, Artesian spring Vikira 1, fallow land (Kedar), a pond covered over with growth aquatic plants Enlivens ihe body during fits of fainting and such like cases. + Imparts strength to the exhausted or emaciated frames. tank large well with its sides protected by buttresses masonry work. § A well with flights of masonry steps descending to its bottom. II An ordinary well, unprotected by buttresses and unpro\-ided with steps. If A flow of subterranean water dug out of a bed of sand
english_text: XLV.] spermatopoietic, and subdues the Vayu and the Kapham, and is pleasant to the taste. The juice sugar-cane otherwise pressed* out is heavy digestion, is long retained in the stomach, and is followed by reactionary acidity, and arrests the evacuation stool and iiTine. The juice ripe sugar-cane heavy digestion, possessed of laxative properties, keen, and demulcent. It subdues the Vayu and Kapham. The inspissated or half boiled juice of sugar-cane (Phanitam) is sweet taste and heavy. increases the slimy secretions of the organs, acts as a flesh-builder, and is devoid of all spermatopoietic properties. brings about simul- taneous derangement of the three bodil}'' humours. Common treacle is found to be charged with a little alkali. sweet taste and not too cooling. acts demulcent and purifier the blood and urine. subdues the deranged Vayu and, to a slight extent, deranges the Pittam well. increases fat, Kapham, and corpulency, and possessed tonic and spermatopoietic properties. White and purified (Shuddha) treacle is sweet taste, and purifies the blood. It subdues the deranged Vayu and Kapham, and is one of the most wholesome diets for man. Its efficacy increases with its years. The different modifications of treacle such the Matsandika, Khamda, and Sharkara (sugar) which are progressivly more refined, should be deemed as gaining THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap, XLV. more in their cooling, demulcent and aphrodisiac pro- perties, and getting heavier digestion in each of the successive stages of refinement. They are successively more frigorific, and beneficial in cases of ha3moptysis. lYIctrica! Texts :—To the properties considered as specially belonging each of these modifications of treacle should be attributed its power of producing its own refinement and efficacy. The virtues sugar such as, laxativeness, etc., should be regarded proportional to its refinement, freedom from alkaline saturation, and the actual quantity of sweetening matter (lit. substance) contained in Sugar prepared from concentrated honey (Madhu Sharkara) is parchifying and liquefacient. proves beneficial cases vomiting and dysentery, pleasant, has sweet and astringent taste, and sweet in digestion. Sugar prepared from a decoction of Yavasa Sharkara (Duralabha) has a sweet and astringent taste, leaves bitter after-taste, and possessed laxative properties, and subdues the deranged Kapham. All kinds of sugar tend assuage burning sensations in the body, and prove curative in hoemoptysis, vomiting, epileptic fits, and thirst. The sweet and concentrated extract (Phanitam) Modhuka flowers should regarded as parchifying. It produces Vayu and Pittam, and subdues Kapham. sweet, astringent its digestive transformation, and deranges the blood. Chap. XLV. Madya Varga ( Wines and spirits):— All species of wine are acid taste, and appetising. They generate Pittam, and impart greater relish to one's food.

english_text: The wine which marked by a deep yellow colour strong and hot, is only imperfectly digested and followed kind of acid re-action. It tends to aggravate the Pittam. The wine, which frothy or putrified, heavy insipid or is marked by the germination of worms in its body, is decanted over-night, tends to enrage agitate the bodily Vayu. The wine which well-matured, and possessed of its characteristic taste, and the virtue of improving the appetite and bringing on a relish for food, and which subdues the Vayu and Kapham, and mild, good, aromatic and exhilarating, should THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLV. garded the only wine fit for use. Wines may divided into variety species according their different tastes and strength. The potency wine promoted the bodily heat man courses upward through the arteries, and ultimately reaches the heart and thence, through its own subti- lity and expansiveness, permeates the entire organism and gradually attacks and overwhelms the organs sense perception, dethrones the mind from her throne of reason, usurps the permanent seat of intellect, and thus brings on intoxication.* A man of phlegmatic tempera- ment (Kapha-prakriti) can carry his wine well, and symptoms of intoxication usually appear later in him. man bilious temparament (Pitta Prakriti\ under such circumstances, gets easily intoxicated, while the man whose temperament is marked by predominance Vayu often found tipsy after his first cup. A man of a Sattvika frame of mind exhibits under the influence of wine, decided predilection for fine dress, jollity, and acts of purity and compassion. He sings, reads, evinces strong desire for female company. A man of a Rajasika frame of mind becomes extremely melancholy pugnaci- ous his cups, indulges in despondent reveries, and evinces suicidal tendencies ; while wine in a subject a Tamasika cast of mind exhibits the latent and innate * These couplets emphatically prove that the framers ancient -Ayurveda were fully conversant with the circulation of the blood—Tr, Chap. XLV.] vileness his soul. Such a person generally sleeps when intoxicated, falsely boasts of his own excellence, and evinces a de5ire for women with whom connection is forbidden by both social and canonical laws. Fermented liquors known the Shukta (treacle, honey, fermented rice gruel, and curd cream kept new and clean vessel underneath a bushel of paddy for three consecutive days) bring on an attack of haemoptysis. They disintegrate the lumps or knots of accumulated Kapham, are digestant and prove curative in jaundice and diseases due to the derangement of Kapham. They are light and vermifugenous, and strong and heat making in their potency. They act as diuretic, are pleasant, and pungent in digestion. Bulbs and roots pickled in Shukta acquire the properties of the latter.

english_text: Of the Shuktas prepared with treacle, juice of sugar-cane, or honey, each preceding one should be deemed heavier and as giving rise to greater secretions of internal organs than the one immediately following it in the order of enumeration. The different kinds of fermented rice gruel known the Tushamvu and Sauvira are pleasant and appetising beverages. They prove efficacious in cases of jaundice, worms in the intestines, dysentery, piles, and in diseases affecting the heart. They are possessed of purgative (Bhedi) properties. The fermented gruel known as the Dhanyamlam good appetiser (tonic—D. R) owing the fact ^66 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLV. its being a preparation of paddy. a plaster, alleviates the burning sensation of the skin, and as a potion it subdues the V^yu and Kapliam, and alla3's thirst. Used as a gargle it forms one of the best remedies for reducing Kapham owing to its keenness. It is light of digestion, acts as a deodorant, removes the sense exhaustion and bad taste from the mouth, allays thirst, acts as good appetiser and dissolvent, possessed of purgative virtues, and is advantageously used as an enemata (Asthapanam). It is very wholesome to sea- faring men. The Urine Group :—The urine cows, buffaloes, goats, sheep, mules, horses, and camels* are commonly hot and bitter, and leave a saline after-taste. They are light and are used as piu-ifying agents, and will prove curative in diseases due to the derangement of the Vayu or Kapham, as well as in worms in the intestines, obesity, poisoning, abdominal glands, piles, ascites, cutaneous affections, oedema, non-relish for food, and jaundice. general they act cardiac stimulants (Hridyam) and appetisers. Authoritative verses on the Sub- ject :—They are all pungent, strong, hot, light and have a saline after-taste. They act blood- piu-ifiers or disinfecting agents, reduce corpulency, act vermi- * The urine of a cow, she-buffalo, ewe and she-goat should taken and used, while similar secretions the male should be taken where man, camel and elephant should be indicated. Chap. XLV. fiiges and are anti-toxic. They subdue the deranged Vayu and Kapham. They prove beneficial in cases piles, ascites, abdominal tumours, oedema and non- relish for food. They prove beneficial jaundice, and act cardiac stimulants, and are possessed appetising, purgative and stomachic virtues. The urine of a cow is pungent, strong and hot, and does not generate Vayu on account of its being saturated with alkali. light, stomachic, slightly generates the Pittam^ and subdues the Vayu and Kapham. proves beneficial cases of colic, abdominal glands, ascites and distention of the abdomen, and is used for the purposes of purging and enematas (Asthdpananr. cases, which prove amenable to the use of urine, the urine of a cow should be used to the exclusion of that of any other animal, even to that of an ox.

chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: The urine (she) baffalo proves beneficial piles, abdominal dropsy, colic, cutaneous affections, Meha, imperfect action emetics purgatives, constipation, oedema, abdominal glands, and jaundice. The urine of a (she) goat has pungent, bitter taste. It slighly agitates the bodily Vayu and proves curative cases cough, dyspnoea, consumption, jaundice and chlorosis. The urine of a ewe contains alkali, and has a bitter pungent taste. subdues the deranged Vayu, and heat-making its potency. It proves beneficial in cough, enlarged spleen, abdomi- THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLV. nal dropsy, dyspnoea, consumption and in obstinate con- stipation the bowels. The urine of a horse appetising and pungent in taste, strong^ and heat-making its potency. subdues the deranged V^yu and Kapha'm, and cures mental aberrations. usuall}^ recommended cases ringworm and worms the intestines. The urine of an elephant has a bitter and saline taste. It is keen and purgative, and subdues the Vayu and enrages the Pittam. is commonly used the treatment (KiUsa) leucoderma and in the preparation of alkalies. The urine of an ass tends to neutralise the effects of poison generated through the chemical action two different substances in the organism. It is strong and proves curative cases of chronic d5'sentery. vermifuge and subdues the Vayu and Kapham, and appetising. The urine camel proves beneficial cases oedema, leprosy, abdominal dropsy, insanitv, worms the intestines, piles and diseases due the action of the deranged Vayu. Human urine strong anti-toxic. I have now briefly described the properties of all kinds of liquid food or drink. An experienced physician should prescribe them for the use of his king according to the nature of season and the country in which they are to be applied. Thus ends tlie forty-fifth Chapter the Sutrasthanam the Sushruta Samhitd which treats of liquids. CHAPTER XLVI. english_text: Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^ THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XLVI. solid food, (Ashitha) drink, Leedham (food taken licking), and the Kh^ditam (food that is chewn and assimilated only for the enjoyment specific taste). variety substances enters into the composition our food, which admit being grouped under various preparations and necessarily involve the co-operation of a large number of dynamic- forces. I am eager to learn of the substances and their specific inherent properties, virtues, potencies and transfonnations (chemical reactions) which constitute the daily food of human beings, inasmuch as a physician, who is ignorant of them, is quite helpless checking or curing any distemper and maintaining health in indivi- duals. And since food (diet) determines the origin all created beings, instruct us, O Lord, the proper regimen of food and drink." The holy Dhanvantari, thus addressed Sushruta, replied follows child, hear discourse the proper regulation food and drink requested you. They tend to slightly increase the secretions of the internal organs and bring on constipation of the bowels. Their general properties are nearly identical with those the aforesaid Shashtikas. The species Krishna- Vrihi the best of them all. hght, and leaves astringent after taste, the remaining varieties gradually deteriorating quality from the one under discussion. Sh^li rice grown in a J^ngala country has taste slightly blended of the pungent, astringent, sweet and has a shade of bitter. subdues the deranged Pittam and Kapham, (generates Vayu — D. R.) and good digestant and stomachic. Shali rice grown in a Kaidara or marshy country has a Chap. XLVI. ] sweet taste with a shade of the astringent. It is tonic and spermatopoietic, aphro disiac and heavy of diges- tion. It reduces the quantity of excrement, subdues the Pittam, and increases the Kapham. Rice of once transplanted paddy plants (Ropya) of those transplanted several times in succession (Ati- ropya) is light, easily digested and comparatively more efficacious. acts as constructive tonic and is not followed any reactionary acidity after digestion. It destroys the deranged humours and is diuretic. Rice of paddy plants, sprouting Irom the stubbles of a previous harvest, is parchifying. It suppresses the evacuations stool, has a bitter and astringent taste, subdues the Pittam, is easily digestible and generates Kapham. have fully described the good and bad species of grain belonging to the Sh^li group. chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: The four (black, red, yellow and white) varieties Priyangus reduce the Kapham and produce a parched condition in the body, each preceding species being superior as regards its virtues the one immediatel)' following it in the order of enumeration. The species Madhulikas as well as the one known as the Nandimukhi is sweet, cool and demulcent. The species Varuka and Mukundaka are largely possessed absorbing virtues. The species Venu-yava, is parchify- ing, and heat-making in its potency and is pungent of digestion, brings retention of the urine, subdues the deranged Kapham, and tends to enrage the bodily Vayu. The several varieties of pulse (Vaidala) commonly used as food are known as the Mudga, Vana-Mudga, Kalaya, Makushtha, Masura, Mangalya, Chanaka, Satina, Triputa, Harenu, and Adhaki, etc. IVIetrical Texts:—They are generally cool and pungent of digestion, and have a sweet and astringent taste. They generate the Vayu, arrest the flow urine and the evacuation stool, and subdue the Pittam and Kapham. The species known the Mudga does not excessivel}' generate Vayu in the system but tends to purify and invigorate the Chap. XLVI. organ of vision. The green species is the best of all the varieties Mudga pulse. The properties Vanya (Mudga) are similar to those of the common variet}'. The species of pulse known Masura sweet digestion and constipates the bowels. The species Makushthaka is vermigenous, while the species Kalaya generates Vayu copiously. The species Adhaki subdues the Pittam and Kapham, and does not excessively agitate the V^yu in the organism. The species known as the Chanaka generates Vayu and cooling its potency. has a sweet and astringent taste, and produces a parched condition of the body. It subdues the Pittam and Kapham, corrects the deranged blood, and tends to bring on a loss of the virile powers. The species known as Satina and Harenu are astring- ent in their virtues and tend to constipate the bowels. All the varieties of pulse except the Mudga and the Masura tend to produce a distension the abdomen caused by gas or air in the organism (Tympanites Adhm^na). The pulse known as the Masha has a sweet taste, is heavy and pleasant, laxative, diuretic, demulcent, heat-making, aphrodisiac, and specifically sperma- topoietic, tonic and galactogoguic. subdues the Vayu and increases the Kapham. The species known as the Alasandra (Rajamasha) does not produce Kapham THE SUSHRtJTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLVI. and is neither laxative nor diuretic on account its astringent nature. is sweet in taste and of digestion, pleasant, glactogoguic and improves-'a relish for food. The properties of Atmagupta and Kakanda seeds are similzir those of the aforesaid Masha pulse. The species known the Aranya-masha astringent in taste, produces condition parchedness the system, and is not followed by any reactionary^ acidity after being digested. The pulse known as Kulattha has an astringent taste, is pungent of digestion and is possessed astringent properties.

chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: the Ena (black deer), the Harina (red deer), the Rishya (blue deer), the Kuranga (antelope\ the Karala, the Kritam^la, the Sharabha, the Shvadanstra, the Prishata, the Chitrila (Spotted deer), the Ch^rushka, Chap. XLVI.J the Mriga-m^trika, etc. These species of venison have sweet and astringent taste, are hght, keen, pleasant (palatable), laxative, and diuretic in their effect. They subdue the Vayu and the Pittam. IVIetrical Text :—The venison the* Ena species sweet and astringent in taste, and palatable, and proves curative in diseases due to the deranged condition of the Pittam, blood and Kapham. It is astrin- gent its effect, imparts strength the system, improves relish for food and febrifuge. The venison the Harina (red) species is sweet taste and digestion, appetising, aromatic, cool, light, and suppresses the discharge stool and urine and pacifies the deranged humours. Deer which are black are called Ena, while those which are red are called Harina. Those, that are neither red nor black, are designated Kuranga. The venison the Mriga-matrik^ species cooling and proves curative cases haemoptysis, Sannipata diseases (due the concerted action of the three deranged humours), consumption, dyspncea, cough, and hiccough and creates a relish for food. Birds such as the Lava, Tittiri, Kapinjala, Vartira, Vartika, Vartaka, Naptrika, V^tika_, Chakora, Kala- vinka, Mayura, Krakara, Upachakra, Kukkuta, S^ranga, Shata-Patraka, Kutittiri, Kuruvahuka and Yavalaka belong to the Vishkira species. [They are called THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XLVI. from the fact their pickmg their food after scattering first with their bills and claws (Skr. kira, scatter)]. The flesh bird this group light, cooling, sweet and astringent taste and tends to pacify the deranged humours of the body. The flesh of the Lava light, has sweet and astringent taste, pungent digestion, and possessed astringent and appetising properties. It is highly efficacious in diseases due to the concerted humours of the body. The flesh the Tittirs slightly heavy, heat-making and sweet taste. spermatopoietic, appetising and astringent. It improves the intellect and complexion, and subdues the three deranged humours. The flesh of the yellow (Gaura) Tittiri proves curative hic-cough and dyspnoea, and subdues the deranged Vayu. The flesh of the Kapinjala light and cooling, and proves curative cases haemoptysis, and recommended diseases brought about through the deranged condition the Kapham V^yu (Manda-vata\ The flesh the Krakara the Upachakra light, pleasant (palatable), spermatopoietic, and appetising. subdues the Vayu and Pittam and impro^es the intellect. The flesh the Mayura astringent and saline taste, and beneficial the skin, helps the growth hair, improves the voice. Chap. XLVi 4^3 intellect, appetite and relish for food, and imparts strength and vigour to the organs of sight and hearing. The flesh of a wild cock is demulcent, heat-making, and spermatopoietic.

english_text: It acts as a diaphoretic, imparts tone the voice and the organism, subdues the deranged Vayu, and useful as a good constructive tonic. The flesh domesticated cock fowl possessed of properties similar those its wild prototype with the exception that hea^ y, and proves curative in rheumatism, consumption, vomiting and chronic (Vishama-Jvara) fever. Birds such the dove, pigeon Bhringaraja, cuckoo, Koyashtica, Kulinga, the domestic Kulinga, Gokshada, Dindimanaka, Shatapatraka, Matrinindaka, Bhedashi, Shuka, Sharika, Valguli, Girisha, Alahva, Dushaka, Sugrihi, Khanjaritaka, Harita, Datyuha, etc. belong to the group known as the Pratuda. Metrical Texts :—The Pratudas live on fruit, and their flesh has sweet and astringent taste. generates Vayu and produces a parched condition the organism. cooling its potency and reduces the Pittam and Kapham. suppresses the discharge of urine and reduces the quantity stool. these the flesh of the Bhedashi tends vitiate the humours and to derange the three excrements the body. The flesh of the domesticated Kulinga highly spermatopoietic, and proves curative in cases of haemoptysis. Animals such the Hon, tiger, wolf, hyena arboreal leopard (Vriksha dipi\ cat, jackal, bear, and jVIrigaervaruka (a jackal-shaped, deer-eating species tiger) belong the group the Guh^shayas (cave- dwelling mammals). IVIetrical Texts :—The flesh of animals belong- ing this family sweet, heavy, demulcent and strength- giving. subdues the deranged Vayu. heat-making in its potency, and proves beneficial in diseases affecting the eyes and anus. The Prasaha Group :—Birds such the Kaka, Kanka, Kurura, Chasa, Bhasa, Shashaghati, Uluka, Chilli, Shyena, Gridhra, etc. belong the family Prasahas (Carnivorous birds that suddenly dart on their prey). Metrical Text :—The flesh of birds belonging this group identical its virtues, potency, taste and digestive transformation with those the aforesaid carnivorous mammals, such as the lion, etc., and specially beneficial in cases of consumption and kindred wasting diseases. Chap. XLVI. The Parna-IVIriga Group :—Animals such as tlie Madgu, the arboreal Musika, the Vriksha-Shayika, Avakusha, Puti-gKasa and the Vanara, etc. belong to the family of Parna-Mrigas (lit tree-dwelling arboreal animals). Metrical Texts :—The flesh animals this group sweet, spermatopoietic and heavy digestion. invigorating the eyesight and beneficial in cases of consumption. is laxative and diuretic and cures cough, pile and dyspnoea.

Metrical Texts :^The general properties the flesh animals belonging this species are to increase the consistency stool and urine. They are heat-making in potency, sweet in digestion as those of the preceding group. The}- subdue the Vayu and generate the Pittam and Kapham. They are demulcent and beneficial cough, dyspnoea and cachexia. The flesh of the Shasha is sweet and astringent taste. reduces the Pittam and Kapham and neither produces nor subdues the Vayu owing its moderately cooling potency. The flesh of the Godha is sweet of digestion and has a pungent, astringent taste. THE SUSHRUtA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLVI. tonic and constructive and pacifies the Vayu and Pittam. The flesh the Shalyaka tasteful, light of digestion, cooling in its potency and anti-toxic, and subdues the deranged Pittam. The flesh the Mrigii-Priyaka proves a wholesome diet diseases due to the action of the deranged Vayu ( Vayu-Roga), while that of the Ajagara is beneficial in piles. The flesh of Sarpa (a species other than those specifically described) is curative piles and derange- ments of the Vayu. is a vermifuge and anti-toxic neutraliser chemical resulting poisons). vigorates the eye-sight, appetising and sweet and improves the intellect. these the flesh of the Darvicara appetising, pungent in digestion, sweet in taste, and extremely efficacious in ej^e diseases. laxative and diuretic, and subdues the deranged Vayu. Domestic Animal Group :—Animals such as horses, mules, cows, bullocks, asses, camels, goats, sheep, and Medapuchhas (fat tailed or Turkish sheep) etc., belong to the group of domestic animals (Gr^myas). IVIctrical Texts :—The flesh domestic animals possessed constructive, tonic and appetising properties, is sweet taste and diges- tion. destro5's the deranged Vayu and produces the Kapham and Pittam. Of these the flesh of the goat Chap. XLVI. SUTRASTHA'NAM. moderately cooling in its potenc}^ does not increase the secretions of the internal organs, is heavy and demul- cent, subdues the'Pittam and the Kapham, and is bene- ficial in nasal catarrh. The flesh of sheep (mutton) constructive, tonic and heav)', and generates the Pittam and Kapham. The flesh of the Medapuchcha is aphrodisiac and has properties similar those of mutton. Beef holy and refrigerant, proves curative dyspnoea^ catarrh, cough^ chronic fever and in cases of a morbid craving for food (Atyagni), and destroys the deranged Vayu. The flesh animal with unbifurcated hoofs (Ekashapha such as, the horse and the mule etc.), has a slightly saline taste, and is possess- ed of properties similar to those of mutton. The flesh of an animal belonging the Jangala group said to increase the secretions of the internal organs.

english_text: The flesh of beasts or birds dwelling remote from a village or pool tends to slightly increase the secretions of the internal organs, while the flesh of an animal living in a village or near reservoir of water, cessively increases that secretion. The Anupa Group -.—Animals, which are generally found frequent marshy places (Anupas) may be divided into five groups, such as, the Kulacharas (frequenting the shores of pools and lakes), Plavas THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLVI. (divers or swimmers), Koshastha (conchiferous aquatic animals such the molluscs etc.), the Fadinas and the (piscatory) Matsya. these, the elephant, the Gavaya, buffalo, Ruru (an animal of the deer species which casts its antlers and wanders roaming about in the forests autumn) Chamara, Srimara animal of the Zebra species with gi'een and red stripes), Rohita (red deer), boar, rhinoceros, Gokarna, K^lapuchchaka, together with the Nynku species of antlered deer) and the wild cow, etc., frequent the cool shores df swamps and lakes, and are accordingly included within the group of Kulacharas (shore-dwellers). The Metrical Texts :—The flesh animal this group spermatopoietic and destroys the deranged Vayu and Kapham. It is sweet in taste and digestion, cooling, tonic, demulcent and diuretic, and increases the quantity of Kapham. chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: The flesh of the Gokarna is sweet, demulcent, mild (soft), sweet in digestion and proves curative in cases of haemoptysis, and generates Kapham in the system. The Plava Croup :— Birds such the '^^^- SUSHKUTA SAMHITA'. i Chap. XLVI. Hansa, Sarasa, Kraimcha, Chakravaka, Kurura (belong also the Prasaha group) Kadamva, Karandava, Jivan Jivaka, Vaka, Valaka, Pimdarika, Plava, Sarari- mukha, Nandimukha, Madgu, Utkrosha, Kachdksha, Mallikaksha, Shuklaksha, Puslikarashdyika, Kon^laka, Amviikukkiitika, Megharava and Shvetacharana etc. belong to the Plava family. These birds are found move about in large flocks. IVIetrical Text :—The flesh any one this family cooling, demulcent, and spermatopoietic and destroys the deranged Vayu. proves beneficial in cases of h9emopt5^sis, is sweet in taste and of digestion, and possessed laxative and diuretic properties. The flesh of the Hansa is heavy (of digestion), heat- making, sweet and demulcent. It tends to improve the voice and complexion, and imparts strength the system. is spermatopoietic, tissue-building and tonic, and proves curative in nervous diseases (V^ta-Vikara). The Conchifcrous (Koshastha) Spe- cies :—Animals such as, the Shankha, Shankhanakha (a species of small bivalve molluscs), Shukti, Shambuka and Bhalluka, etc., belong to the Koshastha (conchifcr- ous) group. The Pa'dina Species :- Animals such the tortoise, alligator, crab, black crab, porpoise, etc., belong to this species. Chap. XLVI. Metrical Texts :—The flesh animals the Shankha and Kiirma orders sweet taste and digestion, cooling* in its potency, demulcent, and bene- ficial to stool and the Pittam. It destroys the deranged Vayu and produces Kapham. Of these, the spectes black crab strength-giving and heat-making its potency, and tends to destroy the deranged Vayu. The white species is laxative and diuretic its effect, and tends to bring about adhesion of fractured bones (or produces fermentation). It destroys the Vayu and Pittam. The Piscatory Order :—The piscatory group may roughly divided into two broad sub- divisions, such as the Marine and the River (fresh water) fish. The species such the Rohita, Pathina, Patala, Rajiva, Varmi, Gomatsya, Krishna-Matsya, Vagunjara, Murala, Sahasra-danstra, etc., belong the fresh water family. Metrical Texts :—The fresh M^ater (Xadeya) fish (river fish) are sweet taste, heavy digestion, bring haemoptysis and destroy the deranged Vayu. They are heat-making their potencies, spermatopoietic and demulcent and tend reduce the quantity stool. these, the Rohita leaves astringent after-taste, and destroys the deranged Vayu. This species lives such aquatic plants and herbs grow fresh-water pools THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLV. and not inordinately generate Pittam. The Pathinas produce Kapham and are spermatopoietic. They are carnivorous and somnolent' in their habits, tend to vitiate the blood and the Pittam, and originate dermal affections.

chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: The species fish known the Murala constructive, tonic, spermatopoietic and galactagoguic. Fish bred in tanks or ponds are palatable the taste and demulcent their effect, while those reared in large lakes are usually found gain in strength and size, while the species reared shallow water is weak and stunted. The Sarnudra (Marine) Species :~ The Timi, Timingila (a species of large whales) Kulisha, Paka matsya, Niralaka, Nandi-Varalaka, Makara, Gargarka, Chandraka, Mahamina, and Rajiva etc., con- stitute the family of marine fish. Metrical Texts :—Sea fish are heavy, demul- cent, and sweet, and do not inordinately produce Pittam. They are heat-making their potency), and sper- matopoietic and beneficial the stool, and destroy Vayu and generate Kapham. Sea fish are extremely strength-giving their effect owing the fact their living animal food. Fresh water fish are possessed greater tissue-building properties than their marine kindred, while those which are found wells and Chuntis Chap. XLVI. 49: are said to be possessed of gi-eater carminative (Vataghna) virtues than the two preceding orders. Fish reared tanks (Vapi) are* superior to the two preceding species owing their greater demulcent and palatable pro- perties. River fish are heavy the middle, &wing the fact their moving about with the help their head and tail, while those which are cultured tanks and ponds (Sarah and Tadaga) are speciall)' light about their heads. Fish, which are found hill streams fountains, are extremely heavy about the parts little below the region their head, account of their being con- fined within narrow limits and the consequent absence of an}^ lengthy sweep. Fish reared large tanks (Sarasi) are lighter the foreparts of their body and heavy their lower parts, as the}' put their entire pressure upon their breast the time of swimming. Thus have finished describing tl^e specific properties of the flesh animals that frequent swamps marshy grounds and which increase the secretions of the internal organs of a person using them for food. Dried putrid flesh, well the flesh diseased, old, emaciated, poisoned, snake-bitten animal, or of one of extremely tender years struck with poisoned dart weapon, that which has fed unnatural food, should carefull}^ avoided inasmuch dried putrid meat I'HE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap.XLVl. shorn of all its potency. The flesh of a diseased snake-bitten animal would be found to be poisoned vitiated in its properties ; that of a wounded animal affected, that of an old animal enfeebled its potency, and that of an extremely young animal immature in its virtue and hence would prove positively injurious to the system. IVIctrical Texts :—Dry meat is heavy, brings catarrh and a non-relish for food. The flesh of an animal killed poison fatal. The flesh animal of tender years may produce vomiting.

Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: The flesh of an old animal produces cough and dyspnoea, while the use that diseased animal may attended with a simultaneous derangement of the three fundamental humours of the body. Putrid meat pro- duces nausea, while the use of that of an enfeebled or emaciated animal tei;ids to aggravate the Vayu. Meat falling under category other than those described above should be deemed as good and whole- some. The flesh of a female quadruped, or of a male bird, of a small-bodied creature large-sized genus, and vice versa, principally recommended to be used amongst quadrupeds, birds, and animals. Similarly the flesh of a small-sized creature amongst large-bodied ones the same species should be pre- ferred as food. Chap. XLVI. Now we shall discourse the heav}' light character flesh obtained from different parts of the body of an«animal. Of the different fundamental principles of the body from the blood to the semen, each succeeding one heavier than the one* mediately preceding in the order of ennumeration. Similarly, the different limbs organs quartered animal such as), the thighs, the shoulders, the loins, the head, the legs_, the fore-extremities, the waist, the back, the skin, the kidneys, the liver and the intestines, each succeeding one respectively heavier than the one immediately preceding it in the order. Metrical Texts :—The head heavier than the shoulders the shoulders, than the waist ; and the waist, than the back. Similarty, the upper parts the Sakthi (thighs) are heavier than their lower ends. Of the seven fundamental principles of the organism (such the lymph chyle, blood, flesh, fat, bone, marrow, and semen) each succeeding one heavier than the principle immediately preceding in the order enumeration. The trunks all animals are heavy. The lo\k^er part of a female frame and the upper one male are respectively heavier [than their upper (fore) and lower (hind) parts.] The head and breast bird are heavier than its other limbs. The trunk the middle part bird equipoised facilitate the movements THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLVI. its pinions flight. The flesh fruit-eating ^bird produces state extreme parchedness the organism person using k food,) while that of a carnivorous one acts good constructive tonic. The flesh bird, vrhich Hves "fish, produces Pittam, while that one, which lives paddy (Dhan3^a), subdues the V^yu. Of the animals, that live dry land frequent marshy places, as well as of those which are domesticated, or are carnivorous in their habits, or are possessed of unbifurcated hoofs, live darting their preys, or dwell in holes, or are possessed of long legs, eat pricking, are the habit first scattering their food with their claws, the flesh of each preceding one is lighter and tends to give rise lesser secretion from the internal organs than the one immediately following it in the order of enumeration.

chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: Of animals belonging the same genus, the flesh one, which attains abnormally large size, should be rejected as inferior in pith or substance, and heavy regards digestion. The flesh about the region of the liver all animals should be regarded the very best respect its dietic proper- ties, in absence whereof the flesh a young animal not all used afllicted with any disease and just quartered that day, should regarded as coming next best. Clmp. XLVI. Authoritative verse on the sub- ject : —The age, body, development of limbs, tem- perament, natures, sex, constitution, size and habit of an animal should taken into consideration before determining whether its flesh wholesome not. The measure which it may used food with decided advantage the organism and what extent will purify improve its virtues, should also be considered. The Fruit Group (Phala-vargaj :---Xow shall discourse the specific virtues) fruits, such the Dadima, Amalaka, Kapittha, Vadara, Kola, Karkandhu, Sauvira, Shimvitikaphala^ Matulunga, Amra, Amrataka, Karamarda, Piyala, Lakucha, Bhavya, Paravata, Vetraphala, Prachinamalaka, Tintidika, Nipa, Koshamra, Amlika, Xaranga, and Jamvira, etc. Metrical Texts :—(We ,may describe the general properties fruits saying that) they are acid in taste and heav)' digestion, heat-making their potencies, (expectorant) (Kapham), generate Pittam and subdue the Vayu. Of the fruits enumerated the above list the Dadima leaves astringent after-taste and does not generate the Pittam inordinate degree.* It acts as an appetiser, brings Charaka and Bhatlaraka Harishchandra exclude Dadima (pome- granate) from the list Pittakara agents, while JejjadScharya opinion that it neither generates nor subdues the Pittam. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLVI. flesh and a relish for food, is palatable to the taste, and tends to constipate the bowels by (imparting a greater consistency to the fecal matter). Dadimas may be classi- fied into two distinct species according as they are sweet or acid to the taste. The sweet species subdues the three deranged bodily humours, while the acid one subdues the Vayu and Kapham. The fruit known the Amalaka has a taste blended of the sweet, pungent and astringent ones with a shade of the bitter. is laxative, spermatopoietic and beneficial to the eye-sight, and is capable subduing all the three deranged humours The Vataghna virtue this fruit should ascribed to its acid taste its power of subduing the Pittam, its sweetness and coolness and its efficacy subduing the Kapham, its affecting the tongue a dr)'- astringent substance. This fruit is by far the best known fruit as possessing high therapeutic properties. chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: The Karpura has a shghtly bitter taste, is aromatic, cooling its potency, and light digestion. is possessed of liquefacient properties and specially recommended in dryness of the mouth and fetid breath. The Lata-Kasturika is similar in its virtue to the Karpura and cooling and both laxative and diuretic. The pith of the Piyala fruit has a sweet taste, is spermatopoietic and subdues the Vayu and Pittam. The pith (the kernel found inside the seeds) of the Vaibhitaki fruit is intoxicating and subdues the deranged Vayu and Pittam. The pith or the kernel of the Kola fruit has an astringent sweet 5o8 THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLVl. taste, subdues the Vayu and Pittam allays thirst and alleviates nausea and vomiting. The kernel of the seed of the Amalaka resembles the last named pith in its properties. The kernels such fruits the Vijapuraka, Shampaka and Koshamra are sweet digestion, appetising, demulcent, and subdue the Vayu and Pittam. These kernels of seeds should be regarded as possessing identical properties as the pulps of the fruits from which they had been extracted. All fruits with the exception of Vilva are efficacious in their ripe and matured condition. The latter (Vilvas) have been enjoined to be eaten unripe for medicinal purposes well with view derive greater efficacy from iheir use. Unripe Vilvas are astringent and appe- tising their eftect, heat-making their potency, and have an astringent-pungent-bitter taste. Fruit, which bliglited that affected by any other disease that which has grown in an improper season, or raw or over-ripe, or worm-eaten, should be lejected unfit for use. Here the description the fruit- group is ended. The group of Potherbs :—Now we shall deal with the properties of potherbs. The fruits of such creepers as tlie Fushpaphala, Alavu and Kalindak des- tro}^ the Pittam, generate the Vayu, and slightly produce the Kapha m. They tend to increase the discharge of the stool and urine, and are sweet in taste and digestion. Chap. XLVI. Of these the tender Kushmandas are h'ght in digestion, heat-making in their potency, and contain a greater pro- portion of alkalifle matter. They are appetising, diuretic and pleasant their effect, and tend to subdue the action of all kinds of deranged humours, and proveVhole- some in cases of mental aberration, such as, insanity, etc. The Kalindak tends to reduce the quantit}^ of semen and impairs the eye- sight. It generates both the Vayu and Kapham, while an Alavu is a purgative, produces a con- dition of parchedness in the system, is heavy in digestion and extremely cooling its potency. The species, known as the bitter Alavu, is unpalatable and has emetic property, and subdues the Vayu and Pittam. The fruits such as the Trapusha, Ervaruka, Karkaru, and Shirna-Vrinta are heavy digestion and long retained in the stomach in an undigested state.

Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. english_text: The Udbhida Group :—Mushrooms are generally found to grow stacks straw (Paiala), or are seen vegetating on the stems of bamboo (^Venu) or sugar-cane (Ikshu , or as sprouting up from beneath tlie surface the ground (Udbhida), growing heap of decomposed cow- dung (Karisha.) these, those which grow stacks (decomposed) straw Chap. XLVI. ^23 (Palalam) are sweet in taste and digestion and tend to produce state of dryness the organism. They subdue the three'deranged humours of the body. Those which vegetate on the stems of sugar-canes (Ikshujam) have a sweet pungent taste. They leave an asti'ingent after-taste the mouth and are cooh'ng their potency. Mushrooms growing on decomposed cow- dung (Karisha) should regarded possessed properties similar to those of the preceding class.
