chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: Pindaluka, Madhvaluka, Hastyaluka, Kasthaluka, Shankhaluka, Raktdluka, Indivara and Utpala etc. alleviate lifemo^tysis, are cooling their potenc)', sweet their taste and lieavy digestion. The}" tend increase the semen large quantities and augment the quantity of milk the breast human mother. The bulb known the Vid^ri- Kanda has sweet taste, and acts construc- tive tonic and is spermatopoietic. cooling its potency, beneficial to the ^oice, and imparts strength to the system. is extremely diuretic and subdues the Vayu and Pittam, The Shatavari has sweet and bitter taste and spermatopoietic. subdues the Vayu and Pittam, the one belonging the large- sized species being palatable and appetising and tonic. The latter improves the intellect and proves curative cases mesenteric diarrhoea (Grahani) and piles, and is spermatopoietic, Rejuvenating, restora- tive, and cooling its potency. The under-sprouts this creeper (large-sized Shatavari) have bitter taste and subdue the Pittam and Kapham. The Visham* proves curative cases haemoptysis, and is long retained the stomach undigested state, though falls under the category sub- stances which can but partially digested. Bulbs lotus plants— though certain authorities aver that the inner lining or membranous fibres lotus-stem are called Visha, the outer covering being known as the MrinSlam, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLVI. is tasteless or insipid, generates the Va3'u, and is hard digest, producing condition of dryness the organism. The bulbs known the "Shringataka and the Kasheruka are heavy digestion, are long tained the stomach undigested state, and are cooling in their potency. The Pindalukam gener- ates the Kapham, heavy of digestion, and tends to enrage or agitate the bodily Vayu. The Surendrakanda is pungent in digestion, generates the Pittam and sub- dues the Kapham. The sprouts of the Venn are heavy of digestion, and tend to enrage the Kapham and V^3'u. The bulbs (Kanda) known as Sthula-Kanda, Shurana- Kanda, and Manaka, etc. have slightly astringent and pungent taste, and tend produce state dryness in the organism. They are heav}' of digestion, and subdue the Pittam, and are long retained the stomach in an undigested condition. The bulb known the Vrahakanda pungent taste and digestion, and is possessed of spermatopoietic, tonic^ rejuvenating and restorative properties. subdues the Kapham, and proves efficacious cases MehU, skin diseases (Kushtham), and aihuents due the presence parasites the intestines.

english_text: The top- piths of such trees, the Tala, Narikela, Kharjura etc., are sweet taste and digestion. They prove curative cases haemoptysis, and are spermato- poietic. They subdue the Vayu and generate the Kapham the body. Edible bulbs, which are tremel}' tender or immature, diseased, decomposed sprouting an improper season the year, or are worm-eaten, should be rejected unfit for use. This finishes the description of the bulb group. The Salt Gnoup (Lavana Varga) :—The different varieties of salt such the Saindhava, the Samudra, the Vida, the Sauvarchala, the Romaka and the Audbhidam (prepared from vegetable alkah), etc., should successively deemed more heat, Vayu, Kapham, and Pittam making, and more demulcent, sweeter and more purgative and diuretic, considered the inverse order of enumeration. Saindhava salt is beneficial the eyes, palatable, relishing, light, appetising, demulcent, slightly sweet digestion, spermatopoietic and cooling its "I'HE StTSHRUTA SAMHlTA. [Chap. XLVi. potency. one the most potent auxiliaries combating the action the deranged humours the body. The variety called the Samudram (sea-brine) sweet digestion, and not inordi- nately neat-making in its potency. not indiges- tible, but purgative, slightly demulcent, and does not in- ordinately generate the Pittam, and is good in attacks of colic pain (Shula). The variety known as Vida salt a little alkaline (in its taste) and is appetising. It tends to produce condition of dryness the organism, and proves beneficial in cases of colic (Shula,) and in dis- eases affecting the heart. It imparts a relish to food, is sharp and heat-making in its potency, and restores the deranged bodily Vayu to its normal state (restoring or setting aflow the nerve-current removing any obstruction). Sauvarchala salt light digestion, heat-making in its potency, and pungent in taste. appetising, has an agreeable aroma, removes any viscid- ness from the internal organism, and proves curative cases of abdominal glands, colic pain and incarceration scybala the bowels. Romaka salt sharp and intensely heat-making. Its action permeates the whole system immediately after its use, and pungent and light digestion. subdues the Vayu, tends increase the secretion of the internal organs, enters into the minutest capillaries the body, and purgative and diuretic. Audbhida salt light, sharp, and heat- making its potency. Chap. XLVI. ^29 It acts as a moistener to the internal organs, has a pung- ent bitter taste, and is charged with a small quantity of alkali. It permeates the minutest capillaries, and tends to restore the deranged Vayu its normal condition. Gutika salt destroys the deranged Vayu and K^pham, and vermifugal, appetising, digestant and purgative. It tends to aggravate the Pittam and acts as an inter- nal liquefacient or antifat.

The Shastika, barley, wheat, Rakta-Shali, and the pulses such the Mudga, the Adhakia, and the Masura should regarded the best and by far the most nutritious of the cereals which fall under the respective heads the Paddy Group (Dhanyavarga). The flesh of the Lava, Tittiri, Saranga, Kuranga, Ena, Kapin- jala, Mayura (peacock), Varmi and Kurma (tortoise) should be regarded as the best of those of all other ani- mals respectively belonging to their own species. fruits, those called Dddimva, A'malaka, Drdkshd, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. english_text: (so as to have all the butter and seasonings fully absorbed its body —Parishushka-Mansa) should be considered as a tooth- some, exhilarating, emulcent food, though heavy of diges- tion. It imparts firmness to the limbs and increases relish for food, improves the appetite and intellect, THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLVI. builds fresh tissues, gives rotundity the frame, and produces increased quantity of semen and (ojas) albumen in the organism. Tue meat cooked and prepared the foregoing manner, when minced and made into cakes, called Ullupta-Mansam, which resembles the above-said Parishushka-Mansam in its properties, though it is lighter than the latter owing to the fact of its having been cooked over a charcoal fire. The same meat, cooked on an iron rod over a charcoal fire, becomes little heavier far digestion concerned, inasmuch roasted with clarified butter, etc. The varieties of cooked meat such as, the Ullupta (minced meat), the Bharjita (fried), the Pishta (made into balls or cakes), the Pratapta (roasted with clarified butter over a charcoal fire), the Kandupachita (dipped in mustard oil and powdered aroiuatic condiments and roasted and done to a honey colour over a charcoal fire), the Parishuska and the Pradigdha go by the general name Shulyam (cabob). the varieties Shulyam, those which are boiled with oil should considered as heat-making in their potency, heavy digestion and generating the Pittam, Avhile those, which have been fried with clarified butter, should regarded light, appetising, agreeable, relishing, beneficial to the eyes, and cooling their potenc}'. They also subdue the Pittam and are pleasant to the Chap. XLVI. taste.* A tbin meat soup is a pleasant tonic, and proves beneficial in cases of dyspnoea, cough, and consumption. It subdues the Pittam and Kapham, destroys the V^yu, and has an agreeable taste. It is wholesome to persons of weak memory and reduced semen, as well as to those suffering from cachexia peculiar to chronic fever, from general emaciation of the body, from ulcerative endo- carditis (Urakshata;, or from diseases affecting the voice or the albumen (ojas). is known bring about adhesion and reduction of fractured and dislocated bones, and increases the quantity of semen and oja (albumen) subjects found wanting those two important principles hfe. Meat-soup, prepared with the juice the Dadima, etc., and seasoned with pungent condiments, increases the quantity of semen and tends to subdue the action of all the three deranged humours of the body. The use of meat of which tjie essence has been previously extracted fails contribute to the growth and strength of the organism, and is long retained in an Addifional text :— In the dish known as the Prataptam, the meat first fried with clarified butter, then pasted and flavoured with the addition of curd, pomegranate-juice etc.

english_text: proves curative in epilepsy and obesity, and subdues the deranged Vayu and Pittam. Mudga soup cooked with Amalakas acquires an astringent property, and proves beneficial in derangements of the Kapham and Pittam. ^4o THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA'. [Chap. XLVI. The soups of the Yava,Kola and Kulattha pulses destroy the Vayu and are beneficial to the larynx. Similarly, soups of all the pulses, which go by the name of Shami- Dh^nyas, increase the strength and rotundity of the body. Khala and Kamvalika soups are respectively agreeable and subdue the V^yu and Pittam. All soups cooked and prepared with the expressed juice pomegranate should be known as strength- giving, demulcent, and heavy digestion. They subdue the Vayu and Pittam. Soups, made and flavoured acid the mixture of whey, produce the Pittam and tend to vitiate the blood and besides aggravate the effects of anv imbibed poison lurking in the system. The soups and gruels respectively known iis the Kharayusha,* the Khara-Yavagu,t the ShadavaJ and the Panaka should prepared with the advice, and according to the instmctions of a physician. The soup, which cooked prepared without the admixture anv salt, condiments (black pepper, pungent spices^ any oily lardaceous substance, is called the Akrita-Yusha (unseasoned soup), whereas the one which cooked and seasoned witli the foregoing * Mudga soup prepared with whej', horse-apple, Amrul (oxalic corniculata), cumin seeds, black pepper and the roots of ChitA (Plumbago Zeylanica). t Gruel prepared in the manner of Khara-Yusha. t Mudga soup the composition of which things of sweet, saline, astringent, acid and pungent tastes largely enter, Chap. XLVI. SUTRASTHAXAAI. spices and* substances oil, clarified butter, etc., called a seasoned soup (Krita Yusha). Of the soups and extracts of meat respectively cooked and prepared with the modifications of cow-milk (curd, whey, etc.), Kanjika and acid fruits (pomegranate, etc.) each siltceeding variety should be deemed lighter and more wholesome than the one immediately preceding it in the order of enumeration. The soup cooked with the cream of the curd and the expressed juice of the Dadima called Kamvalika soup. Articles of food prepared withsesamum and its levigated cake, or those in the composition of which dried pot herbs, rice threshed out of sprouting paddy or Sindaki (a species of potherbs described before) enter, should be considered as hea\y of digestion. They subdue the Pittam and increase the Kapham. The Vatakas resemble the Sindakis in their properties, but are heavy of digestion and admit of being incompletely digested, giving rise to a kind of acid re-acjtion. Certain people oppressed with an acid taste naturally long for sweets, while others full satiet}" of sweet articles have a relish for acid things. Hence something acid is good for the eater of sweets, and sweets are good for men who have partaken of an acid food. Cold water and warm water> Asava ( wine), Madya (spirits), the soup of Mudga pulse etc., the juice of acid fruits, sour paddy gruel, milk and essence meat are generally used drinks after full meal. these, that alone which would prove beneficial person should be given him adequate quantity. The intelligent physician should determine the kind after- drink (potion) required in each case after taking into consideration the nature of the disease under treatment, the season of the year, and the properties of the solid or Hquid substances that enter into the composition the Chap. XLVI. SUTRASTHA'NAM. diet. all kinds after-potions, clear heavenly (atmospheric) water, kept a pure vessel, should be deemed the bes^, inasmuch as such water contributes in every way to the welfare (is conducive to the bodily growth) of a person throughout his life, and all the six different tastes are inherent that heavenly fluid. Briefl}^ we have stated the rule to be observed in respect of after-potions, now we shall dilate upon their specific pro- perties. Hot water should taken after any oih' lardaceous substance other than those known the oils Bhallataka and Tauvaraka. Certain authorities hold that the soup of Mudga pulse and sour rice gruel (Kanjika) should respectively taken summer and winter) after having taken large quantity of oil the course meal. Cold water should taken after cakes and hone)-, well after curd, Payasa porridge, and also cases poisoning and derangements due the effects wine. According several authorities tepid water should taken after an}- kind of cake (preparation of rice paste etc.) Milk meat essence should prescri'bed an after-potion to persons habituated to meals of Shall-' rice or Mudga pulse, or who have become fatigued with the labours battle long journey, w^ho are oppressed with the heat the sun blazing fire, well to those who would THE SI\SHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLM. found be o^-ercome from the effects of poison or wine. Sour rice gruel, or cream of curd should be drunk after having taken Masha pulse, etc. W,me (Madya) the proper after- drink for persons addicted it, which is also recommended alter a meat diet.

chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: Cold water acid fruit juice (such that the pomegranate, etc.) forms the best after-drink for those who are not the habit taking wine. Milk ambrosia persons enfeebled with arduous study, or excessive sexual inter- course, and to every one after an exposure the sun after a long troubled journey. Wine (Sura) is the after- drink for enfeebled subjects, and water saturated with honey is the potion for corpulent persons. Healthy per- sons may use a ^ariety of desserts and beverages during and after meals. Things which are demulcent their virtues and heat-making their potency should considered wholesome diet diseases due the deranged Vayu. Substances, which are heat- making their potetlcy and tend produce con- dition of parchedness (Ruksha) in the organism, should be prescribed as a wholesome diet diseases brought about through the action the deranged Kapham. Similarly, those which are sweet and cooling their potency, prove wholesome in Pittaja distempers. Milk and the expressed juice of sugar-cane are beneficial to persons suffering from haemoptysis. The Asavas (wines) of the Arka, Shelu, and Shirisha are beneficial those suffering from the effects of any poison. Chap. XLVI. 55^ Now wfe shall separately describe the after-drinks to be taken after having used the articles of food dealt with under the several groups discussed before. The acid soup of Indian Jujubes (Kola) should be taken after a meal consisting of any of the afore-said cereAls such as, the Shukadhanyam, Kudhanyam, etc. Sour gruel should be used after a meal of Mudga pulse, or of any similar grain. The Asa\a (vN'ine of the Pippali should be used after a meal of the long thighed venisons, the flesh those the Dhanvaja family. Kola and Vadara wines should used after having taken the cooked flesh the fowls of the \'ishkira species. The wine of Kshira-Vriksha should used after meal consisting of the flesh the Pratuda species. Cocoanut and date palm wines should used after having eaten the flesh of the cave-dwelling .(guha-shaya) species. Krishna Gandha wine should used after the flesh of the P^irna-Mriga (arboreous) familv. Ashvagandha wine should taken after the flesh the Prasaha species. Phalasara wine should be taken after the flesh of any the hole- dwelling (Vileshaya) animals. Triphala wine should be taken after the flesh of animal with unbifurcated^ hoops (Ekashapha). Khauira wine should taken after the flesh of an animal with bifurcated hoofs. The Asava (wine of Shringataka or Kasheruka should be taken after havmg eaten the flesh animal THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap, XLVi, the Kulechara (frequenting the shores lakes etc.l species. The same wines should be regarded as proper after-potions after meal consisting molluscs (Koshastha), of the flesh of an animal of the Padi (lizard) family. Asava of sugar-cane (vinegar) should be taken after having eaten the flesh of an animal of the Plava (diving) family. Mrinalam wine should taken after any Nadeya fish (whose habitat the river).

chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: Matulungu wine should be taken after the flesh of any of the sea fish (Samudra). Padma (lotus bulb), wine should be taken after acid fruits. Pomegranate, Vetra wines should be taken after astringent fruits. The Asava of Kanda [Khanda— D. R.] treated with the three pungent drugs known as the Trikatus should taken after sweet fruits. Sour and fermented rice gruel should be taken after Tala fruits, etc. The wine Durva, Nala, Vetra should taken after pungent fruits. The Asava of Shvadanstra or of Vasuka should taken after Pippalis, etc. Darvi, or Karira wines should be taken after Kushmandas, etc. The Asava of Lodhra should taken after taking the Chuchchu, potherbs etc. Triphala wine should taken after the Jivanti, potherbs, etc., and the same rule should observed respect of Kusumbha potherbs. The Asava (wine) of the drugs known as the Mahd Panchamula should taken after such potherbs Chap. XLVI.] the Mandufta-parni, etc. Wines of acid fruit should be taken after the pitli marrow such trees the Date-pahn, Tala, etc. The wine known the Surasava, or fermented rice gruel should he taken after an excess of Saindhava salt, etc. Water may be* taken absence the proper after-drink in all cases. Authoritative verses on the sub- ject.—Atmospheric or rain water the best of all drinks and cordials. That kind of water whole- some to a person to which habituated from his birth. Hot water is beneficial in derangements of the Vayu and Kapham, and cold water is good in diseases which have their origin the vitiated blood, the deranged Pittam. excessively heavy meal, as well the one which usually tends derange the fundamental humours of the body, may be easily and speedily digested with the help of a suitable after- drink. A proper after-potion taken after a meal brings on a fresh relish for food, increases the bulk of the body, acts as spermatopoietic beverage, disintegrates the combination or accumulation of the deranged bodily humours, soothes the organism, increases the softness of the body, removes the sense of fatigue and exhaus- tion, brings pleasurable sensations its train, stimulates the appetite, subdues or pacifies the deranged bodily humours, allays the thirst, improves the com- plexion, and imparts tone and vigour to the system. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLVI. Any beverage resorted to at the commencement of a meal tends to produce a gradual emaciation of the frame; the one taken during the course meal guards against both its thinning and corpulency, while the one drunk at the close of a meal serves greath' add its growth and rotundity. Hence, drinking at meals should be most judiciously determined and taken with the greatest forethought and discretion. The food carried down into the stomach person unused to such liquids long retains undigested and resists being con- verted into chyme being digested, and becomes a positive source of discomfort.

english_text: Hence, the use of an after- potion is imperatively obligatory on all human beings, except those suffering from dyspnoea, cough, ulcerative endocarditis ( Urahkshata), ptyalism, aphonia, and from diseases affecting the part of the body situated above the clavicles. After the use after-potion, a long walk, lengthy conversation, singing, sleeping and reading should refrained from, order that the im.bibed potion may not affect the stomach, and (aggravate the bodily humours) which taking lodgment about the regions of the throat and chest may give rise to a secretion of mucous, impair the appetite, develop such distressing symptoms as vomiting etc., and produce many other distempers as well. The lightness or heaviness of a food stuff does not Chap. XLVI. SUTkAS'l'HANAM. go contrary to nature.* These properties depend more upon the mode of cooking or preparing them than upon the nature of tl?e substances themselves;* more upon the quantity in which they are taken than the mode their cooking or preparation ;t and more upon the food (ht. boiled rice) itself than upon the quantity its use, I and more upon the time its maturity) than upon the food.§ The question lightness heaviness food stuff affects the idle, the unhealthy, the luxurious, and men delicate constitution and impaired appe- tite, and such nice discernment of the properties of an article of food is not imperatively obligatory on strong, healthy men of active habits and good healthy digestion, who are habituated hard and solid fares, and who can tolerably afford dispense with such thoughts altogether. Here ends the description of the group of after-potions. Hear me, O my child, discourse on the rules observed connection with eating. The kitchen * If it be asked why the flesh animal of the Janigh^l?, family light The answer would be because is naturally so. for example, Wihi corn naturally heavy of digestion, but* fried Vribi seeds are light, t for instance, a heavy food should be taken till the appetite is but half satisfied, while an article which light with regard digestion may eaten satiety. J for instance, of the varieties of food such the Manda, Peya, X'ilepi, Bhakta and Pishtaka, each succeeding one is heavier than ihe one immediately preceding As for example, newly hanested rice heavier than the one of a year's standing. THE SUSHKUTA SAMHITA. [Ch»p. XLM. should made spacious and kept clean and pure, and none but the trusted ones should have access thereto. The physician should get the food diet prepared experienced cooks (so as to impart the ta^ie and the colour desires), and have kept at a clean and pure spot, concealed from the -view of the public. After that, the food having been made innocuous the admixture anti-toxic medicines, and freed from all poison reciting (an Arthavan) mantra over and having been besprinkled with the water mystic incantation, should be served (to the king). chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: Clarified butter should be served out in a vessel of steel (Kanta- Loha) Peya, a silver bowl ; and all kinds fruit and confectionary (such the Laddukas), on leaves. The preparations of meat known as the Parishushka and Pradigdha Mansam should be served on golden plates fluid edibles and meat essences in silver bowls; Katvaras and Kharas stone utensils and cool boiled milk (Payah) copper vessels. Other drinks, wines and cordials should be given earthen pots ; and Raga- Sh^davas and Sattakas, in cool pure glass bowls, or vessels made of crystal and Vaidurya gems. The cook should ]ilace the bowls containing preparations of pulse, boiled rice and lambatives clean, spacious trays Chap. XLVI. SUTRASTHA'NAM. fanciful" design, and spread them out front fof the king/ All kinds desserts, confectionary and dry viands should served his right, while all soups etc., meat- essences, drinks, cordials, milk, Khada- Yusha, and Peya should placed his left. Bowls containing preparations of treacle, Raga-Shadava, and Sattaka should occup)- a place midway between the two sets of bowls described above. The intelligent physician, well conversant with the rules serving dishes as above laid down, should attend upon the king at his table, and spread out on the purified level floor solitary, beautiful, spacious, blissful, perfumed and flower-decorated chamber, and the king should partake those sacred and pleasant dishes, served neither hot nor cold, and cooked and seasoned in the desired mode, and possessed their specific tastes. The physician attendance should see that the king first partakes of the sweet dishes, then of the acid and saline, and the pungent and other ones the close of the meal.* First at the forepart of meal, such fruit as the pomegranate, etc., should be eaten, after that, Peyas and boiled rice and prepared dishes, * The lasle of sweel viands ealeii al the outset would naturally subdue llie Vdyu located in the stomach acid saline taste partaken of at the middle of a meal would rouse u|) the fire digestion located the pancreas (x\gnyashaya), while the pungent taste enjoyed at its close would lend to subdue the Kupham. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. : Chap. XLVi. well as confectionary. Certain authorities maintain that solid thick viands should eaten at the outset, while others a^er that the contrary should the rule. Of fruits, the Amalaka has the greatest humour- destroying properties, and is the most harmless of them all. Amalaka (fruit) is therefore recommended the outset, middle and the close of a meal. Such things the Mrinalam (the stem of a lotus plant), the Visham (the lotus bulb), Shaluka, Kanda and sugar-cane should eaten at the beginning of a meal, and never at its close.

Such things the Mrinalam (the stem of a lotus plant), the Visham (the lotus bulb), Shaluka, Kanda and sugar-cane should eaten at the beginning of a meal, and never at its close.

Food eaten with good appetite tastes pleasant and relishing. The food which congenial to one's temperament begets discomfort after the eating. Light food soon digested. Xl.VI.j SUTRASTHAXAM. fluid components not iniperfecth' digested, nor attended by any acid reaction. Moderation food leads to a happy and perfect digestion and tends maintain the fundamental ]irinciples the bodv tlieir normal state. During the cold months, when the nights are longer, substances, which tend to subdue the bodily humours which are naturally deranged during that season, should be eaten in the morning, while during the seasons, when the days are inordinately long, things which are congenial those seasons should eaten the afternoon. spring and autumn when days and nights are equal, the meal should taken just the middle part of the day and night.* meal should not eaten before the appointed time, nor before the appetite has fully come. Similarly, over or insufficient eating should equally refrained from. Eating at an improper time and before the system feels light and free brings large number of diseases, and may ultimately lead to death. A meal eaten hour long after the appoiiited time tends to aggravate the bodily Vayu, which affects thg This rule holds good in the case of persons, who eat a single meal in the course of a day and night. Those, who are in the habit of eating two meals a day, should eat a light half meal at one and quarter Prahara in the morning and another between the third and the fourth Praharas in the afternoon (Panjikdkfira). According Jejjada, the nieals should be between the third and ilie fourth Prahara both in the day and night. THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chc>p. XLVI. digestive fire, and offers serious obstacles in the way its digestion. The food thus digested with difficulty in the stomach creates discomforts and destroys all desire for second meal. Insufficient diet gives but adequate^satisfaction, and tends weaken the bod)'. Over eating, on the contrary, is attended with such dis- tressing symptoms, as languor, heaviness the body, disinclination for movements, and distension the stomach, accompanied by rumbling in the intestines, etc. Hence it behoves a man to take only as much food he can easily digest, which should be well cooked and made possess all the commendable (adequately nutritive) properties.

chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: Moderation in diet the golden rule, besides taking into consideration the demerits of particular food before partaking thereof and the nature of the time (day or night) it is eaten. Boiled rice food (Annam ) which is impure and dirty, infested with poison, out which another has eaten a portion as well as that which full weeds, pebbles, dust etc., which the mind instinctivel)^ repels, cooked the previous day or which has been kept standing over-night, well that which insipid emits fetid smell, should similarly rejected. Also food which has been cooked long ago, has become cold and hard, and has been rewarmed or which has been imperfectly strained, or is burnt and insipid should also not serve as food. More Chap. XLVi. and more" palatable dishes should be successively taken the course of a meal. During the course of a meal, the mouth should frequently rinsed gargled inasmuch the palate thus constantly being cleansed becomes more susceptible taste, and anythifig eaten thereafter relished the better and gives all the pleasures of a first morsel. The palate affected with a sweet taste at the outset fails to appreciate the tastes of the successive dishes. Hence, the mouth should washed at intervals during the meal. Sweet food eaten with a relish pleasurably affects the mind, brings joy, energy, strength, and happiness its train, and contributes to the growth of the body ; whereas the one contrary character is attended with opposite effects. The food, which does not satiate a man even after repeated eating, should be considered agree- able (Svddu) him. After finishing meal water should be drunk in a quantity whij:h would be beneficial. Food adhering to the teeth should be gently drawn out means tooth-pick, inasmuch not removed a kind of fetor is produced in the mouth. The Vayu increased after the completion digestion, the Pittam, during the continuance the process, while the Kapham is increased immediately after the act of eating. Hence, the Kapham to be subdued after the close of a meal, and the intelli- gent eater should attain that end partaking fruit THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLVi. astringent, pungent, bitter taste, chewing a betel leaf prepared with broken areca nut, camphor, nutmeg, clove, etc., or by smoking, or by means of anything that instantaneously removes the viscidity Mn the cavity of the mouth, and permeates it with Its own essence. Then the eater should take rest, like king, till the sense of drowsiness incidental to eating is removed. After this he should walk a hundred paces and lie down in a bed on his left side. After eating, a man should enjoy soft sounds, pleasant sights and tastes, sweet per- fumes, soft and velvety touch, short anything that ravishes the soul and enwraps the mind with raptures of joy, since such pleasurable sensations greatly help the process of digestion.

english_text: Drinking of an abnormal quantity of water, irregular eating, voluntary suppression of any natural urging of the bodv, sleep the day, keeping late hours the night, partaking light food with strong appetite are the factors which interfere with the proper digestion food and develop symptoms indigestion. The food taken by person under the influence of envv. passion, greed, anger, etc., a man suffering from a chronic distemper, is not properly digested. Types of Indigestion :—A case indi- s^estion which the undis^ested food matter Chap. XLVI. 3UTRASTHAXAM. quires a sWeet taste called chymous mucous; indigestion (Amajirnam), that which the undi- gested food acquires acid taste the stomach called Vidagdha indigestion. The form which the food matter brought down into the stomach is partially or irregularly digested (one portion* being digested, the other being not) followed by a pricking piercing pain the stomach and entire suppres- sion the flatus, called Vishtabdha indigestion. The type known as indigestion unassimilated chyle is characterised the absence of any acid sour eructations, but the patient feels no inclination for food inspite of the normal character of the eructations, if any. The type further characterised pain about the region of the heart, and water-brash. The unfavourable symptoms of indigestion are sudden loss conscious- ness, delirium, vomiting, water-brash, languor with a gone-feeling in the limbs, and v^tigo, etc., which may end in death. Fasting is beneficial in a case of Amajirnam indiges- tion. Ejection of the contents of the stomach giv^s relief in a case of Vidagdha indigestion. Fomentation will alleviate a case of Vishtavdha indigestion, while in an indigestion of unassimilated chyle, the patient should confined bed and fomentations and digestive medicines should be administered as well. In a case Vidagdha indigestion the patient should be made THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [ Chap. XLVI. vomit the contents his stomach with the help warm water saturated with salt, while case chymous indigestion the patient should forego all food till he is restored to his natural condition. patient suffering' from indigestion whose S5'stcm has been cleansed and lightened with the abovesaid appliances should fasting till restored his natural condition regards the strength and humours of the body. The eating together of both wholesome and unwhole- some articles food called promiscuous eating (Samashanam). Over or insufficient eating at intervals and at improper seasons goes the denomination irregular eating (Visham^shanam). Eating before former meal is thoroughly digested in the stomach called Adhyashanam. These three kinds of eating are injurious, and speedily give rise tp a variety of diseases, or may be ultimately Attended with fatal consequences.

chapter: Chapter XLVI: Now we shall discourse the Chapter which treats food and drink (Annapa'na-Vid'hi- madhya'yam). Having made obeisance to the holy Dhanvantari, Sushruta said :—"You have stated before that food determines the origin beings and forms the chief source of their bodily strength and complexion, as well as of the albuminous (Ojas) principle in their organisms. Food primarily depends upon the six different tastes. Moreover tastes are inherent substances. Again the substances, with their specific tastes, virtues, potencies and reactionary chemical transformations, tend diminish increase the deranged humours and the fundamental principles of the body, also bring about normal equilibrium amongst them. Food is the primary cause of the origin, continuance and dissolution even of such beings the divine Brahma, etc. Life impossible without food. Food is the source of the growth, strength, and healthful glow organic beings. food that imparts strength to the organs of sense and makes them operative their respective fields action. irregularity (Vaishamya) diet which brings about ill-health. Diet may be divided into four kinds such as^
english_text: Evolution or Emanativeness is somewhat identical its virtue with expansiveness with the exception that permeates the organism its undigested state, aud tends to disintegrate the Chap. XLVL] root prfhciples the body. Instantaneoiisness, like the expansion of a drop of oil cast into a bowl of water, helps permeate the whole organism simultaneously with the use of a drug. Subtility is the quality in virtue of which a thing can penetrate into the smallest capillaries and channels the body.» These twenty qualities virtues have been described they are. Now we shall describe the transformations which the food stuffs undergo the organism. This animated organism is composed of the five fundamental material principles, and the food living organic being necessarily partakes the character of its corporeal components. The food, which consists of live funda- mental material principles (elements), digested, its turn, by the five elemental heat or fire, and each of its constituent principles goes to augment its own kindred in the human organism.^ The food, which followed by a sweet, digestive reaction, goes to increase the quantity of Pittam, while the one, which completely digested, contributes towards the augmentation the bodily Vayu (nerv^e force). The stool and the urine are the excreted portion the well-digested food, the lymph chyle is the substance drawn from the well-matured chyle, (as has been described in the chapter on the description Blood, Chap. XIV. the present work). The THE SUSHRUTA SAMHITA. [Chap. XLVl. lymph chyle carried a\vay by the vital Vayu known as Vyana tends strengthen all the fundamental pnnciples of the body. Kaphftm the excreted portion the lymph chyle, the Pittam that the blood, the waxy impurities found the tympanum, etc., are the excreted portions flesh. Perspiration the excreted matter fat. Nails and hair are the excreted portions of the bones. The waxy deposits found in the corners of the e3'es, and the oily secretions which sometimes mark the skin are the excreted por- tions of marrow. With the dawn of day a man wakes from sleep, and his heart unfolds like lotus flower, and so remains till sleep folds his eyelids. Hence, the root principles the body continue non- humid during the state waking. Accordingly, man may partake meal night, even the food eaten in the day continues till then undigested, without the fear of committing the physical sin Adhyashanam (super-eating). But the reverse the case respect similar conduct the night when man has recourse sleep and his heart Remains constricted state unconsciousness, and the root principles his body become loose and charged with humidity. Hence, beneficial fast on the following day in the event of the food taken overnight being found to be not properly digested. Chap. XLV. SUTR.ASTHANAM.
